reference: – A well-dressed tourist visiting Germany from China was robbed in Heidelberg, but he filled out the wrong set of paperwork in an attempt to report the crime. Known only as Mr. L and fluent only in Mandarin, he went to the town hall instead of the police station, signed an asylum application instead of a missing item report, handed over his passport, had his fingerprints taken, and was bused 220 miles away to a refugee shelter in Duelmen. All the while he did his best at charades to communicate that he just wanted his passport so he could visit Italy and France, reports the Local. But the 31-year-old had become entangled in the German asylum system, which has processed 1 million refugees in the last year alone, reports the Guardian. Most have fled Syria, Iraq, and Afghanistan; very few have sought asylum from China. "He spent 12 days trapped in our bureaucratic jungle because we couldn't communicate," the head of a Red Cross refugee center tells Reuters. The well-dressed man from Beijing stood out as it was, and he also clearly had a story to tell. But it wasn't until someone went to a local Chinese restaurant for help that it was suggested Red Cross workers use a smartphone app to translate, which did the trick. After more paperwork, which was further delayed by paperwork mishandled when Mr. L first entered the country, the tourist was sent on his way south. The entire ordeal took 12 days, and yet he walked away politely and without anger. "He said Europe was not what he had expected," the Red Cross official says. (Read about another tourist mishap.)

final_summary:
On October 22, 2018, 21-year-old University of Utah student Lauren McCluskey was fatally shot by her ex-boyfriend, 37-year-old Melvin Rowland, in a parking lot on campus. The attack occurred after a month-long relationship between the two had ended, with McCluskey discovering Rowland's history as a registered sex offender. Prior to the shooting, Rowland had been stalking McCluskey, attempting to extort her by threatening to post compromising photos of the couple online unless she paid him $1,000. 

According to University of Utah police chief Dale Brophy, McCluskey had initially declined police assistance in dealing with Rowland, but eventually reported receiving numerous emails and messages using different names trying to lure her to locations, which investigators now believe all came from Rowland. This behavior led Brophy to describe Rowland as a "master manipulator."

The events leading up to the shooting unfolded over several days. On October 9, McCluskey ended her relationship with Rowland after discovering his true identity and background. Over the next few days, Rowland continued to try to contact McCluskey, including sending a text message posing as a police officer to lure her out of her dorm. On October 13, McCluskey reported the extortion attempt to police and deposited $1,000 into an account to prevent the release of the compromising photos.

However, the formal investigation into the extortion case did not begin until October 19 due to workload issues within the police department. Additionally, despite knowing Rowland was a registered sex offender, police were unaware that he was on parole. 

In the days preceding the shooting, Rowland was seen on surveillance footage at various locations around the University of Utah campus, indicating his attempts to confront McCluskey. On the day of the shooting, Rowland spent time socializing with McCluskey's friends in her dorm building before confronting her in the parking lot, dragging her into a car, and shooting her multiple times.

Following the shooting, Rowland was picked up by a woman he had met online, with whom he went to dinner, visited the state Capitol, and then showered at her apartment. The woman later contacted police when she recognized Rowland's name in news reports about the shooting and is not expected to face charges. After dropping Rowland off at a coffee shop, police tracked him to a local church, where he ultimately took his own life.

An investigation into the handling of Rowland's parole revealed that he had been granted parole in April 2018, claiming to have undergone a transformation after participating in a peer leadership program in prison. However, he had previously been sent back to prison twice for violating the terms of his parole, including possession of pornography and failure to complete therapy. 

University of Utah President Ruth Watkins announced that the institution would conduct external reviews of campus safety measures and police protocols to identify potential areas for improvement. Governor Gary Herbert also initiated an investigation into the handling of Rowland's parole, stating that "you never know when these things are going to occur."

reference: – Lauren McCluskey's killer was a master manipulator who penned messages under various names—even posed as a police officer—in an effort to lure and extort the 21-year-old University of Utah student before her death, according to police. Nine days before she was fatally shot on campus by an ex-boyfriend later found dead of a self-inflicted gunshot wound, McCluskey told university police she'd wired $1,000 to an account to prevent the release of "compromising" photos of her and the killer, 37-year-old Melvin Rowland, says University Police Chief Dale Brophy, per Fox News and ESPN. McCluskey had ended her relationship with the registered sex offender days earlier after discovering he'd lied about his background. "If his lips were moving, he was lying," Brophy says of Rowland, who described himself as a manipulator of women at a 2012 parole hearing. Rowland told an acquaintance, for example, that he needed a gun because his girlfriend wanted to learn to shoot, reports CBS News. After shooting McCluskey multiple times in the backseat of a car he’d driven to campus, Rowland was picked up by a woman he'd met online. Before she recognized him as the murder suspect and called police, the pair went to dinner and back to the woman's apartment where Rowland showered before leaving, Brophy says. Meanwhile, the investigation into McCluskey's extortion complaint had been delayed by workload issues, and the Utah Department of Corrections had yet to be notified. Gov. Gary Herbert announced an investigation into the handling of the case on Thursday. Hindsight is 20/20, but "you never know when these things are going to occur," he said. (McCluskey was on the phone with her mom at the time.)

final_summary:
Pope Francis sparked widespread attention and praise within the LGBTQ+ community after stating that the Roman Catholic Church owes apologies to individuals who have been offended or exploited by the institution, including gay people, the poor, women, and children forced into labor. This announcement came during a press conference aboard the papal plane returning from Armenia, where the Pope had visited to commemorate the centenary of the Armenian Genocide.

Francis emphasized that the Church should not discriminate against or judge individuals based on their sexual orientation, citing the Catechism of the Catholic Church, which states that homosexuals should be respected and accompanied pastorally. He acknowledged that while the Church may condemn certain behaviors associated with homosexuality, it should not do so based on theological grounds but rather due to concerns over political behavior.

This stance marks a significant shift in the Church's tone towards the LGBTQ+ community, with many hailing it as a breakthrough. Reverend James Martin, a Jesuit priest and editor at large of America magazine, referred to the Pope's apology as a "groundbreaking moment," highlighting the importance of forgiveness in the Christian faith and the need for the Church to acknowledge its past wrongdoings.

The Pope's comments also extended beyond the LGBTQ+ community, with him calling for the Church to seek forgiveness from those it has marginalized, including women, the poor, and children forced into labor. He specifically mentioned the Church's history of blessing weapons, implying that this practice should also be acknowledged and apologized for.

Francis's statements on the European Union (EU) and Brexit were equally notable, with him advocating for a more decentralized and creative approach to the EU's structure. He cautioned against throwing the baby out with the bathwater, urging leaders to find ways to revitalize the union without sacrificing its core principles.

During his visit to Armenia, the Pope described the mass killings of Armenians under Ottoman Turkish rule in World War I as genocide, a term that Turkey disputes. However, the Pope's spokesman clarified that the Pontiff's intention was not to incite conflict but to promote understanding and bridge-building between nations.

The Pope's comments on the dual papacies of himself and Pope Emeritus Benedict XVI were also noteworthy, with Francis affirming that there is only one Pope, emphasizing Benedict's role as the Pope Emeritus and a wise elder statesman who prays for him.

reference: – It was quintessential Pope Francis: On a plane journey back from a foreign trip on Sunday, the pontiff made a statement that once would have been considered jaw-droppingly radical. In a response to a reporter's question, Francis said the church should apologize for offending and discriminating against gays over the years, the BBC reports. "I believe that the church not only should apologize to the person who is gay whom it has offended," he said, "but has to apologize to the poor, to exploited women, to children exploited for labor; it has to ask forgiveness for having blessed many weapons." Francis stressed that when he says "the church," he means Christians. "The Church is holy, we are sinners!" he said. Francis—who made waves three years ago when he asked: Who am I to judge gays?—has been hailed by gay rights groups for his groundbreaking statements, though he has reaffirmed church policy that actually having gay sex is sinful, the BBC notes. The pope was on his way home from Armenia, where he once again enraged Turkish authorities by describing the mass killing of Armenians a century ago as genocide, reports CNN. He also addressed Britain's vote to leave the EU, saying that while "something ... is not working in that unwieldy union" and EU countries should be given more independence to creatively deal with their problems, "let's not throw the baby out with the bathwater."

final_summary:
On November 20, 2017, the Georgia Dome, a 25-year-old stadium located in Atlanta, Georgia, was scheduled to be demolished in a controlled implosion. The event was live-streamed by the Weather Channel, with editor Jason Rudge setting up his camera across the street from the stadium to capture the spectacle. For 40 minutes prior to the demolition, Rudge live-streamed the crowd gathering for the event, anticipating a powerful display of the stadium's destruction.

According to plans, the explosives wired inside the dome would detonate for 12 seconds, followed by the complete collapse of the 71,250-seat stadium into a pile of rubble within three seconds. As the countdown began, a man announced over a loudspeaker, "Five. Four. Three. Two. One," signaling the start of the implosion. However, just as the initial explosion ripped through the dome's roof, a public bus suddenly appeared in front of the camera, obstructing the view.

Rudge expressed frustration and anger, shouting at the bus to move, but it remained stationary until the implosion was complete. The bus then drove away, leaving behind a massive cloud of dust and debris. Despite the unexpected interruption, the Weather Channel later posted the footage online, editing out profanities and titling it "Bus Photobombs The Weather Channel's Stream of Georgia Dome Implosion." The incident quickly went viral, becoming a meme shared by various media outlets, including Sports Illustrated, the Atlanta Hawks, and others.

The bus's timely arrival was playfully mocked in a series of humorous posts and videos, with some even penning a poem about the event. While the Weather Channel initially missed capturing the desired footage, the unexpected turn of events ultimately made the implosion more memorable, with many predicting that the image of the bus blocking the camera would be remembered for years to come.

reference: – "I am CRYING." That was just one online reaction to an ill-fated photo shoot that was meant to capture a planned stadium implosion. Photographers gathered near the Georgia Dome in downtown Atlanta on Monday, waiting to snap a shot of the controlled destruction of the 71,000-seat structure, and among them was the Weather Channel's James Crugnale, who arrived nearly an hour ahead of time to nab his "perfect" vantage point across the street and livestream the gathering crowd, per the Washington Post. Except that it was also the perfect pull-over spot for a city bus, which pulled up right in front of Crugnale at the exact moment black smoke started to spew from the top of the dome to mark the beginning of the implosion. "Get out of the way, bus! What the f---?!" an incredulous Crugnale can be heard saying, in between frustrated yells, in the now-viral video of his experience. The bus lingered for less than 20 seconds, but by the time it pulled away, the dome had already disappeared into a massive cloud of white dust. "TFW you stream the #GAdome being demolished for 40 minutes and a bus stops in front of the camera at the exact moment it implodes," Crugnale lamented on Twitter.

final_summary:
Eric Deaton, a 42-year-old New Lebanon resident and candidate for the U.S. Senate representing the Constitution Party, has been indicted on a charge of unlawful sexual conduct with a minor. According to the indictment, which was filed by a Montgomery County grand jury, Deaton allegedly engaged in sexual conduct with a 13- to 15-year-old girl between March 20, 2006, and March 25, 2007. This period coincides with Deaton's tenure as an elder at his church, where he reportedly met the alleged victim. 

Deaton's interactions with the minor involved tutoring sessions and occasional payment for odd jobs, with some instances taking place at his residence. Hotel receipts and video evidence obtained by law enforcement are expected to play a crucial role in the prosecution's case. However, Deaton's attorney, Jon Paul Rion, has raised questions about the timing of the indictment, suggesting it may be politically motivated given Deaton's candidacy for the U.S. Senate seat being vacated by Senator George Voinovich.

Rion also pointed out that Deaton had passed a four-hour polygraph test, which he claimed would help prove his client's innocence. Deaton himself denied the allegations, labeling them "lies" during a press conference. He expressed confidence in his ability to defend himself in court and emphasized his commitment to upholding constitutional rights through the due process.

If found guilty, Deaton faces a maximum sentence of five years in prison and a $10,000 fine. His trial is set to begin on September 14th. Despite the serious nature of the charges, Deaton has announced his intention to continue his campaign for the U.S. Senate, citing support from various Tea Party groups and other organizations.

reference: – Another day, another political sex scandal—but this one involves a minor. Ohio's Eric W. Deaton, Senate candidate for the conservative Constitution Party, was indicted Tuesday for having alleged sexual contact with a young girl, the Dayton Daily News reports. Deaton, 42, is accused of having unlawful sexual conduct with the girl, who he reportedly met at his church, while she was between the ages of 13 and 15 ... and he's calling the whole mess "politically motivated." He adds that “the powers that be don’t like” his decision to run for office, and says he'll stay in the race despite his Sept. 14 arraignment and the fact that he faces the possibility of five years in prison. According to WDTN Dayton, police say they have hotel surveillance video and receipts that prove their case ... and Deaton's lawyer says those very items (somehow) help prove that his client is innocent. Deaton, a married Cub Scout leader with three kids, says he has Tea Party endorsements.

final_summary:
A fatal shooting occurred at a party in a garage apartment located in the 300 block of West 23rd Street in the Heights area of Houston. The victim, later identified as Daniel Barber, was shot in the chest by Jason Griffin, who was also attending the party. According to reports, Barber had put on a bulletproof vest prior to the shooting. However, it remains unclear whether the bullet penetrated the vest or if the shooter intentionally missed the protective gear. 

Griffin, 39, was subsequently charged with manslaughter, felon in possession of a weapon, and unlawful possession of body armor. Witnesses stated that Griffin had taken out his gun and shot Barber after the latter made a comment suggesting he wanted to be shot. Griffin's girlfriend claimed that he did not intend to harm Barber, stating that Griffin believed his gun was unloaded and only realized its contents when he fired it. Following the shooting, Griffin reportedly left the scene but returned to speak with authorities. He was taken into custody while receiving treatment for a separate medical issue at a local hospital. Griffin's past criminal history includes a 2001 charge of aggravated robbery in a Fort Bend County court.

reference: – A man was shot dead while wearing a bulletproof vest at a Houston party and police say they've been left to uncover whether the tragedy happened as part of some kind of prank gone terribly wrong. Jason Allen Griffin was arrested early Sunday morning following the death of a man named Daniel Barber. Per KTRK, cops say Griffin and Barber had been at a party together when Barber was shot in the chest while wearing the vest, allegedly by Griffin. He was arrested and has been charged with manslaughter, unlawful possession of a firearm, and felon in possession of a gun. According to the Houston Chronicle, police are trying to determine whether the men thought they were playing some kind of game. A woman who identified herself to KTRK as Griffin's girlfriend, Mary Warstler, said the suspect didn't even know his gun was loaded. What's more, Warstler claims that Barber told Griffin "shoot me" before Griffin allegedly pulled the trigger. After Barber was shot, Warstler said Griffin ripped off the vest in a bid to help the victim, but it was too late. Warstler said Griffin was so distraught following the shooting that he was suicidal and that he had to be checked into the hospital for seizures.

final_summary:
In July 2014, Jonathan J. Pollard's hopes for early release from prison were dashed when a federal panel denied his request for parole after serving 29 years of a life sentence for spying on behalf of Israel. However, this setback triggered an intense effort by his lawyers to secure a favorable outcome at his next parole hearing, scheduled for a year later when he would have completed 30 years behind bars. To support their case, they submitted letters, statistical data, and evidence demonstrating that Pollard had demonstrated good behavior while incarcerated and posed no risk of reverting to a life of espionage.

After a prolonged campaign by Pollard's pro bono lawyers, Eliot Lauer and Jacques Semmelman, the United States Parole Commission ultimately agreed that he met the necessary criteria for parole. As a result, Pollard, now 60 years old, will be released from the federal prison in Butner, North Carolina, on November 20. This development comes exactly 30 years after his initial arrest.

Pollard's impending release has significant implications for US-Israeli relations, particularly given the ongoing tensions surrounding the Iran nuclear deal. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has long advocated for Pollard's release, and the issue has repeatedly arisen during diplomatic talks between the two nations. While the White House maintains that Pollard's parole was granted solely based on standard procedures and not influenced by foreign policy considerations, the coincidence of his release timing may still contribute to easing some of the strain between the US and Israel.

The White House has officially stated that President Barack Obama does not intend to alter the terms of Pollard's parole, despite the potential benefits of releasing him ahead of schedule. The Justice Department and Pollard's lawyers have also confirmed the November 20 release date, bringing an end to nearly three decades of imprisonment for the convicted spy.

Pollard's impending release has significant implications for US-Israeli relations, particularly given the ongoing tensions surrounding the Iran nuclear deal. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has long advocated for Pollard's release, and the issue has repeatedly arisen during diplomatic talks between the two nations. While the White House maintains that Pollard's parole was granted solely based on standard procedures and not influenced by foreign policy considerations, the coincidence of his release timing may still contribute to easing some of the strain between the US and Israel.

The White House has officially stated that President Barack Obama does not intend to alter the terms of Pollard's parole, despite the potential benefits of releasing him ahead of schedule. The Justice Department and Pollard's lawyers have also confirmed the November 20 release date, bringing an end to nearly three decades of imprisonment for the convicted spy.

reference: – After 30 years in prison, convicted spy Jonathan Pollard will indeed go free this year. His lawyers and officials with the Justice Department have confirmed the news, which surfaced last week, reports CNN. Pollard, a former Navy intelligence analyst who got caught passing classified information to Israeli operatives, will go free when he becomes eligible for mandatory parole on Nov. 21. US officials could have objected to the parole and kept the 60-year-old in prison on national security grounds, explains the New York Times. "Mr. Pollard is looking forward to being reunited with his beloved wife Esther," says a statement issued by his lawyers. He "would like to thank the many thousands of well-wishers in the United States, in Israel, and throughout the world, who provided grass roots support by attending rallies, sending letters, making phone calls to elected officials, and saying prayers for his welfare." US officials have denied that his release is any way linked to soothing Israel's anger over the Iran nuclear deal.

final_summary:
A man, described as a white male in his late 20s to early 30s, approximately 6 feet tall, with a thin to medium build, is being sought by authorities in connection with a bank robbery in the Denver Tech Center. According to the FBI Rocky Mountain Safe Streets Task Force, the individual took a taxi to the Public Service Credit Union located at 6850 S. Yosemite St., where he displayed a small pistol and demanded an undisclosed amount of money from a teller before fleeing the scene.

Video footage obtained from the taxi shows the suspect's face, which has been made available to the public in hopes that someone may recognize him. Additionally, photographs taken within the bank reveal the suspect wearing a white dust mask covering his lower face. 

It is reported that the suspect entered the taxi near the Bellco Theater in downtown Denver and instructed the driver to wait outside while he carried out the robbery. Following the incident, the suspect allegedly directed the driver to take him to Denver International Airport. However, officials have stated that they do not know whether the suspect actually boarded a flight after arriving at the airport.

The FBI is urging anyone with information regarding the identity of the suspect to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867 or text "DMCS" along with their tip to 274637.

reference: – Authorities say a man who held up a Denver bank got a cab ride to and from the robbery. The Denver Post reports the FBI is asking for the public's help to find the suspect. Authorities say the man took a cab to the bank, asked the driver to idle while he went inside, and then had the cabbie drive him to Denver International Airport. Officials say it's unknown if the suspect actually boarded a plane after going to the airport, the AP reports. Video from inside the cab clearly shows the man's face, and pictures taken in the Public Service Credit Union show the man wearing what appears to be a white dust mask while inside the bank, where he threatened a teller with a small handgun.

final_summary:
In June, Sarah Anne Markham, a self-proclaimed vegan, was arrested in Casselberry, Florida, on charges of child neglect after allegedly refusing to take her newborn son, Caleb, to the hospital due to her strong convictions against consuming animal products. According to police reports, a pediatrician had informed Markham that her baby was dehydrated and losing weight, necessitating immediate medical attention at Florida Hospital South. However, instead of adhering to the doctor's orders, Markham opted to use a soy-based formula from Whole Foods Market, believing that since it was sold at the store, it must be free from animal-derived ingredients. When questioned by police, Markham claimed that she had consulted a "natural" or "vegan" doctor, but failed to provide any further details about the individual. 

Markham's decision not to seek medical help for her infant led law enforcement officials to obtain a warrant to enter her residence, where they found her uncooperative and unwilling to prioritize her child's well-being. After using a locksmith to gain entry, police discovered that Markham had been attempting to treat her baby with a non-traditional approach, despite the doctor's explicit instructions to seek conventional medical care. As a result, the newborn was taken into state custody and transported to Florida Hospital South for emergency treatment.

Following her arrest, Markham posted bail and eventually regained custody of her son, Caleb, after a Seminole County judge ruled in her favor, citing a lack of evidence supporting claims of abuse or neglect. In a subsequent court hearing, the judge ordered Markham to provide a nutritionist's report within ten days, allowing her to regain full custody of her child. Caleb, who initially weighed significantly less than expected, has since gained weight and is thriving on a soy formula, according to his grandmother.

reference: – A judge in Florida has allowed a vegan mom who defied a doctor's orders to regain custody of her infant son, reports WFTV. The strange case began in June when police in Casselberry charged Sarah Markham with neglect and took away 2-week-old Caleb. The charges came after a pediatrician diagnosed Caleb as dehydrated and underweight, and told Markham to take him to the hospital immediately, recounts WKMG. Instead, Markham bought soy formula to supplement her breast milk. When she didn't show up at the hospital, police came to her house, and she told them she wanted a second doctor's opinion and stressed that she did not want her son getting formula from animal products. Today, a Seminole County judge granted her custody again with the provision that she provide a nutritionist's report in 10 days. "There's no case, there's no abuse, there's no neglect—there's simply a doctor who has been challenged by a mother, and he didn't like it," says Caleb's grandfather, Bo Markham. The child has been with his grandparents since he was taken out of his mother's care, and now weighs about 17 pounds. "He's still on a soy formula," says Bo Markham. The criminal neglect charges are still pending, though Sarah Markham's attorney—Mark O'Mara, who defended George Zimmerman—says he expects they will be dismissed now. (PETA wants an Indiana sheriff to feed an alleged cannibal a vegan diet.)

final_summary:
On Wednesday, a former UCLA student, Mainak Sarkar, 38, carried out a murder-suicide on the UCLA campus, killing engineering professor William S. Klug, 39, and then taking his own life. According to Los Angeles Police Chief Charlie Beck, Sarkar had driven from his home in St. Paul, Minnesota, to the UCLA campus, arriving armed with two semi-automatic pistols and multiple rounds of ammunition. 

Before the shooting, Sarkar had left a note at the UCLA scene requesting that authorities check on his cat, which led police to his Minnesota residence. Inside, they found a "kill list" containing the names of two UCLA professors, including Klug, and a woman, later identified as Ashley Hasti, 31, who was Sarkar's estranged wife.

Hasti's body was found in her Brooklyn Park home, where she had been living with her father, Wayne Hasti, since splitting with Sarkar in 2012. The couple had married in 2011, but did not divorce due to financial constraints, according to Hasti's grandmother, Jean Johnson. 

Sarkar's motive for the killings appeared to stem from a perceived grievance against Klug, whom he accused of stealing his intellectual property. In a blog post from March, Sarkar referred to Klug as a "sick person" who had taken his code and given it to another student, causing him emotional distress. UCLA officials disputed this claim, stating that it was likely a product of Sarkar's mental instability.

Sarkar's background revealed a history of potential mental health issues, with neighbors describing him as "hyper" and needing to see a doctor. His dissertation, completed under Klug's supervision in 2013, expressed gratitude towards his advisor, despite the apparent conflict.

The investigation into the murders revealed that Sarkar had a "kill list" in his possession, which included the names of two UCLA professors and a woman. The second professor on the list was safely located and informed of the situation. Authorities are seeking the public's assistance in locating Sarkar's vehicle, a 2003 Nissan Sentra with the license plate 720KTW, to track his movements and determine a motive for the crimes.

Ashley Hasti, a medical student at the University of Minnesota, was remembered as a bright and talented individual who had a passion for medicine and comedy. Her family and friends described her as kind and gentle, and were shocked by her tragic fate. The UCLA community came together to hold a candlelight vigil for Professor Klug, who was remembered as a beloved educator and mentor.

reference: – The woman on the "kill list" of UCLA gunman Mainak Sarkar was his estranged wife, who was apparently shot to death in her Minnesota home before Sarkar traveled to California, authorities say. Jean Johnson, grandmother of 31-year-old University of Minnesota medical student Ashley Hasti, tells the Star Tribune that the couple married in 2011 and split up around a year later, but they didn't get a divorce because Hasti couldn't afford one. Her body was found in her St. Paul home after cops at UCLA found a note from Sarkar asking them to "check on my cat" and giving his Minnesota address. Police found her name on a list in Sarkar's apartment and realized she could be in danger. Johnson says her granddaughter was a "kind, beautiful, giggly girl" and she doesn't know if there was any tension between her and Sarkar. LAPD Chief Charlie Beck says that Sarkar drove from Minnesota to Los Angeles and was probably in town for a "couple of days" before killing UCLA professor William Klug and shooting himself in Klug's office, the Los Angeles Times reports. The chief says Sarkar's car hasn't been found yet and it's not known whether he committed other crimes between Minnesota and California. Beck says investigators believe Sarkar, who made online postings accusing former mentor Klug of stealing his work, had planned to kill another UCLA professor but couldn't find him. That professor "is fine" and was aware Sarkar had issues with him, Beck says. On Thursday night, hundreds of students and faculty members gathered at UCLA to mourn Klug, known as a kind and dedicated professor who did his best to help students, including Sarkar, the AP reports. "Bill was so much more than my soul mate," his wife, Mary Elise Klug, said in a statement. "I will miss him every day for the rest of my life."

final_summary:
A recent Senate Commerce Committee report has shed light on the multibillion-dollar data industry, revealing that major data companies own an astonishing number of consumer profiles and sell deeply personal information, often to identity thieves. Nine prominent data companies, including Acxiom, Experian, Epsilon, Reed Elsevier, Equifax, TransUnion, Rapleaf, Spokeo, and Datalogix, were queried by the Committee, with varying degrees of cooperation.

Some companies, such as Equifax, were forthcoming about their data collection methods and sales practices, while others, like Acxiom, Experian, and Epsilon, refused to disclose details due to confidentiality agreements in their contracts. Notably, Acxiom has access to around 700 million consumer profiles worldwide, yet the company declined to provide information on how it collects its data or who purchases it.

Equifax, however, shared specifics about the types of data it possesses, including information on consumers' purchasing habits, health conditions, and personal characteristics. For instance, the company has data on whether individuals have visited an OB/GYN doctor within the last 12 months, how many miles they've traveled in the past four weeks, and the number of whiskey drinks they've consumed in the past 30 days. This data is categorized using over 75,000 points, including details on pet ownership, smoking habits, prescription medication usage, charitable donations, and hobbies.

Privacy groups, including the World Privacy Forum, have discovered that data brokers sell lists containing sensitive information, such as the addresses of rape victims, people with HIV/AIDS, and those suffering from cancer. These lists are often used by marketers to target consumers based on their demographics, interests, and behaviors. The World Privacy Forum's executive director, Pam Dixon, emphasized the need for stricter government regulations to prevent the sale of highly sensitive data.

Current laws only require data brokers to maintain consumer data privacy when it's used for credit, employment, insurance, or housing purposes. However, medical information obtained from over-the-counter purchases and other healthcare-related activities remains unprotected. As a result, lists containing sensitive information can put individuals at risk, such as the list of over 30,000 police officers' home addresses, which could compromise their safety and that of their families.

Linda Woolley, president of the Direct Marketing Association, acknowledged that some lists may be misused, but argued that they represent a small fraction of marketing products. Many data brokers offer opt-out options, although these procedures are often unclear or non-existent, leaving consumers unaware of their inclusion on these lists. The Senate Commerce Committee Chairman, John "Jay" Rockefeller, expressed outrage over the revelations and vowed to continue pushing for greater transparency in the data brokerage industry.

reference: – Secretive data companies are tracking almost every American's every move online—and compiling and selling disturbingly targeted lists based on that spying, a new Senate Commerce Committee report concludes. That includes lists of rape victims, people suffering from ailments including HIV, AIDS, and dementia, and people with substance abuse problems, a privacy group said in a hearing yesterday, as per CNN. Mailing addresses for police officers and domestic violence shelters—the latter of which are usually protected by law—are also for sale, as are lists based on demographics and economics, including "Ethnic Second-City Strugglers," and "Rural and Barely Making It." The World Privacy Forum urged Congress to act to "remove unsafe, unfair, and overall just deplorable lists from circulation." The Direct Marketing Association released a statement saying that while the lists are occasionally "used to disparage certain groups," they represented a "tiny minority" of marketing products. Overall the committee looked at nine companies, some of which refused to explain how they got their data or who they sold it to, Gawker reports. One company admitted it had sold social security numbers and banking information to an alleged identity theft ring.

final_summary:
Christopher "Big Black" Boykin, a former cast member of MTV's reality shows "Rob & Big," "Fantasy Factory," and "Ridiculousness," passed away on Tuesday at the age of 45. According to his representative, no cause of death has been officially announced, although some individuals close to Boykin speculate that it may have been related to a heart attack. 

Boykin was known for his friendship and role as bodyguard to fellow MTV personality Rob Dyrdek, with whom he starred in "Rob & Big" from 2006 to 2008. During their time on the show, the pair embarked on various adventures, including setting Guinness World Records and participating in unusual stunts. Although their relationship reportedly became strained during the show's run, they eventually reconciled and continued to collaborate on subsequent projects.

Before gaining fame through television, Boykin served in the United States Navy. In addition to his entertainment career, he launched a clothing line inspired by his catchphrase "Do work." Boykin leaves behind a 9-year-old daughter, Isis. Fellow MTV personalities, including Chanel West Coast, paid tribute to Boykin following his passing, expressing sadness and fond memories of working with him.

reference: – Christopher "Big Black" Boykin, star of MTV reality show Rob & Big, died Tuesday of an apparent heart attack at the age of 45, TMZ reports. “He was a long time and beloved member of the MTV family and will be greatly missed," Variety quotes MTV as saying in a statement. Rob & Big ran from 2006 to 2008 with Boykin appearing as best friend, bodyguard, and roommate to professional skateboarder Rob Dyrdek. The show chronicled various shenanigans, including adopting a mini horse, breaking Guinness World Records, and snaring people with net guns. "We truly were brothers that lived an unexpected unforgettable adventure," Dyrdek tweeted. "I just can't fathom that it would end so suddenly." Boykin, a veteran of the Navy, also started his own clothing line based on his catchphrase, "Do work." He's survived by a 9-year-old daughter.

final_summary:
Violence in South Sudan has continued unabated, entering its second month with no end in sight. The conflict, sparked by clashes between army units loyal to President Salva Kiir and those supporting ex-vice president Riek Machar on December 15, has resulted in the displacement of over 400,000 civilians, with up to 10,000 believed to have been killed, including over 200 civilians who drowned in a boat accident while fleeing the fighting in Malakal. 

Malakal, a key northern oil town in Upper Nile state, has become a focal point of the conflict, with the army battling rebels in the streets. Despite rebel claims of having recaptured the town, army spokesman Philip Aguer dismissed these assertions, stating that the fighting is ongoing. The town has changed hands multiple times since the conflict began, with thousands of civilians attempting to flee across the River Nile, many unable to afford the cost of a boat passage.

In neighboring Ethiopia, peace talks brokered by the East African regional bloc IGAD have made little progress, with the release of political leaders arrested in Juba remaining a key sticking point. Rebel delegate Hussein Mar Nyout stated that a ceasefire and prisoner release are "hooked together" and will only be agreed upon simultaneously. The army continues to push forward with efforts to retake the town of Bor, the capital of Jonglei state, with frontlines reportedly quiet but prone to sudden clashes.

As the conflict rages on, the humanitarian situation worsens, with over 350,000 people displaced and more than 1,000 reported dead since the conflict began. The UN estimates that the death toll could be significantly higher, with many cases going unreported due to the remote nature of the affected areas. The international community remains concerned about the escalating violence, particularly the reports of mass killings along ethnic lines, despite both Kiir and Machar having supporters within their rival's communities.

reference: – Some 400,000 people have now fled South Sudan amid weeks of fighting, the UN says; at least 200 drowned on a ferry trying to escape. "The reports we have are of between 200 to 300 people, including women and children. The boat was overloaded," an army rep tells AFP. He says the incident occurred today, while other reports indicate it was overnight Sunday. "They all drowned." He said they were fleeing Upper Nile state capital Malakal, which has been under both rebel and government control; it’s situated near the state’s oil fields. The rebels have launched a new attack on the area, where some 19,000 people have sought shelter at a peacekeeping base, a UN spokesman says. Malakal, the BBC notes, sits near the White Nile, which thousands of civilians have tried to cross in a bid to escape. Many, however, can’t afford a boat ride; one refugee tells the BBC she had to borrow $66 to get across. A recent estimate puts the death toll of the conflict at 10,000.

final_summary:
On September 19, 2018, the US Department of Agriculture's Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS) announced a massive recall of approximately 132,606 pounds of ground beef products produced by Cargill Meat Solutions, a Fort Morgan, Colorado-based establishment. The products, which were manufactured on June 21, 2018, were found to potentially contain Escherichia coli O26, a bacterium that can cause serious foodborne illness. 

The recalled ground beef items were shipped to retail locations across the country and bore the establishment number "EST. 86R" inside the USDA mark of inspection. The products included 3-, 10-, and 20-pound packages of ground beef under several brand names such as Our Certified, Excel, Sterling Silver, Certified, and Fire River Farms, with specific use-by or freeze-by dates of July 11, 2018.

According to the FSIS, the ground beef products were linked to an outbreak of E. coli O26 illnesses that resulted in 17 cases and one fatality between July 5 and July 25, 2018. An investigation led by the FSIS, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and state public health and agriculture partners revealed that raw ground beef was the likely source of the outbreak. Further investigation connected the illnesses to Cargill Meat Solutions' ground beef products, which were sold at various retail stores.

E. coli O26, similar to its more common counterpart E. coli O157:H7, is a serovar of Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) that can cause severe gastrointestinal symptoms, including diarrhea and vomiting, in those infected. While most people recover within a week, some may develop more severe infections, including hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS), a type of kidney failure that affects vulnerable populations such as young children, older adults, and individuals with weakened immune systems.

Consumers who have purchased the recalled products are advised not to consume them and instead discard or return them to the point of purchase. The FSIS is conducting recall effectiveness checks to ensure that the products are no longer available to consumers. To minimize the risk of E. coli contamination, the agency recommends cooking ground beef to an internal temperature of 160°F using a food thermometer and taking proper precautions when handling raw meat products.

In response to the recall, Cargill Meat Solutions stated that all affected products have been removed from supermarkets, and the company is reviewing its facilities to guarantee continued delivery of safe food. The incident marks the second E. coli-related recall for the company in recent months, following a smaller recall of Excel ground beef in August 2018 without reported illnesses.

The FSIS is conducting recall effectiveness checks to ensure that the products are no longer available to consumers. To minimize the risk of E. coli contamination, the agency recommends cooking ground beef to an internal temperature of 160°F using a food thermometer and taking proper precautions when handling raw meat products.

In response to the recall, Cargill Meat Solutions stated that all affected products have been removed from supermarkets, and the company is reviewing its facilities to guarantee continued delivery of safe food. The incident marks the second E. coli-related recall for the company in recent months, following a smaller recall of Excel ground beef in August 2018 without reported illnesses.

reference: – A Colorado meatpacker is recalling more than 132,000 pounds of ground beef after a suspected E. coli outbreak killed one person and sickened 17, officials said. The US Department of Agriculture said Wednesday the beef was produced and packaged at Cargill Meat Solutions in Fort Morgan on June 21 and shipped to retailers nationwide. The products include 3-, 10- and 20-pound packages of ground beef under the Our Certified, Excel, Sterling Silver, Certified, and Fire River Farms brands with July 11 use or freeze by dates. Regulators warned that people should also check for the products in their freezers. They advise throwing the products away or returning them to the location of purchase, the AP reports. In a statement on Thursday, Cargill said all of the affected products have been removed from supermarkets. Food safety teams are reviewing the Fort Morgan facility and others "to ensure we continue to deliver safe food," the statement said. "We were distressed to learn a fatality may be related to an E.coli contamination of one of our products," it said. "Our hearts go out to the families and individuals affected by this issue." The USDA's Food Safety and Inspection Service did not release information about the people who died or became ill, including locations. The Cargill plant had a smaller recall of Excel ground beef in August, but no illnesses had been reported at that time. The USDA's recall info can be found in full here.

final_summary:
A recent study published in the American Journal of Preventive Medicine has shed light on the potential risks associated with working rotating night shifts, particularly for women. Conducted over 22 years, the study analyzed data from nearly 75,000 registered US nurses participating in the Nurses' Health Study. The research revealed a link between long-term exposure to rotating night shifts and increased mortality rates, including all-cause mortality and cardiovascular disease mortality.

According to the study, women who worked rotating night shifts for five years or more experienced a modest increase in all-cause mortality, while those who worked these shifts for 15 years or more saw a significant rise in lung cancer mortality, with a 25% higher risk compared to their counterparts. The study defined rotating shift work as working at least three nights per month in addition to days or evenings in the same month.

The analysis showed that the longer the duration of rotating night shift work, the greater the risk of adverse outcomes. Specifically, women who worked rotating night shifts for six to 14 years faced a 19-23% higher risk of dying from cardiovascular disease, while those who worked these shifts for 15 or more years were at a 23% higher risk of cardiovascular disease mortality. 

Researchers found no association between rotating shift work and overall cancer mortality, but noted that further investigation into this area is warranted. The study's lead author, Eva Schernhammer, emphasized the need for additional research to understand how individual characteristics, such as chronotype, interact with rotating night shift work to impact health outcomes.

reference: – Working the night shift has already been linked to higher risks of cancer and cardiovascular disease. Now researchers at Harvard—who've combed through 22 years of data tracking 75,000 nurses in the US—write in the American Journal of Preventive Medicine that rotating night shifts for five or more years results in higher mortality rates in women. Specifically, death from cardiovascular disease is as much as 23% higher and death from lung cancer is as much as 25% higher, reports Fox News. "These results add to prior evidence of a potentially detrimental relation of rotating night shift work and health and longevity," the lead researcher says. Rotating shift work is defined as working at least three nights a month in addition to working day and evening shifts. And while the researchers acknowledge that more study is needed to learn how a person's individual traits might play a role, people who work rotating night shifts for more than five years do experience an 11% hike in risk of death from all causes, reports HealthDay News. (Researchers blame the disrupted balance of light and dark in the increased cancer rates.)

final_summary:
On October 11, 2018, a tragic incident occurred on the set of the upcoming biographical drama film "You Are My Friend," which tells the story of the life of legendary children's television host Fred Rogers. James M. Emswiller, a 61-year-old sound mixer from Pittsburgh's Lincoln Place neighborhood, fell from a balcony at an apartment building in Mt. Lebanon, Pennsylvania, while on a break between scenes. According to Mt. Lebanon Police Chief Aaron Lauth, Emswiller may have suffered a medical event prior to falling over the brick wall surrounding the balcony, which led to his fatal fall.

Emswiller was rushed to UPMC Mercy in critical condition but succumbed to his injuries approximately an hour after the incident. The Allegheny County Medical Examiner's Office later confirmed his death, citing blunt impact injuries to the head and torso as the cause of death, with the manner of death classified as an accident.

The incident occurred around 7:30 pm, and although no one witnessed the fall, people heard Emswiller hit the ground. The film's star, Tom Hanks, who played the role of Fred Rogers, was on set at the time but left after the incident. Filming was subsequently halted for the evening and remained suspended pending further investigation.

James Emswiller had an extensive background in sound production, having worked on several notable projects, including the films "Jack Reacher," "Abduction," and "Concussion." He also won an Emmy Award in 2015 for his work on the TV movie "Bessie." The film "You Are My Friend" is based on the true story of the unlikely friendship between Fred Rogers and journalist Tom Junod, who wrote a profile on Rogers for Esquire magazine in 1998.

reference: – Sad news out of Pennsylvania, where a biopic about Fred Rogers (aka "Mister Rogers") is filming. WPXI and the AP report that a 61-year-old crew member for You Are My Friend has died after falling off a second-story balcony at a Mount Lebanon apartment building. Police say James Emswiller, described as a sound mixer on his IMDb page, had gone out onto the balcony for a smoke in between shooting scenes for the film and apparently plummeted over the balcony's brick wall about 7:30pm Thursday. No one saw him fall, but others nearby heard a sound and soon discovered Emswiller on the ground below. "He appears to have suffered from a medical emergency while on the balcony," Mount Lebanon Chief of Police Aaron Lauth tells People. Emswiller was initially listed in serious condition, but he soon deteriorated and was pronounced dead at a local hospital about an hour after the fall. He had previously done work on The Avengers and Jack Reacher, per his IMDb creds; the Pittsburgh Tribune-Review notes he won an Emmy for the TV movie Bessie in 2015. "This is a devastating tragedy and the studio is investigating the matter," Sony Pictures/TriStar Pictures said in a statement to USA Today. Tom Hanks, who stars as Rogers, was reportedly on the set Thursday and left after the incident.

final_summary:
Following a string of incidents involving hoverboard fires and explosions, Amazon has taken steps to address safety concerns surrounding the popular self-balancing scooters. The e-commerce giant has begun pulling certain hoverboard models from its platform, including all five models previously reviewed by consumer affairs site BestReviews, and is requiring manufacturers to provide documentation proving compliance with applicable safety standards. This includes certifications from organizations such as UL (Underwriters Laboratories), which tests chargers and batteries for safety in the US. 

The decision comes after numerous reports of hoverboard fires, including at least 10 incidents in nine states, prompting the US Consumer Product Safety Commission to launch an investigation. Major airlines have also banned hoverboards from flights due to concerns over battery safety, with Delta citing investigations into product specifications showing devices containing battery varieties exceeding the government-mandated 160 watt-hour limit allowed on aircraft.

Overstock.com has also discontinued the sale of hoverboards, citing safety concerns, and has offered refunds to customers who purchased the products. eBay, another major online marketplace, has not issued a statement on the matter, despite reporting significant sales of hoverboards, including nearly 7,500 units on Cyber Monday alone.

Amazon's actions have led to a significant reduction in hoverboard listings on its platforms, with only select brands such as Razor and Jetson remaining available for purchase. The company has informed its sellers that they must provide documentation demonstrating compliance with safety standards, including UN 38.3 (battery), UL 1642 (battery), and UL 60950-1 (charger).

Swagway, a prominent hoverboard manufacturer, has stated that it meets all necessary certifications and welcomes Amazon's efforts to weed out low-quality boards. However, a Swagway board caught fire in Westchester, New York, raising questions about the effectiveness of current safety protocols.

The hoverboard industry has faced criticism for its lack of regulation, with many products being manufactured in China and sold through various channels without adequate safety testing. The situation has highlighted concerns over patent liability, with Amazon requiring sellers to indemnify the company against potential claims of patent infringement.

As the investigation into hoverboard safety continues, consumers are advised to exercise caution when purchasing these products, ensuring that they buy from authorized retailers and verify the authenticity of the product.

reference: – Consumers planning on scouring Amazon to find a hoverboard to place under the Christmas tree may have to look elsewhere. Joining fellow online retailer Overstock.com, Amazon has decided to halt sales of most of the two-wheeled products on its US and UK sites in the wake of recent reports of explosions and fires. Although hoverboards from Razor and Jetson remain on the site, as do hoverboard accessories, Amazon says it won't put any other manufacturers' hoverboards back on until makers can prove their units meet appropriate safety requirements set by the private company UL, notes Quartz. The BestReviews.com site, which recently reviewed five models (none of which are available any longer on Amazon), said in response to the Amazon move that "we are not recommending any hoverboards until they are proven to be safe." So far, at least 10 hoverboard fires have been reported in nine states, per Fox59, and the Guardian reports that 88% of the non-EU hoverboards tested by the UK's trading standards authority didn't pass basic safety requirements. The Verge notes that the problem has become so worrisome that some airlines are now banning hoverboards from their flights. "Criminals and irresponsible manufacturers will often exploit high demand and attempt to flood the market with cheap and dangerous products," the CEO of the Chartered Trading Standards Institute tells the Guardian. One company that may end up profiting from the other sites' reluctance to carry hoverboards: eBay, which EcommerceBytes.com notes still had plenty of listings for the product as of Sunday night. (This guy's hoverboard didn't catch fire as he allegedly shoplifted.)

final_summary:
In a groundbreaking study, researchers from South Africa employed cutting-edge forensic technology to analyze ancient clay tobacco pipes discovered in the garden of renowned British playwright William Shakespeare. The pipes, dating back over 400 years, were analyzed using gas chromatography mass spectrometry, a highly sensitive technique capable of detecting residue even after centuries. 

The analysis revealed a diverse range of substances smoked in the pipes, including cannabis, nicotine, and cocaine derived from Peruvian coca leaves. While none of the cocaine-laced pipes originated from Shakespeare's garden, four out of eight pipes containing cannabis were found there. The study, published in the South African Journal of Science, suggests that Shakespeare may have been familiar with the stimulating effects of cannabis, possibly preferring it over other substances.

This finding is supported by literary analysis of Shakespeare's Sonnet 76, where he mentions "invention in a noted weed," which some interpret as a reference to cannabis as a creative stimulant. Additionally, the sonnet contains a phrase "compounds strange," which could imply a reluctance to associate himself with unusual or illicit substances, such as cocaine.

The study raises intriguing questions about the cultural practices of Shakespeare's time, particularly in relation to substance use. It is possible that the playwright and his contemporaries, including Sir Francis Drake and Sir Walter Raleigh, who introduced various New World plants to Europe, regularly smoked pipes filled with a variety of substances, including cannabis and cocaine.

Furthermore, the discovery of cannabis and other psychoactive substances in the pipes has sparked speculation about the potential influence of these substances on Shakespeare's creativity and artistic output. Some speculate that the performances of his plays might have taken place in a smoke-filled atmosphere, with the playwright and actors drawing inspiration from their surroundings.

While the exact extent of Shakespeare's involvement with these substances remains unclear, the study highlights the value of interdisciplinary approaches combining literature and scientific analysis to gain a deeper understanding of historical figures and their times.

reference: – "To smoke or not to smoke" was not the question. Something had been smoked in the pipe bowls and stems unearthed from William Shakespeare' garden in Stratford-upon-Avon; the question was what. Researchers in South Africa now have gas chromatography mass spectrometry to thank for their answer. A piece in the Conversation based on the report published in the South African Journal of Science explains the "technique is very sensitive to residues that can be preserved in pipes even if they had been smoked 400 years ago." Eight of the 24 pipe-fragment samples tested were shown to contain cannabis; another had evidence of nicotine, and two more "evidence for Peruvian cocaine from coca leaves." Of those, only four of the cannabis samples were from Shakespeare's garden; the others were from the Stratford-upon-Avon area. Study author Francis Thackeray writes that the research establishes that a wide range of plants were smoked in the area during the early 17th century, and he leans on Shakespeare's own words to try to draw connections. Thackeray references Sonnet 76, which refers to "invention in a noted weed"; he interprets weed as cannabis and invention as writing. As for whether we can conclude that Shakespeare got high, Thackeray writes "one can well imagine" it. (In April, Texas researchers reported another Shakespeare find.)

final_summary:
Residents on the Big Island of Hawaii were alerted to rising levels of toxic gas from lava-oozing fissures, prompting authorities to warn of a potential shift in wind patterns bringing sulfur dioxide gas, which can be fatal in large quantities. Hawaii County authorities issued a text message to residents in the southeast corner of the island advising them of the impending change in wind direction. 

Evacuated resident Robynn Stagg described the scene as "horrible," citing the inability to breathe due to the thick, orange sulfur dioxide haze. Hawaii's governor warned that mass evacuations may be necessary as more fissures open, spewing lava and gas into semi-rural residential areas on the east flank of Kilauea, one of the world's most active volcanoes.

According to the USGS's Hawaii Volcano Observatory, while no new fissures have opened in the past 24 hours, significant ground cracking has occurred, releasing steam. Scientist Steven Brantley stated that the likelihood of new eruptive activity at the surface remains high due to ongoing lava intrusion and earthquakes.

The eruption at Kilauea began last week following a sudden collapse of the crater floor, triggering a massive release of magma through underground passages. This led to the formation of numerous fissures in a residential area miles away, causing widespread destruction and displacement.

Geologists warned that Kilauea may enter a new phase of explosive eruptions, potentially hurling "ballistic blocks" weighing up to 12 tons for half a mile and raining pebble-sized fragments over a wider area. The immediate vicinity around the summit, within Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, was closed indefinitely due to the increased risk of such explosions.

As the eruption progresses, other areas of the lower East Rift Zone may also be at risk, with the potential for additional outbreaks and further damage to residential neighborhoods. Hawaii Governor David Ige requested federal disaster assistance, stating that a mass evacuation of the lower Puna District would exceed the capabilities of local authorities.

Meanwhile, authorities removed highly flammable chemicals from a nearby geothermal power plant in the path of creeping lava. Local meteorologists predicted that the volcanic smog, or vog, could spread northwest to Maui and other islands in Hawaii, affecting surfers and residents alike.

In addition to the threat of explosive eruptions and lava flows, residents face the risk of acid rain, caused by sulfur dioxide mixing with water vapor in the atmosphere. While not life-threatening, acid rain can irritate eyes and skin, and pose a longer-term threat to aquatic ecosystems and infrastructure.

Air quality monitoring teams detected no hazardous levels of sulfur dioxide near the fissures, but hospitals reported an increase in patients with respiratory issues. The state Health Department deployed monitoring equipment around the eruption site, with data expected to be available online once operational.

As the situation unfolds, authorities continued to prioritize the safety of residents, with workers accelerating the removal of flammable pentane from a geothermal plant near the affected areas. With new fissures emerging and the risk of explosive eruptions increasing, the situation remains volatile, with residents advised to remain vigilant and follow evacuation orders.

reference: – The ongoing eruption of Hawaii's Kilauea volcano is creating new worries for residents—including the risk of "vog," or volcanic smog. Big Island residents have been warned that a change in the wind is expected to increase the risk of vog, which contains potentially deadly sulfur dioxide gas, Reuters reports. Gov. David Ige has warned that more mass evacuations will be needed as new cracks in the ground send lava and toxic gas into new neighborhoods near the volcano. Ige has asked President Trump to declare a disaster in the state so that federal money can be used in the event of large-scale evacuations by air and sea, the AP reports. Forecasters say Big Island residents are likely to face higher levels of sulfur dioxide Friday, which is especially dangerous for children, the elderly, and those with respiratory conditions, the Honolulu Star-Advertiser reports. Trade winds are expected to ease conditions by Saturday morning. Amid fears that the volcano, one of the world's most active, could erupt explosively, flammable pentane has been removed from a geothermal plant near two neighborhoods that had to be evacuated because of lava and gas. Kilauea is surrounded by Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, which closed Thursday night until further notice. Scientists had warned that if the volcano erupts, park visitors within a mile or so of the crater were at risk of being hit by refrigerator-sized boulders.

final_summary:
On August 24th, a 6.0-magnitude earthquake struck the Napa Valley area in Northern California, causing widespread destruction and injuring over 170 people. The epicenter of the quake was located near the West Napa Fault, which runs for approximately 35 miles from the city of St. Helena to San Pablo Bay. The earthquake's impact was felt throughout the region, with 70 buildings in Napa being red-tagged as uninhabitable due to significant damage. 

Residents and visitors alike rallied together to assist in the cleanup efforts, with volunteers from various organizations, including Home Depot, pitching in to help with debris removal and rebuilding. Lori Lemieux, a retired clerical supervisor, witnessed firsthand the generosity of strangers as they helped her and her husband clean up the damage to their home. "When you have something so devastating happen, to see this teamwork is just awesome," she said.

Among the volunteers was Premysl Balaz, a 33-year-old engineer from the Czech Republic who was on vacation in San Francisco when the earthquake struck. He joined forces with other volunteers to create a database of red-tagged homes and expressed his admiration for the sense of unity and cooperation among the community. "Everyone is connected through the idea that they want to help," he said.

In the aftermath of the disaster, Governor Jerry Brown declared a state of emergency for Napa, Solano, and Sonoma counties, allowing for additional resources to be allocated to aid in the recovery efforts. Inspectors worked tirelessly to assess the damage, with many buildings being deemed unsafe for occupancy. The U.S. Geological Survey estimated the damage from the earthquake could cost upwards of $1 billion.

Despite the devastation, many residents and business owners expressed gratitude for the relatively minimal damage sustained by their properties. Wineries, a staple of the Napa Valley economy, suffered losses, particularly in terms of wine spills and damaged equipment. However, some winemakers reported that the extent of the damage was less severe than initially feared, with many wine barrels and bottles having been stored in protective cases.

As the community comes together to rebuild and recover, residents are facing challenges such as water shortages, with over 600 properties in Napa lacking access to potable water. Schools were closed temporarily, and a youth drop-in program was established to provide activities for children. The recovery process is expected to be lengthy, with officials estimating it may take months to complete.

One of the most critically injured victims, a 13-year-old boy named Nicholas Dillon, underwent surgery to treat pelvic fractures and is expected to make a full recovery. His family expressed gratitude for the outpouring of support from the community. As the region continues to heal, residents and visitors alike are coming together to rebuild and restore the affected areas, demonstrating the resilience and solidarity that defines the Napa Valley community.

reference: – People in Napa Valley, Calif., are picking up the pieces after Sunday's 6.0 earthquake wrecked scores of homes and businesses, and they're getting plenty of help from outside. Volunteers have flocked from across Northern California to assist in any way they can, reports the Los Angeles Times. The helpers include scores of Home Depot workers and an engineer from the Czech Republic who was on vacation in San Francisco when the quake struck and drove out to offer his services. "When you meet people who are helping each other, there's no difference between religions or between colors," he says. More: The damage is estimated at up to $1 billion, but wineries say they will pull through and they hope tourists won't stay away, the New York Times reports. "In the end, people who are growing grapes are farmers," says an official of an organization that represents 500 local vintners. "This is a particularly strong expression of Mother Nature, but they deal with Mother Nature every day, and they are very resilient." Dozens of homes have been declared uninhabitable and more than 250 people are recovering from injuries, but there have been no fatalities from the area's worst earthquake since 1989, the San Francisco Chronicle reports. The two most seriously injured victims, one of them a 13-year-old boy hurt by a falling chimney, are both expected to survive. Seismologists are still working to determine the exact cause of the quake, LiveScience reports. It was initially believed to be connected to the Franklin Fault—which has been dormant for thousands of years—but researchers now believe it was the West Napa Fault, part of a wide network of faults around the San Andreas Fault, which lies 31 miles away.

final_summary:
In a dramatic turn of events, a 22-year-old woman identified as Beatriz, who was denied a medical abortion due to El Salvador's strict anti-abortion laws, underwent a successful cesarean section to deliver a baby that doctors had deemed unlikely to survive. The operation was performed at 27 weeks of pregnancy, after the country's Health Ministry intervened following a ruling from the Inter-American Court on Human Rights that El Salvador should prioritize protecting Beatriz's life. 

Beatriz, who suffers from lupus and kidney failure, had been carrying a fetus with a severe condition known as anencephaly, characterized by the absence of a fully formed brain. Despite this, the country's Supreme Court initially refused to grant her an abortion, citing the constitution's protection of life from conception. However, the Health Ministry eventually allowed the cesarean section, reasoning that the pregnancy was advanced enough that the country's strict abortion laws no longer applied.

The operation was carried out at approximately 2 pm local time, and Beatriz was reported to be in stable condition afterwards. Unfortunately, her baby girl was born without a brain and passed away around five hours later. The decision to proceed with the cesarean section was made to ensure Beatriz's safety, as her health was at significant risk due to the pregnancy.

The case drew widespread international attention, with many criticizing El Salvador's stance on abortion. The country has some of the most restrictive abortion laws in Latin America, alongside several other nations. Abortion opponents argued that the case was being used to push for the legalization of abortion in El Salvador, while supporters saw it as a matter of prioritizing the woman's life and health.

Morena Herrera, a representative of the abortion rights group Colectivo Feminista, acknowledged that Beatriz could have been spared unnecessary suffering, but expressed relief that her life had been saved. In contrast, Claudia Handal, a spokesperson for the anti-abortion group Red Familia, stated that the rights of both the baby and Beatriz had been respected, emphasizing that the focus should be on providing care and healthcare to the woman rather than terminating the pregnancy.

reference: – After being denied an abortion despite serious health concerns, a Salvadoran woman known only as Beatriz has undergone a cesarean section to save her life, the AP reports. The baby girl was born without a brain 27 weeks into the pregnancy and died five hours later, the Guardian reports. El Salvador's health ministry was able to circumvent its strict anti-abortion laws and allow the C-section because the pregnancy had passed the 26-week mark. Beatriz, who had suffered from lupus and kidney failure, is now in stable condition, says the health minister. "She's in good hands, being looked after well." A rep for an abortion rights group says she faced unnecessary suffering, reports Reuters. Last week, the Inter-American Court on Human Rights ruled that the abortion should be allowed, but that ruling came as El Salvador settled the issue with the C-section plan.

final_summary:
Thirty years after the brutal murder of 14-year-old Claire Hough on Torrey Pines State Beach in San Diego, California, in 1984, investigators have finally identified two suspects in the case. Claire, who was visiting her grandparents in the area, was found beaten, strangled, and stabbed, with one of her breasts severed. The investigation into her murder began in earnest in November 2012, when DNA evidence collected at the crime scene was analyzed and matched to two individuals.

One of the suspects, Kevin Charles Brown, a former San Diego Police Department criminalist, was identified through DNA analysis. Brown, 62, had worked for the department from 1982 to 2002, but investigators claim he had no involvement in the case and did not process any evidence related to it. Despite this, Brown was set to be arrested in connection with the murder, but his plans were foiled when he took his own life at Cuyamaca State Park on October 21, 2014.

The second suspect, Ronald Clyde Tatro, was also identified through DNA analysis. Tatro, 67, died in a boating accident in Tennessee in 2011, prior to the identification of the DNA match. Investigators have stated that they do not believe Brown and Tatro knew Claire or were previously suspected in her death, but rather that DNA testing, which did not exist at the time of the murder, provided the crucial evidence needed to crack the case.

The investigation into Claire's murder has shed light on a similar case from six years prior, in which 15-year-old Barbara Nantais was found murdered at the same beach in 1978. Like Claire, Barbara was beaten, strangled, and had one breast severed. However, investigators have stated that there is no direct evidence linking the two murders, and they are not treating them as related cases.

reference: – San Diego police have named two suspects in a teen girl's cold-case murder, including a former San Diego Police Department criminologist. DNA evidence found in November 2012 led police to Ronald Clyde Tatro and Kevin Charles Brown—though as of Tuesday, both men are dead. Claire Hough, 14, was found strangled with sand pushed into her mouth at Torrey Pines State Beach on Aug. 24, 1984, the Los Angeles Times reports. One of her breasts had been cut off. Brown, who processed evidence for the force from 1982 to 2002 but was not "assigned to any part of the murder investigation," knew he was a person of interest, FOX 5 reports. The 62-year-old was found dead in a state park on Tuesday, and his death has been ruled a suicide. Tatro was 67 when he died in a 2011 boating accident in Tennessee. Officers had been building a case against Brown over the last two years and were planning an arrest, NBC San Diego reports. "I can only surmise that was part of the reason" he killed himself, a police rep says. Authorities aren't saying what kind of DNA evidence led them to the suspects, how the two men knew each other, or their relationship to the victim. The Rhode Island teen had been visiting her grandparents at the time of her death; it's unclear why she went to the beach that night. Police continue to investigate a similar killing at the same beach in August 1978. The body of Barbara Nantais, 15, was found beaten and strangled, with one breast cut. Police say there's no evidence to link the murders. "We are not treating them as related cases," a rep tells the AP. (A TV show helped bring about an arrest in a 1997 murder.)

final_summary:
In late 2014, two-year-old Rumer Rose Maujean was diagnosed with stage 4 neuroblastoma, a form of aggressive childhood cancer originating from her adrenal glands. Her father, Adam Koessler, was arrested on January 2, 2015, for administering medicinal cannabis oil to his daughter, despite the substance being illegal in Australia at the time. As a result, Koessler was prohibited from having direct or indirect contact with Rumer while she underwent treatment at the Lady Cilento Children's Hospital in Brisbane.

Rumer's mother, Sacha Maujean, took to social media to share their story and raise funds for her daughter's treatment, stating that they faced a 2-year stay in Brisbane, away from their home, family, and friends. The community rallied around them, raising over $8000 for Rumer's treatment and accommodation costs. However, Koessler continued to advocate for the decriminalization of medicinal cannabis, citing its potential benefits for cancer patients and the cruel separation from his daughter.

Adam Koessler's case drew parallels with another Australian family, the Wallaces, whose son Cooper had also benefited from medicinal cannabis oil, leading to the dismissal of charges against his parents. This sparked a wider movement, with nearly 200,000 Australians signing a petition calling for the decriminalization of medicinal cannabis use. Koessler himself started a Facebook page, "Adam Fearless Father," to share updates on Rumer's condition and garner support for his cause.

As Rumer began her chemotherapy treatment, her father expressed hope for her recovery and a potential return to their hometown of Cairns within 12 months. Despite the challenges ahead, the community's outpouring of support gave the family strength and confidence in the face of adversity.
