reference: – You can't make this stuff up: The Daily Caller is giving away handguns engraved with the Bill of Rights. To enter to win a gun, just sign up for one of the right-wing news site's four email newsletters; it's giving away one gun per week until Election Day. (Note: Winners will have to pass a background check.) Media Matters spotted the contest, and notes that the Caller first launched a Guns and Gear news section in December, concerned that gun owners "are currently without the sort of daily news coverage that is allotted to most other American interests."

final_summary:
Hurricane Harvey, which made landfall in Texas nearly two months ago, has claimed another victim, a 31-year-old man named Josue Zurita, who died from a rare flesh-eating bacterial infection known as necrotizing fasciitis. According to the Galveston County Health District, Zurita visited the hospital on October 10 with a severely infected wound on his upper left arm and was subsequently diagnosed with necrotizing fasciitis, a condition that kills the body's soft tissue.

Zurita, a hardworking carpenter who had moved to the US from Mexico to support his family, was involved in the rebuilding efforts following the devastating floods caused by Hurricane Harvey. His death brings the total number of fatalities linked to the storm to at least three, including a 77-year-old woman, Nancy Reed, who died in September from a similar infection.

Dr. Philip Keiser, the local health authority for Galveston County, stated that the infection likely occurred when bacteria from hurricane debris or floodwater entered Zurita's body through a wound or cut. Keiser emphasized that necrotizing fasciitis is a rare condition, with approximately 700 to 1,100 cases occurring annually in the US since 2010, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

The infection can spread rapidly, killing the body's soft tissue, particularly if the wound is not properly cared for. Keiser shared a harrowing account of witnessing the infection spread over mere hours, noting that even in one instance, he observed the redness spreading in just minutes during an examination. The infection can become lethal within a short period, and Keiser stressed the importance of prompt medical attention if symptoms such as redness, swelling, or fever appear.

Proper wound care is crucial in preventing such infections, and the Galveston County Health District advises keeping open wounds covered with clean, dry bandages until they heal, avoiding contact with natural bodies of water if there is an open wound, and washing hands frequently with soap and water. Additionally, tetanus booster shots can serve as a defense against infectious diseases.

J.R. Atkins, a former first responder, contracted a flesh-eating bacterial infection while kayaking through floodwaters to check on neighbors affected by the hurricane, highlighting the risks associated with exposure to contaminated water. Another individual, Nancy Reed, died from necrotizing fasciitis while helping her son clear out his home after the storm.

Infectious disease experts warn that stress, poor food hygiene, and crowded living conditions can increase the risk of getting sick after a natural disaster. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention advise against consuming or drinking anything contaminated by floodwater that can cause diarrheal disease, such as E. coli or salmonella infection.

reference: – A 31-year-old Texas man has died from flesh-eating bacteria, the third reported victim to be infected with the rare bug linked to Hurricane Harvey. Josue Zurita, a carpenter helping repair flooded homes, died on Oct. 16, six days after he went to the hospital with a serious infection to his upper left arm, KHOU reports. Necrotizing fasciitis had already claimed the life of a 77-year-old Houston-area woman; a man who'd kayaked through floodwaters to check on neighbors and contracted the bacteria survived, per CNN. Dr. Philip Keiser, the local health authority for Galveston County, which announced Zurita's death on Monday, says the risk of contracting necrotizing fasciitis is "well within what we would expect" given the amount of construction and resulting injuries after the storm. "You get some kind of break in the skin, and ... it's a fairly open space where the bacteria can grow," he notes. The infection can "spread over hours," Keiser adds. Several types of bacteria can cause necrotizing fasciitis, which kills the body's soft tissue. The risk of disease following a flood is usually short-lived, per the Washington Post. But to prevent infection, it's important to treat even minor wounds by cleaning them thoroughly, then covering them with clean, dry bandages. Tetanus shots can prevent infection. Zurita was remembered in an obituary as a "loving father and hardworking carpenter" who moved to the US from Mexico to support his wife and daughter back home. "He's a very caring person," a relative tells KHOU. "He was very young and always smiling." (Sewage in floodwaters was a prime culprit for spreading infection.)

final_summary:
The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved Yescarta, a groundbreaking cell-based gene therapy, for treating adult patients with certain types of large B-cell lymphoma who have not responded to or have relapsed after at least two other treatments. Developed by Kite Pharma, Inc., Yescarta is the second gene therapy approved by the FDA and the first for specific types of non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL).

Yescarta uses a patient's own immune system to fight lymphoma by collecting and genetically modifying their T-cells to target and kill cancer cells. The treatment involves infusing the modified T-cells back into the patient, where they can attack the lymphoma. According to the FDA, the complete remission rate after treatment with Yescarta was 51%, with 70% of patients experiencing tumor shrinkage.

However, the treatment comes with significant risks, including severe side effects such as cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurologic toxicities, which can be fatal or life-threatening. Due to these risks, Yescarta is being approved with a risk evaluation and mitigation strategy (REMS), requiring specialized training for healthcare professionals and patient education on potential side effects.

The FDA granted Yescarta Priority Review and Breakthrough Therapy designations, as well as Orphan Drug designation, providing incentives for the development of treatments for rare diseases. The approval was based on data from a multicenter clinical trial involving over 100 adults with refractory or relapsed large B-cell lymphoma.

Josh Feldman, a 55-year-old photographer, was among the first patients to benefit from Yescarta, undergoing the treatment after trying various other therapies. His experience highlights the potential of CAR-T cell therapies, which involve genetically engineering a patient's white blood cells to target and destroy cancer cells. Feldman's case is particularly notable, as he achieved remission 15 months after treatment and credits the therapy as a "walk in the park" compared to his previous experiences.

While Yescarta's approval marks a significant milestone in cancer treatment, its high price tag of $373,000 per patient has raised concerns. Critics argue that the cost is excessive, given the role of government-funded research in developing the treatment. However, Kite's chief scientific officer, David Chang, emphasizes the challenges of transitioning from government-sponsored research to commercialization, highlighting the need for private companies to invest in manufacturing and regulatory processes.

Kite is working on developing CAR-T treatments for other cancers, including multiple myeloma and cancers caused by the human papilloma virus. The company aims to expand the technology beyond blood cancers, with Chang describing the clinical results as "really, really different from anything else that preceded it."

In comparison to Novartis' Kymriah, another CAR-T treatment, Yescarta's price appears lower, but Novartis offers a value-based pricing structure that only charges insurance companies for patients who achieve remission within three months. This approach could potentially reduce the overall cost of treatment.

reference: – The Food and Drug Administration has approved a second "living drug" to fight cancer—a personalized treatment that alters a patient's own cells to turn them into cancer-killers. The new "CAR T-cell therapy" has been approved for non-Hodkins lymphoma patients with few other treatment options, a group that could number around 3,500 per year, the Washington Post reports. But the innovative therapy won't come cheap: Forbes reports that the cost will be $373,000 per patient, not including the cost of treating side effects that could include dangerous fevers. A similar treatment approved for leukemia in July costs $475,000 per patient. Doctors believe the new treatment, produced by Kite Pharma under the brand name Yescarta, could save thousands of lives, the New York Times reports. In the study that led to FDA approval, 54% of 101 patients went into complete remission, and 39% of the 101 were still in remission an average of 8.7 months later. "Today marks another milestone in the development of a whole new scientific paradigm for the treatment of serious diseases," FDA Commissioner Scott Gottlieb said in a statement. "In just several decades, gene therapy has gone from being a promising concept to a practical solution to deadly and largely untreatable forms of cancer." (The fight against breast cancer is also making progress.)

final_summary:
As Hurricane Irma bore down on Florida, authorities issued a stern warning to residents: shooting bullets into the storm would not keep them safe. The Pasco County Sheriff's Office responded to a Facebook event page created by two Florida men, inviting people to shoot at Irma, stating "DO NOT shoot weapons @ #Irma. You won't make it turn around (and) it will have very dangerous side effects." Despite the invitation being a joke, 80,000 people indicated they were "going" or "interested" in the event. The sheriff's office then requested that those who shared the page also share their request for volunteers needed at hurricane shelters.

Forecasters predicted that Irma's center would cross the Florida Keys, with the northern eyewall reaching the island chain early Sunday. The U.S. National Hurricane Center stated that the storm's center remained offshore but was set to make landfall soon, with maximum sustained winds of 130 mph (215 kph) and wind gusts of 90 mph (145 kph) near its Key West office. After passing the Florida Keys, Irma was forecast to move up the state's Gulf Coast later Sunday.

Tornado warnings were issued for a wide swath of Monroe, Miami-Dade, and Broward counties in South Florida, with officials urging people to remain indoors until the storm passed. Those who chose to ride out the storm in the Florida Keys were advised to stay inside until the storm had fully passed, with the Key West Police Department warning against venturing outside during the eye of the storm.

As the storm made landfall, hundreds of thousands of people lost power, with Florida Power & Light Company reporting approximately 430,000 customers without electricity. The utility company mobilized crews to restore power as soon as possible. In Key West, Carol Walterson Stroud and her family huddled in a third-floor apartment at a senior center, describing the wind as "blowing hard" but assuring they were okay.

The storm's eye was mere miles from the lower Florida Keys, with the U.S. National Hurricane Center predicting weakening but maintaining Irma's status as a powerful hurricane as it moved through the Florida Keys and near the state's west coast. French and Dutch officials announced that their Caribbean islands were spared significant damage from Hurricane Jose, which passed farther away from the islands than anticipated.

Dutch King Willem-Alexander planned to visit survivors of Hurricane Irma in the Caribbean, while stranded tourists hoped to secure flights back home. The Dutch navy intended to evacuate tourists from the island's shattered resorts. As Irma continued to bear down on the lower Florida Keys, a National Ocean Service station recorded sustained winds of 66 mph (105 kph) with a gust up to 85 mph (137 kph), and Key West International Airport measured sustained winds of 43 mph (69 kph) with a gust up to 73 mph (117 kph).

With tens of thousands huddled in shelters, Irma regained Category 4 strength, with maximum sustained winds increasing to near 130 mph (210 kph) as it moved toward Florida. The storm's trajectory shifted, potentially sparing Tampa and Miami a direct hit, but putting St. Petersburg in its path instead. The storm's leading edge knocked out power to over 170,000 homes and businesses, bending palm trees and spitting rain across South Florida as the eye approached Key West.

reference: – If you're worried about the people in the path of Hurricane Irma, perhaps you should also worry about the segment of them that thinks shooting at the storm will make it go away. As the AP reports, the following is an actual tweet from the Pasco County Sheriff's Office: "DO NOT shoot weapons @ (hashtag) Irma. You won't make it turn around (and) it will have very dangerous side effects." The sheriff's office, which is in the Tampa Bay area, was responding to a Facebook event page created by two Florida men inviting people to shoot at Irma. The page reads: "YO SO THIS GOOFY ... LETS SHOW IRMA THAT WE SHOOT FIRST ..." The invitation presumably was a joke, but 80,000 people indicated they were "going" or "interested" in the event. In another tweet, the Sheriff's Office also had another suggestion for the thousands who shared the page. Irma hit Key West early Sunday.

final_summary:
In the early hours of Thursday morning, two police officers were shot outside the Ferguson Police Department in Missouri, leaving them seriously injured. The shooting occurred during a protest that followed the resignation of Ferguson's police chief, Thomas Jackson, in response to a Justice Department report revealing widespread racial bias within the police force. The two officers, one from the St. Louis County police and the other from the Webster Groves police department, were standing in a line of approximately 25 officers when they were ambushed by gunfire from a distance of about 125 yards. 

According to eyewitnesses, including a neighbor who witnessed the shooting, bullets flew past them, and the officers dropped to their knees. The shots were reportedly fired from a handgun, not a rifle, and the victims were struck in areas consistent with a handgun wound. One officer was shot below the right eye, and the bullet remains lodged just below his right ear, while the other officer was shot in the right shoulder and the bullet exited through his back.

A manhunt is currently underway to identify and apprehend those responsible for the shooting. Police have questioned three individuals, including a woman who rented the house from which the shots were allegedly fired, but none have been arrested yet. The FBI has offered its assistance in the investigation, and Attorney General Eric Holder has condemned the attack, calling it a "pure ambush" and labeling the perpetrator a "damn punk."

Holder emphasized that the shooting undermines the efforts of non-violent protesters in Ferguson and nationwide who have been pushing for reform in the wake of the fatal shooting of Michael Brown, an unarmed black teenager, by a white police officer last August. The incident sparked widespread protests and a national debate about racism in the US justice system.

As the investigation continues, authorities have received support from various politicians, including President Barack Obama, who expressed his condolences to the officers and reiterated the importance of finding a peaceful resolution to the situation. Local leaders, such as Governor Jay Nixon and Senator Claire McCaskill, have also condemned the attack and called for calm.

CrimeStoppers has offered a $10,000 reward for information leading to the identification of the shooter(s), and additional rewards have been pledged by local representatives. The Missouri Highway Patrol and St. Louis County Police have taken over security duties in Ferguson, allowing the local police department to focus on routine policing tasks.

reference: – He's out of the hospital and expected to be OK, but one of the two police officers shot last night in Ferguson still has a bullet lodged behind his ear. The 32-year-old got shot below his right eye, and the bullet ended up just below his right ear, reports the St. Louis Post-Dispatch. The other officer, 41, got hit in the right shoulder, and the bullet exited out his back. He's out of the hospital, too. Police haven't made any arrests in the shootings, but they took three people in for questioning this morning. "We're lucky by God's grace that we didn't lose those two officers last night," said St. Louis County Police Chief Jon Belmar. "We could have buried two police officers next week because of this." Belmar called the attack an "ambush," and Eric Holder used the same word along with some stronger ones, reports CBS News. “This was not someone trying to bring healing to Ferguson," the attorney general said today. "This was a damn punk, a punk, who was trying to sow discord.” Protests are expected to resume tonight, and the Missouri Highway Patrol and St. Louis County Police will take the reins from the Ferguson department, whose chief resigned yesterday. Police think the bullets came from a pistol fired from across the street from the department. In amateur video, two shots are heard, followed by a man screaming in pain, reports AP. The voice of an unidentified person is heard saying, "Acknowledgement nine months ago would have kept that from happening."

final_summary:
As the 2012 Republican primary enters its elimination round, the remaining candidates face a critical challenge: survival. With less than two months until the Iowa caucuses, the pressure is mounting, and any missteps could prove disastrous. Herman Cain's campaign hangs in the balance, threatened by allegations of sexual harassment that have derailed his momentum. His rivals, including Newt Gingrich and Rick Santorum, must capitalize on this opportunity or risk being left behind.

Mitt Romney, the front-runner, appears relatively insulated, but his challengers are trying to chip away at his lead. The "not-Mitt" movement has gained traction, with critics like George Will and Erick Erickson launching scathing attacks. Jon Huntsman, a dark horse candidate, is attempting to capitalize on this sentiment with his own anti-Romney ad. However, it remains unclear whether Huntsman or anyone else can dent Romney's armor.

Newt Gingrich sees an opening in Cain's decline and is poised to rise in the polls. His experience and charisma make him a formidable contender, but his past debate performances have been divisive. To succeed, he must tone down his contrarian tendencies and showcase his economic expertise. Meanwhile, flagging candidates like Rick Perry and Michele Bachmann struggle to regain relevance, with Perry's wealth serving as a temporary lifeline.

The upcoming CNBC debate in Michigan will be a crucial test for these candidates. Cain must navigate the fallout from the harassment allegations and refocus attention on his economic policies. The other contenders will be watching closely, deciding whether to confront Cain directly or let him falter. As the clock ticks down to the Iowa caucuses, the stakes are higher than ever, and only the strongest candidates will survive.

reference: – With Iowa just two months away, the stakes are high for tonight’s GOP debate. Politico describes it as an “elimination round,” with everyone except Mitt Romney—and maybe the seemingly immovable Ron Paul—“one misstep away from political death." Here’s what to watch for: Will anyone touch Herman Cain’s scandal? Focusing on that circus might hurt the GOP brand, the Daily Beast points out, “so they may instead let him bleed slowly as they avert their eyes.” Who can be the Not-Mitt? Anti-Romney sentiment coalesced this week, but has no standard bearer. Newt Gingrich is gaining some buzz, especially after his heart-to-heart with Cain, so some speculate that he's next. Who matters? “For Huntsman, Santorum, and Bachmann, the debate is totally about relevance and trying to maintain some respectability,” an ex-Huntsman campaign manager says. “Not much more, I don’t think.” Will Rick Perry be sober? Perry is reeling after his, um, interesting New Hampshire speech. Perry can come off “like a deer caught in headlights,” says one strategist. “He’s got to decide, what are the two or three messages I’m going to drive?” What about Mitt? “Romney can begin to play ball-control politics,” says a former McCain adviser. “He just needs to be reassuring and not say anything really dumb."

final_summary:
At the recent Group of 20 (G-20) summit in Toronto, world leaders agreed on a plan to cut their annual budget deficits in half by 2013 and stabilize their overall debt levels by 2016. However, the agreement was reached after a contentious discussion between the US and European leaders, with the latter pushing for faster deficit reduction. US President Barack Obama expressed concerns that cutting too quickly could harm the fragile global economic recovery.

Canadian Prime Minister Stephen Harper, the host of the meeting, had proposed the deficit-reduction targets, which were backed by Germany and Britain. The US, along with Japan, India, and some other countries, had initially objected to the targets, citing concerns that rapid deficit reduction could lead to a double-dip recession. To address these concerns, the G-20 statement emphasized that deficit reduction should be tailored to each country's unique circumstances.

Despite the agreement, experts warn that the plan may not be enough to prevent a new downturn. Some economists believe that the pace of deficit reduction may be too aggressive, particularly in Europe, where austerity measures are already being implemented. Others argue that the plan does not address the underlying structural issues driving the global economic imbalance, such as the large trade deficits in the US and the excessive savings rate in China.

The G-20 leaders also failed to reach a consensus on a global bank tax, with Canada and Australia opposing the idea. They did, however, commit to implementing new capital standards for banks, with a deadline of 2012. However, some countries may not meet this deadline, and the implementation process is expected to be complex.

In addition to the deficit-reduction plan, the G-20 leaders discussed other key issues, including banking regulations and the need for emerging markets like China to rebalance their economies by increasing spending and investment. China was encouraged to allow its currency to fluctuate more freely and to strengthen its social safety net. The G-20 statement also acknowledged the need for advanced surplus countries, such as Germany, to narrow the gap between their imports and exports.

The summit was marked by a sense of caution and uncertainty, with leaders aware of the risks of a new economic downturn. While the agreement on deficit reduction was seen as a victory for German Chancellor Angela Merkel, who had pushed for faster action, others saw it as a compromise that may not be sufficient to address the underlying economic challenges facing the world.

The G-20 leaders also welcomed the announcement by China that it would allow a gradual appreciation of its currency, which is seen as a step towards rebalancing the global economy. However, the removal of a proposed clause praising China for its commitment to greater exchange-rate flexibility suggests that Beijing may be reluctant to be singled out for special treatment.

Overall, the G-20 summit highlighted the ongoing challenges facing the global economy, including high debt levels, slow growth, and uneven economic development. While the agreement on deficit reduction is a step in the right direction, it remains to be seen whether it will be enough to prevent a new economic downturn and promote sustained growth and stability in the years ahead.

reference: – The leaders of the Group of 20 nations wrapped up their 2-day summit today in Toronto, striking an agreement to reduce deficits over the next 3 to 6 years without endangering the global economic rebound. The major economic powers vowed to cut their deficits in half by 2013 and "stabilize" debt by 2016 while saying the reductions would be "carefully calibrated to sustain the recovery," reports the Washington Post. The dates are not formal deadlines, thanks in large part to pressure exerted by the US and other allies—including Japan, to which the timeline doesn't apply, the New York Times notes. The American delegation was in the minority cautioning about a double-dip recession. Said one expert: "Aiming for a gradually improving debt-to-GDP ratio by 2016 is hardly wild-eyed fiscal conservatism."

final_summary: Taylor Swift, a 26-year-old country-pop artist, has pledged to donate $1 million to the state's disaster relief fund following the devastating floods in Louisiana. The musician expressed her sympathy towards the affected residents, recalling the warm welcome she received from them during her performance in the state last year as part of her "1989 World Tour." Swift stated that the Louisiana fans made her team feel "completely at home," but noted that it is "heartbreaking" to see many people displaced from their homes due to the flooding. At least 11 fatalities have been reported, and over 40,000 homes have been damaged, while approximately 60,000 individuals have registered for disaster aid through the Federal Emergency Management Agency. Swift encouraged others to contribute to the relief efforts and offer support to those affected by sending their love and prayers. This generous donation marks one of several philanthropic gestures made by Swift, including previous contributions to various causes such as a young fan battling cancer, the Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, and the Red Cross in Cedar Rapids, Iowa.

reference: – Louisiana was good to Taylor Swift and now the singer is giving back in the form of a $1 million donation to help victims of the devastating floods that have killed at least 11 people, reports the AP. The 26-year-old says the donation is a big thank you to her “wonderful fans” in Louisiana “who made us feel completely at home” when Swift kicked off her last world tour there. “The fact that so many people in Louisiana have been forced out of their own homes this week is heartbreaking," she says in a statement, urging "those who can to help out and send your love and prayers their way during this devastating time." The contribution isn’t Swift’s first. Last year the pop idol gave $50,000 each to a young fan battling cancer and an ailing nephew of one of her backup dancers, notes US Weekly. Among others: $50,000 to Philadelphia Children’s Hospital in 2014, and $100,000 to flood relief in Iowa in 2008. (For much more on the scope of the devastating flooding, click here.)

final_summary:
Bloom Energy, a Silicon Valley-based startup, recently announced its low-emissions energy server, dubbed the Bloom Energy Server, which has garnered significant attention worldwide. The server, developed from a NASA project intended for planetary exploration, boasts a 2-to-1 efficiency over electric utilities, allowing a unit the size of a parking space to produce 400Kw of electricity using less fuel than a traditional power plant. 

The Bloom Energy Server relies on a fuel source, such as natural gas, to operate, converting it into electricity through a chemical reaction involving oxygen. This process is not unique, as coal and petroleum are also derived from ancient organic matter that harnessed solar energy. The server's ability to utilize various fuels, including biogases, sets it apart from other fuel cells.

Initially, the Bloom Energy Server is marketed towards companies willing to invest $700,000-$800,000 in the device, which promises to pay for itself through reduced electricity bills within 3-5 years. Electric utilities are also expected to be interested in the server as a replacement for less environmentally friendly and costly power-generating equipment. However, the long-term viability of the server remains uncertain, with concerns surrounding its lifespan, downtime, and heat management.

While Bloom Energy aims to bring the server to residential markets within 10 years at a cost of $3,000, this timeline seems ambitious, considering the complexity of scaling up production while maintaining quality control. The company's secrecy regarding its technology has raised eyebrows among experts, who point out that the Bloom Energy Server is not revolutionary and lacks transparency in its pricing and efficiency.

Several experts have expressed skepticism about the Bloom Energy Server's capabilities, citing concerns over its reliability, safety, and durability. They argue that the server's operational lifetime, energy efficiency, and ability to utilize heat generated during operation are crucial factors that need to be addressed before widespread adoption. Additionally, the reliance on subsidies to make the server economically viable raises questions about its long-term sustainability.

Despite these concerns, Bloom Energy's CEO, K.R. Sridhar, remains optimistic about the server's potential, stating that it can power a neighborhood before being scaled down for residential use. The company's focus on reducing costs and increasing efficiency is essential to achieving its goals, particularly in reaching developing markets where subsidies may not be readily available.

In conclusion, while the Bloom Energy Server shows promise as a low-emissions energy solution, its long-term viability and scalability remain uncertain. As the company continues to develop and refine its technology, it must address the concerns raised by experts and demonstrate its commitment to transparency and accountability.

reference: – Bloom Energy's plans to put a zero-emission energy server into mass production is an "exciting development," writes David Coursey, but the company has loads of work to do before the so-called Bloom Box becomes the game-changer the hype suggests. The company is promising miniature $3,000 servers will be on the market in 10 years to power homes, but that's a long way from the big $800,000 device it's currently pitching to companies. The company didn't offer many details on how it will get there, notes AP, and plenty of other companies are at work on the same concept. One fuel cell expert tells National Geographic he's "pretty pissed" at the lack of explanation and dismissed it as "all hype." Coursey is more hopeful at PCWorld. "Who can really say? If I were guessing, I'd imagine Bloom servers might appear to power whole neighborhoods and be operated by traditional power companies before the home server appears. It all depends on how the technology and market place develops over time."

final_summary: In a recent visit to Boston, former Alaska Governor Sarah Palin shared her interpretation of the significance of Paul Revere's famous midnight ride during the Revolutionary War. According to Palin, Revere's actions were aimed at warning the British that they would not take away American arms, achieved through the use of bells and warning shots while riding through towns. However, historical records indicate that Revere's actual mission was to alert John Hancock and Samuel Adams, key figures in the American resistance, about the approaching British army, rather than the British themselves. This discrepancy highlights a fundamental misunderstanding of the event's purpose and context.

This discrepancy highlights a fundamental misunderstanding of the event's purpose and context.

reference: – Lately, it seems that the history of the American Revolution is not the strong suit of the two most well-known Republican women who could decide to run for president. Back in March, Michele Bachmann stated that "the shot heard 'round the world" at Lexington and Concord was fired in New Hampshire. Then yesterday Sarah Palin made a history error of her own: Stopping in Boston on her bus tour, she stated that Paul Revere warned the British about the American arms locations. ABC News has her comment: "He who warned, uh, the ... the British that they weren't gonna be taking away our arms, uh, by ringing those bells and, um, by making sure that as he's riding his horse through town to send those warning shots and bells that, uh, we were gonna be secure and we were gonna be free ... and we were gonna be armed." Click for more.

final_summary:
On September 6th, a violent altercation occurred at a birthday party hosted by Korey Klingenmeyer in Wasilla, Alaska, attended by several members of the Palin family, including parents Sarah and Todd, children Bristol and Willow, and family friend Matthew McKenna. The incident began when a 60-year-old woman allegedly pushed Willow, prompting Bristol to intervene. However, according to multiple accounts, Bristol then became involved in a physical confrontation with a man identified as Korey Klingenmeyer, who claimed he was defending himself against her repeated punches.

In a recorded conversation with police, Bristol alleged that Klingenmeyer pushed her to the ground, dragged her by her feet, and verbally abused her with profanity-laced insults, including calling her a "c**t" and a "sl*t." Her mother, Sarah, was also present and expressed frustration with the situation, stating that the perpetrator was being allowed to leave while her daughters were being detained for questioning.

However, other partygoers disputed Bristol's account, with Matthew McKenna claiming that she had initiated the physical altercation by punching Klingenmeyer multiple times. Another witness corroborated McKenna's version, stating that several women had thrown Bristol to the ground to restrain her. 

According to the police report, Klingenmeyer stated that he had asked Bristol to leave the party after she began punching him, and that he had grabbed her arm to hold her back, eventually pushing her down while holding her hand as she attempted to strike him.

Despite the conflicting accounts, no charges were filed in connection with the incident. The Palin family has largely remained silent on the matter, although Sarah initially posted on Facebook expressing pride in her children's defense of family values. The exact circumstances surrounding the altercation and the extent of the injuries sustained by those involved remain unclear due to the varying testimonies and lack of formal charges.

reference: – Sarah Palin has already offered an explanation for the reportedly booze-fueled brouhaha in Anchorage her family was involved in last month, but now there's apparently audiotape to supplement that account. TMZ has released what it says is a copy of Bristol, Willow, and Sarah Palin's complaints to police outside a Sept. 6 party that got out of control (Radar and Extra also posted portions of the audio). The woman on the tape alleged to be Bristol "hysterically" tells cops that sister Willow had come up to her at the bash and said, "‘Some old lady just [expletive] pushed me. She just hit me." Bristol then adds that when she went to confront the alleged pusher, "some guy gets in my face, pushes me down on the grass, drags me across the grass! [Says to me], 'You sl-t, you f---ing c-nt." (Willow says on the tape that that guy was Korey Klingenmeyer, the host of the party.) Bristol then expands her litany of injustices: "They took my $300 sunglasses. They took my f---ing shoes and I’m f---ing left here? … Where is my s--t? I have a 5-year-old in the car!" The police report indicates Klingenmeyer asked Bristol to leave because he didn't want her to pick fights with anyone. Sarah Palin—who can allegedly be heard on the tape advising her progeny not to use obscenities and saying, "But they let the bad guys go!"—posted support for Bristol on Facebook on Sept. 19, raving, "I love my Bristol! My straight-shooter is one of the strongest young women you'll ever meet. I have to say this as a proud mama: … my kids' defense of family makes my heart soar!"

final_summary:
In early October 2014, a devastating outbreak of the Enterovirus D68 (EV-D68) swept across the United States, claiming its first victim in a four-year-old New Jersey boy named Eli Waller. Eli, a triplet born smaller and lighter than his sisters, lived in Hamilton Township with his parents and siblings. On September 23, his mother kept him home from school due to mild pinkeye symptoms, but he seemed fine and had attended classes on September 21 and 22 without incident. However, between bedtime on September 24 and waking up on September 25, Eli tragically passed away in his sleep, displaying no other symptoms besides the initial pinkeye.

The Mercer County Medical Examiner's Office subsequently confirmed that Eli's cause of death was Enterovirus D68, a highly contagious respiratory virus that had resurfaced in the US in August and spread to at least 43 states. According to the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the virus has been linked to the deaths of four individuals, including Eli, although the exact role of the virus in these fatalities remains unclear. 

Eli's father, Andy Waller, remembered his son as a determined and loving individual who always strived to please others, despite facing challenges in his daily life. In response to his son's passing, Andy established The First Day of School Foundation to provide support for students with special needs. 

Health officials emphasized that there are no specific treatments or prevention methods available for EV-D68, and the best course of action is to practice standard hand hygiene protocols, such as frequent handwashing and covering coughs. The CDC noted that while the virus primarily affects healthy children, it can be particularly severe for those with pre-existing asthma or compromised immune systems. As the country navigated the peak of the enterovirus season, concerns arose regarding the potential long-term effects of the virus, including reports of limb weakness and paralysis in nine children in Colorado, which is currently under investigation by local and national health authorities.

reference: – A 4-year-old New Jersey boy who died after contracting enterovirus-68 had no symptoms other than pinkeye when his mom put him to sleep on the night of Sept. 24; by the time she went in the next morning to wake him up, he had died, Bloomberg reports. Eli Waller stayed home from school on the 24th because of the pinkeye, but had attended school earlier in the week. And unlike other children hard-hit by the virus, Eli was not asthmatic. He was "a beautiful mix of eagerness and hesitancy, need and striving, caution and surprise, all of which were grounded in a pure, unconditional love," his father tells ABC News. Born last in a birthing of triplets, Eli came out "smaller and lighter" than his two sisters but didn't let that hold him back, his dad says: "Eli was not the type to give up, and even though things never really came easily to him, he would just plug away, day after day." Enterovirus-68 has struck at least 43 states, hospitalizing hundreds of children, paralyzing others, and apparently playing a role in at least four US deaths. On the brighter side, the enterovirus season should subside later this fall—but with the rare 68 strain dominating, experts say it's smart to follow basic hygiene practices like covering coughs, staying home when unwell, and washing hands in warm water. (Meanwhile, the CDC is trying to confirm links between EV-68 and cases of paralysis.)

final_summary:
On November 7, 2018, following the midterm elections, President Donald Trump announced that Attorney General Jeff Sessions had submitted his resignation, effective immediately. Sessions' departure placed oversight of the special counsel's Russia probe in the hands of Matthew Whitaker, a critic of the investigation and advocate for limiting its scope. The move came after months of tension between Trump and Sessions, who had recused himself from the inquiry into alleged Russian interference in the 2016 elections. 

Trump had frequently expressed dissatisfaction with Sessions, blaming him for allowing the appointment of special counsel Robert Mueller, who was tasked with examining potential obstruction of justice by the President. Sessions' recusal had been a point of contention between the two men, with Trump publicly criticizing Sessions for his decision and Sessions pushing back, stating that he had consulted with career ethics officials at the Justice Department before making the decision.

Whitaker, who will serve as acting attorney general until a permanent replacement is confirmed, has been vocal in his criticism of Mueller's investigation and has suggested that it should be limited in scope. His appointment has raised concerns among Democrats, who have called for him to recuse himself from overseeing the investigation. 

In a statement, Whitaker vowed to lead the Department of Justice with "high ethical standards" and to uphold the rule of law. However, his past comments on the investigation have sparked fears that he may attempt to undermine Mueller's work.

Sessions' departure comes amid a backdrop of controversy surrounding the Trump administration, including the guilty pleas of former Trump associates Michael Cohen and Paul Manafort, who were both charged with various crimes related to their involvement in the 2016 campaign. The move has also drawn attention to the ongoing Mueller investigation, which has been a source of tension between Trump and Sessions.

As the investigation continues, Democrats have vowed to hold the administration accountable, with Senator Chuck Schumer calling for Whitaker to recuse himself from overseeing the investigation. Representative Jerry Nadler, who is poised to chair the House Judiciary Committee next year, has also pledged to seek answers about the reasons behind Sessions' removal and the implications for Mueller's investigation.

reference: – Jeff Sessions is out as attorney general, report CNN and the AP. White House chief of staff John Kelly reportedly asked him to resign Wednesday, and Sessions' resignation letter was subsequently delivered to the White House. The speed of the move, coming so soon after polls closed, "suggested how eager Mr. Trump was for Mr. Sessions to depart," observes the Wall Street Journal. "At your request, I am submitting my resignation," Sessions wrote in a letter addressed to the president, which you can read here. "Since the day I was honored to be sworn in as Attorney General of the United States, I came to work at the Department of Justice every day determined to do my duty and serve my country. I have done so to the best of my ability, working to support the fundamental legal processes that are the foundation of justice." More:

final_summary:
In recent months, several disturbing cases of municipal code enforcement abuse have come to light, highlighting the need for reform in various cities across the United States. One such instance involves Ebony Conner, a single mother of five in Cahokia, Illinois, who was arrested and briefly jailed for failing to mow her lawn and clean up her yard, despite not receiving a ticket prior to her arrest. Conner's experience is not unique, as a 70-year-old woman in Prichard, Alabama, was arrested for not mowing her lawn, but the citation was issued for a property she no longer owned.

A similar pattern of abuse has been observed in Pagedale, Missouri, where residents are fined for minor infractions such as chipped paint, mismatched curtains, and walking on the wrong side of a crosswalk. Between 2010 and 2014, the number of non-traffic tickets issued to residents increased by 495%, generating a significant profit incentive for law enforcement. As a result, many residents find themselves trapped in a vicious cycle of fines and debt, making it impossible for them to address the issues cited by the city.

Plaintiffs Vincent Blount and Valarie Whitner, who reside in Pagedale, have accumulated substantial fines and fees, including a $2,800 debt, largely due to tickets related to their home. Despite their efforts to pay off the debt through a long-term payment plan, they were unable to make the necessary repairs to meet the city's standards, leading to additional fines and threats of demolition. Valarie was even arrested in front of her home and taken to city hall due to an unspecified ticket.

Mildred Bryant, an 84-year-old resident of Pagedale, has faced numerous code violations, including demands to repaint her entire two-story home, including the foundation, and install matching curtains and window treatments. Given her advanced age and limited physical and financial capabilities, these demands are unrealistic and pose a significant burden on her.

These cases illustrate the widespread problem of municipal code enforcement abuse, where residents are subjected to excessive fines, harassment, and even arrest for minor infractions. The plaintiffs in the Pagedale case are seeking to put an end to the city's unconstitutional treatment of its residents through a class-action lawsuit.

Meanwhile, in a separate development, former New York City Mayor Rudy Giuliani has been criticized for his role in spreading misinformation about the Mueller investigation. In a candid moment, Giuliani revealed that his media tour was "all just a big PR campaign," aimed at undermining the credibility of Special Counsel Robert Mueller's investigation. However, Giuliani's attempts to spin the truth have been met with ridicule, with comedian Samantha Bee pointing out that his "brilliant propaganda" is nothing more than a desperate attempt to salvage the reputation of President Trump.

reference: – An Illinois woman with five kids was arrested July 7 and briefly thrown in jail for an offense that the town of Cahokia obviously feels strongly about: not mowing her lawn, the Week reports. Single mom Ebony Conner tells KMOV she was verbally warned by a municipal code-enforcement officer back in June and also told to get rid of garbage and broken-down cars from her driveway—but she thought she had more time to fix things, and she definitely didn't think she'd be hauled off to jail. "I understand I violated a code, but … give me a ticket first, make me appear in court," she says. "I know there's gotta be channels other than, 'If you don't cut your grass, we're arresting you right now.'" Conner says she hails from Wisconsin and has spent time in St. Louis, Mo., and she'd never heard of such a thing in either place. While she was detained (cops say she was held for about a half-hour in jail), her five children remained at home, with just a code enforcement officer patrolling the outside of her house, Conner says, adding, "I said 'I don't trust him. I don't know him at all.' … And they still hauled me off." She says she would've cut her grass by hand if she had known her long grass was an arrestable offense. The Institute for Justice lists similar cases that some consider to be an abuse of municipal code enforcement, including those in the town of Pagedale, Mo., where residents can be slammed with fines and tickets for such offenses as barbecuing in front of one's house, having mismatched curtains, and walking on the left-hand side of a crosswalk. (Postal workers mow residents' lawns in Finland.)

final_summary:
Pope Francis has reaffirmed the Catholic Church's stance on traditional marriage being between a man and a woman, yet expressed openness to considering civil unions in certain contexts. In an interview with Corriere della Sera, an Italian daily, the Pope stated that states often justify civil unions to provide economic security to cohabiting couples, ensuring rights such as access to healthcare. This marks the first time a Pope has shown tentative acceptance of civil unions.

Francis' comments come amidst ongoing discussions within the Catholic Church regarding LGBTQ+ issues. While some Catholic bishops have supported civil unions for same-sex couples, others strongly contest the idea. The Pope's previous stances on these matters have been nuanced; during his tenure as Archbishop of Buenos Aires, he reportedly supported civil unions, but later clarified that he opposed same-sex marriage.

A Vatican spokesperson attempted to clarify the Pope's remarks, stating that Francis did not intend to engage in debate on the topic of civil unions and instead emphasized the importance of the state fulfilling its responsibilities towards its citizens. However, the Pope's comments suggest a willingness to explore alternative forms of union recognition.

Beyond the issue of civil unions, Pope Francis discussed various other contentious topics, including the Catholic Church's ban on contraception and the role of women within the Church. He praised Pope Paul VI for reiterating the ban in 1968, but emphasized the need for mercy and attentiveness to individual circumstances. Francis also hinted at potential changes to the Church's stance on women's roles, suggesting that women should be involved in decision-making processes and that the Church is engaging in "deep reflection" on the matter.

Regarding the clergy sex abuse scandal, the Pope acknowledged the severity of the issue, stating that it has left "very deep wounds" on victims. He claimed that the Church has been more transparent about the issue than other institutions, despite criticism from a UN panel that accused the Vatican of failing to acknowledge the scope of the problem and protect children.

Additionally, Pope Francis discussed his relationship with Pope Emeritus Benedict XVI, describing him as a "wise grandfather" and emphasizing the importance of his continued involvement in the Church. Francis also dismissed the notion that he sneaks out at night to feed the homeless, calling it a form of "mythology" that he finds "offensive."

The Pope's comments were made in the context of his first anniversary as leader of the Catholic Church, marked by various celebrations and commemorations. The Vatican has released commemorative stamps, coins, and a DVD featuring behind-the-scenes footage of the Pope's early days in office.

reference: – As an Argentine cardinal, Jorge Bergoglio backed gay civil unions, and Pope Francis is signaling in an interview that the Catholic Church as a whole could tolerate at least some of them. While maintaining the church's position that "marriage is between a man and a woman," Francis nonetheless conceded that "we have to look at different cases and evaluate them in their variety." He says civil unions can be a financial help to couples when it comes to matters like "medical care," notes CNN. Other highlights in his interview with Italy's Corriere della Sera, as per the AP: He's been the subject of comic-book-inspired street art and a fan recently offered him an imitation Oscar, but Francis isn't digging the whole Superpope thing. "I don't like ideological interpretations, this type of mythology of Pope Francis. If I'm not mistaken, Sigmund Freud said that in every idealization there's an aggression. Depicting the pope as a sort of Superman, a star, is offensive to me," he noted. "The pope is a man who laughs, cries, sleeps calmly, and has friends like everyone else. A normal person." A new magazine looks unlikely to alter that "superhero" perception. Today sees the launch of Il Mio Papa, or My Pope—"a sort of fanzine," says editor Aldo Vitali. The new magazine will cover the pope's pronouncements, offering a weekly papal centerfold emblazoned with a quote. It will take a look at Francis' personal life, "but of course it can’t be like something you’d do for One Direction," Vitali tells the New York Times. "We aim to be more respectful, more noble." Francis is opening up the gardens of Castel Gandolfo, the papal summer residence, to members of the public, the Telegraph reports. "It was Pope Francis himself" who made the decision," says a Vatican official.

final_summary:
In response to a tweet referencing Abraham Lincoln's inaugural address, a user expressed their hopes that President Donald Trump would serve all Americans as vigorously as they had served their ratings during their tenure as Governor. This message was accompanied by a comparison of Trump's ratings performance on The Celebrity Apprentice to that of his successor, Arnold Schwarzenegger, who hosted the show's premiere after Trump stepped down. According to Nielsen's tracking numbers, The New Celebrity Apprentice averaged approximately 4.9 million total viewers across its two-hour premiere, falling short of rival networks ABC and CBS in the 8 p.m. ET primetime slot. 

The show's premiere, which featured Schwarzenegger as host, trailed behind ABC's The Bachelor premiere, which drew nearly 6.6 million viewers, and CBS's lineup of four half-hour sitcoms, which averaged over 6 million viewers. In contrast, Trump's previous season of The Celebrity Apprentice, which aired in 2014-2015, averaged 7.6 million nightly viewers and boasted a 46% higher rating in the key demographic of viewers aged 18 to 49. 

NBC heavily promoted Schwarzenegger's takeover before the show's January debut, but the ratings for the premiere were down 22% from Trump's final season. Despite facing stiff competition from The Bachelor and a college football game, NBC was likely expecting a stronger start to the post-Trump era of the Apprentice franchise. However, the show still managed to provide a solid lead-in for the new NBC game show The Wall, which retained most of the Apprentice's viewers and secured the top ratings in the 10 p.m. time-slot among the key younger demographic.

reference: – Arnold Schwarzenegger seems to think Donald Trump needs a lesson in making friends after the president-elect chose to criticize his Celebrity Apprentice successor. "Wow, the ratings are in and [new host] Arnold Schwarzenegger got 'swamped' (or destroyed) by comparison to the ratings machine, DJT," Trump tweeted Friday after the Trump-less show premiered Monday to 4.9 million viewers, according to Nielsen. The last season hosted by Trump averaged 7.6 million viewers, per Fortune. "So much for … being a movie star," Trump continued. "But who cares, he supported [John] Kasich & Hillary [Clinton]." Schwarzenegger offered up a reply on Twitter, tweeting, "I wish you the best of luck and I hope you'll work for ALL of the American people as aggressively as you worked for your ratings." He next shared a video he made after the election in which he quotes Abraham Lincoln's inaugural speech, beginning, "We are not enemies, but friends. We must not be enemies." "Please study this quote ... @realDonaldTrump," he tweeted with the video. "It inspired me every day I was Governor, and I hope it inspires you." (The New York Times is unimpressed with the new season.)

final_summary:
Hugh Hefner's personal life was recently affected by the departure of his fiancée, Crystal Harris, who left the Playboy mansion following their breakup. However, it appears that Hefner did not stay alone for long. According to reports, the Playboy twins, Karissa and Kristina Shannon, who were previously involved with Hefner, have returned to the mansion approximately one and a half years after they initially moved out.

The twins' decision to move back into the mansion comes shortly after Karissa ended her relationship with Sam Jones III, allowing both sisters to reunite with Hefner at the mansion. This development suggests that Hefner may be receiving emotional support from the twins as he navigates the aftermath of his breakup with Crystal Harris.

Hugh Hefner's personal life was recently affected by the departure of his fiancée, Crystal Harris, who left the Playboy mansion following their breakup. However, it appears that Hefner did not stay alone for long. According to reports, the Playboy twins, Karissa and Kristina Shannon, who were previously involved with Hefner, have returned to the mansion approximately one and a half years after they initially moved out.

The twins' decision to move back into the mansion comes shortly after Karissa ended her relationship with Sam Jones III, allowing both sisters to reunite with Hefner at the mansion. This development suggests that Hefner may be receiving emotional support from the twins as he navigates the aftermath of his breakup with Crystal Harris.

reference: – Crystal Harris and Hugh Hefner didn't tie the knot yesterday, and they didn't mope, either. Hef took to Twitter to share his chin-up attitude: "This was going to be my wedding day, but life is full of surprises," the 85-year-old wrote. "After all is said & done, staying single is probably for the best." Single, however, is not code for "alone." Perez Hilton reports that exes Karissa and Kristina Shannon (yep, twins) have already moved back into the mansion, which they left a year and a half ago. And TMZ reports that Hef has been cozying up to Miss January 2011. And how did the runaway bride spend her day? Hanging poolside in Las Vegas in a teeny bikini and high heels with a group of friends, one of which was Heidi Montag. But do we detect a hint of sadness in Hugh's voice? A series of tweets posted in rapid succession this afternoon sound a little down. An example: "The Sunday night Mansion movie is Elle Fanning, Amanda Michalka & Kyle Chandler in JJ Abrams' Super 8. Life goes on."

final_summary:
Following his arrest on charges of attempting to rape a Manhattan hotel maid, International Monetary Fund Chief Dominique Strauss-Kahn was taken into custody at New York City's Rikers Island jail complex. Due to concerns over his safety, Strauss-Kahn was placed on suicide watch, a measure typically reserved for inmates deemed at high risk of self-harm. This decision was reportedly made after a mental health assessment revealed Strauss-Kahn's mood and the sensitive nature of the case warranted additional precautions. 

As part of his special treatment, Strauss-Kahn was assigned to a private wing within the jail, housing approximately 14 cells, all of which were initially left vacant for his exclusive use. The wing is typically used to isolate inmates with contagious diseases such as measles or tuberculosis. Within his cell, Strauss-Kahn has access to basic amenities, including a toilet, sink, and meals served at set times - 5 a.m. for breakfast, 11 a.m. for lunch, and either 4 p.m. or 5 p.m. for dinner.

To ensure his safety, Strauss-Kahn is subject to constant monitoring, with guards checking on him every 15-30 minutes. Additionally, he has been fitted with a Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) machine due to his diagnosed sleep apnea, a condition that causes temporary cessation of breathing during sleep. The CPAP machine delivers supplemental oxygen through a mask worn while sleeping.

Strauss-Kahn is permitted to leave his cell periodically and roam the wing, although he is always accompanied by guards when doing so. He is also granted one hour of outdoor recreation per day, during which time he remains under guard supervision and does not interact with other inmates. In contrast to other prisoners, Strauss-Kahn is not required to wear a standard issue prison uniform and may choose his own attire, with the exception of surrendering his shoes, which are replaced with slip-on sneakers. However, he is allowed to wear dress shoes for court appearances.

Strauss-Kahn's unique status as a pre-trial detainee is reflected in his identification method, using a statewide criminal justice system code rather than an inmate number. His NYSID number is 09132366L. Despite these accommodations, Strauss-Kahn's lawyer maintains that there is no evidence supporting the allegations against him, and he remains held without bail until his next court appearance, scheduled for Friday.

reference: – It's a long way from the hotel suites he's used to, but Dominique Strauss-Kahn still has plenty of space to himself. The IMF chief is being held at Rikers Island on sexual assault charges in an empty 14-cell wing of the massive jail, reports AP. He has been placed on suicide watch and issued a medical device to make sure he doesn't stop breathing during the night. The suicide watch was ordered as a precaution, not because Strauss-Kahn seems likely to harm himself, a source tells NBC. Because of his status, the Frenchman was assigned to a facility usually used to house inmates with extremely contagious diseases. Officials say Strauss-Kahn is free to leave his cell and wander the wing from time to time. He is allowed to go outside for recreation for an hour once a day, accompanied by guards, in a spot where he won't encounter other inmates. As a pre-trial detainee, he is not required to wear a prison uniform.

final_summary: Andrew Simes' grandfather began a long-standing tradition in 1952, sending a Christmas card to Queen Elizabeth II every holiday season, which continued until his death in 2011. This gesture caught the attention of the Queen, who reportedly recognized the sender during a meeting in Izmir, Turkey in 1972, where she jokingly acknowledged the consistent correspondence. The Queen even went so far as to write a congratulatory letter to the elderly man on his 100th birthday. Following his passing in 2011, Andrew Simes, his grandson, decided to carry on the tradition, sending a Christmas card to the Queen in 2012. To his surprise, he received a heartfelt condolence letter from the Queen's office, expressing her sadness over the loss of his grandfather and extending her sympathies to the family. This remarkable exchange has left a lasting impact on Andrew Simes, who continues to send Christmas cards to the Queen annually, highlighting the power of small acts of kindness and the enduring legacy of his grandfather's tradition.

reference: – If you're a commoner who wants to get Queen Elizabeth II's attention, apparently it is possible, but it will take decades of work. In a touching Facebook post pointed out by Mashable, Andrew Simes writes that his grandfather had always made it a point to send a Christmas card to his king or queen. Elizabeth had been getting the cards since 1952, and happened to meet Simes' grandpa at a reception in Turkey in 1972—whereupon she told him, "so it's you who keeps sending me those lovely Christmas cards," Simes says. The queen later sent a note on Simes' grandpa's 100th birthday. The older man died at age 102 in 2011, and Simes took over sending the Christmas cards that year—which is where the story gets really poignant. In January 2012, Simes got a letter from Buckingham Palace. "In it was written: 'When I received a letter from a different Simes this Christmas, I instructed my office to research your grandfather's whereabouts. Therefore it is with much sadness, I have learned of his passing and extend my condolences to you and your family,'" Simes writes. "I couldn't fight back the tears then, nor can I fight them back every time I remember this story of two people who left a lifelong impression on each other." He's still sending Her Majesty the annual holiday greeting; see an image here.

final_summary:
In a shocking twist, the United States Air Force Academy revealed that racial slurs posted outside the dorm rooms of five black students in September 2017 were written by one of the students themselves. The incident occurred at the academy's prep school, which is located on the same grounds as the main academy but serves a separate purpose, helping promising students meet the entrance requirements for the four-year program. The prep school houses around 240 students, significantly fewer than the approximately 4,000 students enrolled in the main academy.

The discovery came after a stern warning from the academy's superintendent, Lt. Gen. Jay Silveria, who addressed the issue in a widely-viewed speech to cadets. In it, he emphasized that racism had no place at the academy and instructed cadets to treat others with dignity and respect, famously saying, "If you can't treat someone with dignity and respect, get out."

Silveria, a veteran fighter pilot who took command of the academy in August 2017, has prioritized creating a climate of respect and dignity among cadets and staff. However, the academy has faced issues with sexual misconduct in recent years, although instances of racial incidents have been relatively rare.

The revelation that the student responsible for writing the slurs was one of the five targeted individuals adds a complex layer to the situation. While the student is no longer at the academy, the reason for their departure remains unclear due to privacy laws. The incident unfolded amidst a broader national conversation about racism and social justice, following high-profile events such as the racial violence in Charlottesville, Virginia, and debates over NFL players kneeling during the national anthem.

reference: – Racial slurs posted outside the dorm rooms of five black students at the Air Force Academy were written by one of those students, the school said Tuesday. The announcement was a jarring turn in an episode that prompted the academy's superintendent to warn students that racists were not welcome at the school—a speech that attracted nationwide attention. The student is no longer at the school, the academy said. A spokesman declined to say whether the student withdrew or was expelled, citing privacy laws, the AP reports. The student's name wasn't released. The slurs were found in September at a dormitory that houses students attending the academy's prep school. In a written statement Tuesday, the academy said, "We can confirm that one of the cadet candidates who was allegedly targeted by racist remarks written outside of their dorm room was actually responsible for the act. The individual admitted responsibility and this was validated by the investigation." The statement added, "Racism has no place at the academy, in any shape or form." Academy Superintendent Lt. Gen. Jay Silveria gave his stern speech to cadets shortly after the slurs were reported. "If you can't treat someone with dignity and respect, get out," he said. At one point, he insisted that everyone in the audience take out their phones and record him so his message was clearly heard.

final_summary:
The University of Missouri has launched at least two separate investigations into the potential use of "date rape" drugs at the Delta Upsilon (DU) fraternity. According to a letter from the Title IX Office, dated September 13, the office received multiple complaints from students and social media posts alleging that active members of the fraternity provided new recruits with three pills to incapacitate women before engaging in sexual activity. The letter stated that if the allegations were found to be true, an immediate investigation would commence. 

These allegations stem from a series of incidents that occurred at DU parties on August 27 and September 10. On those dates, a 20-year-old woman was taken to the hospital due to an alcohol overdose, a male student was injured in a fight, and an 18-year-old female student fell and hit her head after consuming excessive amounts of alcohol. Prior to these incidents, the fraternity had already received two warnings from the university for underage drinking and failing to cooperate with the University Police Department during a theft investigation.

A police report detailing a medical call on September 16 further supports the allegations. An 18-year-old student was found acting strangely in Johnston Hall, wearing no pants and with feces smeared on the walls. She claimed to have consumed alcohol at various locations, including DU, but did not recall making her drinks at the fraternity. Although she did not remember having sex, she did not identify anyone responsible for drugging her.

University officials have acknowledged the ongoing investigations, with MU spokesman Christian Basi stating that the fraternity's status regarding the allegations cannot be commented upon. Major Brian Weimer confirmed that the investigation into this incident remains open. In response to the allegations, the national director of communications for DU, Ashley Martin, emphasized the fraternity's commitment to investigating the claims and ensuring a safe campus environment. However, the fraternity remains under temporary suspension pending the outcome of the investigation.

If found guilty of violating student conduct codes, individual students may face expulsion, while the fraternity as a whole could face withdrawal of recognition, effectively stripping it of access to university resources and participation in events like Homecoming. This decision would allow the fraternity to appeal or reapply for recognition based on the specific punishment outlined by the Office of Student Conduct.

reference: – A University of Missouri fraternity with a track record of problem behavior has been accused of telling new members to drug women and sexually assault them as part of their initiation, the St. Louis Post-Dispatch reports. According to the Columbia Daily Tribune, multiple people informed the school in August that members of Delta Upsilon had given new members three pills each and told them to drug woman and have sex with them as the final step of initiation. Justin Kirk, executive director of Delta Upsilon, calls the allegations "deeply concerning" but notes there have been no claims of sexual assault brought against the frat. But in September, an 18-year-old girl was hospitalized after being possibly drugged, ABC 17 reports. She was found naked from the waist down and smearing feces on a wall after drinking at Delta Upsilon. She didn't see who was making her drinks and didn't remember if she had sex that night. This is just one in a string of problems at Delta Upsilon—which was temporarily suspended last month by both its national organization and the University of Missouri—including underage drinking, physical abuse, at least one assault, and a "racial incident." In September, frat members reportedly yelled racist and sexist slurs at black female students. Members also reportedly forced drunk pledges to fight each other. In one incident of underage drinking last year at Delta Upsilon, a woman had to be hospitalized. In 2014, the frat removed 90 of its 132 members and hired a live-in graduate student as well as an off-duty police officer to keep an eye on things. Multiple school investigations into Delta Upsilon are ongoing.

final_summary:
A recent study published in the Journal of Gerontology: Social Sciences has shed light on the often-debated topic of parental favoritism, revealing that being the favored child may not be as beneficial as previously thought. Researchers from Purdue University, led by Professor Jill Suitor, analyzed data from 725 adult children across 309 families, where mothers were between the ages of 65 and 75 when the study began in 2001. 

The study aimed to explore how adult children perceive favoritism and disfavoritism from their mothers, assessing four key dimensions: emotional closeness, conflict, pride, and disappointment. The researchers found that adult children who felt emotionally closer to their mothers than their siblings were more likely to experience depressive symptoms. Conversely, those who believed they were the sibling their mother was most disappointed in also reported higher levels of depression.

Interestingly, the study revealed that the negative effects of perceived favoritism were not limited to just the favored child. Those who experienced conflict with their mothers or felt they'd disappointed them also struggled with depression. Notably, black adult children were disproportionately affected by feelings of disappointment from their mothers, suggesting that cultural factors may play a role in shaping these dynamics.

The study's findings challenge the conventional wisdom that being the favored child provides a sense of security and protection. Instead, the researchers propose that the constant comparisons and judgments made by siblings can lead to increased stress and anxiety. Furthermore, the pressure to live up to their mother's expectations can weigh heavily on favored children, leading to feelings of responsibility and guilt.

In contrast to previous studies, which suggested that mothers tend to favor certain children over others, this study found no significant differences in the experiences of sons and daughters. The researchers also noted that the effects of perceived favoritism were consistent across different racial groups, except for the disproportionate impact on black adult children.

The study's authors plan to build upon these findings by investigating the effects of perceived favoritism from fathers and exploring ways to predict favoritism between mothers and adult children. These insights could provide valuable guidance for families seeking to navigate complex relationships and promote healthy communication.

Ultimately, the study highlights the importance of recognizing that perceived favoritism can have far-reaching consequences for adult children's mental health and well-being. By acknowledging these dynamics, families can work towards creating a more supportive and equitable environment, where all members feel valued and respected.

reference: – Always hoped you're mom's favorite? A new study out of Purdue University finds that the favorite child is actually more prone to be depressed as an adult, researchers report in the Journal of Gerontology: Social Sciences. Looking at 725 adult children from 309 families in the Within-Family Differences Study—a longitudinal project documenting various aspects of relationships between parents and their adult children—they say that depressive symptoms were most common in the adult children who claimed to be emotionally closer to their mother than their siblings, reports Medical News Today. That also held true for "the children with whom their mothers had the greatest conflict, and in whom the mothers were most disappointed," write the authors. Interestingly, the study found no difference between male and female children, but it did find a racial disparity, notes Medical Daily. Adult black children felt their mother's disappointment more acutely than their white counterparts. Researchers hypothesize that sibling rivalry may be behind the depression, meaning more attention or support from mom doesn't necessarily beat out the negative attention from jealous siblings. Or that the favorites are more often asked to help the aging mother, which is its own source of stress. (The adult children in the study had mothers who were 65 to 75 when interviewed.) And yet a third possibility is posed by Bustle: A mother's attention may naturally drift toward the weakest or most sensitive child, thus her favoritism doesn't necessarily cause depression. Researchers plan to look at whether similar results play out with fathers, and whether it's possible to predict favoritism. (One study finds that young kids in religious households are less willing to share stickers.)

final_summary:
Juan Jose Vidrio Bibriesca, a 43-year-old man, found himself in a precarious situation during a court hearing in Eagle County, Colorado. He was already facing a felony drug charge and had been brought before Judge Katharine Sullivan for a bond violation. In an attempt to show respect, Bibriesca removed his hat, holding it behind his back in both hands. However, as he did so, a small square of folded paper fell from his hat and landed on the floor behind him.

A local law enforcement officer, seated in the gallery, witnessed the incident and initially believed the item to be dropped by one of the other two men at the podium. Nevertheless, another officer suggested reviewing the courtroom's video surveillance footage, invoking the "video review" rule similar to the NFL's instant replay system. Upon reviewing the tape, the officers discovered that the folded paper contained cocaine, which had apparently fallen from Bibriesca's hat.

As a result of this new evidence, Bibriesca was subsequently charged with additional felonies: narcotics possession and another bond violation. Furthermore, being in the country illegally, he will likely face further scrutiny from Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Following the discovery, Bibriesca was taken into custody and transported to the Eagle County Jail. His booking documents do not indicate whether he has retained an attorney.
