reference: – Authorities say a man who was in a Colorado court for violating his bond on a drug charge is in even more trouble after a wad of cocaine fell from his hat while he was in front of the judge. The Vail Daily reported Wednesday that 43-year-old Juan Jose Vidrio Bibriesca was standing next to two other defendants at an Eagle County District Court podium when he took his hat off and a square of folded paper fell out. A police officer watched the paper filled with cocaine fall to the floor, reports the AP, and after reviewing surveillance footage, authorities determined it fell from Bibriesca's hat. Bibriesca was then walked to the county jail, and now faces new charges of narcotics possession and another bond violation. Booking documents don't indicate if he has hired an attorney. The Vail Daily notes that Bibriesca is in the country illegally, meaning his legal woes aren't over.

final_summary:
For the first time in 146 years, the National Weather Service documented no snow on the ground in Chicago in January and February, marking a record that has left some residents optimistic while others worry about the implications of climate change. The measurement, taken at 6 a.m. at O'Hare International Airport, did not account for small amounts of snow that fell later in the day and melted, such as the 0.1 inch recorded on January 30 and a trace of snow on February 25. 

Despite the record-breaking warmth, severe storms swept through the state on Tuesday, bringing large hail, strong winds, localized flooding, and tornadoes, resulting in one fatality in Ottawa. Meteorologists predict further stormy weather, including possible snow, for the remainder of the week. 

Chicagoan Patricia Orta, 56, expressed delight at the unseasonably warm weather, saying, "I hope it happens more often. While it lasts, this is great!" However, experts caution that the absence of snow does not necessarily indicate a shift away from climate change. WGN-TV meteorologist Tom Skilling attributed the anomaly to a changing climate, stating, "This is occurring against a backdrop of a changing climate... I think the door is open to additional unusual weather events as we go forward."

State Climatologist Jim Angel noted that January and February are typically the snowiest months, but the prolonged warmth led to a lack of snow accumulation due to warm air and ground temperatures. This has significant consequences for trees, insects, and animals, as well as fruit growers, who face the risk of frost damage to budding plants. Angel warned that the premature growth of trees and shrubs makes them vulnerable to late-season frosts, citing instances in 2012 and 2007 when an April frost destroyed blooming trees, resulting in substantial losses for orchards.

Meteorologist Amy Seeley explained that the record-low snowfall is not solely indicative of climate change, as natural variations in weather patterns contribute to anomalies. Skilling agreed, stating that the absence of snow does not mean Chicago is out of the woods for snow, predicting that it is still possible to experience snow in March. 

Historical context reveals that the least amount of snow in a Chicago January was in 1928, with 0.2 inches, while this January saw 0.5 inches. February 2023 tied with 1987 and 1998 for the least amount of snow, with only a trace of snow recorded. Experts initially predicted a harsh winter, but Skilling and others anticipated a milder season due to the warm summer and autumn of 2016.

reference: – The arrival of March brings a weird milestone to Chicago this year: The city recorded no snow on the ground in January and February for the first time in at least 146 years, reports the Weather Channel. In fact, the last snowfall of more than an inch occurred on Dec. 17, days ahead of the official start of winter, which means the city has been virtually snow-free all season. The Chicago Tribune notes that flurries were seen on a few days, but not enough to register when the official morning tally was taken at O'Hare International Airport. It's possible the streak goes back longer, but the 146-year mark reflects the start of National Weather Service data. Forecasts called for possible snow this week. Winter wraps up on March 20.

final_summary:
Martin Tripp, a former process technician at Tesla's Gigafactory in Nevada, has filed a counterclaim against the company, alleging that it defamed him after accusing him of hacking and sabotage. Tripp, who earned $28 an hour, claims that Tesla is not living up to its mission to improve the environment and is instead generating large quantities of waste and scrap vehicle parts. He alleges that the company reused battery parts that had been discarded as waste, including punctured battery cells that were reworked with glue and put back on the manufacturing line without proper quality inspections. 

According to Tripp, Tesla discontinued systems to track vehicle parts, making it difficult to determine where the parts were made, when, and by whom. He claims that he tried to bring his concerns to his superiors and to CEO Elon Musk, but nothing came of it. Tripp denies allegations that he hacked into Tesla's manufacturing operating system and transferred secret information to third parties. 

Tesla had initially sued Tripp, accusing him of hacking, theft, and making false claims to the media about punctured battery cells and excess scrap material. However, Tripp's counterclaim alleges that Tesla made several false and defamatory statements about him, including publishing a claim that he had threatened to "shoot up" the Gigafactory. Tripp's lawyers argue that Tesla failed to conduct a reasonable investigation to verify the claim and instead relied on an anonymous caller who claimed to be a friend of Tripp's. 

The counterclaim also addresses a series of tweets by Musk that falsely imply Tripp accepted a bribe from a Business Insider reporter in exchange for providing information. Tripp is seeking at least $1 million and punitive damages for claims of defamation, invasion of privacy, and intentional infliction of emotional distress. 

Tripp's concerns about Tesla's operations predate his departure from the company. He had raised his concerns about high levels of waste and scrap, unnerving, dangerous, and wasteful business practices, and punctured battery modules to his managers and supervisors, but no action was taken. He emailed Musk directly on May 16, 2018, sharing graphical charts to support his claims, but was reassigned to the battery module production line the following day. 

Tesla has denied Tripp's allegations, saying that he is either not telling the truth or has no idea what he is talking about. The company has also maintained that it conducted a thorough investigation into the alleged threat and found no credible evidence to support it. However, Tripp's lawyers argue that Tesla's actions constitute at least negligence and that the company should be held accountable for its behavior.

reference: – The technician accused of stealing data from Tesla's battery factory in Nevada is countersuing his former employer and CEO Elon Musk for defamation. "Tesla has made several false and defamatory statements about [Martin] Tripp in an effort to discredit him," a lawyer says. The Guardian specifically mentions Musk claiming on June 20 that Tesla "received a call at the Gigafactory that he was going to come back and shoot people." Per CNBC, a Tesla rep added "a friend of Mr. Tripp" claimed he would "shoot the place up." Tripp—who's painted himself as a whistleblower concerned by Tesla's "dangerous" practices, including alleged use of punctured battery cells repaired with glue, per Business Insider—described the claim as "insane." A day later, the local sheriff's department announced "there was no credible threat." That's because there was no phone call, and if there had been, Tesla acted with "at least negligence" in publicizing the information before investigating, reads Tripp's lawsuit, filed Tuesday in Nevada, per Ars Technica. It further notes Tesla seemed to know Tripp's location during the law enforcement investigation and "provided false information" to authorities when claiming it could "verify" the 40-year-old was "armed." "We're … interested in getting to the bottom of how the story was concocted," a lawyer for Tripp tells the Guardian. Tripp, who denies Tesla's claim that he wrote software used to lift data from factory computers (he says he can't even code), is seeking at least $1 million plus punitive damages for alleged defamation, invasion of privacy, and intentional infliction of emotional distress. His lawsuit notes he's received "numerous threats" to his safety.

final_summary:
In a breakthrough that has brought closure to a decades-long mystery, two men, Larry Don Patterson and William Lloyd Harbour, aged 65, have been arrested in connection with the shotgun slayings of two young girls, Doris Karen Derryberry, 13, and Valerie Janice Lane, 12, in California over 40 years ago. The girls were reported missing on November 12, 1973, after failing to return from a shopping trip to a nearby mall, and their bodies were discovered hours later alongside a dirt road in a wooded area near Marysville, north of Sacramento. 

The case remained unsolved despite a large-scale investigation that involved over 60 interviews and numerous leads, but ultimately went cold in 1976 due to a lack of concrete evidence. However, in 2014, an investigator revisited the case and sent DNA samples collected from the crime scene to the California Department of Justice Forensics Lab for re-examination using advanced technology. The results revealed a match between the DNA evidence and the genetic profiles of Patterson and Harbour, who were both living in Olivehurst, California at the time of the murders.

Patterson and Harbour, who are cousins, were 22 years old in 1973 and had since served prison sentences for various crimes. Patterson was arrested in 1976 for raping two women and again in 2006 for failing to register as a sex offender, while Harbour had felony drug convictions. Patterson was arrested in Oklahoma, where he was being held as an unregistered sex offender awaiting extradition to California, while Harbour was apprehended during a traffic stop in Linda, California.

The victims' families, many of whom are now elderly, have endured decades of suffering and grief without knowing who was responsible for the brutal murder of their loved ones. The families have requested privacy as they process the news of the arrests, and authorities have acknowledged that the revelation may lead to frustration, anger, and other emotions within the community. The case, which shocked the nation at the time, is a reminder of the importance of advances in forensic technology in solving cold cases and bringing justice to victims and their families.

reference: – Authorities have finally made arrests in the death of two girls in California more than 40 years later. Police say 65-year-old cousins Larry Patterson and William Harbour murdered Doris Derryberry, 13, and Valerie Lane, 12, after they vanished from a shopping mall in 1973. The girls were found dead, each shot by a shotgun, beside a dirt road in Marysville hours after they were reported missing, per the AP. Doris had been sexually assaulted and semen was collected from her body. However, it was only sent for testing in March 2014 by an investigator who "had a bit of free time and really looked very closely at this case," Sheriff Steve Durfor tells the AP. The semen matched DNA samples taken from Patterson and Harbour during previous arrests, police say. Patterson—convicted of rape in 1976 and wanted for failing to register as a sex offender, per Reuters—was arrested Tuesday in Oklahoma. Harbour, who has felony drug convictions, was also arrested Tuesday in the girls' hometown of Olivehurst, Calif. The men were 22 at the time of the murders. After "decades of suffering and grief," Durfor says the girls' families can finally have some closure. They "have waited ... for 43 years for an answer as to what happened to their kids," adds a Yuba County DA. (This missing woman could be buried on the Cal Poly campus.)

final_summary:
A groundbreaking study led by researchers at the University of Southern California's Leonard Davis School of Gerontology has shed light on the long-standing phenomenon where women tend to live longer than men across the globe. The study, which analyzed data from 13 developed nations spanning three centuries, revealed that significant disparities in life expectancy between the sexes emerged as recently as the late 19th century. This divergence in mortality rates became apparent around 1870, with women experiencing a 70% faster decline in death rates compared to men, particularly in the 50-70 age range.

The researchers attributed the primary cause of excess deaths among adult men to cardiovascular disease, despite controlling for smoking-related illnesses. Interestingly, smoking accounted for only 30% of the difference in mortality between the sexes after 1890. The study's findings suggest that men and women may face distinct heart disease risks due to inherent biological vulnerabilities and protective factors at different stages of life.

University of Southern California Professor Eileen Crimmins noted that the uneven impact of cardiovascular illness-related deaths on men raises questions about the role of biology and lifestyle factors in shaping sex-specific mortality patterns. To further explore this topic, the researchers propose examining diet and exercise habits, genetic variations, and the relationship between brain health and aging.

In related research, scientists at Stanford University have discovered that adult stem cells exhibit sex-specific regulation and response to sex hormones, which may influence lifespan. Estrogen, in particular, has been shown to enhance the regenerative capacity of stem cells in female mice, while its supplementation has been linked to increased lifespan in males. Additionally, studies involving mice and humans have revealed that certain genetic mutations can confer longevity advantages to one sex but not the other.

These findings highlight the significance of considering sex as a critical factor in understanding aging and lifespan. Researchers believe that sex may play a crucial role not only in determining overall lifespan but also in defining the proportion of a person's lifespan spent in good health, known as "healthspan." The study's authors emphasize the need for future research to control for sex in studies examining age-related variables to better comprehend the complex interplay between sex, aging, and lifespan.

reference: – There's a reason we don't typically call Earth's oldest person the oldest woman in the world—the oldest person is pretty much always a woman. In fact, just two of the world's 53 living supercentenarians (people 110 and older) are men, reports IFL Science. But why is the longevity gap so stark, and how long has it been so? An international team of researchers is investigating the lifespans of men and women born between 1800 and 1935 in 13 developed nations in search of answers, reports PhysOrg, and they've found that women's superior longevity only emerged recently. Before 1840, death rates were very similar between men and women. But since those rates began to plummet in 1880 thanks in large part to improved diets, vaccinations, and better health care, female death rates have dropped 70% faster than male ones, and heart disease seems to be holding men back the most, the researchers report in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. Men may be more biologically susceptible to cardiovascular health problems, and their different weight distribution could play a role here, reports Live Science. The researchers plan to further explore the differences between male and female biology, genetics, and lifestyles to look for answers. (The oldest man in the US says his trick has been to enjoy a can of beer a day.)

final_summary:
President Donald Trump's attorneys are advising him against participating in a wide-ranging interview with special counsel Robert Mueller due to concerns that he may incriminate himself through false statements and potentially face charges of lying to investigators. This stance contradicts Trump's previous public declarations that he is eager to speak with Mueller as part of the investigation into potential ties between his associates and Russia's election interference, as well as allegations of obstruction of justice. 

According to multiple sources, Trump's lawyers believe that Mueller's team has not met the necessary threshold to justify a presidential interview, which has led to discussions about refusing the request altogether. If Trump declines to participate in the interview, Mueller's team may opt to subpoena him to testify before a grand jury, prompting a lengthy and contentious court battle that could ultimately reach the Supreme Court. 

This development raises significant implications for both the ongoing investigation and the upcoming midterm elections. A refusal to cooperate with Mueller's inquiry would likely lead to accusations that Trump is attempting to conceal information, potentially damaging his reputation and that of the Republican Party ahead of the elections. Furthermore, a prolonged court battle could extend the investigation, casting a shadow over Trump's re-election campaign if he decides to seek another term.

reference: – President Trump has claimed he would "love to" be interviewed under oath by special counsel Robert Mueller and that he's "looking forward" to it. His lawyers, however, are worried that Trump will make false statements, potentially leading to a charge of lying to investigators, and have urged him to turn down any interview request, the New York Times reports, citing "four people briefed on the matter." Rebuffing the interview might lead to a subpoena and a legal battle that could go all the way to the Supreme Court, but lawyers including John Dowd, who's representing Trump in the investigation, believe Mueller won't be willing to subpoena Trump and force him into a showdown, the insiders say. White House lawyer Ty Cobb supports full cooperation with Mueller's investigation, but he's outnumbered by opponents, the Times' sources say. Newt Gingrich is among those close to Trump who have publicly said that he should reject any request to be questioned. "The idea of putting Trump in a room with five or six hardened, very clever lawyers, all of whom are trying to trick him and trap him, would be a very, very bad idea," Gingrich said on Fox and Friends recently. CNN reports that Trump's team is arguing that Mueller hasn't reached the "high threshold" of evidence he would need to have Trump interviewed. (Trump has to decide whether to release a Democrat-authored memo on the Russia investigation.)

final_summary:
Apple's unveiling of its latest touchscreen device, dubbed the iPad, sparked widespread reaction and ridicule, particularly among women who felt the name overlooked their needs and preferences. The name choice was met with skepticism from various quarters, including Business Insider, which deemed it "terrible." Some speculated that Apple should have foreseen the potential for menstrual-related mockery, referencing a 2007 MADtv parody.

The blogosphere engaged enthusiastically in the name debate, with some questioning how the iPad would hold up to being exposed to liquids, such as blue water. Social media platforms like Twitter also saw a surge in iPad-related activity, with the hashtag #iTampon becoming a trending topic. Many users expressed surprise at the lack of female representation on Apple's panel during the San Francisco announcement, leading some to speculate that the name "iPad" would appeal primarily to men.

Despite the initial backlash, Apple described the iPad as a "magical and revolutionary device" on its website, without addressing the concerns raised by critics. The company declined to comment on the matter when contacted. The situation drew comparisons to Nintendo's Wii console, which faced similar ridicule before going on to break sales records.

Women pointed out several practical issues with the iPad, including its inability to be used while swimming, which they considered a significant drawback. Online commentators questioned whether the device would come equipped with features catering to women's needs, such as waterproofing or feminine hygiene considerations. Overall, the iPad's naming controversy highlighted the importance of considering diverse perspectives and user needs in product development.

Business Insider immediately declared the name “terrible,” and the Internet soon filled with snark. “How will it stand up to other tablets if I pour a test tube full of blue water on it?” asked one blogger. “Are there NO women in the Marketing or Biz Dev department of Mac?” wondered one user, and indeed, no women were present at the tablet’s unveiling, the Washington Post notes. MadTV even coined the term iPad in a 2007 parody about—you guessed it—an Apple brand maxi pad (see story). Then again, people bought something called the “Wii” in droves, so maybe all bets are off.

final_summary:
A severe sewer main break occurred on March 18, 2014, in the Valley Creek Trunk Sewer Force Main near the intersection of Routes 252 and 23 in Upper Merion Township, Chester County, Pennsylvania. The incident happened around 10:30 a.m. and was reported to have released approximately 5 million gallons of raw sewage into the Valley Creek, which flows into the Schuylkill River. 

The affected area includes Valley Forge National Historical Park, where multiple roads were closed due to the spill. These closures included Route 23 eastbound from Davis Road to Route 252, Route 23 westbound from North Gulph Road to Route 252, Route 252 northbound from the Pennsylvania Turnpike, North and South Outer Line Drives, Gulph Road from Thomas Road to Route 23, and Yellow Springs Road at the Covered Bridge.

According to officials, the 30-inch main is shared by five local municipalities: Tredyffrin Township, Easttown Township, East Whiteland Township, Malvern Borough, and Willistown Township. Repair efforts involved a multi-agency response including contractors and environmental and engineering consultants. Crews worked throughout the night to remove the damaged pipe and install a new one, which was expected to arrive at the scene around 7 a.m. on March 19.

While officials stated that the sewage leak does not pose a direct threat to human health, it could potentially harm local wildlife. The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection was contacted regarding concerns over the potential impact on the surrounding area and downstream communities, but no response was received. The cause of the break remains undetermined, although another similar incident had occurred six weeks prior.

reference: – Perhaps today's most disgusting news: A sewer main at Valley Forge National Historic Park broke yesterday, sending 5,000 gallons of sewage per minute into the creek at the Pennsylvania park. Crews worked overnight to repair the 30-inch main, which had also broken six weeks ago, NBC Philadelphia reports. A replacement pipe was expected to arrive this morning. At least 5 million gallons of raw sewage have leaked into Valley Creek, WPVI reports. "All the water goes down into the Schuylkill River and then it goes into Philadelphia in the Delaware River. People get their drinking water out of this and there's the health problem," says one state conservation officer; but NBC Philadelphia says officials insist humans are at no risk from the leak. Wildlife, however, could be. All roads running through the park are currently closed, Philly.com reports; they are expected to remain closed for a few days as crews continue to work in the area.

final_summary:
On June 15, during a routine inspection of mail addressed to Michael Gill, an inmate at the Somerset County Jail, administrative personnel detected unusual items hidden within children's drawings. Upon further investigation, a K-9 unit, named Apex, was brought in and confirmed the presence of Suboxone, a prescription medication that can produce a high when used alone and is typically used to aid heroin addiction recovery, concealed within the drawings. 

Following this discovery, two more instances of suspicious mail were intercepted on June 20 and June 28, both containing children's drawings with suspected Suboxone. In each case, the K-9 unit again detected the presence of the illicit substance.

The Somerset County Sheriff's Office launched an investigation, which led to the identification of Casey Giles, a 37-year-old resident of Bridgewater, as the individual responsible for attempting to smuggle the Suboxone into the jail. Giles was subsequently charged with third-degree possession with intent to distribute a controlled dangerous substance and was remanded to the jail in lieu of $20,000 bail, either in cash or bond.

Gill, Giles' boyfriend and the recipient of the suspicious mail, was charged with attempting to possess a controlled dangerous substance, a third-degree offense, with a bail set at $10,000, also in cash or bond. According to Sheriff Frank J. Provenzano, the Somerset County Sheriff's Office takes seriously the issue of contraband smuggling into the jail and will continue to investigate and prosecute such cases to maintain a safe and secure environment for all individuals involved.

Jail officials regularly inspect incoming mail to ensure the safety of the facility, its personnel, and inmates. However, due to state regulations, strip searches are not permitted at the jail, as many inmates have not yet been convicted and are awaiting trial.

reference: – A woman is accused of using children's drawings to smuggle prescription drugs to her boyfriend in a New Jersey jail. Somerset County Sheriff Frank Provenzano says staff noticed something strange about mail addressed to inmate Michael Gill on June 15, the AP reports. A K-9 unit was called and alerted on what turned out to be the opioid Suboxone concealed in the drawings, authorities say. The prescription drug, which usually comes in patch form or as a film that can be placed under the tongue, is used to help people addicted to heroin or other opiates avoid withdrawal symptoms, but can produce a high of its own when abused. The sheriff says another letter with children's drawings addressed to Gill arrived June 20 and more mail was sent to the jail eight days later. Again, suspected Suboxone was found hidden in the drawings, authorities say. The sheriff tells the Courier News of Bridgewater that to to prevent copycats, he won't disclose how the drug was concealed in the drawings. Authorities charged 37-year-old Casey Giles, of Bridgewater, with possession with intent to distribute a controlled dangerous substance. She was jailed on $20,000 bail. Gill was charged with attempting to possess a controlled dangerous substance, and his bail was set at $10,000.

final_summary:
On March 18, Captain Håvard Melvaer, 40, of the Norwegian multipurpose supply ship Edda Fjord, stumbled upon a remarkable coincidence while navigating through the waters between Port Fourchon, Louisiana, and Haugesund, Norway. As he stepped out of his office for some fresh air, his wife suggested he take a break, prompting him to glance out the door and spot a 19-foot SeaCraft boat approximately 30-40 feet off the port side. This unexpected sighting occurred despite the presence of a 16-person crew, including an officer on the bridge using binoculars, who was focused on the opposite side.

Captain Melvaer described the encounter as a "stroke of luck," emphasizing that there was no skill involved in the discovery. He noted that the Edda Fjord was passing directly above the boat at the time, making it a matter of being in the right place at the right time. The captain expressed amazement at the sheer improbability of the event, likening it to a miracle.

The boat in question belonged to two missing teenagers, Austin Stephanos and Perry Cohen, who departed from Jupiter Inlet, Florida, last July and were never seen again. Initially, the US Coast Guard located the boat 67 miles off the coast of Daytona Beach, but a retrieval attempt failed due to a malfunctioning data marker buoy. The boat was eventually rediscovered by Captain Melvaer's crew, who carefully secured it to prevent further damage.

After notifying the Coast Guard, Captain Melvaer learned that the boat was connected to the missing teenagers. He and his crew took precautions to safeguard the vessel, ensuring it remained secure during transit. The boat is currently stored in a shipping container and is scheduled to arrive at Port Everglades on May 16. Among the recovered items are Austin's iPhone, two fishing rods, and two small tackle boxes, which may hold crucial information regarding the boys' disappearance.

Captain Melvaer expressed empathy for the families of the missing teenagers, having himself experienced the importance of protecting valuable personal belongings. He acknowledged that the discovery brought a sense of responsibility to handle the situation delicately, respecting the ongoing investigation led by the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission. While declining to provide specific details about the boat or its contents, Captain Melvaer hopes that the rediscovery will lead to positive outcomes for the families affected.

On March 18, Captain Håvard Melvaer, 40, of the Norwegian multipurpose supply ship Edda Fjord, stumbled upon a remarkable coincidence while navigating through the waters between Port Fourchon, Louisiana, and Haugesund, Norway. As he stepped out of his office for some fresh air, his wife suggested he take a break, prompting him to glance out the door and spot a 19-foot SeaCraft boat approximately 30-40 feet off the port side. This unexpected sighting occurred despite the presence of a 16-person crew, including an officer on the bridge using binoculars, who was focused on the opposite side.

Captain Melvaer described the encounter as a "stroke of luck," emphasizing that there was no skill involved in the discovery. He noted that the Edda Fjord was passing directly above the boat at the time, making it a matter of being in the right place at the right time. The captain expressed amazement at the sheer improbability of the event, likening it to a miracle.

The boat in question belonged to two missing teenagers, Austin Stephanos and Perry Cohen, who departed from Jupiter Inlet, Florida, last July and were never seen again. Initially, the US Coast Guard located the boat 67 miles off the coast of Daytona Beach, but a retrieval attempt failed due to a malfunctioning data marker buoy. After notifying the Coast Guard, Captain Melvaer learned that the boat was connected to the missing teenagers. He and his crew took precautions to safeguard the vessel, ensuring it remained secure during transit. The boat is currently stored in a shipping container and is scheduled to arrive at Port Everglades on May 16. He acknowledged that the discovery brought a sense of responsibility to handle the situation delicately, respecting the ongoing investigation led by the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission. reference: – A Norwegian ship captain says it was an incredible "stroke of luck" that he discovered the boat of two teenagers lost at sea last July, the AP reports. Havard Melvaer was in his office aboard the huge Edda Fjord supply ship last month when he stepped outside for fresh air and spotted the 19-foot boat, the Palm Beach Post reports. Nobody else on the 16-person crew spotted the boat, and Melvaer says he would have missed it if he hadn't stepped out at that exact moment. "You kind of think a little bit and try to understand the concept of how big it really is," he said. "It's amazing. We kind of felt like it wanted to be found." The captain says that after the boat was hoisted aboard, he Googled "missing fisherman" and was stunned to discover that it belonged to Austin Stephanos and Perry Cohen, both 14, who left Jupiter Inlet, Fla., in it and never returned. Melvaer tells the Post that he and his crew treated the boat, which is being shipped to Florida, as very precious cargo. "I'm a parent myself, so I can identify with the situation," he says. "I've been boating since I was a kid as well." Items found inside the boat, including Austin's iPhone, have been returned to the US. Austin's father, Blu Stephanos, said in a statement that he received a heartfelt message from the captain, explaining that he's the father of three boys and was deeply affected by the story of Austin and Perry. "He went on to say that, since recovering the boat, his entire crew had thought a lot about the boys and said, 'I think they will follow us in our hearts and minds for the rest of our lives,'" Stephanos said.

final_summary:
Colgate Total toothpaste has been the go-to choice for maintaining healthy teeth and gums for over two decades, earning the recommendation of dentists worldwide due to its unique formulation featuring 0.3% triclosan, an antibacterial agent that combats plaque-causing germs responsible for common oral health issues. Backed by more than 90 clinical studies involving over 20,000 individuals, Colgate Total's efficacy and safety have been extensively researched and validated by regulatory bodies globally.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention reports that nearly half of American adults aged 30 and above suffer from severe periodontal disease, while over 75% experience gingivitis, an early stage of gum disease that can progress to periodontitis, leading to tooth and bone loss if left untreated. Colgate Total stands out as the only toothpaste providing both antibacterial protection and direct anti-inflammatory action, crucial factors in maintaining optimal oral health and its connection to overall well-being.

A comprehensive review by the Cochrane Oral Health Group analyzed 30 studies spanning 1990 to 2012, involving 14,835 participants, and demonstrated the numerous benefits of using a fluoride toothpaste containing triclosan/copolymer, including a 22% reduction in plaque and gingivitis, 41% decrease in plaque severity, and 48% reduction in gum bleeding compared to traditional fluoride toothpaste.

However, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has expressed concerns regarding the long-term safety and effectiveness of triclosan, particularly in antibacterial soaps and body washes. Despite initial claims suggesting triclosan offered enhanced protection against illnesses and infections, the FDA found insufficient evidence supporting its benefits over plain soap and water. As a result, the agency issued a final rule prohibiting the sale of consumer antibacterial soaps and body washes containing triclosan and 18 other active ingredients unless manufacturers provide new safety and effectiveness data within a year.

Triclosan has been linked to hormone disruption in animal studies, raising concerns about its potential impact on human health. Furthermore, research suggests it may contribute to antibiotic resistance, compromising the effectiveness of medical treatments. The FDA and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) are collaborating to assess the effects of triclosan from different perspectives, with the EPA reevaluating its use as a pesticide every 15 years.

In light of these developments, the FDA advises consumers to revert to using plain soap and water for hand hygiene, citing it as one of the most effective methods for preventing the spread of infections and illnesses. As manufacturers adjust their product lines to comply with the new regulations, consumers can expect changes in the availability of antibacterial soaps and body washes containing triclosan and other restricted ingredients.

reference: – The FDA last week banned the use of a chemical from soap, but not from the nation’s top-selling toothpaste. Why? The New York Times reports it was because Colgate-Palmolive convinced the FDA that the benefits of triclosan are greater than the risks. Toothpaste containing the chemical "demonstrated to be effective at reducing plaque and gingivitis," an FDA spokeswoman said. The decision came after the agency announced that there was not enough evidence to prove that antibacterial soap containing triclosan was any better "at preventing illness than washing with plain soap and water." Worse, long-term use of the soap, the agency said in a statement, "has raised the question of potential negative effects on your health." Scientists have long raised concerns that prolonged exposure to germ-fighters could result in strains of resistant bacteria. The only toothpaste in the US that contains triclosan Colgate Total, the Times reports. Colgate-Palmolive insists that rigorous testing has shown the toothpaste is safe. "The full weight of scientific evidence amassed over 25 years continues to support the safety and efficacy of Colgate Total," spokesman Mike DiPiazza told the paper. But critics questioned the FDA’s logic. "We put soap on our hands, and a small amount gets into our body," said Rolf Halden, an expert at Arizona State University who has studied triclosan. He said that with exposure through the gums "chemicals get rapidly absorbed into the bloodstream." (The feds have also targeted fluoride.)

final_summary:
A 95-year-old New York City man, identified as Jakiw Palij, believed to be a former guard at the Trawniki Labor Camp in Nazi-occupied Poland, has been deported to Germany after being arrested and detained by US immigration authorities. According to the White House, Palij served as an armed guard at the camp where approximately 6,000 Jewish men, women, and children were brutally killed on November 3, 1943, in one of the largest massacres of the Holocaust. 

Palij, born in what is now Ukraine, immigrated to the United States in 1949 and became a naturalized citizen eight years later, concealing his past involvement in the Nazi regime. However, in 2001, he revealed to Department of Justice officials that he had undergone training at the Nazi SS Training Camp in Trawniki in 1943. 

As a guard, Palij allegedly prevented Jewish prisoners from escaping, contributing significantly to the tragic outcome of the massacre. Following this revelation, a federal judge revoked his US citizenship in 2003, and a deportation order was issued in 2004. Despite efforts by the US government to deport him, no European country initially accepted him due to concerns over his age, health, and potential prosecution.

However, after negotiations between the US and German governments, led by Chancellor Angela Merkel's administration, Germany agreed to take Palij in. He was subsequently transferred to a care facility in the town of Ahlen, located near Düsseldorf. 

US Ambassador to Germany, Richard Grenell, attributed the successful deportation to a direct request from US President Donald Trump, emphasizing the importance of holding individuals accountable for their actions during the Holocaust. Germany's decision to accept Palij comes amidst criticism of the country's historical record on prosecuting Nazi war criminals, with many high-ranking officials and SS members evading justice while lower-ranking perpetrators faced trials decades later.

Foreign Minister Heiko Maas acknowledged Germany's responsibility in addressing its moral obligations, stating, "Germany, in whose name the worst injustice was perpetrated under the Nazis, is confronting its moral obligations." This move follows a long history of inconsistent prosecution, with only 6,656 convictions out of over 36,000 investigations into 170,000 suspects between 1945 and 2005.

The investigation into Palij's case remains uncertain, with Germany's Central Office for the Investigation of Nazi Crimes facing challenges in proving his involvement in specific crimes beyond his training at the Trawniki camp. As of now, there are no ongoing proceedings or arrest warrants against Palij. His deportation marks another step towards accountability for those involved in the atrocities committed during the Holocaust, with the US having previously denaturalized 16 former SS members who served at Trawniki.

reference: – Now that former Nazi prison guard Jakiw Palij is out of the US, the New York Times explores how he got in, and it wasn't through his efforts alone. As Nazi records of his SS membership weren't uncovered until the 1990s, Palij successfully applied for a visa under the Displaced Persons Act at the American consular office in Schweinfurt, Germany, in June 1949 alongside Jaroslaw Bilaniuk, whose SS identification number turned out to be only one digit away from Palij's, says Peter Black, a former Justice Department historian who worked on Nazi deportation cases. Both claimed to have worked in Germany during the war—Palij as a factory worker, Bilaniuk as a farmhand. Mykola Wasylyk, who also served with the men at the Trawniki labor camp and who'd already arrived in the US after securing a visa, vouched for both. The records suggest Palij and Bilaniuk weren't coerced into joining the SS as Palij later claimed, but volunteered together in February 1943. Together with Wasylyk, they guarded prisoners who made uniforms and brushes at Trawniki, says Black, a camp known for having executed 6,000 Jews in a single day in November 1943. Upon their arrival in the US, Palij and Bilaniuk settled 100 miles from Wasylyk in Queens, NY. Bilaniuk and Wasylyk would each die in the US, even after officials uncovered their true past. Deported to Germany this week at President Trump's direction, per Reuters, Palij is unlikely to face charges.

final_summary:
On April 22, 2018, a devastating bus accident occurred in North Hwanghae province, south of Pyongyang, North Korea, resulting in the deaths of 32 Chinese tourists and four North Koreans, with two Chinese individuals sustaining serious injuries. The tourist bus reportedly fell from a bridge during the incident. 

Chinese authorities confirmed that a medical team and diplomats were dispatched to assist in the aftermath of the tragedy. The North Korean government released photos showing leader Kim Jong Un visiting the hospital where the injured Chinese tourists were receiving treatment, as well as meeting with Chinese Ambassador Li Jinjun at the Chinese Embassy in Pyongyang. During these visits, Kim expressed his condolences and sympathy to the affected families, stating that the accident had brought him "bitter sorrow."

The cause of the crash remained unmentioned in reports. However, experts pointed out that North Korean roads are notorious for being poorly maintained, with inadequate lighting, especially at night, which contributes to hazardous driving conditions. This, combined with drivers often traveling at unsafe speeds, makes the roads perilous, particularly during inclement weather.

This tragic event marked a rare instance of North Korea's tightly controlled media acknowledging a negative occurrence, and even more unusually, Kim Jong Un publicly expressing his emotions regarding the incident. The accident highlights the importance of China as North Korea's primary ally and largest trading partner, with Chinese tourists accounting for approximately 80% of foreign visitors to the country, generating significant revenue for Pyongyang.

Kim's swift response to the crisis, including his visit to the hospital and the Chinese Embassy, may be seen as an attempt to maintain a positive image among the Chinese population, given the significance of their relationship. Notably, Kim had previously visited Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing earlier in the year, marking his first known foreign trip since taking office in 2011. China plays a crucial role in supporting North Korea, providing nearly all of its food and fuel aid, solidifying its position as the country's most vital partner.

reference: – A crash that killed 32 tourists from North Korea's chief ally brought "bitter sorrow" to the heart of leader Kim Jong Un, according to Pyongyang's official KCNA news agency. The agency—in a rare admission of bad news—said the Chinese tourists and four North Koreans were killed when a tour bus plunged off a bridge in North Hwanghae province, south of the capital, the BBC reports. Kim "couldn't control his grief at the thought of the bereaved families who lost their blood relatives," KCNA said. The AP reports that China, source of the vast majority of foreign tourists to North Korea, has sent diplomats and a medical team to assist.

final_summary:
Chelsea Manning, a former US Army analyst turned whistleblower, has been living in New York since her release from Fort Leavenworth prison on May 17, after serving seven years for leaking classified documents to WikiLeaks. In a recent interview with Vogue magazine, Manning reflected on her experiences, revealing a complex and tumultuous past. Born in Oklahoma, Manning grew up feeling awkward and unsure of her place in the world, struggling to reconcile her attraction to boys and her sense of self. She eventually enlisted in the Army in 2007, hoping to "figure herself out," but her time in Iraq as an intelligence analyst proved to be a transformative experience that ultimately led to her leaking of classified documents.

Manning's decision to leak the documents, which included the infamous "Collateral Murder" video, was motivated by a desire to expose government wrongdoing and spark worldwide discussion and reform. However, her actions were widely condemned, and she was sentenced to 35 years in prison. Despite this, Manning has maintained that she accepts responsibility for her actions and believes that the proper channels for addressing government wrongdoing do not exist.

Since her release, Manning has become a prominent figure in the LGBTQ+ community, using her platform to advocate for trans rights and visibility. She has spoken publicly about her struggles with her identity and her experiences in prison, where she faced harassment and mistreatment due to her gender identity. Manning has also been open about her mental health struggles, including suicidal thoughts and attempts, and has credited her support system and therapy with helping her cope.

Manning's relationship with her family has been complicated, particularly with her mother, who struggled with addiction and depression. Manning has stated that she regrets not seeking support from her family earlier in her life, particularly regarding her transition. She has also spoken about the importance of unconditional love and acceptance, values that she believes are essential for individuals to thrive.

In addition to her advocacy work, Manning is working on a memoir and has expressed interest in pursuing a career in politics. She has also been involved in various projects, including a documentary about her life and a forthcoming book. Despite facing criticism and backlash from some quarters, Manning remains committed to using her platform to promote positive change and visibility for marginalized communities.

Manning's appearance on the cover of Vogue magazine's September issue, shot by Annie Leibovitz, sparked controversy and debate, with some praising her courage and others criticizing the magazine for featuring a convicted felon. However, Manning's supporters argue that her story serves as a powerful reminder of the importance of empathy, understanding, and acceptance. As Manning navigates her newfound freedom, she remains focused on promoting unity and inclusivity, using her experiences to inspire others and challenge societal norms.

reference: – If you've ever wanted to know the effect that liberation has on a person, Chelsea Manning is glad to show you. ABC News reports that the transgender soldier released from prison earlier this year posted a photo Thursday to her social media accounts that shows her smiling at the beach in a red one-piece swimsuit, with the caption, "Guess this is what freedom looks like." The picture was taken by celebrity photographer Annie Leibovitz, who documented Manning for an article appearing in the September issue of Vogue. In that interview, Manning talks about how she's adjusting to being out of jail, her regrets that she didn't come out as transgender earlier in her life, and her future plans, which may or may not include running for political office. "My goal is to use these next six months to figure out where I want to go," she notes. Online reaction to the Vogue coverage has been mixed, CBS News notes, with some offering support and others blasting the magazine for highlighting both a transgender woman and someone who many believe should still be behind bars. "This ugly whatever should be in prison, forever," one non-fan tweeted, to which Manning replied, "And here I am," accompanied by her trademark slew of positive emojis. The conservative National Review also weighed in, with a column by David French that asks: "If Bradley Manning had stayed Bradley Manning, would he be basking in celebrity, enjoying fawning photo shoots?" The Guardian notes that Manning's appearance in Vogue isn't the magazine's first transgender presence: Models Hari Nef and Andreja Pejic have both appeared in its pages, and model Valentina Sampaio has graced the cover of the publication's French version.

final_summary:
According to the latest data from the U.S. Census Bureau, Florida has surpassed New York to become the nation's third-most populous state, with approximately 19.9 million residents as of July 1, 2014. This milestone marks a significant shift in the country's demographic landscape, with Florida adding an average of 803 new residents daily over the past year, resulting in a total increase of 293,000 people. In stark contrast, New York's population growth was significantly slower, with a mere 51,000 new residents added during the same period.

The Census Bureau's annual report highlights the overall growth of the U.S. population, which increased by three-quarters of a percent to reach 318.9 million residents between July 1, 2013, and July 1, 2014. While 44 states and the District of Columbia experienced population gains, six states – Illinois, West Virginia, Connecticut, New Mexico, Alaska, and Vermont – suffered losses. Notably, Puerto Rico witnessed the largest decline, losing 1.3% of its population, equivalent to 47,000 residents.

The fastest-growing states were predominantly located in the Western and Southern regions, with the notable exception of North Dakota, whose booming energy industry attracted a substantial influx of new residents, leading to a 2.2% increase in population. Other states experiencing rapid growth included Nevada, Texas, Colorado, Arizona, Utah, and Idaho, each growing by more than 1.3%. Among these, Texas recorded the highest population gain, with 451,000 new residents, followed closely by California, Florida, and Georgia, which all saw their populations swell by six figures.

Georgia, in particular, achieved a historic milestone by surpassing the 10-million-resident mark, joining a select group of seven states, including Ohio, Pennsylvania, Illinois, New York, Florida, Texas, and California, boasting populations exceeding one million. The top ten most populous states in the United States, according to the Census Bureau's estimates, are California (38.8 million), Texas (27 million), Florida (19.9 million), Illinois (12.9 million), Pennsylvania (12.7 million), Ohio (11.6 million), Georgia (10.1 million), North Carolina (9.9 million), Michigan (9.9 million), and New York (19.7 million).

reference: – The state known as America's Waiting Room is moving up in the ranks: According to Census data, Florida has officially deposed New York to become the third most populous state in the union, reports the Washington Post. The Sunshine State is now home to 19.9 million residents, compared to 19.7 million in the Empire State, but it's the difference in growth that's notable: Florida took in 293,000 new residents in the year ending July 1, or about 803 a day. New York managed just 51,000. Florida likely pushed past New York sometime in the spring, notes the AP, but it takes the Census to make it official. Interestingly and unsurprisingly, former New Yorkers make up about 10% of new Floridians. Other demographic nuggets, per the AP: Don't look for Florida to mess with Texas, America's No. 2 state with 27 million people, or No. 1 California's 38.8 million souls. The rest of the 10 most populous states: Illinois, Pennsylvania, Ohio, Georgia, North Carolina, and Michigan. Six states lost population: Alaska, Connecticut, Illinois, New Mexico, Vermont, and West Virginia. North Dakota was the fastest-growing state, with a 2.2% jump that stood solidly on the shoulders of its energy boom. It's still the fourth least-populous state.

final_summary:
On August [date], Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump embarked on his first foreign trip as the party's nominee, traveling to Mexico to meet with President Enrique Peña Nieto. The visit was seen as a high-stakes gamble for Trump, who had spent months criticizing Mexico and its people, labeling them as "rapists" and "criminals." Despite widespread outrage and protests in Mexico, Trump arrived in Mexico City and met with Peña Nieto at the presidential residence, Los Pinos. 

During the meeting, Trump and Peña Nieto engaged in a joint press conference, where Trump defended the US's right to build a massive border wall along its southern flank, but surprisingly declined to repeat his frequent promise to force Mexico to pay for it. Instead, he stated that the topic of payment was not discussed during the meeting. Trump also sidestepped his repeated criticism of Mexican immigrants, instead emphasizing the importance of a "secure border" and the need to prevent the flow of people, drugs, and weapons across the border.

Peña Nieto, who has compared Trump to Adolf Hitler, struck a more measured tone, acknowledging the differences between the two leaders but describing their conversation as "open and constructive." He emphasized the importance of respecting the rights of Mexican immigrants living in the US, stating that they are "honest and hardworking people" who "respect the law" and "deserve the respect of everyone."

Trump's visit to Mexico was seen as a crucial test of his ability to navigate complex international diplomacy and to pivot from his divisive rhetoric on the campaign trail. While some Republicans praised Trump's performance, arguing that he had exceeded expectations, others saw the visit as a desperate attempt to salvage his campaign, which has struggled to connect with Hispanic voters.

In the aftermath of the meeting, Trump traveled to Phoenix, Arizona, where he was set to deliver a highly anticipated speech on immigration. His running mate, Mike Pence, emphasized that Trump would make clear that there would be no path to legalization or citizenship for undocumented immigrants, and that they would need to leave the country to obtain legal status or citizenship.

Meanwhile, Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton seized on Trump's visit to Mexico, criticizing his lack of consistency and his failure to demonstrate a clear understanding of the complexities of international diplomacy. She argued that Trump's visit was little more than a publicity stunt, designed to try to make up for a year of insults and insinuations directed at Mexico and its people.

As Trump's speech in Phoenix approached, questions remained about whether he would stick to his original promises on immigration, including the construction of a massive border wall and the deportation of millions of undocumented immigrants. The stakes were high, with Trump facing intense scrutiny from both his opponents and his own party members, who were eager to see him clarify his position on the key issue.

reference: – In a situation that was unthinkable just 24 hours ago, Donald Trump and Mexican President Enrique Peña Nieto gave a joint press conference Wednesday in Mexico City following an hour-long meeting between the two, reports the Los Angeles Times, which calls Trump's jaunt to Mexico both "hastily arranged" and "stunning." "We did discuss the wall," the AP quotes Trump as saying, answering the question on everyone's mind. But: "We didn't discuss payment of the wall." But ABC News reports Peña Nieto later took to Twitter, claiming he did in fact tell Trump Mexico wouldn't pay for the wall, essentially calling the candidate a liar. During the news conference, Trump maintained it was America's right to build a wall, Peña Nieto says any talk of border security needs to include stopping the flow of money and guns into Mexico, according to Politico. In addition to not addressing his long-made claim that Mexico would pay for the wall, Trump also softened his rhetoric on Mexican immigrants. "I happen to have a tremendous feeling for Mexican-Americans," Salon quotes Trump as saying. And Peña Nieto insisted that all Mexican nationals living in the US "deserve the respect of everyone." Trump ended his speech by calling Peña Nieto, a man who once compared him to Hitler, a "friend." Peña Nieto characterized his discussion with Trump as "open and constructive." He says he believes Trump really does want to help Mexico via his policies, the AP reports. Both men are "extremely unpopular" in Mexico, as the Times puts it.

final_summary:
In recent developments, the United States and China have imposed new sanctions on North Korea following the country's latest nuclear test, aiming to hinder its nuclear and missile programs. Meanwhile, concerns have emerged regarding the effectiveness of genetically modified organisms (GMO) in agriculture.

A widely used variety of GMO corn designed to combat rootworms has fallen victim to the very pests it was intended to repel. Despite initial success, the corn's protective toxin has shown signs of being ineffective against certain strains of rootworms. Experts warn that if the issue persists, farmers might be compelled to revert to heavy pesticide usage, potentially exacerbating environmental issues.

Researchers have discovered that the rootworms' resistance to the corn's toxin may be linked to prolonged exposure to the genetically modified crop. This theory suggests that repeated planting of the same GMO seeds without adequate rotation or refuge strategies may accelerate the development of resistant pests. Monsanto, the developer of the GMO corn, acknowledges the issue but maintains that its current products remain effective, citing a 99% success rate across planted acres.

However, the problem is not limited to corn. Similar issues have arisen with Monsanto's Bt cotton in India, where a cotton-attacking pest called the bollworm has developed resistance. Furthermore, fields in the United States, particularly in Illinois and Iowa, have reported severe rootworm damage in areas where Bt corn was planted.

The emergence of resistant pests has sparked concerns about the long-term viability of Monsanto's business model, which relies heavily on its genetically modified seeds. Critics argue that the company's focus on high-tech solutions creates a cycle of dependence on increasingly expensive and potent chemicals, ultimately harming both farmers and the environment.

Monsanto's stock price has surprisingly remained resilient, increasing by over 25% in the past year despite the setbacks. The company appears to be shifting its focus towards developing new technologies, including RNA interference, which could potentially create crops that are lethal to insects. This move has raised eyebrows, with some questioning whether Monsanto is merely addressing symptoms rather than the underlying issues driving the development of resistant pests.

As the agricultural industry grapples with these challenges, regulatory bodies are facing pressure to reassess their stance on genetically modified organisms and the role of companies like Monsanto in shaping the future of food production.

reference: – Farmers in Iowa who planted corn seeds genetically modified to fend off the dreaded corn rootworm are seeing a troubling sign: The rootworm is apparently developing a resistance to the Monsanto seeds and gobbling up cornfields again, say Iowa University researchers. It's still just a small percentage of rootworms that have adapted, reports AFP, but the development is renewing fears that biotech crops will create superbugs. "These are isolated cases, and it isn't clear how widespread the problem will become," an Iowa State entomologist tells the Wall Street Journal. "But it is an early warning that management practices need to change." At Mother Jones, blogger Tom Philpott thinks Monsanto has put farmers on a "treadmill" of sorts: "the need to apply ever more, and ever more novel, high-tech responses to keep up with ever-evolving pests."

final_summary:
On May 1, 2014, Gerry Adams, the Sinn Fein leader, was arrested and questioned at Antrim police station in Northern Ireland regarding the 1972 IRA murder of Jean McConville, a 37-year-old widow who was abducted from her Belfast home, shot, and secretly buried. The case is part of the Troubles, a period of sectarian violence in Northern Ireland. 

Adams was arrested under the Terrorism Act and held for four days before being released on May 4, 2014. During his detention, police conducted 33 taped interviews with Adams, presenting him with old photographs and statements from individuals who were critical of the peace process. Adams maintained his innocence throughout the questioning and stated that he had voluntarily offered to meet with police two months prior to his arrest.

Adams criticized the timing of his arrest, suggesting that it was politically motivated and occurred during an election campaign. He argued that the police could have used discretion instead of invoking "pernicious, coercive legislation" to address the legacy of the Troubles. Adams also expressed concern that his arrest sent the wrong signal, particularly given his efforts to support the peace process.

Michael McConville, Jean McConville's son, expressed disappointment with Adams' release, stating that the family's pursuit of justice would continue. The McConville family had been waiting for 42 years for answers about their mother's disappearance and death. The police have sent a file detailing the evidence collected during Adams' questioning to the Public Prosecution Service (PPS), which will determine whether to pursue charges.

The case is linked to the Boston College tapes, a collection of interviews with former paramilitary leaders, including Ivor Bell, who was recently charged with aiding and abetting the murder of Jean McConville. The tapes were recorded as part of an oral history project on the Troubles. The decision on whether to prosecute Adams will be made by the PPS, with consideration given to the strength of the evidence and whether a prosecution is in the public interest.

Various politicians have weighed in on the situation, with some arguing that the timing of Adams' arrest was not politically motivated. However, others have raised concerns about the potential for interference in policing and the impact on the peace process. The release of Adams without charge has sparked debate about the handling of legacy cases related to the Troubles.

reference: – Sinn Fein leader Gerry Adams, lately the very high-profile suspect in a 1972 murder, will go free without charge, Northern Ireland police officials tell the BBC. Adams has been questioned since Wednesday in County Antrim over allegations that he, as an IRA commander ordered the murder of a 37-year-old widow and mom of 10, Jean McConville. The AP confirms the report, saying that Adams will likely be freed today, but notes that police say they'll send an evidence file to their British counterparts.

final_summary:
A 220-pound Fukang meteorite, estimated to be 4.5 billion years old and potentially worth up to $1 million, is at the center of a heated ownership dispute in federal court. The meteorite, a type of pallasite, originated from the asteroid belt near Mars and was first discovered in Fukang, China around 15 years ago. 

Stephan Settgast, a renowned asteroid collector from Marin County, California, claims to be the rightful owner of the meteorite, having purchased it in 2004 for an undisclosed amount. However, in 2014, Settgast allegedly sold the meteorite to Lawrence Stifler and Mary McFadden, founders of the Maine Mineral and Gem Museum in Maine, for $425,000 through a New York meteorite expert, Darryl Pitt. 

The transaction was facilitated by Pitt, who is known for his expertise in identifying the beauty and value of meteorites. According to court documents, Settgast agreed to sell the meteorite to the museum founders, who intended to display it in their newly constructed museum. However, Settgast claimed that the sale was contingent upon the meteorite not being publicly displayed, a condition that the museum founders deny existed.

After the purchase, the meteorite was sent to Keith and Dana Jenkerson, a Kansas-based couple specializing in meteorite polishing, to clean and prepare it for display. The Jenkersons spent nearly two years working on the meteorite, estimating its value to be around $1 million due to its exceptional quality. However, Pitt and the museum founders disputed this valuation, claiming it was excessively high.

In January 2023, the meteorite was reported stolen from the Jenkersons' laboratory in Osawatomie, Kansas. Settgast's lawyers maintain that he reclaimed the meteorite due to concerns over its potential public display, while the museum founders and Pitt accuse Settgast of engaging in "seller's remorse" and stealing the meteorite back for himself.

The case has drawn the attention of local law enforcement, with the Miami County Sheriff's Office in Kansas investigating the incident. Undersheriff Wayne Minckley stated that the investigation is ongoing, awaiting the outcome of the federal court case to determine whether a crime was indeed committed. A hearing is scheduled for June before U.S. District Court Judge Saundra Brown Armstrong to resolve the dispute over ownership of the Fukang meteorite.

reference: – The question of who owns a particular chunk of a famed meteorite is heading to federal court with the filing of a lawsuit and countersuit, SF Gate reports. It all started billions of years ago with the Fukang meteorite slammed into China's Gobi Desert. Fast-forward to the year 2000: when the meteorite was discovered and collectors began acquiring slices of it. Among them was Stephan Settgast, currently of California, who says he bought his 220-pound piece in 2004. In 2014, according to court documents, Settgast agreed to sell it to Lawrence Stifler and Mary McFadden of Massachusetts for $425,000. And then it all went bad. Settgast says the couple violated the conditions of the sale by planning to show it in their rock museum. They say not showing the meteorite was never a condition of the sale and suggest that Settgast got "seller's remorse" after learning he might have undervalued it. And, they allege, that remorse manifested in what they call the "outrageous act" of Settgast stealing back the rock from the studio of a pair of Kansas rock polishers who spent two years preparing the stone for the buyers, per NBC News. After all, the rock polishers did tell Settgast the meteorite could fetch up to $1 million (which, documents point out, would bump up their 5% fee to $50,000). How the alleged theft of the gold-flecked meteorite actually went down is unclear. Settgast filed a suit in February alleging breach of contract. Stifler, McFadden, and Darryl Pitt, the meteorite expert who brokered the deal, countersued last month. Local authorities in Kansas say they are going to hang back on pursuing Settgast for theft until a federal judge determines who owns the meteorite. "This isn't a typical theft," Miami County undersheriff tells NBC. (No one noticed this meteor the size of a large living room plowing into the earth.)

final_summary:
Documents recently unearthed by the Bangladesh Center for Worker Solidarity suggest that Wal-Mart had a connection to the Ether Tex factory, located on the fifth floor of the Rana Plaza building that collapsed last month, killing over 1,100 workers. Photos obtained by The New York Times show purchase orders from a Canadian company, Fame Jeans, for dark blue wash skinny-fit jeans to be delivered to Wal-Mart in the fall of 2012. However, Wal-Mart maintains that these documents date back a year and that there is no evidence of authorized or unauthorized production at the time of the tragedy. 

Fame Jeans CEO Alen Brandman attributed the order to a rogue employee who used the factory without his knowledge, stating that no further products were produced at the factory for Wal-Mart or Fame Jeans after the initial order. This incident raises questions about Wal-Mart's ability to track its supply chain, a concern echoed by Judy Gearhart, executive director of the International Labor Rights Forum.

In response to growing pressure to improve factory safety in Bangladesh, Wal-Mart announced its own plan to conduct in-depth safety inspections at 100% of its 279 factories in the country. The company will hire an outside auditor, Bureau Veritas, to inspect the factories and publish the results on its website by June 1. If serious safety issues are found, Wal-Mart will require factory owners to make necessary renovations or risk being removed from its list of authorized factories. However, unlike the legally binding pact signed by European retailers, Wal-Mart's plan is non-binding and lacks enforcement mechanisms.

Wal-Mart's decision to opt-out of the European-led safety agreement has been criticized by some experts, who argue that voluntary efforts have a poor track record in addressing worker safety issues. The company's plan has also been seen as a way to distance itself from the Rana Plaza disaster, similar to its response to a fire at another Bangladesh factory last November that killed 112 workers.

Other American retailers, such as Gap and Sears, have also expressed reservations about the European-led agreement due to concerns about potential legal liabilities. Gap has proposed amendments to the pact that would limit the remedies available to workers in case of a factory fire, while Sears has stated that it will participate in discussions on the agreement but is not ready to sign it yet.

Meanwhile, the European retailers that have signed the agreement, including PVH, H&M, and Inditex, have committed to having rigorous independent inspections and helping to fund fire safety upgrades, such as installing fire escapes. The agreement also establishes a governing board that includes labor representatives, retailer representatives, and a chairman chosen by the U.N. International Labor Organization to oversee safety inspections and dispute resolution.

Wal-Mart has released a list of over 250 factories whose authorization to produce goods for the company has been revoked, although it has not disclosed the reasons behind the revocation. The company plans to set up an independent call center for garment workers to report unsafe conditions and conduct fire safety training for every worker in the Bangladesh factories that make clothes for Wal-Mart.

reference: – Major European retailers are lining up to sign a Bangladesh safety plan aimed at preventing disasters like Rana Plaza, but American companies, including the Gap and Walmart, are backing away. Walmart—which blames a "rogue employee" for an order for its clothes found in the rubble of the building collapse—says it has its own safety plan for the 279 factories it uses in Bangladesh, and it will quickly deliver results as good as or better than the European plan, the New York Times reports. Walmart plans to audit factories for safety and remove them from its suppliers list if hazards are found. But while the European plan is legally binding, Walmart's is voluntary, and experts say such plans have a poor record for delivering results. "Without the legally binding contract that the European retailers have signed, it's just putting lipstick on a pig," a sociology professor who has been a vocal critic of sweatshops tells the Wall Street Journal. The Gap, meanwhile, says it won't sign the European plan unless language is removed that it fears could make it liable in US courts for safety failures at Bangladesh factories. The only American firm to have signed the plan so far is PVH, the parent company of Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger.

final_summary:
Journalists often engage in a delicate dance with celebrities, walking a fine line between friendliness and aggression in pursuit of scoops. This dynamic is particularly evident in interviews with movie stars, where the journalist's goal is to extract revealing information while maintaining a veneer of cordiality. Such encounters can be likened to interrogations, taking place in a controlled environment where the celebrity is often forced to confront uncomfortable questions. 

In a recent encounter with Kate McKinnon, the Emmy-winning Saturday Night Live (S.N.L.) mainstay, this tension was palpable. Meeting at a low-key eatery in the East Village, Kate arrived promptly, dressed in casual attire that belied her status as a Hollywood A-lister. Her appearance was striking, with pale blue, almond-shaped eyes and tawny skin that seemed almost otherworldly. Despite her beauty, Kate exuded a sense of vulnerability, as if constantly on guard against potential threats.

As the conversation progressed, it became clear that Kate's newfound fame had left her feeling exposed and sensitive. Her role as Hillary Clinton on S.N.L. had catapulted her to international attention, making her a target for criticism and scrutiny. The experience had taken a toll on her, and she found herself becoming emotionally invested in the character, imagining Hillary's inner life and struggles. This level of empathy was a hallmark of Kate's performances, which often walked a fine line between humor and pathos.

Kate's ability to inhabit various personas was a key aspect of her success on S.N.L. Her impressions of politicians, including Hillary Clinton, Elizabeth Warren, and Kellyanne Conway, were notable for their nuance and depth. By staying open and raw, Kate was able to tap into the complexities of her subjects, creating characters that were both humorous and relatable. This approach required a level of psychological vulnerability, as Kate necessarily took on the emotions and experiences of those she portrayed.

Beyond her work on S.N.L., Kate had begun to appear in major feature films, including the all-female Ghostbusters reboot and the bachelorette-party-gone-bad comedy Rough Night. While these projects showcased her range as a performer, they seemed secondary to her work on the show. Kate's connection to S.N.L. was deep-seated, and she had risen through the ranks from the Upright Citizens Brigade to become one of the show's leading talents.

The symbiotic relationship between S.N.L. and the White House had become a defining feature of the show's success. The program's skewering of politics had captivated audiences, and the White House had taken notice, offering feedback and critiques. Alec Baldwin's portrayal of Donald Trump had been particularly influential, capturing the public's imagination and sparking a heated debate about the boundaries between reality and satire. The show's impact extended beyond the realm of entertainment, serving as a cultural touchstone and a reflection of the nation's mood.

As Kate navigated the complex landscape of celebrity and politics, she remained committed to her craft, using her platform to explore the intricacies of human nature. Her performances were a testament to her skill and dedication, and her influence extended far beyond the confines of the stage or screen.
