reference: – 50 Cent, once at the forefront of rap music, returns with Before I Self Destruct—but his fourth solo effort isn’t impressing the critics. “Though packed with tough beats, dense production and 50's most ferocious delivery in years, the gloats about shootings, drugs and bloody 'hoods feel about as fresh as a Saw sequel,” writes Edna Gundersen in USA Today. The album “isn't likely to renew 50 Cent's currency.” Fitty’s ferocity is refreshing when you consider “the current hip-pop landscape where meat'n'potatoes gangsta is actually kinda novel,” writes Ian Cohen for Pitchfork, “but the fun of talking about the record overstates its actual quality. After initially promising a return to form, 50 doesn't have the ability or initiative to hold the listener's interest over the long run.”

final_summary:
Actress Kelly Preston recently spoke with 'TODAY' host Natalie Morales about her experiences with Scientology, specifically its influence on her birthing style and parenting choices. Preston credits the "silent birth" technique, a common practice among Scientologists, for the peaceful demeanor of her children. According to Preston, the silent birth method involves minimizing verbal communication during delivery, refraining from shouting instructions or making loud comments to encourage the mother.

Preston, who gave birth to her youngest son Benjamin in November, believes that this approach contributed to his calm nature, stating that her children have "always been amazing, just very calm, very peaceful, happy." She attributes this to the peaceful atmosphere created during their births, saying, "it's just bringing them in, in as peaceful and gentle a way as possible."

The actress, who is 48 years old and had previously struggled to conceive, did not consider the risks associated with giving birth at an advanced maternal age when deciding to have Benjamin. Instead, she focused on the challenges of conceiving, taking three years to become pregnant. Despite these difficulties, Preston expressed gratitude for the opportunity to have another child, saying, "we're just really happy that we're so fortunate."

Benjamin's arrival has brought solace to the family, particularly following the tragic loss of their 16-year-old son Jett in 2009 due to complications from a seizure disorder. Preston described the experience of having Benjamin as "wonderfully healing," citing the positive impact on those around them, including receiving heartfelt letters and messages of support.

In addition to discussing her experiences with Scientology and parenthood, Preston also talked about her return to acting after Jett's passing. She credited actor Kevin Spacey, her co-star in the film "Casino Jack," with helping her cope with her emotions and find motivation to continue working. Spacey's presence on set reportedly lifted her spirits, providing a much-needed distraction and sense of humor during a challenging time.

reference: – John Travolta's wife gave birth to new son Benjamin in total silence because that's what Scientology demands, right? Well, sort of. "It's just no words as much as possible. If you need to moan, if you cry out or all of that, of course, that's normal," Kelly Preston said on the Today Show. "But just bringing them in as peaceful and gentle a way as possible." Why? She explains, somewhat confusingly, that Scientology founder L. Ron Hubbard found that "stress, fears, worry, things like that have to do with the reactive mind and in that part of the mind is different words and commands that can come back to affect you later in life." And she thinks that birthing approach works: "My kids have always been very calm, very peaceful, happy, and I absolutely know that it's very much because of that." In the rest of the interview, Preston goes on to talk about the baby's chosen name (Benjamin is "gentle"), her age (she didn't consider the risks), and her new film. Click for more from the interview.

final_summary:
A long-held notion that people should clean their plates after every meal has been largely perpetuated by societal norms and cultural expectations. However, recent research suggests that adults tend to follow this advice, consuming nearly 92% of the food they put on their plates. According to a study published in the International Journal of Obesity, which analyzed data from 14 studies involving over 1,000 participants across eight developed countries, including the US, Canada, France, Taiwan, South Korea, Finland, and the Netherlands.

The study found that the percentage of consumed food remained relatively consistent, regardless of location or demographic factors such as age and sex. Men and women, as well as individuals from different countries, tended to eat similar amounts of food when serving themselves. In fact, the results were so uniform that researchers described the phenomenon as a global trend, dubbing it the "Clean Plate Planet."

Interestingly, the study also revealed that people who consumed healthier foods, such as fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean meats or dairy products, tended to eat slightly more (91.2%) compared to those who opted for less nutritious options (80.6%). This finding may indicate that individuals who prioritize healthy eating are more attuned to their hunger cues and better able to gauge their desired portion sizes.

In contrast, children exhibited a significantly lower rate of consumption, with an average of around 60% of the food they served themselves being eaten. Researchers speculate that this discrepancy may be due to children's uncertainty about their food preferences, making it more challenging for them to accurately estimate their appetite.

The study's lead researcher, Brian Wansink, emphasized the significance of understanding human eating behaviors in addressing the growing issue of overeating and obesity. By recognizing that people tend to consume most of what they serve themselves, individuals can become more mindful of their portion sizes and make informed choices about their diet. As Wansink suggested, simply acknowledging this tendency can encourage people to be more intentional about their eating habits and strive for a healthier relationship with food.

reference: – Feeling guilty about that huge serving you heaped onto your plate at dinner last night and promptly finished? You're not the only one who does it. New research published in the International Journal of Obesity finds that the average adult eats 92% of the food on his or her plate. The reasons have yet to be hashed out, but the reality is clear: "If you put it on your plate, it's going into your stomach," says Brian Wansink, director of the Cornell Food and Brand Lab, in a Eureka Alert press release. The findings hold across several developed countries. Researchers observed 1,179 diners in the US, Canada, France, Taiwan, Korea, Finland, and the Netherlands, and regardless of location or gender the results were almost identical. There is, however, one exception: kids. Observing 326 participants under 18, researchers found that the typical child eats 59% of the food he or she puts on the plate. Whatever the reason, we grow up to belong to the Clean Plate Club, as researchers call it, they say this can be a wake-up call to be more mindful of what we serve ourselves in the first place. The LA Times susses out one piece of good news along those lines: Those who pile on fruits, veggies, whole grains, and other healthful stuff tend to clean their plates more thoroughly than those who eat less healthful foods. (Other research suggests it's all the sitting, not eating, that's putting on the pounds.)

final_summary:
Sidney Bouvier Gilstrap-Portley, a 25-year-old man, posed as a Hurricane Harvey evacuee and enrolled as a freshman at Hillcrest High School in Dallas, Texas, under the alias Rashun Richardson. He initially attended Skyline High School in August before transferring to Hillcrest two months later. Gilstrap-Portley's enrollment was facilitated by the district's policy of exempting students who claim to be homeless or evacuees from providing certain documents, such as identification and birth certificates, due to federal law. 

According to a district spokesperson, Gilstrap-Portley may have only provided minimal documentation, including immunization records and proof of a physical examination. Despite being 25 years old, Gilstrap-Portley presented himself as a 17-year-old student and even participated in the school's basketball team, scoring 17 points in a playoff game. His true identity was discovered when his former basketball coach recognized him playing for another high school team.

Gilstrap-Portley was arrested and charged with tampering with government records. He was booked into the Dallas County Jail and subsequently released. The incident has raised concerns about the vulnerability of schools to impostors taking advantage of policies designed to support students affected by natural disasters. Hillcrest Principal Chris Bayer acknowledged that the school had welcomed students displaced by Hurricane Harvey, but noted that the incident highlights areas where the district needs improvement in verifying the identities of new students.

reference: – A "savvy" 25-year-old gamed the Dallas school system and posed as a high school student, all apparently so he could play some basketball, officials say. The Dallas Morning News reports Sidney Bouvier Gilstrap-Portley was arrested Friday after authorities say he pretended to be 17-year-old "Rashaun Richardson" and enrolled at two high schools—first Skyline in August, then Hillcrest in October, where he joined that school's basketball team and was voted District 11-5A's offensive player of the year. The Dallas ISD superintendent says the "perfect storm" that led to this involved an actual storm: Gilstrap-Portley reportedly said he'd been left homeless by Hurricane Harvey and came to Dallas alone. More: The district tells WFAA students who've been through a natural disaster or are homeless aren't asked for the same documentation that's typically required, per federal law. Gilstrap-Portley may have scammed his way in with just immunization and physical records, a district rep says.

final_summary:
Bathrooms.com, a UK-based online retailer specializing in bathroom furniture, has collaborated with chocolatier Choccywoccydoodah to create a unique and extravagant bathroom suite made entirely out of Belgian chocolate. The idea behind this unusual project stems from the company's observation that many customers mistakenly type "bathroom sweets" instead of "bathroom suites" when searching for bathroom-related products online. According to Bathrooms.com's Chief Executive Ian Monk, over a million Britons make this error every year.

The resulting product, dubbed the Maderno Sweet, consists of four key components: a £7,000 chocolate bidet containing 210,000 calories, a £9,000 chocolate basin also holding 210,000 calories, an £50,000 chocolate bath weighing in at 8 million calories, and a £14,000 chocolate toilet with 980,000 calories. These items collectively total 9.4 million calories and are priced at $133,040 when purchased as a complete set. Alternatively, individual pieces can be bought separately, although this may result in a longer wait time due to the need for each item to be crafted fresh.

While the chocolate bathroom suite appears luxurious and decadent, it is not intended for actual use. The chocolate is susceptible to melting when exposed to direct sunlight, radiator heat, or hot liquids, rendering it unsuitable for bathing purposes. However, the company suggests that the suite can be preserved for up to six months or stored at room temperature for extended periods if not consumed. To further extend its lifespan, a layer of varnish can be applied to protect the chocolate.

This unusual collaboration between Bathrooms.com and Choccywoccydoodah has sparked curiosity among those who stumble upon the product through incorrect search queries. While the chocolate bathroom suite may not be practical for everyday use, it undoubtedly adds a touch of whimsy and indulgence to the world of bathroom design.

reference: – The wait is over, folks: You can finally eat your entire bathroom. British bathroom designers and chocolate makers have joined forces to build a sink, bathtub, and, yes, toilet out of chocolate, the New York Daily News reports. Altogether, the set will cost you about $133,000. But the real cost could be to your health: The Maderno Sweet, as it's called, contains about 9.4 million calories' worth of Belgian chocolate. That would theoretically keep you going for about 12 years, the Daily News notes. The makers say that "one shouldn't attempt to consume the entire suite in one session." The idea for the set came from a spelling mistake. "People are regularly searching for 'bathroom sweets,'" when they (presumably) mean suites, the makers note on the chocolate bathroom's site. "We decided to answer their search, literally," Bathrooms.com CEO Ian Monk notes. His company joined with chocolatiers Choccywoccydoodah to create the set, which should last about six months. They don't actually recommend using it; for one thing, hot liquid will melt it, so "it's not ideal for running that relaxing bath!" Monk notes, via the Los Angeles Times. Meanwhile, Hershey's is working on 3D-printed chocolate.

final_summary:
On Monday, the body of Juneau Mayor Greg Fisk was discovered by his adult son inside their residence on the 400 block of Kennedy Street. The discovery was made after a 911 call was placed at approximately 3:30 pm, prompting a response from the Juneau Police Department (JPD) and Capital City Fire/Rescue. Upon arrival, Fisk was pronounced dead at the scene, and the investigation into his death is currently underway. 

According to JPD Spokeswoman Erann Kalwara, the case is considered an unattended death, and while the investigation is still in its early stages, no determination has been made regarding whether the death was suspicious. However, rumors circulating about an alleged assault connected to Fisk's death have been dismissed by police as speculation. Detectives are actively collecting and documenting evidence to determine the circumstances surrounding the mayor's passing.

The cause of death remains unknown pending an autopsy by the State Medical Examiner, which is expected to yield results within several days. In light of Fisk's untimely death, the City and Borough of Juneau canceled scheduled Assembly meetings for Monday evening. 

Fisk, who was elected mayor in October, secured nearly twice as many votes as his predecessor, Merrill Sanford. His sudden passing has left the community reeling, with Juneau Deputy Mayor Mary Becker describing him as a "wonderful person and a friend" who will be deeply missed. As per state law, Becker will assume the role of acting mayor until further notice, although specific procedures and protocols are still being researched by the city's attorney.

reference: – The mayor of Juneau has been found dead in his home less than two months after he was elected. The body of Greg Fisk, 70, was found by his adult son on Monday afternoon and police, who have not released a cause of death, have been searching the area and interviewing neighbors, the Alaska Dispatch reports. Juneau police are "aware of rumors that an assault occurred in connection with Fisk's death. Those rumors are speculation," police said in a statement Monday night. "Detectives are actively investigating facts of the incident and all evidence is being preserved and documented." Fisk, a former state fisheries specialist, easily beat incumbent Merrill Sanford last month to become mayor of Alaska's capital, the Dispatch reports. Deputy Mayor Mary Becker, who says Fisk was an old family friend, will now become acting mayor. He was a "wonderful person and a friend and from the calls I've been receiving tonight, I'm not the only one who felt he was a wonderful person and a good friend to Juneau," she tells KTUU. "It's so devastating to have this happen, it's basically unbelievable."

final_summary:
President Trump addressed concerns about the lack of nominees for key positions at the State Department, stating that he is the only one who truly matters in terms of policy decisions. He claimed that the State Department does not need all the people it wants, citing cost-saving measures. Trump argued that Senate Democrats are obstructing his nominees, pointing out that he has nominated fewer people than his predecessors.

During an interview with Laura Ingraham on Fox News, Trump discussed various topics, including the Fusion GPS dossier, which he described as "totally fake and made-up." He criticized Hillary Clinton for defending the dossier and accused her of hypocrisy. Trump also expressed frustration with the US Justice Department, calling it a "joke" and a "laughing stock."

On the topic of immigration, Trump reiterated his stance on chain migration, stating that it is a "disaster for this country" and that he intends to end it as part of any potential DACA deal. He emphasized the importance of securing the southern border and preventing the flow of drugs into the country.

Regarding his upcoming trip to Asia, Trump stated that he will discuss trade with China and address the issue of North Korea. He praised China for its efforts to help the US on the North Korean issue and noted that Japan is also concerned about the situation.

Trump also spoke about his family, expressing his appreciation for their support and acknowledging that they have been treated unfairly by the media. He highlighted his accomplishments in his first year in office, including the passage of numerous bills and the appointment of several conservative judges.

In a separate development, the State Department remains understaffed, with several key positions vacant. According to an appointee tracker, Trump has fallen behind his predecessors in the number of appointments confirmed by the Senate. Trump attributed the delay to Senate Democrats, claiming that they are obstructing his nominees.

Meanwhile, the Department of Defense has seen a high rate of confirmations, with several key officials appointed to their positions. These include General James McConville, who was confirmed as the Army's vice chief of staff, and Lieutenant General Daniel Akerson, who was confirmed as the Army's deputy chief of staff for operations.

Other notable confirmations include the appointment of Dr. Mark T. Esper as the Secretary of the Army, and the confirmation of Dr. Ellen M. Lord as the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology and Logistics. Additionally, the Senate confirmed several other officials, including Dr. Lisa Porter, who was confirmed as the Deputy Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering, and Dr. Alan Ray Shaffer, who was confirmed as the Deputy Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment.

In the Department of Commerce, the Senate confirmed several officials, including Dr. Walter Copan, who was confirmed as the Under Secretary of Commerce for Intellectual Property and Director of the United States Patent and Trademark Office. The Senate also confirmed Dr. Karen Dunn Kelley, who was confirmed as the Under Secretary of Commerce for International Trade.

The Department of Agriculture saw several confirmations, including the appointment of Dr. Stephen Censky as the Deputy Secretary of Agriculture, and the confirmation of Dr. Naomi Kooker, who was confirmed as the Under Secretary of Agriculture for Marketing and Regulatory Programs.

Overall, the Trump administration continues to face challenges in filling key positions, particularly at the State Department. However, the Department of Defense has seen a higher rate of confirmations, with several key officials appointed to their positions.

Trump also spoke about his family, expressing his appreciation for their support and acknowledging that they have been treated unfairly by the media. He highlighted his accomplishments in his first year in office, including the passage of numerous bills and the appointment of several conservative judges.

In a separate development, the State Department remains understaffed, with several key positions vacant. According to an appointee tracker, Trump has fallen behind his predecessors in the number of appointments confirmed by the Senate. Trump attributed the delay to Senate Democrats, claiming that they are obstructing his nominees.

Meanwhile, the Department of Defense has seen a high rate of confirmations, with several key officials appointed to their positions. These include General James McConville, who was confirmed as the Army's vice chief of staff, and Lieutenant General Daniel Akerson, who was confirmed as the Army's deputy chief of staff for operations.

Other notable confirmations include the appointment of Dr. Mark T. Esper as the Secretary of the Army, and the confirmation of Dr. Ellen M. Lord as the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition, Technology and Logistics. Additionally, the Senate confirmed several other officials, including Dr. Lisa Porter, who was confirmed as the Deputy Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering, and Dr. Alan Ray Shaffer, who was confirmed as the Deputy Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment.

In the Department of Commerce, the Senate confirmed several officials, including Dr. Walter Copan, who was confirmed as the Under Secretary of Commerce for Intellectual Property and Director of the United States Patent and Trademark Office. The Senate also confirmed Dr. Karen Dunn Kelley, who was confirmed as the Under Secretary of Commerce for International Trade.

The Department of Agriculture saw several confirmations, including the appointment of Dr. Stephen Censky as the Deputy Secretary of Agriculture, and the confirmation of Dr. Naomi Kooker, who was confirmed as the Under Secretary of Agriculture for Marketing and Regulatory Programs.

Overall, the Trump administration continues to face challenges in filling key positions, particularly at the State Department. However, the Department of Defense has seen a higher rate of confirmations, with several key officials appointed to their positions.

reference: – President Trump sat down with Laura Ingraham Thursday night, and when the Fox News host pressed him on why there are still so many vacancies for top posts in the State Department, he seemed nonplussed. "Let me tell you, the one that matters is me," Trump said, per the Hill. "I'm the only one that matters because when it comes to it that's what the policy is going to be." He also reminded Ingraham that he comes from the business world and that he often tells his own teams, "When you don't need to fill slots, don't fill them." He also put some of the blame on "[Chuck] Schumer and the Democrats" for "obstructing" nominees he's made for these positions, though a Washington Post/Partnership for Public Service tracker points out he's made far fewer nominations than the four presidents before him by this time in their tenure. Trump did touch upon one slot that's filled at State: Rex Tillerson's post. Per Politico, the president was asked if the secretary of state would remain with his administration "for the duration," to which Trump replied: "We'll see. I don't know who's going to be [here for the] duration." He did add that Tillerson was "working hard" and "doing his best." Also receiving mentions from Trump: Pyongyang and Putin. Trump called North Korea a "thing that I think we will solve," per Fox News. "If we don't solve it, it's not going to be very pleasant for them. It's not going to be very pleasant, I guess, for anybody," he said, without giving further details. As for the Russian president, Trump noted he wouldn't be against meeting with him during his upcoming trip to Asia. "Putin is very important because they can help us with North Korea," he said. RealClearPolitics has clips from the interview.

final_summary:
Tensions in Afghanistan escalated on Friday, marking the bloodiest day yet in protests sparked by the desecration of copies of the Muslim holy book at a NATO military base. Twelve people were killed, bringing the total death toll to 23, including two US soldiers, as riot police and soldiers remained on high alert for further violence. The protests, which began on Tuesday, have seen hundreds of Afghans marching towards the presidential palace in Kabul, chanting slogans such as "Death to America!" and "Long live Islam!", while others hoisted the white flag of the Taliban.

The burning of the Korans at the Bagram compound has deepened public mistrust of NATO forces, which are struggling to stabilize the country ahead of their withdrawal in 2014. US President Barack Obama had previously apologized for the incident, stating that it was unintentional, but many Afghans demand a public trial for those responsible. The Koran is considered the literal word of God and its desecration is considered one of the worst forms of blasphemy.

Protests were reported in various parts of the country, including the western province of Herat, where seven people were killed, and the eastern province of Khost, where two people lost their lives. In Kabul, police used tear gas and rubber batons to disperse crowds, while in Herat, protesters were armed and some appeared to be agents provocateurs, according to government officials. The situation was further complicated by allegations that Iranian agents may have been involved in inciting the violence.

In response to the unrest, Germany withdrew its troops from a small base in the northern Takhar province due to security concerns. The German military had originally planned to close the base in late March, but accelerated the process following demonstrations in the region. Meanwhile, NATO is still investigating the circumstances surrounding the burning of the Korans, with a spokesperson stating that the books were being held as evidence and that a few had been taken out of the base by Afghan employees.

As the protests continue, many Afghans feel that justice has not been served and that the US will not hold anyone accountable for the incident. "Just by saying 'I am sorry,' nothing can be solved," said one protester. "We want an open trial for those infidels who have burned our Holy Koran." The situation remains volatile, with many fearing that the violence could escalate further.

reference: – Protesters are rallying once again to decry the burning of Korans at Bagram, with more shouts of "death to America"—and deaths—throughout the country. The New York Times reports that gunfire was audible as demonstrators assembled at a Kabul mosque following Friday prayers. Another 4,000 people carrying sticks and rocks moved toward the center of the city; some waved Taliban flags and had a jihadist slogan written on their clothes: "I sacrifice myself." Demonstrations occurred in at least six more provinces throughout Afghanistan, and Reuters reports that nine more were killed today: seven in the western province of Herat, and two in Khost in the east. Meanwhile, Germany says it has withdrawn troops from an outpost in northern Afghanistan amid the protests. The German military had planned to shutter its base in the Taloqan area in late March, but the 50 remaining soldiers have been transferred out of the base in light of the demonstrations, reports the AP. And at home, Obama is facing GOP criticism for his apology.

final_summary:
President Barack Obama's well-documented struggles with smoking have been a persistent theme throughout his presidency. Despite his efforts to quit, he has continued to rely on nicotine gum as a substitute, often sparking controversy among critics who view the habit as unbecoming of a world leader. According to his physicians, Obama's smoking habits have been ongoing, with his doctor recommending continued smoking cessation efforts, including the use of nicotine gum, to help him overcome the addiction.

In 2010, Obama's medical team reported that he was still smoking, although he claimed to have made significant progress in reducing his consumption. However, his struggles with nicotine remained evident, with his doctor noting that quitting would be a "lifelong struggle." The president's reliance on nicotine gum has led to numerous instances of public scrutiny, including during official visits to foreign countries, where cultural norms around gum-chewing vary greatly.

During a visit to China in 2012, Obama was criticized for chewing gum during a meeting with President Xi Jinping, with some commentators viewing the behavior as a sign of disrespect. Similarly, during a trip to India in 2015, Obama was spotted chewing gum during the Republic Day parade, earning him ridicule from local media outlets. These incidents highlight the complexities of navigating cultural differences in diplomatic settings, where seemingly innocuous behaviors can be misinterpreted.

Despite the criticism, Obama's love affair with gum remains unwavering. His fondness for the habit dates back to his teenage years, when he started smoking. Since then, he has relied on nicotine gum as a way to manage his cravings, often using it discreetly during high-profile events. His doctor has encouraged him to continue this approach, citing its effectiveness in helping him maintain a smoke-free lifestyle.

The president's health has been subject to scrutiny beyond his smoking habits. A 2010 medical examination revealed that he had developed borderline-high cholesterol levels, which his doctor attributed to his diet. To address this issue, Obama was advised to modify his eating habits and engage in regular exercise to lower his LDL cholesterol below 130. Additionally, his doctor noted that his tendinitis in the left knee, likely caused by his frequent basketball playing, required a modified exercise regimen to prevent further complications.

Throughout his presidency, Obama has demonstrated a commitment to addressing the nation's health concerns, particularly those related to tobacco use. In 2009, he signed a bill granting authority over tobacco products to the FDA, aimed at curbing the aggressive marketing tactics employed by the tobacco industry towards young people. The legislation banned flavored cigarettes and restricted youth-oriented marketing practices, marking a significant step towards reducing tobacco-related harm.

As Obama navigates the final stages of his presidency, his health remains a priority. His doctors have emphasized the importance of maintaining a balanced lifestyle, including regular exercise, a healthy diet, and continued smoking cessation efforts. While his gum-chewing habit may raise eyebrows, it serves as a testament to his ongoing struggle with nicotine addiction, a challenge he continues to face head-on.

reference: – It's got to be better than smoking in public, but President Obama's gum-chewing habit keeps getting him in trouble. The latest kerfuffle came in India, where Obama was watching a parade; reports noted his chewing of what was likely Nicorette. That incident has now made a number of headlines, with Politico reporting that the chewing occurred multiple times during the event as well as when Obama greeted PM Narendra Modi; the Times of India called it an "ungainly sight." The gum prompted some commentary on Twitter, noted by the Times. "Seriously—gum during a formal parade?" asked author Shobhaa De, while filmmaker Shekhar Kapur posted: "Glad to see @BarackObama is so human." Previous such moments have occurred in France and China, Vocativ reports, pointing to a Washington Post article from last year which noted, per earlier reports, that it was a doctor-recommended activity. Amid Obama's "lifelong struggle" to get off cigarettes, his physician recommended he stick with the chewing.

final_summary:
On a Saturday night, a group of friends, including Cody Ha, a 23-year-old Wichita man, and his mother, Huong Pham, 62, were brutally murdered in their home in the 7500 block of East Huntington. The crime was witnessed remotely by a Houston man named Ashley Martinez, who was playing video games with Ha and others in an online group. Martinez and the other players heard loud popping noises, later confirmed to be gunshots, coming from Ha's location, and attempted to contact him, but his phone rang unanswered.

Martinez and his fellow gamers reported hearing at least two shots, which they believed came from a handgun. They then spoke with Ha's sister, who had arrived home to find her brother and mother lying unconscious and covered in blood. The police were notified, and an investigation into the double homicide began.

According to Tommy Ong, a close friend of Ha's, the victim often stopped playing video games to answer the door, claiming that people were visiting to purchase prescription medications. Ong stated that Ha's mother, Pham, shared the profits from these transactions with her son. Neighbors reported frequent visitors to the residence, describing increased traffic compared to other houses in the area.

Pham had a history of conflicts with others, as documented in Sedgwick County District Court records. In 2014, she obtained a temporary protection-from-stalking order against another woman, alleging threats and harassment. However, both parties' petitions were eventually dismissed due to lack of further incidents. Additionally, Pham was accused of stalking a woman in 2009, but the charges were dropped due to insufficient evidence.

The victims' community is reeling from the news, with friends describing Ha as kind, inclusive, and even-tempered. As the police continue to investigate, they are seeking tips and information from the public to aid in solving the case. The incident marks the 29th and 30th homicides of the year in Wichita, with two more occurring the following day.

reference: – Cody Ha may have been 600 miles from his friend in Texas, but the 23-year-old Kansas man sounded as though he were in the same room via the microphones and headphones the pair used to communicate while playing video games online with others Saturday. Then, around 10pm, Ashley Martinez of Houston heard at least two loud popping noises that hurt his ears, reports the Wichita Eagle. Ha didn't speak again, and when players called his phone, they heard only the unanswered ring through his microphone, Martinez says. An hour later, they heard something else: Ha's sister arriving at the Wichita home to find her brother and 62-year-old mother, Huong Pham, covered in blood, dead of gunshot wounds, per KAKE. "She was panicking," Martinez recalls. "She said she didn't feel safe." Another friend tells the Eagle that people often visited the family's home to buy what Ha described as prescription drugs. He suggests Pham sold the drugs and gave Ha a cut of the proceeds. Court records show that Pham had prior conflicts with others: In 2014, a woman accused Pham of sending two men "looking for me and my husband" over a money dispute. Pham later said the woman "hired somebody to cause me harm for $2,000 … after I talked about the money owed my family." Pham said someone broke windows of a family business and slashed the tires of her son's car around the same time. Police, who've named no suspects, are investigating whether the Saturday shooting death of a Pizza Hut delivery driver three blocks from Ha and Pham's home is related, per the Washington Post.

final_summary:
A bombshell report by The Washington Post revealed that the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) concluded that Russia intervened in the 2016 US presidential election to help Donald Trump win. The report sparked a firestorm, with President-elect Trump responding indignantly on Twitter, attacking the CIA's credibility and dismissing the allegations as "fake news." However, the CIA's assessment aligns with previous findings by other intelligence agencies, including the National Security Agency (NSA) and the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), which have also concluded that Russia engaged in a massive cyberattack on the US election infrastructure.

According to the Post, the CIA's assessment is based on high-confidence intelligence that Russia's goal was to favor Trump over Hillary Clinton, and that the agency believes Russia's efforts helped swing the election in Trump's favor. The report also revealed that Republican Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell played a key role in keeping the explosive information under wraps during the election, voicing doubts about US intelligence and threatening to publicly accuse the Obama administration of partisan politics if the White House challenged the Russians publicly.

The New York Times added to the controversy by reporting that intelligence agencies have "high confidence" that Russians hacked the Republican National Committee but conspicuously released no documents related to Republicans, raising the disturbing question of whether Russia is holding onto these materials to use as leverage over the Trump administration. Julian Assange of WikiLeaks, which published the hacked Clinton emails, denied receiving the materials from the Russian government, while Russia itself has denied the allegations.

President Barack Obama, concerned that President-elect Trump may ignore US intelligence on Russia's cyber activities, is attempting to box Trump in politically by ordering a definitive report on Russia's cyber-intrusions before he leaves office in six weeks. The CIA assessment is supported by bipartisan support in Congress, with Republican Senator Lindsey Graham calling for investigations into the hacking and urging Trump to take a tougher line with Moscow.

Senior congressional Republicans are divided on how to respond to the allegations, with some wanting to investigate Russia's meddling in the election and others giving Trump the benefit of the doubt. To address this divide, some national security experts suggest that Congress could hold hearings, take action against Russia overseas, start a war of words with Trump, or hope Russia shows its true colors by continuing to undermine Trump's presidency.

Senate Armed Services Committee Chairman John McCain, Intelligence Committee Chair Richard Burr, and Foreign Relations Committee Chair Bob Corker are planning to hold hearings on Russia's ability to hack into US political parties and weapons systems, aiming to explain to the American public what Russia did to try to influence the election and build a case that Russia took unprecedented steps to meddle in American democracy. However, these hearings risk dividing Republicans in Congress, particularly given McConnell's initial skepticism of the intelligence assessment.

Meanwhile, Trump has shown a willingness to defy his party on issues related to Russia, suggesting that he may be open to taking a tougher stance against Moscow. However, his advisors have also accused the intelligence community of politicizing the hacking analysis to tarnish Trump and his presidency, further complicating the situation. As the debate rages on, the stakes are high, with some experts warning that Russia's actions could have significant implications for US national security and the integrity of future elections.

reference: – The fallout from Friday night's "bombshell" report from the CIA that Russian hackers directly interfered with the US election in order to get Donald Trump elected continued Saturday. Here are seven things you need to know: With Trump criticizing the CIA—rather than Russia—in the wake of the report, the New York Times reports he's opening an "extraordinary breach" between himself and the national security establishment he's bound to need during his presidency. While mostly at a loss, Michael Tomasky at the Daily Beast has a few pieces of advice for what Democrats should do next: "Demand the release of the information. Demand a real investigation...The media needs to get the message that conservatives aren’t the only people who get pissed off." In a "no-win situation for the GOP," some Republicans in Congress want to investigate Russia's involvement in the election against Trump's wishes. The Washington Post has four ways they could take on Russia, with or without the president-elect. Harry Reid is calling for the resignation of FBI director James Comey, who he compares to J. Edgar Hoover and accuses of covering up information about Russia's activities to get Trump elected, the Guardian reports. Pamela Kruger at Fortune points out that Trump did Saturday what he normally does when faced with a major negative story: spend the morning tweeting about something completely unrelated; Celebrity Apprentice, in this case. Worried Trump will ignore Russian threats to US democracy when he takes office, Quartz reports President Obama is "racing against the clock" to get a full report on Russia's involvement in US affairs before he steps down in six weeks. Meanwhile, John McCain says the close relationship between Exxon CEO Rex Tillerson—Trump's likely secretary of state—and Vladimir Putin "a matter of concern," the Hill reports. With Democrats needing only three GOP senators to vote against Tillerson, a McCain aide says a confirmation is unlikely. "Putin is a thug, bully, and a murderer, and anybody else who describes him as anything else is lying," McCain says.

final_summary:
The International Criminal Court (ICC) has opened a preliminary investigation into allegations that Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte and other government officials committed crimes against humanity during the country's "war on drugs" campaign. The probe, led by ICC Prosecutor Fatou Bensouda, will examine events dating back to July 1, 2016, and assess whether there is sufficient evidence to build a case. This marks the ICC's first preliminary examination in a Southeast Asian nation.

According to a 77-page report submitted to the ICC by Philippine lawyer Jude Sabio, Duterte has been directly responsible for "extrajudicial executions and mass murder" since he began his anti-drug campaign as mayor of Davao in 1988. Official statistics indicate that around 4,000 people have been killed by police in anti-drug operations under Duterte's presidency, while Sabio's report puts the death toll at over 8,000.

Duterte's administration has maintained that the thousands of deaths were due to suspects resisting arrest, but human rights groups and opposition politicians claim that many murders were carried out and covered up. As mayor of Davao, Duterte's crackdown on crime was often carried out by vigilante groups, known as death squads, allegedly with his approval.

The ICC's investigation comes after years of criticism from human rights organizations, including Human Rights Watch, which has called for an independent international investigation into Duterte's war on drugs. The group's Deputy Director Phil Robertson welcomed the ICC's move, stating that the court "absolutely should initiate a full investigation into these alleged crimes."

Duterte's spokesperson, Harry Roque, dismissed the investigation as a "waste of the court's time and resources," arguing that many of the allegations predate the Philippines' accession to the ICC in 2011 and therefore cannot be considered. However, ICC Prosecutor Bensouda specifically referenced killings since July 1, 2016, in her statement.

Opposition politician Gary Alejano, who attempted to impeach Duterte last year and submitted evidence to the ICC, sees the investigation as a validation of their claims and a glimmer of hope for the victims of Duterte's war on drugs. Alejano believes they have a strong case and hopes to be given the opportunity to present their evidence before the ICC.

Duterte himself has long been critical of the ICC, labeling it "bullshit," "hypocritical," and "useless." He has also threatened to withdraw the Philippines from the ICC on multiple occasions, daring the court to bring him to trial. Despite this, the ICC remains committed to investigating the allegations, citing its duty to uphold the rule of law and protect civilians from serious human rights abuses.

reference: – Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte welcomes a preliminary investigation into allegations of crimes against humanity now underway by the International Criminal Court, a spokesman said Thursday, marking an about-face for a man who once described the ICC as "bulls---." Duterte is "sick and tired of being accused" and is prepared to defend himself in court, though the investigation is "a waste of the court's time and resources," spokesman Harry Roque said in announcing the inquiry that is to decide whether there's evidence to build a case, report the New York Times and Guardian. Roque maintained the government crackdown that's left an estimated 12,000 people dead under Duterte's leadership—though police put the number at 4,000—is a "legitimate police operation" to weed out drugs. Duterte swore to "kill" criminals when he took office in June 2016. He has since faced accusations of mass murder while keeping up a "great relationship" with President Trump. It's those allegations that launched the ICC probe, confirmed by a prosecutor, per the Guardian. In a complaint sent to the ICC last April, a Filipino lawyer representing two men who say they were assassins for Duterte called him a "mastermind" of killings spanning the three decades since he became mayor of Davao in 1988. "Hopefully a warrant of arrest will be issued by the ICC against Duterte and his cohorts," 11 of whom are named in the complaint, the lawyer said, per the Times. Arguing the government is attempting a cover-up, Duterte political opponent Gary Alejano says ICC officials "are the only ones who can step in."

final_summary:
On February 28, 2018, two weeks after a mass shooting at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School in Parkland, Florida, students and teachers returned to the school for the first time since the tragedy that claimed the lives of 17 people. As students arrived, they were met with a heavy police presence, with about 50 armed officers stationed around the school. Principal Ty Thomas tweeted that the focus was on emotional readiness and comfort rather than curriculum, advising students to leave their backpacks at home.

The return to school came amidst growing calls for stricter gun control measures. Dick's Sporting Goods announced it would immediately cease selling assault-style rifles in its stores and raise the minimum age for purchasing firearms to 21, citing the failure of current systems to prevent sales to potentially dangerous individuals. The decision was made after the company discovered that the accused shooter, 19-year-old Nikolas Cruz, had purchased a gun at one of their stores, although not the specific firearm used in the attack.

David Hogg, a student who lost friends in the shooting and has become a prominent advocate for gun reform, expressed mixed emotions upon seeing a police officer armed with a rifle outside the school. He described it as a symbol of the "education in fear" prevalent in the country. Hogg has been actively campaigning for an assault weapon ban and has met with lawmakers in Tallahassee to push for legislation.

As students returned to school, many were visibly shaken but determined to move forward. Junior Madison Geller acknowledged her fears but emphasized the importance of getting back into a routine and trying to heal. Senior Angelyse Perez, however, expressed a sense of urgency, stating that she wanted to graduate and leave the traumatic experience behind.

In the days leading up to the return to school, relatives of the victims continued to push for stronger gun control laws, including a proposal to raise the minimum age for buying long guns from 18 to 21. The plan also included allowing trained teachers to carry concealed weapons in the classroom, which the school's superintendent opposed. Despite these challenges, students and community members remained resolute in their efforts to reclaim their school and work towards a safer future.

reference: – Members of the Broward County Police Benevolent Association handed carnations out to students as they entered Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School two weeks after a former student gunned down 17 people inside the freshman building. A long line of cars bringing people back to the school in Parkland, Fla., was guided by police as dozens of television trucks and vehicles camped out nearby. About 50 armed officers were on hand, and only students, parents, and staff were allowed through a security cordon. The main entrance is covered with a sign saying "Welcome Back Eagles." Students were told to leave their backpacks at home. Principal Ty Thomas said in a tweet Tuesday that "our focus is on emotional readiness and comfort not curriculum: so there is no need for backpacks. Come ready to start the healing process and #RECLAIMTHENEST." For Madison Geller, Wednesday offered an opportunity to get back into a routine, in spite of her fears, reports the AP. "When I walk in there, I'm going to replay the whole thing in my head. But we have to come here and try to learn," the high school junior said. "This week we will try to be comfortable and get back into the same routine." Angelyse Perez, a senior, said returning offers a chance for everyone to "get through this and be together. But I'm graduating," she said. "I just want to get out of here."

final_summary:
An internal report at News of the World uncovered'smoking gun' evidence of widespread phone hacking and bribery of police officers four years ago, but the findings were not handed to Scotland Yard until last month. The report was based on 300 emails suggesting that hacking was rampant and journalists were paying police for information. The emails implicated a group of six journalists who acted as 'gatekeepers' to private investigator Glenn Mulcaire, who carried out extensive hacking for the paper. 

Sources stated that the emails showed clear proof of criminal offenses, including payments to police officers totaling around £120,000. The emails also revealed that phone hacking extended beyond the actions of a single rogue reporter, contrary to the News of the World's initial claim. 

In 2007, News International hired Harbottle & Lewis to review emails related to the hacking scandal, but the firm failed to uncover evidence of wrongdoing by other journalists besides Clive Goodman, who was jailed for phone hacking. However, when News International re-examined the emails, they found evidence of broader malpractice and potential police bribery. 

News International's chairman, James Murdoch, has faced criticism for his handling of the scandal, particularly his approval of out-of-court settlements that included gagging clauses. He has acknowledged that he was unaware of the full extent of the hacking at the time and has expressed regret for his decisions. 

Scotland Yard's Assistant Commissioner, John Yates, has apologized for his handling of the initial investigation, stating that he could have done more to address the issue. Yates denied rumors of a relationship with News International CEO Rebekah Brooks, calling them "malicious gossip." 

Rebekah Brooks is set to be questioned under caution in connection with the scandal, along with at least nine former News of the World journalists and three police officers. The scandal has raised concerns about the potential involvement of News Corp in the United States, where the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act could be applied due to alleged payments to police officers. 

The phone-hacking scandal has led to the closure of the News of the World and threatens to derail Rupert Murdoch's bid to acquire full control of BSkyB. Labor leader Ed Miliband has announced plans to delay the takeover until the police investigation is complete.

reference: – Rupert Murdoch's shamed and now shuttered News of the World tried to pay a New York City police officer for phone records on British victims of 9/11, Reuters reports. The police officer, who now works as a private investigator, turned down the offer because it would "look bad," a source told the Daily Mail. Other substantial sums were apparently paid to British cops for information on a number of other news stories, reports the BBC. Emails dated 2007 indicate that then-editor Andy Coulson authorized payments to cops for inside info, according to sources. That email evidence, uncovered by a law office, was not turned over to investigators until last month. Coulson, who has been arrested in connection with the News of the World's burgeoning phone hacking scandal, was subsequently hired to be Prime Minister David Cameron's director of communications—but stepped down early this year.

final_summary:
On August 7, a Jefferson Parish judge ruled that there was probable cause to hold Shantel Parria Wagner on a second-degree murder charge for the shooting death of her husband, Jefferson Parish Sheriff's Office Sgt. Troy Smith. The decision followed a two-day hearing in commissioner's court, during which prosecutors presented evidence contradicting Wagner's claims that her husband had taken his own life. 

According to the investigation, Wagner initially called 911 on Father's Day, reporting that her husband had attempted to commit suicide in their Waggaman home. However, forensic evidence and interviews later revealed that Smith's injuries were not self-inflicted. Wagner was subsequently arrested on July 11.

Prosecutors pointed out inconsistencies in Wagner's statements to investigators, including changes to her account of the events surrounding her husband's death. Assistant District Attorney Kellie Rish stated that Wagner admitted to shooting her husband, citing testimony from a witness who corroborated this claim. Wagner's defense attorneys, Leo Palazzo and Wiley Beevers, maintained that their client had consistently stated that Smith shot himself, pointing to his history of depression, PTSD, and previous threats to take his own life.

The defense also highlighted that Wagner's DNA was not found on the gun used in the shooting, raising questions about how she could have shot her husband without leaving behind any genetic material. Chief Deputy Tim Scanlan, commander of the Sheriff's Office Crime Lab, acknowledged that the absence of Wagner's DNA on the gun was a significant factor in the investigation, but emphasized that other evidence contradicted the possibility of a self-inflicted wound.

Magistrate Commissioner Paul Schneider ultimately agreed with prosecutors that Wagner's inconsistent statements and alleged confession rose to the level of probable cause, reducing her bond from $500,000 to $300,000. As part of her release conditions, Wagner would be required to wear a GPS-enabled ankle monitor and remain confined to her home at all times except for medical appointments.

Sgt. Troy Smith, a 44-year-old instructor at the Sheriff's Office training academy and former member of the New Orleans Police Department, died on June 24, one week after Wagner reported finding him injured in their home. Smith had a history of service in the military and law enforcement, earning recognition as Non-Commissioned Officer of the Year in 2017 for both the Mississippi State Guard and the State Guard Association of the United States.

reference: – Jefferson Parish Sheriff's Office Sgt. Troy Smith is dead. The question is whether it was by his own hand or his wife's. A judge on Tuesday determined there was sufficient probable cause to hold Smith's wife, Shantel Parria Wagner, in connection with his June 24 death. The timeline in the case begins one week prior, on Father's Day, when Wagner called 911 saying her husband had shot himself in the head. Wagner is at times crying and near-hysterical in audio of the call posted by Fox 8. The Times-Picayune reports Smith was hospitalized until his death the following week. Wagner's lawyer says Smith, 44, suffered from depression and PTSD, was dealing with financial strain, and texted a suicide threat the day before he was shot. But investigators say the evidence suggests the fatal gunshot wound Smith suffered wasn't self-inflicted. The autopsy report hasn't been finished, however, so the pathologist who handled the autopsy wouldn't deem it a homicide or suicide on the stand, though the Advocate reports she did say Smith exhibited no gunpowder burns or abrasions. The head of the Sheriff's Office Crime Lab testified the "totality of evidence," as the Times-Picayune puts it, isn't in line with a suicide—though he also acknowledged Wagner's DNA was not present on the gun found at the scene. But the judge said inconsistencies in Wagner's story—WDSU reports prosecutors say she altered her story 16 times—and a witness who alleges Wagner copped to the murder amounted to probable cause. Her bond has been set at $300,000.

final_summary:
A Minnesota judge, Carver County Judge Kevin Eide, has significantly narrowed down the list of potential heirs to the estate of the late music superstar Prince, excluding nearly 30 claimants in the process. This move bolsters the inheritance claims of Prince's sister, Tyka Nelson, and his surviving half-siblings. According to the court order, which was issued on Thursday, the six individuals who will undergo genetic testing to confirm their relationship to Prince are Tyka Nelson, and three of her half-siblings: John, Norrine, and Sharon Nelson. Additionally, two individuals claiming to be Prince's niece and grandniece, Brianna Nelson and Victoria Nelson, will also undergo testing. 

These six individuals claim to be related to Prince through his father, John L. Nelson, who is listed in court records as Prince's father. However, the judge's order does not include two other half-brothers, Alfred Jackson and Omar Baker, who share a common mother with Prince but claim to be related through different fathers. 

In contrast, the judge dismissed claims of heirship from 29 other individuals who had previously sought a share of Prince's estate, which is estimated to be worth over $300 million. These rejected claimants included a woman who claimed to be Prince's secret wife, stating that the CIA had classified their marriage records as top-secret, as well as five individuals who claimed Prince was their biological or adoptive father. Several others claimed that Prince's father was someone other than John L. Nelson, although the judge found no credible evidence to support these claims. 

Prince's estate has been the subject of intense scrutiny since his death in April at the age of 57, reportedly due to a drug overdose. As he had no known children and left no will, the process of determining his heirs and distributing his assets has fallen to the courts. The special administrator of the estate, Bremer Trust, is currently working to determine the fair-market value of Prince's estate.

reference: – There will be no payday for 29 people who claimed to be Prince's heirs, including a woman who said the CIA covered up her marriage to the superstar, a Minnesota judge ruled in an order disclosed Friday. Reuters reports that Carver County Judge Kevin Eide ordered genetic testing for six other claimants: Prince's sister, Tyka Nelson, three half-siblings by his father, and a possible niece and grandniece. Two half-brothers from Prince's mother will not be tested under the judge's ruling, which will determine the future of an estate thought to be worth up to $500 million. Other rejected claimants include at least five people who claimed Prince was their father, and several who claimed that their father had an affair with Prince's mother, making their father Prince's real father and the late star their half-brother, the AP reports. Under Minnesota law, the estate of Prince—who left no known will or surviving offspring—will be split between siblings, half-siblings, and the offspring of any deceased siblings. Despite the order for genetic testing, Eide's ruling says he "is not aware of any objection or dispute" to the six siblings or half-siblings being legitimate heirs. (A DNA test ruled out an inmate in Colorado who claimed he was Prince's son.)

final_summary:
On January 27, 2016, Orange County Sheriff Sandra Hutchens announced that five arrests had been made in connection with the escape of three inmates from the Orange County Central Men's Jail. The escapees, identified as Hossein Nayeri, 37, Jonathan Tieu, 20, and Bac Duong, 43, were awaiting trial for various violent crimes, including kidnapping, torture, murder, and attempted murder. According to Hutchens, the inmates escaped sometime after the 5 a.m. head count on Friday, using tools to cut through 1/2-inch-thick steel bars and plumbing tunnels to reach the unsecured roof, where they rappelled to the ground using sheets and fled on foot.

The investigation revealed that the inmates had received help from outside the jail, with Hutchens stating that Nayeri, who faces charges related to a 2012 kidnap and torture case, was believed to be the mastermind behind the escape. Nayeri's military background and sophistication led investigators to suspect that he orchestrated the elaborate escape plan. Tieu, a documented gang member, and Duong, another documented gang member, were also involved in the escape.

Hutchens acknowledged that the escape was a major failure for the Orange County Sheriff's Department, stating that it was "every sheriff's nightmare." The incident highlighted concerns about the outdated technology and infrastructure of the Central Men's Jail, which was built in 1968 and has experienced 16 escapes over the years. The sheriff emphasized that the jail's design and maintenance were inadequate, particularly in the dormitory-style module where the escape began.

An investigation into the escape found that the inmates had exploited weaknesses in the jail's head count policy, which allowed them to go unnoticed for 16 hours. As a result, the department implemented changes to the head count procedure, requiring deputies to conduct more thorough checks to confirm inmate movements during the day. Hutchens also stated that the department would review its policies and procedures to prevent similar incidents in the future.

The five individuals arrested in connection with the escape were suspected of being part of a larger group that assisted the inmates. However, their identities were not disclosed to avoid compromising the investigation. A $200,000 reward was offered for information leading to the capture of the escapees, who were described as armed and dangerous. Authorities urged the public to contact the Orange County Sheriff's Department hotline or Crime Stoppers if they had any information about the inmates' whereabouts.

reference: – Police have arrested five suspected gang members in connection with the escape of three prisoners last week from the Central Men's Jail in Santa Ana, Calif. The fugitives, however, remain at large. When announcing the arrests Wednesday, Sheriff Sandra Hutchens did not identify the men or say what charges they face, the Los Angeles Times reports. She did say, according to the Orange County Register, that more arrests were expected overnight and into Thursday, adding that there's a possibility guards or other jail employees may have provided "inside help" to the escapees. Since the Friday escape, deputies have served some 30 warrants, ABC7 reports. A $200,000 reward has been offered for information leading to the escapees. More from Hutchens' press conference: Those arrested in connection to the escape were affiliated with the same street gang as 20-year-old fugitive Jonathan Tieu, knew the escaped inmates, or were arrested on probation violations. Hutchens believes Hossein Nayeri, 37, was the "mastermind" of the escape. Authorities have not recovered the tools used, and Hutchens believes the escapees would have needed help getting their hands on them. ("It's not something we think could have occurred with a jail-made shank, if you will.") "It's every sheriff's nightmare," Hutchens said of the escape, which has sparked criticism over how inmates are housed at the nearly 50-year-old jail. Nayeri, Tieu, and Bac Duong, 43, were not noticed missing for some 16 hours. Hutchens says she has made changes to the way inmates are counted throughout the day, per the Register. She also defended the decision to house the escapees in a dormitory instead of individual cells, the Times reports. "We house based on behavior, and there were no issues … while they were in our custody," she says.

final_summary:
Deven Davis, the estranged wife of Korn lead singer Jonathan Davis, has died at the age of 39. The news comes after a tumultuous period in their marriage, marked by allegations of domestic violence and severe struggles with substance abuse. In August 2018, Jonathan filed for a domestic violence restraining order against Deven, claiming she had a long history of addiction to prescription and illicit narcotics, dating back over 20 years. He alleged that her substance abuse had caused emotional harm to their two children, Pirate and Zeppelin, born in 2005 and 2007 respectively, as well as their family dog, Chaos.

According to court documents obtained by TMZ, Jonathan accused Deven of being constantly under the influence of nitrous oxide, cocaine, and Norco, and that she had previously checked herself into rehab six times without success. He also claimed that she had a drug dealer boyfriend who would often stay at their home, and that she would frequently bring home strangers, including fans of his music, who would enter their master bedroom and try on his clothes.

Jonathan and Deven were married in 2004, after meeting in Hawaii, where they exchanged vows. However, their relationship began to deteriorate, and Jonathan filed for divorce in October 2016, citing irreconcilable differences. Despite efforts to address her addiction, Deven reportedly relapsed just days before her death, leaving behind a trail of concern among her loved ones.

In a statement released to PEOPLE, the Davis family expressed their grief and asked for privacy during this difficult time. The cause of Deven's death remains unknown, although her struggles with sobriety and substance abuse were well-documented. As the family mourns her passing, they urge anyone struggling with addiction to seek help, providing resources such as the SAMHSA substance abuse helpline (1-800-662-HELP).

"The Davis family is brokenhearted over the devastating loss of Deven Davis," says the family in a statement. "We ask that you respect their privacy." The cause of death is unknown, but TMZ reports that 39-year-old Deven had lifelong substance abuse problems and had gone to rehab six times. Jonathan, who had two children with the former porn star, doesn't hold back in his court declaration: Deven "is constantly under the influence of ... nitrous oxide, cocaine and Norco," the document reads. Jonathan adds that when she got high, she would "routinely bring home unsavory characters, including strangers. Some of these people are random fans of my music who she allows to come into the house, go into the master bedroom closet and try on my clothes." He also accuses her of having a drug-dealer boyfriend who was recently unconscious on her sofa when Jonathan brought over the kids. Jonathan, 47, had filed for divorce in 2016 and the pair remained estranged. Deven apparently went missing from a sober home last week and hadn't been in contact with Jonathan since. Sources tell TMZ she never even saw the restraining order.

final_summary:
In the vast expanse of the Namibian desert, a 500-year-old maritime mystery has finally been unraveled. The discovery of the shipwreck of the Bom Jesus, a Portuguese vessel that vanished in 1533 while en route to India, has sent shockwaves through the archaeological community. The ship, which was carrying a substantial treasure, including gold coins, copper ingots, and ivory, was unearthed by a group of miners in April 2008 while they were searching for diamonds in the Sperrgebiet, a 10,000-square-mile area controlled by the joint operation of DeBeers and the Namibian government.

According to Dieter Noli, the chief archaeologist of the southern Africa Institute of Maritime Archaeological Research, the discovery was made possible by the unique conditions of the Sperrgebiet, which has been heavily restricted due to mining operations for over a century. This limited human activity has helped preserve the site, allowing the shipwreck to remain relatively intact for centuries. Additionally, the presence of large quantities of copper on board the ship is believed to have prevented microorganisms from consuming the wooden structures, further contributing to the preservation of the wreck.

The ship's cargo, which includes 44,000 pounds of copper ingots, 2,000 mint-condition gold coins from Portugal and Spain, and over 50 elephant tusks, matches the description of the Bom Jesus as listed in the 16th-century book "Memorias Das Armadas." The discovery of the ship's navigational tools, such as compasses and astrolabes, suggests that it made at least one stop before attempting to enter the Indian Ocean. The presence of a 500-year-old musket, which was found in remarkably good condition, is another significant find, providing insight into the technology used during the Age of Exploration.

A week into the excavation, the team discovered a treasure chest containing gold coins, which dated between 1525 and 1538, confirming that the ship had indeed set sail during that period. The coins were in mint condition, likely due to the ship breaking on rocks, tipping over, and the chest being buried under the superstructure. This discovery not only solved one of the biggest maritime mysteries but also marked the oldest shipwreck ever found in sub-Saharan Africa.

The excavation yielded thousands of other artifacts, including bronze cannons, tonnes of copper ingots, pewter tableware, copper cooking utensils, swords, and muskets. Over 5,400 artifacts of cultural, scientific, and intrinsic value were recovered, providing a unique glimpse into the lives of sailors and traders during the 16th century. While plans have been proposed to open the site as a museum, the remains of the shipwreck remain protected by mining security, with limited access granted to the public.

The discovery of the Bom Jesus shipwreck has sparked excitement within the archaeological community, offering a rare opportunity to study a ship from the early days of European exploration. The find serves as a testament to the importance of preserving historical sites and the significance of the Sperrgebiet, a region that has been shrouded in secrecy for centuries.

reference: – Miners have been pulling diamonds from a vast area of Namibia's Namib Desert called the Sperrgebiet (or "forbidden territory") for more than a century. But in 2008, workers hunting for diamonds where the desert meets the Atlantic Ocean found something even more precious: the likely remains of the Bom Jesus, a Portuguese sailing ship that disappeared some 500 years ago on its way to India. It's a discovery, per recent coverage by News.com.au, that solved "one of the biggest maritime mysteries." Also, says archaeologist Dieter Noli, it's the oldest shipwreck ever found in sub-Saharan Africa. And there's treasure as well: Included in the find were some 2,000 gold coins from Spain and Portugal, according to Gainesville News. Upon unearthing the site of the shipwreck, per CNN, workers found pieces of metal, wood, and what looked like pipes. Not sure what they had found, they called in Noli. "It just looked like a disturbed beach," he told CNN earlier this month, "but lying on it were bits and pieces." Among those were a centuries-old musket and an elephant tusk. "I thought, 'Oh, no no, this is definitely a shipwreck,'" Noli says. Ultimately, in addition to the gold, workers recovered cannons (the pipes), navigational instruments, tons of copper ingots, swords, and a lot more—some 5,438 artifacts. Not much of the actual structure of the ship remains. But archaeologists have matched the cargo with that of the Bom Jesus (which means the "Good Jesus") based on details found in a 16th-century book that lists the ship as lost. The stretch of coastline is known for storms. Speaking to CNN, Noli speculates that the Bom Jesus "came in, it hit a rock, and it leaned over." (A famed shipwreck closer to home is getting a closer look.)

final_summary:
On November 5th, voters in six US states cast ballots on a total of 31 measures, with some notable outcomes emerging in Colorado, Washington, and New Jersey. In Colorado, voters overwhelmingly supported a measure to tax marijuana, with 65% voting in favor and 35% opposed. The new tax, which includes a 15% excise tax and a 10% sales tax, aims to generate nearly $70 million in additional state revenue next year, primarily for school construction and marijuana-related law enforcement efforts. 

In another Colorado measure, voters in 11 counties considered seceding from the state to form a new entity called North Colorado. However, preliminary results showed that the majority of voters in the most populous county, Weld, opposed secession, with 58% voting against it. Although the secession plan has no chance of becoming a reality due to the need for statewide and congressional approval, it reflects growing conservative discontent with the state's liberal policies.

Voters in Washington state weighed in on two contentious initiatives. One measure aimed to require labeling of genetically modified (GMO) foods, but with over 800,000 votes counted, 53% opposed the proposal, while 47% supported it. The measure pits local consumer advocates against large agribusinesses like Monsanto, which argues that the labeling would unfairly imply that GMO products are unsafe. The GMO labeling debate has gained traction nationwide, with 95 bills introduced in various state legislatures this year alone.

Another Washington initiative focused on raising the state's minimum wage to $8.25 per hour, indexed to inflation. While the outcome remains uncertain, the measure has garnered significant attention due to the influx of outside funds, particularly from California billionaires and national environmental groups. Critics argue that this outside spending threatens the integrity of the initiative process and representative democracy.

New Jersey voters considered amending their constitution to increase the state's minimum wage from $7.25 to $8.25 per hour, with future annual increases tied to the cost of living. This move aligns with 18 other states that already exceed the federal minimum wage requirement. Additionally, New York voters were asked to approve a constitutional revision allowing the state legislature to authorize up to seven casinos, aiming to generate revenue for public schools and reduce property taxes.

The influx of outside funds in these elections has sparked concerns among experts and locals. Campaign finance records show that out-of-state donors have significantly contributed to both sides of the GMO labeling and minimum wage debates in Washington state. The Grocery Manufacturers Association, a Washington D.C.-based food industry group, donated $11 million to combat the GMO labeling initiative, while Monsanto contributed over $5 million. Proponents of the GMO labeling measure received $5.8 million from outside Washington, highlighting the complex web of national interests influencing local elections.

reference: – Could "North Colorado" soon become the 51st state? Voters in 11 northern Colorado counties will get to weigh in tomorrow on whether or not they'd like to secede from the state, CNN reports. Ten of the conservative-leaning counties would become North Colorado, while the other would become a panhandle for Wyoming. Of course, CNN thinks there's zero chance this actually happens, because the entire state and congress would have to vote to approve the move. But it still stands as one of the more intriguing ballot measures up for vote tomorrow. Others include: Colorado is also voting on whether or not to impose a 15% excise tax on now-legal marijuana sales, which would go to fund school construction, and an additional 10% sales tax to fund marijuana enforcement laws. Washington state has a measure that would require special labeling for genetically-modified food—a proposal that met with ardent opposition and eventual defeat in California last year. Sure enough, out-of-state opponents have poured money into this race as well, the Seattle Times reports; of the whopping $22 million raised in opposition, $550 has come from Washingtonians. New Jersey will consider a constitutional amendment to raise the state's minimum wage to $8.25 an hour. New Yorkers are considering whether or not to allow the construction of seven casinos, with the proceeds going towards education and/or cutting property taxes. New Jersey is also considering a gambling bill of sorts, the Star-Ledger reports, allowing veterans' groups to raise money via Bingo games, raffles, and similar games.

final_summary:
Kayla Mueller, a 26-year-old American aid worker, was abducted by the Islamic State (IS) in Aleppo, Syria on August 4, 2013, while working with Doctors Without Borders. Her family initially kept her disappearance private due to concerns it would put her life in greater danger. Over the next two years, Mueller's family maintained secret communication with IS, receiving at least three photographs of her corpse, which showed her wearing a black hijab and a white burial shroud, and bearing facial bruises. 

In a letter written from her cell last year, Mueller described herself as being in a "safe location, completely unharmed and healthy," and stated she was being treated with "utmost respect and kindness." However, US officials believed she was given to an IS commander and possibly married to him, based on intelligence indicating her presence alongside a tracked IS leader. 

Despite efforts to rescue her, Mueller remained in captivity until her death. The US attempted to rescue her in a July 4 raid on an IS prison in Raqqa, but she was not present. In a separate operation in summer 2014, US commandos raided an oil refinery facility in northern Syria, arriving a day or two after Mueller and other hostages had been moved. 

Mueller's family received confirmation of her death from IS via a private message containing photographic evidence, which showed her deceased body with trauma injuries inconsistent with those typically sustained in airstrikes. The US government acknowledged Mueller's death but disputed IS's claim that she was killed in a coalition airstrike. 

President Barack Obama praised Mueller as an "outstanding young woman and a great spirit," stating that the US had devoted "enormous" resources to freeing her. Mueller's family released a statement expressing their grief, citing letters she wrote to them, including one in which she expressed her desire to use her life to relieve suffering. Mueller's humanitarian work spanned various countries, including India, Israel, and the Palestinian territories, before she was drawn to Syria to assist refugees.
