reference: – President Obama says his reaction to the death of ISIS hostage Kayla Mueller is "heartbreak"—but he thinks her "great spirit" will live on. "The more people learn about her, the more they appreciate what she stood for—and how it stands in contrast with the barbaric organization that held her captive," he tells BuzzFeed in an interview yesterday. He stresses the government did all it could to free her, including deploying an entire operation "at significant risk" to free her and other US hostages. The government did, however, stick to its policy of not paying ransoms to organizations like ISIS; ABC News reports ISIS demanded $6.6 million for her release, and had set an August 2014 deadline for payment. More emerging details: Although the government says it has been unable to confirm the cause of Mueller's death, per the New York Times, ISIS sent Mueller's family three photos of her body, according to two people briefed on the family's correspondence with ISIS. Two photos reportedly showed Mueller in a black Muslim hijab that partly covered her face; the third showed her in a traditional white burial shroud. And while the Times sources said they saw bruises on Mueller's face in the photos, there was no evidence of "puffiness or other concussive effects associated with a bomb blast," though they acknowledge she could have been located in a nearby building or injured by flying debris. The rescue operation referenced by Obama took place July 4 in a predawn raid on a Raqqa prison by Delta Force commandos, the Los Angeles Times reports. But the two dozen soldiers were too late: The hostages—who also included James Foley and Steven Sotloff—had apparently been moved a day or two before. "We never stopped trying to get her," a defense official not authorized to speak publicly tells the Times. "We never lost that focus." Officials yesterday told ABC News that Mueller may have been "given over" to an ISIS commander, and that forced marriage could have even been involved. "ISIS didn't see her as a hostage or a bargaining chip," said one. Devastated friends and relatives in Mueller's hometown of Prescott, Ariz., say they hope her death will raise awareness of the plight of the Syrian refugees she worked with, NBC reports. "The things that were important to Kayla are finally getting the attention they deserve," an aunt said. "The world grieves with us."

final_summary:
As Pope Benedict XVI prepares to retire on February 28, the Vatican is grappling with the unprecedented question of what role he will play in the future. This marks the first time in nearly six centuries that a living former pope will reside within the Vatican walls, raising concerns about potential interference with his successor's work. Despite these worries, Vatican officials insist that Pope Benedict has no intention of influencing the election of his successor, which will take place in a secret conclave starting between March 15 and 20.

However, the former pontiff's continued presence in the Vatican has sparked debate about his title, attire, and level of involvement in the Church. Analysts suggest that he should relinquish his papal regalia and adopt the robes of a cardinal, referring to himself as Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger. This proposal aims to prevent confusion and potential conflict with his successor.

Benedict's impending departure comes amidst a backdrop of controversy and division within the Church. His papacy has been marred by scandals, including the sexual abuse of children by priests, criticism of Islam, and the rehabilitation of a Holocaust denier. These issues have strained relationships with various faith communities and raised questions about the Church's leadership.

Despite these challenges, Pope Benedict remains committed to a life of prayer and contemplation. In a recent address to priests, he expressed his desire to withdraw from public life and focus on spiritual pursuits. However, his exact role and responsibilities post-resignation remain unclear, leaving the Vatican to navigate uncharted territory.

The selection process for his successor is underway, with 117 cardinals eligible to participate in the conclave. Informal consultations have begun, with a focus on identifying a candidate who can address ongoing crises and promote unity within the Church. While it is unlikely that the next pope will alter core teachings, there is a growing sentiment that a more inclusive and listening leader is needed.

Ultimately, the legacy of Pope Benedict XVI will be shaped by his actions following his resignation. As he transitions to a life of quiet devotion, the Vatican must balance the need for continuity with the demands of change, ensuring a smooth transition and preserving the integrity of the Church.

reference: – What do you do with an ex-pope? If you happen to know, call Rome, because the Church isn't sure yet. Many fear Benedict will become an implicit rival to the new pope, despite his apparent desire to keep a low profile. He told Roman diocese priests yesterday that he would be "withdrawing into prayer," Reuters reports. "I will always be close to all of you," he said, "even if I remain hidden from the world." The "close to you" bit is somewhat literal; Benedict has announced he'll be living in the Vatican. That's caused some controversy, the Wall Street Journal reports, but one senior papal adviser says it's "better to have him here than somewhere else, where he could become another center of power." Benedict is likely to exert influence over the next pope by picking a new head of the embattled Vatican Bank, and he'll keep writing as a respected theologian. The Church also hasn't decided what vestments he'll wear, whether he'll give audiences, or even what to address him as—a decision that has "juridical" ramifications, a spokesman said. One thing's for sure: He won't be infallible anymore.

final_summary:
Ted Cruz's victory in Colorado's Republican convention has sparked controversy, with the Drudge Report labeling it a "corrupt power grab." The site, known for its influence on conservative media, has published numerous articles questioning the legitimacy of Cruz's win, claiming that the process was unfair and that many Republicans were disenfranchised. However, Cruz has fired back, accusing the Drudge Report of becoming an "attack site" for the Donald Trump campaign, stating that the site has stopped covering news and instead focuses on attacking him daily. 

Cruz claimed that the Drudge Report has become biased against him, pointing out that when he wins a state, the site fails to report on it, citing the example of Colorado where he won all 34 delegates without any mention on the site. Drudge responded by linking to a previous interview where Cruz praised the Drudge Report for its role in exposing liberal excesses.

Meanwhile, Trump has also complained about the Colorado convention, claiming that it was a "crooked deal" and that he was unfairly treated. However, an analysis of the process reveals that Trump's campaign failed to organize effectively, with errors on delegate guides and ballots, and a lack of a website to aid supporters. In contrast, Cruz's campaign was well-prepared and utilized a website to assist supporters.

The controversy surrounding the Colorado convention highlights the increasingly contentious nature of the Republican primary, with both Cruz and Trump engaging in a war of words over the legitimacy of the process. The Drudge Report's biased reporting has contributed to the tension, with some accusing the site of promoting Trump's interests above all else.

In related news, Trump has been criticized for his charity work, with an analysis revealing that 2,900 of his reported charitable contributions were actually free rounds of golf at his golf courses. Trump's response to the criticism has been to claim that the Colorado convention was rigged against him, further fueling the controversy.

The situation has also drawn attention to the importance of understanding the rules of the contest, with Trump seemingly unable to grasp the basics of the caucus process. His failure to adapt to the rules has led to his losses in several states, including Colorado, Utah, and Kansas.

Overall, the controversy surrounding the Colorado convention reflects the intense competition and polarization within the Republican Party, with both Cruz and Trump engaged in a fierce battle for the nomination. The role of the Drudge Report in perpetuating this conflict remains a topic of debate, with some accusing the site of promoting Trump's interests and others defending its right to report on the controversy.

reference: – Matt Drudge and his Drudge Report have become a big focus of the Republican race this week. Things really took off Monday when Ted Cruz told a conservative radio host that Drudge's website "has basically become the attack site for the Trump campaign," reports BuzzFeed. Cruz took exception to several headlines on the site suggesting that he had won in Colorado over the weekend thanks only to insider politics by party leaders bent on defeating Donald Trump. "And most days, they have six-month-old article that is some attack on me, and it’s whatever the Trump campaign is pushing that day will be the banner headline on Drudge," said Cruz. He added that "they no longer cover news." As the Hill reports, Drudge has responded by linking to a January story in which Cruz sounded pleased with the site: "We have got the Internet, we have got the Drudge Report," he said at the time. Drudge also linked to an analysis in the Washington Post headlined, "Ted Cruz’s war with Matt Drudge could become a huge problem for his campaign." In that piece, James Hohmann writes that a "word cloud" from social media shows that Drudge is making an impact, with the words "cheating" and "drudge" showing up among mentions of Cruz's Colorado win. This should worry Cruz because it could make his victories seem illegitimate to conservatives. "If Cruz wins the nomination at a contested convention in Cleveland, he will need these grass-roots activists to rally around him," writes Hohmann. "If regular Drudge readers believe he did not win fair and square, they will be less inclined to do so." A blogger at the American Spectator, meanwhile, dismisses the "reprehensible" coverage on Drudge as "cheap tabloid tricks." Trump is looking for a "distraction" from the reality that he was beaten soundly in Colorado, where the rules were clear, writes Ross Kaminsky.

final_summary:
On October 6, 2013, 19-year-old Jamal Harmon was killed in a South Side alley after an argument over a dice game at a party hosted by his killer's nephew. Derrick A. Hillard, 37, was initially charged with first-degree murder and unlawful use of a weapon by a felon in connection with Harmon's death. However, a second man, Andre Davis, 52, was later charged with murder and kidnapping in relation to the crime.

Davis had previously served 32 years in prison for the 1980 rape and murder of 3-year-old Brianna Stickel in Rantoul, Illinois. He was exonerated by DNA evidence in 2012 and released from prison in July of that year. Prosecutors chose not to retry him due to the age of the case.

According to investigators, Harmon had been at the party hosted by Davis' nephew when he got into an argument with the nephew over money lost at a dice game. The nephew allegedly retrieved a handgun from the house and shot Harmon, who was still alive after being shot. Davis and another person then carried Harmon to the trunk of a Cadillac, with Davis stating he would find a location to dispose of the body. Harmon's body was later discovered in an alley, suffering from multiple gunshot wounds and stab injuries.

An autopsy revealed that Harmon died from multiple stab and gunshot wounds, and his death was ruled a homicide. Davis allegedly confessed to cutting Harmon's throat the following day. This is not the first time Davis has been charged with serious crimes; he has also been charged with attempted murder, aggravated discharge of a weapon, and two counts of aggravated battery, in addition to two current misdemeanor counts of DUI.

The case bears striking similarities to Davis' previous conviction, which was also based on circumstantial evidence and questionable witness testimony. In the 1980 case, Davis was accused of raping and murdering 3-year-old Brianna Stickel, but DNA evidence ultimately cleared his name. The similarity in circumstances has raised questions about whether Davis may have been wrongly accused again.

Davis had previously served 32 years in prison for the 1980 rape and murder of 3-year-old Brianna Stickel in Rantoul, Illinois. He was exonerated by DNA evidence in 2012 and released from prison in July of that year. Prosecutors chose not to retry him due to the age of the case.

According to investigators, Harmon had been at the party hosted by Davis' nephew when he got into an argument with the nephew over money lost at a dice game. The nephew allegedly retrieved a handgun from the house and shot Harmon, who was still alive after being shot. Davis and another person then carried Harmon to the trunk of a Cadillac, with Davis stating he would find a location to dispose of the body. Harmon's body was later discovered in an alley, suffering from multiple gunshot wounds and stab injuries.

An autopsy revealed that Harmon died from multiple stab and gunshot wounds, and his death was ruled a homicide. Davis allegedly confessed to cutting Harmon's throat the following day. This is not the first time Davis has been charged with serious crimes; he has also been charged with attempted murder, aggravated discharge of a weapon, and two counts of aggravated battery, in addition to two current misdemeanor counts of DUI.

The case bears striking similarities to Davis' previous conviction, which was also based on circumstantial evidence and questionable witness testimony. In the 1980 case, Davis was accused of raping and murdering 3-year-old Brianna Stickel, but DNA evidence ultimately cleared his name. The similarity in circumstances has raised questions about whether Davis may have been wrongly accused again.

reference: – Andre Davis served 32 years in prison for a murder he didn't commit. Now he might be going back for one that prosecutors say he did. As NBC Chicago explains, Davis served his lengthy prison sentence after being convicted of the 1980 rape and murder of a young girl, but he was released in 2012 after DNA evidence overturned the conviction. He had been a free man since—until this week, when he got arrested and charged with the kidnapping and murder of a 19-year-old, reports the Sun-Times. Police say the victim got into a fight with Davis' nephew during a house party, and the nephew shot him. According to prosecutors, witnesses say the victim was still alive when Davis put the teen into his trunk and said he would find a place to dump the body, reports the Chicago Tribune. He told another witness the next day that he cut the victim's throat, say prosecutors. Both he and his nephew have been charged, and Davis is once again behind bars awaiting trial. For an in-depth look at the original case, see the Chicago Reader. (Click to read about another man who spent three decades on death row before his exoneration.)

final_summary:
North Korea issued an air raid warning on Thursday, citing a radio broadcast monitored in Seoul, according to South Korea's Unification Ministry. The warning, which was issued at 0032 GMT, instructed military units to stand ready, but it appeared to be part of a defense drill under an air raid scenario, according to Yonhap news agency. However, the exact nature of the warning remains unclear, and an official at the Unification Ministry could not confirm whether it was indeed a drill.

This development comes amidst heightened tensions between North and South Korea, following the latter's annual joint military exercises with the US, which began in early April and will run until the end of the month. The exercises involve the deployment of B-52 bombers, which North Korea views as a provocation. In response, the North Korean military issued a statement warning that US bases in Guam and Japan are within its "striking range."

Pyongyang's statement followed the US announcement that its B-52 bombers were making flights over South Korea as part of the annual military exercises. Analysts suggest that the North's rhetoric is largely driven by domestic concerns, aimed at propping up the regime of leader Kim Jong-un. The UN sanctions imposed in response to North Korea's latest nuclear test have further fueled tensions, with the regime viewing them as "nuclear blackmail and threat."

Tensions have been escalating since North Korea's latest underground nuclear test last month, which prompted the UN Security Council to impose tougher sanctions on the country. The sanctions have enraged the North, leading to increased threats, including suggestions of a potential pre-emptive nuclear strike on the US and South Korea. While experts believe Pyongyang is still far from being able to carry out such an attack on the US mainland, its conventional weapons do appear to be capable of targeting US bases in Japan and Guam.

According to Professor Lee Jung-hoon of Yonsei University in Seoul, the North's current stance reflects a significant shift from the past, with the regime now openly declaring its nuclear ambitions. This change necessitates a revised strategy from the US and other global powers to address the evolving situation. The US has responded by announcing plans to deploy additional ground-based missile interceptors on the West Coast, aimed at enhancing national defense capabilities against potential threats.

Said the country's military: "The US should not forget that the Andersen Air Force Base on Guam where the B-52s take off and naval bases in Japan ... where nuclear-powered submarines are launched are within the striking range of (North Korea's) precision strike means." Pyongyang said it would "take corresponding military actions" over US "nuclear blackmail and threat." The bases do seem to be within the North's conventional-weapons range, CNN notes. Meanwhile, the North announced an air raid warning today, calling on military units to be ready, according to South Korea—but the warning was apparently linked to a defense drill, Reuters reports.

final_summary:
President Donald Trump expressed skepticism towards the recent United Nations Security Council's unanimous vote to impose additional sanctions on North Korea, describing it as "just another very small step, not a big deal." Despite the 15-0 vote, Trump stated that the sanctions may not have a significant impact and hinted that more drastic measures might be needed to curb North Korea's rapidly advancing nuclear program. 

State Department spokeswoman Heather Nauert countered that the sanctions represent the strongest set yet imposed on North Korea, emphasizing that the international community is not yet at its limit in terms of economic pressure. However, North Korea's Foreign Ministry vehemently condemned the sanctions, labeling them a "heinous provocation" aimed at depriving the country of its right to self-defense.

Meanwhile, US lawmakers expressed frustration that the US is not doing enough to pressure China, North Korea's closest ally and largest trading partner, to halt the country's nuclear ambitions. Representatives Brad Sherman and Ed Royce, from both parties, urged the administration to impose stricter sanctions on Chinese banks that engage in transactions with North Korea. 

US officials acknowledged that China and Russia are not fully complying with existing sanctions, allowing North Korea to continue conducting illicit activities through its banking system and front companies. Assistant Secretary Marshall Billingslea testified that the US is working to track North Korean trade in banned goods and will take action even if China fails to cooperate.

In response to growing concerns over North Korea's nuclear capabilities, the US has hinted at implementing further measures, including potentially cutting off trade with China. Treasury Secretary Steven Mnuchin emphasized that the US has sent a message to countries willing to trade with North Korea that they risk facing economic sanctions.

South Korea, meanwhile, is taking a more proactive stance in addressing the North Korean threat. The country's defense minister, Song Young-moo, announced plans to establish a special forces unit, dubbed the Spartan 3000, dedicated to carrying out nighttime raids in North Korea, potentially targeting the leadership, particularly Kim Jong-un. This move is part of a broader strategy to prepare for a potential conflict, including increasing the payload capacity of its missiles and deploying additional military equipment from the US.

South Korean President Moon Jae-in has emphasized the need for his country to take the initiative in resolving issues related to the Korean Peninsula, citing concerns over North Korea's military capabilities and the potential consequences of a war. With Seoul located mere miles from the inter-Korean border, the city is vulnerable to a North Korean attack, which could result in significant casualties.

reference: – South Korea has taken the unusual step of publicly speaking about plans to assassinate Kim Jong Un and other North Korean leaders in the event of conflict. After North Korea's latest nuclear test, South Korean Defense Minister Song Young Moo told lawmakers that he was speeding up the creation of a "decapitation unit" capable of crossing the border for nighttime missions against North Korean facilities and its "wartime command," the New York Times reports. Officials say the special forces unit, which will apparently be comprised of members of the "Spartan 3000" unit, will be capable of striking anywhere in the Korean Peninsula within 24 hours. Song has said he wants to have the unit ready by the end of the year. The creation of the unit is part of South Korea's "Massive Punishment and Retaliation" plan for possible war with the North, Vox reports. Analysts say that by disclosing facts about the "decapitation unit," Seoul appears to want to make Pyongyang nervous enough to think twice about its nuclear program and return to the negotiating table. "The best deterrence we can have, next to having our own nukes, is to make Kim Jong Un fear for his life," says retired Gen. Shin Won Sik, formerly the South Korean military's chief strategist. President Trump, meanwhile, said Tuesday that tough new sanctions are "no big deal" compared to "what ultimately will have to happen" to North Korea, CNN reports.

final_summary:
On September 22-23, 2015, a Chicago-based television station, WGN-TV, faced widespread criticism and backlash after airing a segment about Yom Kippur, the holiest day in the Jewish calendar, accompanied by a graphic containing a Nazi-era symbol. The symbol, a Star of David badge with the German word "Jude" (meaning "Jew") emblazoned on it, was displayed on a uniform-style background reminiscent of those worn by prisoners in concentration camps. This image was visible over the shoulder of anchor Tom Negovan while he delivered a 20-second description of the holiday.

The mistake was first brought to light by Marc Karlinsky, an editor at Chicago Lawyer magazine, who took to Twitter to express his shock and outrage at the use of such an image. His tweet quickly went viral, sparking a social media storm and prompting the station to issue a formal apology.

In response to the controversy, WGN-TV's General Manager Greg Easterly and News Director Jennifer Lyons released a joint statement acknowledging the error and expressing deep regret. They explained that the image had come from the station's image bank and that they had failed to recognize its significance as a Nazi symbol. The statement emphasized that ignorance was no excuse and promised that steps would be taken to prevent similar mistakes in the future.

Additionally, anchor Tom Negovan issued a major correction on air, stating that the station had mistakenly used a symbol associated with Nazi Germany to identify Jews. He acknowledged that the anchors themselves were unaware of the symbol's presence during the initial broadcast.

The incident sparked widespread condemnation, with many viewing the mistake as a grave offense against the Jewish community. WGN-TV's apology was met with a mix of acceptance and skepticism, with some calling for greater sensitivity and awareness among journalists and media outlets.

reference: – For a news report last night commemorating Yom Kippur, a Chicago TV station selected a Star of David as accompanying artwork—except someone picked the absolute worst one possible, USA Today reports. As the anchor talked about the holiday during the broadcast, a graphic appeared of a yellow star with the word "Jude" ("Jew" in German) against a blue-and-white-striped background, which resembled the badge Nazis used to separate Jewish people from the rest of society, the New York Times notes. About half an hour later came the station apology on Twitter: "We are truly sorry for inadvertently using an offensive image in our story. We apologize and deeply regret the error." The station's "extremely embarrassed" GM and news director also posted a statement online noting they "failed to recognize that the artwork we chose to accompany the story contained an offensive symbol" and that they'll be reviewing the graphics-selection process. "Ignorance is not an excuse," they wrote. (Hallmark had to yank "swastika" Hanukkah wrapping paper last year.)

final_summary:
In a bizarre phenomenon observed in Templewo, Poland, a massive colony of wood ants has taken up residence in an abandoned Soviet-era nuclear arms base, situated beneath a 10-inch-high mound of earth. The ants, part of the species Formica polyctena, have been living in the bunker since the 1960s, when the site was operational, and continued to thrive even after the facility ceased operations in 1992. Unbeknownst to the ants, the bunker's ventilation system, which once brought fresh air into the facility, eventually collapsed, creating a gaping hole that funnels thousands of worker ants into the dark, cold environment each year.

Researchers, led by Wojciech Czechowski of the Polish Academy of Sciences, first discovered the colony in 2013 while investigating a nearby infestation of bats. As they crawled through the cramped space, they encountered an astonishing sight: a thriving community of nearly a million worker ants, all female, living in a state of perpetual near-starvation. Despite the harsh conditions, characterized by constant cold temperatures and scarce food resources, the ants have adapted to their surroundings, constructing a complex network of tunnels and chambers within the bunker.

The ants' existence is marked by a peculiar cycle: each year, thousands of worker ants fall down the ventilation pipe, never to return to the surface. These newcomers sustain the colony, replacing the ants that perish due to starvation, disease, or old age. The researchers have estimated that the colony's population remains stable, with potentially up to a million ants calling the bunker home. However, despite their resilience, the ants appear unable to reproduce, producing neither queens nor males, and thus perpetuating their own extinction.

A grim aspect of the ants' existence is the sheer scale of mortality within the colony. The researchers have documented an "ant cemetery" comprising millions of dead ants, with estimates suggesting that up to 2 million bodies may be buried around the nest mound. This macabre landscape supports a miniature ecosystem, with mites and other invertebrates feeding on the deceased ants. The ants' primary source of sustenance remains unknown, with theories ranging from cannibalism to the consumption of nutrients from bat guano.

While the colony's fate seems sealed, the possibility of a queen ant entering the bunker and initiating a reproductive cycle remains a glimmer of hope. However, the absence of a reliable food supply makes it unlikely that the ants could support a new generation. Consequently, the colony's survival hinges on the continuous influx of new ants from the surface, perpetuating a cycle of life and death in the desolate, subterranean world beneath Templewo.

reference: – Call it the road to nowhere. Scores of wood ants living near an old nuclear weapons bunker in western Poland plunge seemingly to death each year—and their descendants follow them—in a bizarre ritual that has amazed scientists. The story begins with hard-working ants that built a colony atop a defunct Soviet-era bunker, reports ArsTechnica. When metal covering a ventilation pipe rusted way, the ants began tumbling down. What sounds like a death sentence (they can’t climb out, there is no food, and it is very cold) has turned into a survival story because these worker ants keep doing what they always do: building until they die. And when they do? "A younger generation is on its way, ready to cart off the fallen to a two-million-strong ant cemetery beyond the mound and continue their endless, pointless labor," reports Inverse. Researchers write in the Journal of Hymenoptera that a million or so trapped ants have managed to carry on in a virtual Cold War nightmare, living in a near-starvation state and total darkness. The colony rests atop a thick cemetery of ant ancestors, indicating this has been going on for quite some time. Zoologists who stumbled upon the odd discovery in Templewo while counting bats doubted cannibalism was keeping the insects alive, suggesting bat excrement could be doing the trick. The ants produce no offspring, and so the colony's survival depends on surface ants making the same mistake they did. Wood ants are known to be resilient, though, with one colony surviving on a barren islet for nearly 30 years with only pine trees to munch, the researchers write. (A historian thinks a $500 million treasure hides in an old Nazi bunker.)

final_summary:
On December 21, an 18-year-old man, Adrian Simon, suffered severe injuries when he was struck by a freight train at a level crossing on Bushland Drive in Taree, New South Wales, during his 18th birthday celebrations. The incident occurred around 4:30 am, and the train driver managed to stop the train shortly after impact, allowing paramedics to arrive at the scene. Unfortunately, the man's legs were severed above the knee in the accident, and he was rushed to John Hunter Hospital in Newcastle in a critical condition. 

Over the next few days, the man remained hospitalized, fighting for his life, but ultimately succumbed to his injuries on Christmas Eve, three days after the accident. An investigation into the circumstances surrounding the incident is ongoing, with police seeking to determine why the man was on the train tracks at the time of the collision. 

In addition to the investigation into the cause of the accident, police are also looking into the behavior of a group of individuals who allegedly interfered with the treatment of the injured man. According to reports, these individuals attempted to block paramedics from providing aid, hurled rocks at emergency service workers, including the train driver and police officers, and subjected them to verbal abuse. The driver and officers were forced to take shelter in the train's cabin until additional police support arrived. 

When paramedics finally reached the scene, their ambulance windshield was damaged, and they faced further obstruction and harassment from the group. As a result, police are considering laying charges against those responsible for this unacceptable behavior. Local authorities, including Taree Mayor Paul Hogan, have condemned the actions of the group, describing them as "unwarranted" and emphasizing that such behavior will not be tolerated.

reference: – An Australian teen whose legs were severed by a train while celebrating his 18th birthday died on Christmas Eve in the hospital, three days after the incident, ABC Australia reports. "The death of the young lad is very sad, very upsetting," Paul Hogan, mayor of Taree—where the incident took place in New South Wales—tells the Sydney Morning Herald. Alcohol is believed to have been a factor in Adrian Simon's death. Simon had been in serious condition since the accident on Dec. 21, when he was hit by a train around 4:30am, severing his legs above the knee. Police are still investigating why Simon was on the tracks; ABC reports he was asleep. Police are also investigating a group of partygoers from that night who attacked emergency workers and police as they attempted to tend to Simon; charges may be pending. "It was a very unfortunate accident that had happened, but (the attack) was definitely unwarranted, and no one will tolerate that," Hogan says. (Earlier this year, a California teen died trying to retrieve her phone from train tracks.)

final_summary:
Germanwings Flight 9525, an Airbus A320, crashed into the French Alps on March 24, 2015, resulting in the deaths of all 150 people on board. The investigation into the crash has centered on the role of co-pilot Andreas Lubitz, who was 27 years old at the time of the incident. 

According to a summary of the cockpit voice recorder transcript published by German newspaper Bild, Lubitz locked the captain, Patrick Sondenheimer, out of the cockpit and deliberately crashed the plane. The transcript reveals that Lubitz had been experiencing personal struggles, including depression and vision problems, which may have motivated his actions. 

Lubitz had passed his annual pilot recertification medical examination in 2014, but an official with Lufthansa, the airline's parent company, stated that the exam only tested physical health, not psychological health. Lubitz had also visited a Dusseldorf clinic twice, most recently on March 10, concerning a diagnosis, but the university clinic denied treating him for depression. 

Prosecutors in France and Germany have suggested that Lubitz deliberately crashed the plane, pointing to a torn-up sick note from his flat in Dusseldorf, which was dated for the day of the disaster. Lubitz had a history of depression, which he hid from his employer and colleagues. 

The cockpit voice recorder transcript reveals a series of disturbing events leading up to the crash. After Captain Sondenheimer left the cockpit to use the restroom, Lubitz told him he could take over, and the sound of a seat being pushed back and the cockpit door clicking shut can be heard. The plane then began a steady descent, and minutes later, Captain Sondenheimer tried to re-enter the cockpit, shouting "For God's sake open the door!" 

Passengers can be heard screaming uncontrollably for minutes before the crash. The transcript also suggests that Lubitz gave clues about his intentions, responding to Captain Sondenheimer's order to prepare the plane for landing with "hopefully" and "let's wait and see". 

French officials have refused to confirm or deny the transcript, but the investigation is ongoing. The flight data recorder, which could provide crucial information about the crash, remains missing. The European Cockpit Association has criticized the leak of data from the cockpit voice recorder, calling it a serious breach of international accident investigation rules. 

As the investigation continues, the focus remains on understanding what drove Lubitz to commit such a horrific act. Theories abound, including depression, vision problems, and a possible detached retina. The exact circumstances surrounding the crash may never be fully known, but the transcript provides a chilling glimpse into the final moments of the doomed flight.

reference: – Investigators carrying out the grim task of recovering wreckage and human remains from the Germanwings crash say they have not found an intact body—or the flight data recorder. To aid the search, an access road is being built to the remote site in the French Alps, where forensic teams have so far recovered DNA belonging to 78 of the 150 people killed, the Guardian reports. Investigators say that so far, only the empty protective casing of the black box has been found. In other developments: Co-pilot Andreas Lubitz is believed to have deliberately crashed the plane, but with the failure to find the flight's second black box, some in the aviation world are saying it is too early to rule out other causes. Airbus chairman Tom Enders slammed TV coverage of the crash in an interview published yesterday, Deutsche Welle reports. "Sometimes people there speculate, fantasize, and lie with no basis in fact," he said, calling some of the reporting "outrageous nonsense" that was a "mockery of the victims." The cockpit voice recorder was recovered soon after the crash, and German newspaper Bild has published what it says is a leaked transcript. Capt. Patrick Sondenheimer (also spelled Sonderheimer or Sondheimer, depending on the source) can be heard pleading for Lubitz to let him back in the cabin, shouting, "Open the goddamn door!" as passengers scream minutes before impact, according to the transcript. A spokeswoman for France's accident investigation agency tells CNN that investigators are "dismayed" by the leak. Over the weekend, there were reports that Lubitz suffered from a psychomatic illness that may have been linked to a vision problem. German newspapers, including Bild, also reported that Lubitz's girlfriend, a teacher, was pregnant and the couple had planned to marry, according to the Independent. In Lubitz's hometown of Montabaur, the local Lutheran pastor tells the AP that despite mounting evidence that Lubitz caused the crash, he was still part of the community and the church is standing by his family. He says Lubitz's mother is a part-time organist for the church and he knew the co-pilot as a teenager. "This does not make sense. It is incomprehensible for me, for us, for everyone who knew her and the family," he says.

final_summary:
On August 14, 2014, President Barack Obama addressed the ongoing crisis in Ferguson, Missouri, and Iraq from his vacation on Martha's Vineyard, Massachusetts. He called for calm and peace in Ferguson, urging both police and protesters to exercise restraint. The president condemned the violent protests and looting, stating that there is no excuse for violence against police or for using the tragedy as a cover for vandalism or looting. He also emphasized that there is no excuse for police to use excessive force against peaceful protesters or to throw them in jail for lawfully exercising their First Amendment rights.

Obama specifically criticized the police for detaining two journalists during protests and for threatening others, stating that officers should not bully or arrest journalists who are simply trying to do their job. He commended the Department of Justice for working with local officials to investigate the death of 18-year-old Michael Brown, who was shot and killed by a police officer on August 9.

Meanwhile, in Ferguson, Missouri, Governor Jay Nixon attempted to calm the situation by announcing a change in command, transferring police oversight to the Missouri State Highway Patrol under the command of Captain Ronald S. Johnson, a St. Louis area native. Johnson pledged to protect Ferguson while allowing protesters and marchers to express themselves. He stated that he understands the anger and fear felt by the citizens of Ferguson and respects both emotions.

As the situation in Ferguson continued to unfold, protests and demonstrations took place, with some turning violent. On August 13, police used tear gas and smoke bombs to disperse crowds, and several people were arrested, including St. Louis Alderman Antonio French and two journalists from The Washington Post and The Huffington Post. The American Society of News Editors condemned the treatment of journalists, stating that the police had made conscious decisions to restrict information and images coming from Ferguson.

The NAACP demanded that Attorney General Eric Holder take immediate action to address the unjustified use of lethal and excessive force against unarmed African Americans by police forces nationwide. The organization called for a comprehensive federal review of each instance of excessive police violence against African Americans and for all involved officers and public servants to be held accountable.

Business leaders in St. Louis denounced the violence and police actions in Ferguson, with some expressing concerns about the economic impact of the unrest on the region. A vigil was planned in downtown St. Louis to commemorate Michael Brown and other victims of police-involved shootings.

In an effort to stem the riots, Ferguson city leaders urged protesters to assemble only during daylight hours, citing a desire to give people an opportunity to voice their frustrations through prayer vigils and peaceful protests. However, the protests continued, with some turning violent, and police responded with tear gas and other measures to disperse the crowds.

Captain Ron Johnson, the newly appointed commander of the Missouri State Highway Patrol, announced that he would take a new approach, focusing on protecting Ferguson while allowing protesters to express themselves. He stated that he would go to "ground zero" on Thursday evening, the area where Brown was killed, to assess the situation and develop a plan to restore peace and order.

The Justice Department, the FBI, and the St. Louis County prosecutor's office were investigating Brown's death, and U.S. Attorney General Eric Holder stated that police had accepted an offer of technical assistance from the Justice Department to help conduct crowd control and maintain public safety without relying on unnecessarily extreme displays of force.

reference: – The unrelenting protests in Ferguson, Mo., after the police shooting of Michael Brown clearly have the full attention of Washington now. Both President Obama and Missouri Sen. Claire McCaskill weighed in today, while Anonymous claimed to ID the officer who did the shooting. Details: McCaskill: Speaking at a local church, she got a standing ovation when she said St. Louis County must "demilitarize the police response," reports the St. Louis Post-Dispatch. Afterward, she told reporters that the "police response has been part of the problem." Obama: The president called for "peace and calm" and a transparent investigation, reports USA Today. He said there's no excuse for police to use excessive force against protesters, but nor is there "an excuse for violence against police or those who would use this tragedy as a cover for vandalism or looting." He also criticized police for arresting journalists at the protests. Gov. Jay Nixon: Speaking after McCaskill at the same church, he promised an "operational shift" in police tactics. "You will see a different tone," he said, promising details later. Anonymous: The hacker collective claimed to identify via Twitter the officer who shot the unarmed Brown last weekend, though police say the name is wrong, reports Reuters. Anonymous is standing by its post, while authorities are standing by their decision to keep the officer unidentified for safety reasons. "We can't let anonymous groups or even public groups pressure us into doing anything we don't think we should do," said a spokesman for the St. Louis County prosecutor's office. (Anonymous previously released police audio from the night of the shooting.)

final_summary:
A recently discovered bug affecting iPhones has left some users unable to use their devices after setting the system date to January 1, 1970. This unusual date, which may seem innocuous at first glance, triggers a critical error in the device's functionality, rendering it unusable. According to reports, attempting to change the date to this specific date, often done in jest or as part of a prank, results in the device becoming unbootable, displaying only the Apple logo upon startup. 

The issue affects Apple devices equipped with 64-bit processors, including iPhone 5S, iPad Air, iPad Mini 2, and the sixth-generation iPod Touch, or newer models of these devices. Users who fall victim to this bug find themselves unable to restore or update their devices through iTunes, leaving them with no choice but to seek assistance from an Apple Store, which typically offers a replacement rather than a repair.

This problem stems from a long-standing issue within the Unix-based operating system used by Apple's iOS, which tracks time as a continuous count of seconds since January 1, 1970. When the system date is manually set to this exact date, it creates a conflict that effectively freezes the device. A similar issue occurred on Facebook in the past when users were mistakenly led to believe it was the platform's anniversary, prompting a celebratory message that read "46 years of friendship."

The bug gained attention after being shared on social media platforms such as 4chan, Facebook, and Reddit, where users warned others about the potential consequences of tampering with their device's date settings. As of yet, Apple has not commented on the issue.

reference: – Setting your iPhone's date and time to Jan. 1, 1970, will instantly make you experience what life in the '70s was like—because you'll no longer have a cellphone. "I changed the time to January 1st 1970," NBC News quotes one poor iPhone user's complaint to Apple. "I shutdown my phone and restarted it, the result is a bricked iPhone. I've tried restoring, updating, but nothing seems to be working." Wired reports it's a bug that appears to be affecting 64-bit iOS devices, such as the iPhone 5S, iPad Air, and other newer models. Moving an iPhone's date setting back by 46 years isn't something most people would do without some prompting. So, of course, there's been some prompting. An official-looking promotional image popped up on the 4Chan forum on Thursday before spreading to Facebook and Reddit, Business Insider reports. The image instructs people with iPhones to change the date to Jan. 1, 1970 for a fun "easter egg": a rainbow-colored retro Apple logo when the phone is rebooted. Instead, the phone refuses to start back up and requires either a physical fix or complete replacement. So what's causing this odd bug? "It’s almost certainly related to the same Unix glitch that caused Facebook to wish people a happy 46 years on the service," Wired states.

final_summary:
At the 2014 Winter Olympics in Sochi, Russia, Shaun White, the reigning champion of the halfpipe event, failed to secure a third consecutive gold medal, finishing in fourth place behind Iouri Podladtchikov of Switzerland, who won the gold medal with a score of 94.75. Podladtchikov's victory marked a turning point in the sport, as he executed a rare trick known as the "Yolo" – a 1,440-degree rotation including two aerial somersaults – that none of the other competitors could match.

White's disappointing finish was attributed to a series of mistakes, including a hard landing and a bobble, which prevented him from achieving a near-perfect run. Despite his struggles, White remained optimistic, stating that his performance would not define his career and that he was eager to continue competing.

The halfpipe competition was marred by controversy due to the subpar condition of the course, which was deemed inadequate by several top riders, including three-time Olympian Hannah Teter, who suggested that the event be postponed to allow for improvements to be made. The pipe's surface was described as bumpy, with uneven snow and ice, causing several riders to take falls and struggle to maintain control.

Riders expressed frustration with the course, citing its differences from the high-quality pipes found in regular competitions. US snowboarding head coach Peter Foley stated that the Sochi pipe was far below the standard set by the US, which builds some of the world's best pipes. However, Kelly Clark, a four-time Olympian, believed that despite the imperfections, the best riders in the world should be able to adapt and perform well.

Danny Davis, an American snowboarder, echoed Clark's sentiments, stating that while the course was not ideal, it was not necessarily dangerous. The riders were given the option to adjust their planned runs to accommodate the challenging conditions, with some opting to play it safe and others pushing themselves to execute complex tricks.

Ayumu Hirano, a 15-year-old Japanese snowboarder, secured the silver medal with a score of 93.5, while Taku Hiraoka took home the bronze. This marked the first time since the introduction of the halfpipe event in 1998 that no American snowboarder appeared on the podium. The outcome was seen as a positive development for the sport, highlighting the emergence of new talent and the growing depth of international competition.

Shaun White had initially considered competing in the slopestyle event but ultimately decided to focus solely on the halfpipe, citing concerns over the quality of the slopestyle course. The decision proved costly, as White struggled to find his footing on the halfpipe and ultimately finished outside the top three.

reference: – Snowboarders in Sochi have been raising serious concerns about the state of the Olympic halfpipe, the scene of actual competition today: "Everyone is not happy right now," 2006 US gold medalist Hannah Teter told the Washington Post after yesterday's practice. But that hasn't stopped the event from going forward, the New York Times reports. Qualifying rounds have begun, and officials will decide, based on conditions, whether semifinals and finals should take place tonight as planned. Yesterday, officials were working to improve the halfpipe. Yesterday's practice—postponed from the morning for improvements—didn't inspire confidence among the athletes. "I saw everyone take a hard fall today. That never happens," Teter said. Is it dangerous? "It's just not as fun," says teammate Danny Davis. Athletes said the halfpipe, which Shaun White calls "disappointing," was bumpy, its curvature was off, and the snow was loose; and those who avoided falling yesterday struggled to maintain speed, leading to less aggressive tricks. But the Times notes that halfpipe quality can fluctuate wildly depending on weather conditions and how it's maintained. Still, "this is going to be showcased to billions of people, and we want the best representation of halfpipe snowboarding," Teter says.

final_summary:
Karlie Hay, an 18-year-old from Texas, was crowned Miss Teen USA on Saturday night at The Venetian Resort and Casino in Las Vegas. However, hours later, she faced intense backlash on Twitter due to a series of old tweets containing racist language, including the N-word, that resurfaced shortly after her coronation. 

Hay acknowledged her past mistakes on Twitter, stating that she had used language in the past that she was not proud of and that there was no excuse for it. She attributed her change to hard work, education, and the support of her peers within the pageant community. Despite her apology, some critics felt that she should have been more forthcoming with her regret and taken greater responsibility for her actions.

The Miss Universe Organization, the parent company of Miss Teen USA, released a statement defending Hay, stating that her language was unacceptable at any age and did not reflect the organization's values. They also acknowledged that Hay had grown and learned from her experiences, and they were committed to supporting her continued development. As a result, Hay was allowed to retain her title.

Former Miss Teen USA Kamie Crawford expressed surprise at Hay's lack of preparation for the scrutiny that comes with winning a high-profile pageant, advising young contestants to clean up their online presence before entering such competitions. Meanwhile, model Chrissy Teigen jokingly commented on the similarity in physical appearance among the top five finalists, including Hay.

In the aftermath of the controversy, Hay announced her intention to attend Texas A&M University after graduating from Tomball High School, with aspirations to become a businesswoman and advocate for individuals affected by substance abuse.

reference: – Miss Teen USA 2016 was supposed to take a step in the direction of feminism and equal rights as the contest ditched the swimsuits for athletic wear. But in the wee hours following the event that crowned Miss Texas Karlie Hay, the 18-year-old high school senior took to Twitter to do damage control on, well, previous comments on social media. Accused of repeatedly using racial slurs, people began to take screenshots and call her out on it, reports the Houston Chronicle. Hay, her school's varsity cheer captain and yearbook editor, apologized on Twitter at 2am Sunday: "I admit that I have used language publicly in the past which I am not proud of and that there is no excuse for. Through hard work, education and thanks in large part to the sisterhood that I have come to know through pageants, I am proud to say that I am today a better person." Former Miss Teen USA Kamie Crawford issued her own message on Twitter: "If you win any pageant—first things first. Clean up your page. Because if you're under 21, you shouldn't be drinking and if you're white, the N-word ain't your word." The Miss Universe Organization, parent company of Miss Teen USA, tells ABC News that while Hay's language was "unacceptable at any age," she will keep her crown because she "learned many lessons through those personal struggles that reshaped her life and values." Meanwhile, E! News reports that model Chrissy Teigen took issue with the final five, who were all white and blond. "Wow how can we choose from such a diverse bunch," she tweeted, later adding, "It's fiiiiiine. Not their fault. I'm sure they are delightful women. Just funny. I'm not gonna write a think-piece on a damn pageant."

final_summary:
The USS Independence, originally known as the light cruiser USS Amsterdam, was launched in August 1942 and commissioned in January 1943. As the first of its kind, it represented a new class of carriers built on converted cruiser hulls. Joining the Pacific Fleet in June 1943, the Independence played a pivotal role in various key battles throughout World War II.

In August 1943, the Independence participated in the attack on Marcus Island, marking the first combat deployment of the Grumman F6F Hellcat fighter jet. The ship then engaged in a similar operation at Wake Island in October, where it demonstrated its capabilities in a real-world setting. 

Following the Allied invasion of Bougainville, the Japanese responded by sending a large force of cruisers to Rabaul to disrupt Allied supply lines. The Independence, along with the USS Bunker Hill and USS Essex, formed Task Group 50.3, which arrived outside Rabaul on November 7, 1943. On November 11, carrier-based planes launched a combined attack on the island, destroying over 70% of Japanese installations. The Independence suffered minor damage during the engagement but continued to play a crucial role in subsequent battles.

In November 1943, the Independence was involved in the Battle of Tarawa, where it was targeted by Japanese planes and suffered significant damage from a torpedo hit. The ship underwent repairs at Funafuti and later at San Francisco, where an additional catapult was installed to enable night carrier operations. This innovation allowed the Independence to become the first aircraft carrier to conduct nighttime operations.

Throughout 1944, the Independence continued to participate in critical battles, including the Battle of Leyte Gulf, where it played a key role in sinking the Japanese battleship Musashi and disabling a cruiser. The ship also supported the Allied invasion of the Philippines, conducting pre-invasion strikes and providing night reconnaissance and combat air patrols.

On December 18, 1944, the Independence was caught in a typhoon while operating off the Philippines, resulting in significant losses, with one confirmed death and 790 sailors reported missing or injured. Despite this setback, the ship continued to contribute to the Allied efforts, supporting the Lingayen landings on Luzon and striking at air bases on Formosa and in Indo-China.

After the war, the Independence was assigned as a target vessel for the Bikini atomic bomb tests in July 1946. Although severely damaged, the ship did not sink and was later towed to Kwajalein for decommissioning. The highly radioactive hulk was eventually sunk off the California coast on January 29, 1951.

In 2019, renowned oceanographer Robert Ballard led an expedition to locate and document the wreck of the Independence using two unmanned submersibles. The team captured stunning images of the ship's flight deck, a Hellcat fighter plane, anti-aircraft guns, and other features, revealing the remarkable state of preservation of the wreck. The discovery shed new light on the history of the USS Independence and its significance in World War II.

reference: – The USS Independence has remained submerged a half-mile deep off the coast of California for 65 years, out of human sight since it was purposely relegated to the bottom of the ocean by the US Navy in 1951, reportedly with a bunch of 50-gallon barrels containing radioactive waste, per the Mercury News. This week, however, thousands of viewers watched as the first photos other than sonar scans of the 623-foot aircraft carrier were shown live online, courtesy of the same ocean explorer who discovered the Titanic, among other famous shipwrecks—and those pics show the Independence is in remarkably good shape in its watery grave. "There's very little change from when the Navy scuttled it," Robert Ballard says of his team's documentation, in conjunction with the Navy and the NOAA, adding the Titanic was similarly well-kept. "The deep sea is the largest museum on Earth." Viewers saw such wonders as the Independence's sponge-covered flight deck, a Grumman Hellcat fighter plane, and even the ship's name imprinted on its hull, all at the same time as researchers on the Nautilus exploration vessel, who sent out two remote-controlled submersibles Monday and Tuesday to capture the pictures about 30 miles west of Half Moon Bay. What the deep-sea dive didn't find: any evidence of the atomic waste rumored to have been sunk with the ship, per USA Today, though a UC Berkeley nuclear engineering expert tells the Mercury News any danger posed by such waste would've long since dissipated. The 74-year-old Ballard says he treated his newest subject with the same respect he does all his shipwrecks, leaving artifacts intact. "Taking things off would be like bringing a shovel to Gettysburg," he says. (Sonar images of the Independence were first seen last year.)

final_summary:
Lois Lerner, the head of the IRS division overseeing tax-exempt groups, was placed on administrative leave following widespread criticism and calls for her resignation due to her involvement in the agency's targeting of conservative groups. Despite initial claims that she was unaware of the targeting until 2012, an inspector general's report revealed that Lerner knew about it as early as 2011. Lerner's actions drew further scrutiny after it emerged that she had planted a question about the controversy at an American Bar Association conference, leading to her public apology and subsequent fallout.

Lerner's handling of the situation has been widely criticized, with several lawmakers calling for her dismissal. Senator Chuck Grassley stated that Acting IRS Commissioner Danny Werfel had requested Lerner's resignation, which she declined. At a House Oversight hearing, Lerner invoked her Fifth Amendment rights against self-incrimination, claiming she had done nothing wrong despite evidence suggesting otherwise.

The IRS announced that Ken Corbin would take over as acting director of the tax-exempt organizations division, replacing Lerner. President Obama had previously ousted Acting IRS Commissioner Steven Miller over his role in the targeting, and Lerner's boss, Joseph Grant, was set to retire in early June. 

A Treasury audit revealed that Lerner had knowledge of the targeting in June 2011, when she discovered that IRS staff were scrutinizing conservative groups applying for tax-exempt 501(c)(4) status based on keywords such as "Tea Party" and "patriots." Although Lerner ordered changes to the criteria, staff in a Cincinnati office reinstated more political screening methods. The audit also found that some groups faced lengthy delays in receiving approval for their tax-exempt status.

In response to the controversy, several lawmakers, including Senators Carl Levin and John McCain, and Representatives Sandy Levin, Joe Crowley, and Elijah Cummings, have called for Lerner's removal. Representative Darrell Issa concluded that Lerner waived her right against self-incrimination by defending herself during the House Oversight hearing, potentially paving the way for her return to Capitol Hill to face further questioning.

Lerner's actions drew further scrutiny after it emerged that she had planted a question about the controversy at an American Bar Association conference, leading to her public apology and subsequent fallout.

Lerner's handling of the situation has been widely criticized, with several lawmakers calling for her dismissal. Senator Chuck Grassley stated that Acting IRS Commissioner Danny Werfel had requested Lerner's resignation, which she declined. At a House Oversight hearing, Lerner invoked her Fifth Amendment rights against self-incrimination, claiming she had done nothing wrong despite evidence suggesting otherwise.

The IRS announced that Ken Corbin would take over as acting director of the tax-exempt organizations division, replacing Lerner. President Obama had previously ousted Acting IRS Commissioner Steven Miller over his role in the targeting, and Lerner's boss, Joseph Grant, was set to retire in early June. 

A Treasury audit revealed that Lerner had knowledge of the targeting in June 2011, when she discovered that IRS staff were scrutinizing conservative groups applying for tax-exempt 501(c)(4) status based on keywords such as "Tea Party" and "patriots." Although Lerner ordered changes to the criteria, staff in a Cincinnati office reinstated more political screening methods. The audit also found that some groups faced lengthy delays in receiving approval for their tax-exempt status.

In response to the controversy, several lawmakers, including Senators Carl Levin and John McCain, and Representatives Sandy Levin, Joe Crowley, and Elijah Cummings, have called for Lerner's removal. Representative Darrell Issa concluded that Lerner waived her right against self-incrimination by defending herself during the House Oversight hearing, potentially paving the way for her return to Capitol Hill to face further questioning.

reference: – The IRS official at the center of the recent scandal has been placed on administrative leave, reports the National Review. Lois Lerner had been in charge of the agency's tax-exempt unit, which singled out conservative groups for added scrutiny. Her immediate boss already has been forced into early retirement, and critics have been calling for Lerner's head to roll as well. (Her role in using a planted question to make the controversy public hasn't helped her cause.) On Capitol Hill yesterday, Lerner denied any wrongdoing but invoked the Fifth in refusing to answer questions. The chair of the House Oversight panel, Darrell Issa, says he plans to call her back to testify, arguing that she waived her right to the Fifth by making an opening statement, reports the Hill. The decision to put Lerner on leave comes days after the new acting chief of the IRS, Daniel Werfel, took over.

final_summary:
A recent study published in JAMA Internal Medicine has shed light on the relationship between how old individuals perceive themselves to be and their mortality rates. Researchers from University College London analyzed data from the English Longitudinal Study of Aging, which included 6,489 participants aged 65.8 years on average, and found that those who felt younger than their actual age had a significantly lower death rate compared to those who felt their age or older.

The study, led by Isla Rippon and Dr. Andrew Steptoe, discovered that participants who felt 3 or more years younger than their actual age had a mortality rate of 14.3%, whereas those who felt their age or more than 1 year older had a mortality rate of 24.6%. This association remained even after accounting for factors such as baseline health, physical disability, and health behavior.

Interestingly, the researchers found that the proportion of participants who felt older than their actual age was relatively small, comprising just 4.8% of the sample. Conversely, 69.6% of participants felt 3 or more years younger than their actual age, with an average self-perceived age of 56.8 years.

The study also revealed a significant link between self-perceived age and mortality caused by cardiovascular disease, but not cancer. While the exact mechanisms behind this association are unclear, the authors speculate that greater resilience, a sense of empowerment, and adherence to healthy behaviors may contribute to the lower mortality rates observed in individuals who feel younger than their actual age.

Notably, the researchers emphasized that self-perceived age can change over time, suggesting that interventions aimed at promoting positive health behaviors and attitudes towards aging may be effective in reducing mortality rates. As such, individuals who feel older than their actual age could potentially benefit from targeted health messages encouraging them to adopt healthier lifestyles.

This study adds to a growing body of evidence highlighting the importance of subjective experience and psychological factors in determining health outcomes, particularly in older adulthood. Further research is needed to fully understand the complex relationships between self-perceived age, health behaviors, and mortality risk.

Further research is needed to fully understand the complex relationships between self-perceived age, health behaviors, and mortality risk.

reference: – If you eat well and exercise, you'll probably feel younger than your chronological age. But new research suggests that simply feeling younger than your age—even when accounting for other longevity factors, such as alcohol intake, wealth, illness, education, and smoking—improves longevity, reports Medical News Today. Studying 6,489 participants in the English Longitudinal Study of Aging, researchers report in the journal JAMA Internal Medicine that mortality was only 14.3% among those who felt younger than their age, but was 18% for those who felt their age, and 24.6% for those who felt older. "Someone who feels younger is possibly healthier than someone who feels older—they have fewer diseases, they may be more mobile," a researcher tells CBS Philadelphia. The study couldn't connect self-perceived age with death by cancer, but did establish a strong link between self-perceived age and fatal cardiovascular disease. Factors that speed up self-perceived aging include becoming a parent at an early age, experiencing stress, and developing serious health problems such as cancer. Still, more than 66% of participants say they felt at least three years younger than their chronological age. According to the study, older people who feel old may well slow the aging process by adopting a fresh outlook: "Interventions may be possible," the authors say. "Individuals who feel older than their actual age could be targeted with health messages promoting positive health behaviors and attitudes toward aging." (Other research suggests soda can age our cells as much as cigarettes.)

final_summary:
Tiger Woods is currently undergoing rehabilitation for sexual addiction at Pine Grove Behavioral Health and Addiction Services in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, where he is participating in a six-week program under the direction of Dr. Patrick Carnes, a renowned expert in treating sexually compulsive behavior. According to a source close to the situation, Woods plans to return to the PGA tour in the spring, following completion of his rehab, with the goal of convincing everyone, including corporate sponsors, that he has genuinely changed.

Woods' decision to seek treatment comes after a series of high-profile infidelity scandals, including an affair with Rachel Uchitel, which was exposed by the National Enquirer, leading to a string of revelations about his secret life of serial cheating. During his marriage to Elin Nordegren, Woods has been linked to over 14 women, with many more alleged relationships remaining unreported.

The rehabilitation center, Pine Grove Behavioral Health and Addiction Services, is considered one of the top facilities for treating sex addiction in the country. Woods has chosen to stay in a private room without a roommate, reportedly at his own request. Despite his efforts to keep a low profile, news organizations have swarmed Hattiesburg in search of sightings of the golfing legend, although the facility remains closed to visitors.

Elin Nordegren, Woods' wife, has not visited him during his stay at the rehab center, fueling speculation about the future of their marriage. Although she has not filed for divorce, she has been spotted without her wedding ring, leaving many wondering whether the couple's relationship can be salvaged. The Masters tournament, which Woods has won four times, is scheduled to begin on April 5, but it remains unclear whether he will be ready to participate.

By returning to the PGA tour in the spring, Woods aims to demonstrate his commitment to recovery and rebuild his reputation, both personally and professionally. However, the road ahead will be challenging, given the severity of his past transgressions and the scrutiny he faces from fans, sponsors, and the media.

final_summary:
Following the devastating earthquake and subsequent tsunami that struck Japan, concerns about radiation released from damaged nuclear plants have led to a surge in sales of Geiger counters and potassium iodide supplements across the United States. Despite reassurances from experts and government agencies that the risk of harm from radiation reaching the US is "negligible" and "impossible," many Americans are stocking up on these products, driven by fears of radiation exposure. 

Elmer Lewis, a nuclear expert and professor emeritus of mechanical engineering at Northwestern University, stated that it is "exceedingly improbable" that the accident would deliver any detectable amount of radiation at ground level in the US, emphasizing that any potential radiation would be "barely detectable" and pose "absolutely no health consequences." The US Nuclear Regulatory Commission also confirmed that the country is "not expected to experience any harmful levels of radioactivity."

However, despite these assurances, potassium iodide supplements, which can protect the thyroid gland from radioactive iodine but do not shield against other types of radiation, are flying off shelves. Stores in various locations, including Chicago, have reported selling out of the product, with some retailers unable to meet the high demand due to backorders. Companies that manufacture Geiger counters are also experiencing a significant increase in orders, with some reporting hundreds and thousands of requests.

Experts emphasize that potassium iodide is not a panacea for all types of radiation exposure and should only be used to prevent thyroid cancer in children and pregnant women. Dr. Irwin Redlener, a pediatrician and disaster preparedness specialist at Columbia University, noted that the pill is most effective when taken within a few hours of radiation exposure and is not recommended for individuals over 40 years old.

The current situation has reignited debates about whether households near nuclear reactors should maintain a supply of potassium iodide in case of a local radiation emergency. While the federal government stocks the drug and provides it to states for distribution, some experts argue that a 10-mile radius around a reactor is insufficient and advocate for individual households to store their own supplies. Radiation health specialists are urging the Obama administration to reconsider its stance on this issue.

In response to the crisis, internet sellers are struggling to meet the high demand for potassium iodide, with some donating their stock to areas in dire need, such as Japan. As the situation unfolds, it remains unclear whether the US will adopt a more proactive approach to preparing for potential radiation emergencies, but for now, the public's anxiety about radiation exposure continues to drive the demand for these products.

reference: – An ocean away from the unfolding nuclear crisis in Japan, worried Americans are stocking up on Geiger counters and supplements that can block one effect of radiation exposure. Pharmacies around the country are rapidly running out of potassium iodide, which can protect the thyroid gland from radiation damage, the AP reports. Suppliers say they are receiving calls from panicked customers, but experts say Americans shouldn't worry about being exposed to radiation from Japan, and shouldn't bother buying potassium iodide unless they live near a nuclear plant. "I think it's exceedingly improbable—I'd say impossible—that this accident would deliver any detectable amount of radiation at ground level in the United States," a nuclear expert tells the Los Angeles Times. "It would be barely detectable and have absolutely no health consequences."

final_summary:
On February 2, 2017, Nordstrom, a US-based department store, quietly announced that it would no longer carry Ivanka Trump's line of branded apparel due to poor sales performance. This decision was made public through a statement from a Nordstrom spokesperson, who explained that the company had chosen not to purchase the brand for the current season.

However, President Donald Trump remained silent on the matter for nearly a week, until February 8, 2017, when he took to Twitter to express his outrage over the decision. He claimed that his daughter Ivanka had been "treated unfairly" by Nordstrom and described the situation as "terrible."

The President's tweet sparked a response from the White House, with Press Secretary Sean Spicer characterizing Nordstrom's decision as a "direct attack" on Trump administration policies and Ivanka's personal reputation. Spicer's comments were seen as an attempt to frame the issue as a partisan dispute rather than a purely commercial decision.

In contrast to previous instances where Trump's tweets had negatively impacted private companies, Nordstrom's stock price actually surged following the controversy, rising by nearly 5% on the same day. This unexpected reaction may have been driven by a combination of factors, including increased media attention and potential benefits from being associated with a high-profile feud involving the President.

Interestingly, another retailer, T.J. Maxx, also announced that it would discontinue carrying Ivanka Trump's line around the same time, although there was no immediate response from the President on this development. Overall, the episode highlighted the complex dynamics between politics, commerce, and social media, as well as the unpredictable nature of Trump's online presence and its impact on various stakeholders.

reference: – Vox has declared a winner in the great Nordstrom-Trump war of 2017, and it isn't the president. After the retailer announced it was dropping Ivanka Trump's clothing line due to poor sales, Trump on Wednesday took to Twitter to share his displeasure. After very briefly dipping, Nordstrom's stock rebounded in a big way. It was up nearly 5% by the end of the day—a day when the rest of the market was "essentially flat," the Los Angeles Times reports. A financial reporter for the Seattle Times tweeted that Wednesday was Nordstrom's sixth best day on the market in the past year and ninth best day in the past five years. This despite White House press secretary Sean Spicer calling Nordstrom's decision to drop Ivanka's line a "direct attack" on the president, his policies, and his family that is "not acceptable."

final_summary:
Mike Megna, a 44-year-old former Pompton Lakes police officer and Marine veteran, is struggling to make ends meet due to a rare kidney disease he believes was caused by his exposure to toxic dust at Ground Zero during the 9/11 rescue efforts. Despite his desperate situation, including a foreclosed home and mounting debt, Megna faces significant hurdles in obtaining compensation from the World Trade Center Health Program, which requires a scientific association between his illness and 9/11 exposure.

Megna spent three weeks at Ground Zero, searching for survivors amidst the rubble of the World Trade Center, and later returned daily to aid in the rescue efforts. However, it wasn't until 2007 that he began experiencing symptoms of his kidney disease, including urinating blood, which led to a series of tests and eventual diagnosis at the Mayo Clinic. Doctors confirmed that Megna suffers from monotype atypical anti-GBM nephritis, a super-rare kidney disease affecting only 20 people worldwide.

Despite his dire situation, Megna's condition is not currently recognized as a compensable illness under the James Zadroga Health and Compensation Act of 2010, which governs the World Trade Center Health Program. To qualify for compensation, researchers must establish a causal link between 9/11 exposure and the illness through published, peer-reviewed studies. A medical researcher at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai Medical Center, Mary Ann McLaughlin, has petitioned the program to add kidney disease to the list, citing preliminary research, but the program awaits the completion and publication of her study.

Megna's plight has sparked public attention, with his story featured on billboards near MetLife Stadium and the Holland Tunnel, as well as on New York City buses. He has also launched a website, DoRightByMike.com, detailing his struggles and advocating for recognition of his illness. However, despite his efforts, Megna remains uncertain about his future, facing the possibility of needing dialysis within eight to nine years and struggling to make ends meet.

In a glimmer of hope, Megna has received some financial assistance from the Victim Compensation Fund, a separate program run by the Department of Justice, but the payout was based on his initial diagnosis of less severe respiratory and digestive ailments, not his kidney disease. If kidney disease becomes a recognized illness, Megna will be allowed to reapply for compensation, but the deadline to submit an amended claim is December 18, 2020.
