reference: – A 9/11 first responder with kidney disease close to losing his New Jersey home is taking his plight public in a big way: through his website and a series of tri-state area billboards, the Record reports. "Add Kidney Disease for 9/11 Rescue Workers!" the signs outside MetLife Stadium and the Holland Tunnel and on NYC buses read, referring to the illness 44-year-old Mike Megna says he came down with due to dust at the decimated World Trade Center, where he toiled for three weeks after the attacks. Megna started seeing smaller symptoms, like a constantly runny nose and digestive issues, early on, but in 2006 blood began showing in his urine, and a diagnosis delivered heart-sinking news: He had a "super-rare" form of kidney disease that only 20 people in the world have. And while Megna, who's been disabled since 2011, has applied for compensation cash from the James Zadroga Health and Compensation Act of 2010, there's a complication: Kidney disease isn't one of the recognized illnesses covered by the fund. That means the divorced dad of three isn't eligible for any money—even though Mary Ann McLaughlin, a Mount Sinai Medical Center researcher who petitioned the fund two years ago to add the disease based on initial research, has examined Megna's records and believes the WTC toxins "may more likely than not" be tied to his sickness. However, a rep for the CDC's National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, which runs the program, says a disease can be added to the list—and compensation issued—only after "published, peer-reviewed epidemiologic evidence" shows "substantial support" for a direct link between the WTC fallout and the disease. Megna will have to hold tight, per the rep, who says once McLaughlin publishes her study, kidney disease eligibility will be considered. But that's time Megna—$120,000 behind on his mortgage and anticipating dialysis down the road (his kidneys are now functioning at 45%, per the Star-Ledger)—says he doesn't have. "When they asked us to come down to help that day, I didn't wait," he tells the paper.

final_summary:
For the first time in its 63-year history, the Miss World pageant has announced that it will no longer feature a swimsuit round in its competition. The decision comes from Chairwoman Julia Morley, who stated that the organization wants to shift its focus towards emphasizing the contestants' inner qualities and charitable endeavors, rather than their physical appearance. The change aims to align with the pageant's mission of promoting "beauty with a purpose."

In recent years, Miss World has placed greater emphasis on its "Beauty with a Purpose" initiative, which encourages contestants to engage in charity work and raise awareness for various causes. The organization believes that this approach better reflects the values of a true leader and ambassador, rather than simply a beauty queen.

The decision to eliminate the swimsuit round was met with approval from Chris Wilmer, the national director of Miss World America/Miss United States organization, who stated that the pageant should focus on showcasing the contestants' abilities as ambassadors and leaders, rather than solely on their physical appearance. Wilmer noted that the swimsuit round did not serve a purpose in highlighting these qualities.

The change in format will see contestants participating in other rounds, including talent, fashion sense, sport, service work, social media, and an in-depth interview. The swimsuit round, which was previously a key component of the competition, will be replaced by a beachwear round, described as a "fashion competition" rather than a bikini show.

The decision to remove the swimsuit round marks a significant departure from the pageant's origins, which date back to 1951 when Eric Morley staged a publicity stunt featuring 26 women in bikinis floating on a barge along the River Thames. Since then, the pageant has undergone numerous changes, including the introduction of the interview portion and the service element, "Beauty with a Purpose," which was launched by Julia Morley in 1974.

The elimination of the swimsuit round has sparked debate, with some critics arguing that it is a step forward in promoting a more inclusive and empowering representation of women. Others have expressed concerns that the change may undermine the pageant's core identity and appeal. However, Julia Morley remains committed to her vision, stating that she wants to move away from the objectification of women and focus on their intellectual and charitable contributions.

The change will come into effect starting from next year, with the current crop of contestants, including the newly crowned Miss World, Rolene Strauss of South Africa, being the last to compete in the swimsuit round. With over 1 billion people tuning in to the competition, the decision is likely to have far-reaching implications for the pageant and the wider industry.

reference: – Just days after South Africa's Rolene Strauss won this year's crown, Miss World organizers have announced it will no longer feature the swimsuit portion of the contest, reports the New York Daily News. Next year's competition will be the first year in the event's 63-year history in which the contestants put on a "beachwear" fashion show instead of parading down the catwalk in bikinis, ABC News reports. It's not exactly clear what "beachwear" means, though a rep for the event frames it as "more of a fashion competition than a bikini show." The Daily News notes that Miss World had already stopped airing the bathing suit portion of the competition, and had made it optional. Donald Trump, who owns the Miss Universe pageant, puts what Elle calls a "strong emphasis" on swimsuits and doesn't include interviews or service elements during his contest, as Miss World does. After Miss World Chairwoman Julia Morley's husband (the founder of Miss World) died in 2000, Trump called Morley and reportedly asked, "So little lady, are you ready to throw in the towel?" His remarks spurred Morley to get a $5 million loan and keep her contest going. As for the latest change, "I don't need to see women just walking up and down in bikinis," Morley tells Elle. "It doesn't do anything for the woman. And it doesn't do anything for any of us." (Miss World got rid of bikinis in 2013 out of respect to host country Indonesia.)

final_summary:
According to an informal survey conducted by the American Farm Bureau Federation (AFBF), the cost of a traditional Thanksgiving Day dinner for ten people has decreased to $49.04 this year, marking a decline from its 2012 price of $49.48. The AFBF's survey shopping list includes essential items such as a 16-pound turkey, bread stuffing, sweet potatoes, rolls with butter, peas, cranberries, a relish tray of carrots and celery, pumpkin pie with whipped cream, and beverages like coffee and milk, all in quantities sufficient to provide for a family of ten with leftovers.

The most significant contributor to the overall decrease in cost was the 16-pound turkey, which dropped to $21.76 this year, representing a price reduction of approximately 47 cents compared to 2012. At $1.36 per pound, the turkey's price decrease translates to a savings of three cents per pound. Notably, the 2013 Thanksgiving dinner cost is lower than the 2011 cost of $49.20.

LPL Financial's Jeffrey Kleintop observed that there appears to be "no inflation on the table" regarding the prices of the ingredients included in the AFBF's survey.

reference: – Thanksgiving dinner will cost you a little less this year—44 cents less to be exact—according to a 34-state survey from the American Farm Bureau Federation, picked up by Business Insider. A 10-person feast with turkey and all the trimmings—including stuffing, sweet potatoes, rolls, veggies, cranberries, and pumpkin pie with whipped cream—plus enough for leftovers, costs about $49.04 this year, a drop from 2012's $49.48. At about $5 per serving, it's "an excellent value for consumers," AFBF's president said. The most expensive buy is of course the turkey, which averages $1.36 per pound—down three cents from last year—or $21.76 for a 16-pounder. Newser points out that wine and other alcoholic libations aren't included in the grand total. Don't have time to dress a turkey? And more penny-wise news: Assuming you're not the host, driving to Thanksgiving dinner will be a little cheaper, too: The Los Angeles Times reports national gas prices are down 16 cents per gallon over last year, to an average $3.27 a gallon.

final_summary:
Karina Vetrano, a 30-year-old woman, was found dead on August 2, 2016, in a marshy area of Spring Creek Park in Howard Beach, Queens, after going missing during a solo jog. Surveillance footage shows Vetrano jogging near her home, wearing a ponytail and earbuds, in what may have been her final moments alive. The video, released by police, does not depict anyone following her or in a car behind her, leaving detectives puzzled. 

Vetrano's body was discovered approximately 15 feet off the jogging path, and an autopsy revealed she died from strangulation, with signs of severe beating and sexual assault. The investigation led to the collection of DNA from three areas: Vetrano's neck, phone, and fingernails. The DNA from her fingernails is considered the strongest lead, pointing to a single individual, although no match has been found in state or local databases.

A sketch of a man wearing a woven wool cap was released by police, who stated he was seen in the area around the time Vetrano went missing. However, he was not identified as a suspect or person of interest, merely someone who could have witnessed the crime. The man was described as being between 35 and 45 years old, 5 feet 10 inches tall, and having a medium build. His distinctive wool cap stood out on the hot summer day.

The police have received over 85 tips regarding the case, with nine remaining uninvestigated. A reward of over $300,000 has been offered for information leading to Vetrano's killer, including a $35,000 contribution from the NYPD and a GoFundMe account established by Vetrano's family, which has raised over $270,000. Despite these efforts, the case remains unsolved, with police urging anyone with information to contact Crime Stoppers at 1-800-577-TIPS.

reference: – A new possible clue in the "extraordinary case of murder" of Queens jogger Karina Vetrano, CBS New York reports. A surveillance video showing the 30-year-old on her run shortly before she was killed last month. The video is being shown with her family's permission on Crime Watch Daily, although dad Phil Vetrano tells CBS it's "very disturbing" to watch his daughter's final moments. The video, which shows Vetrano casually jogging down a street at 5:46pm on Aug. 2 near Spring Creek Park in Howard Beach, doesn't appear to offer any explicit clues as to what happened afterward—"It doesn't show anybody following her," Phil Vetrano says—but police are hoping it opens doors in the case. "Somebody in Arkansas, someplace … may hear something about it and come forward with a piece of information," Police Commissioner Bill Bratton says. Last month, cops released a sketch of a man they want for questioning, per NBC News. A reward worth almost $300,000 is being offered for info leading to an arrest.

final_summary:
In a significant development for the Catholic Church, a small chapel in Champion, Wisconsin, known as Our Lady of Good Help, has gained international recognition as a site of authentic apparitions of the Virgin Mary. This designation, granted by Bishop David L. Ricken of the Diocese of Green Bay, makes it one of only a dozen sites worldwide and the first in the United States to receive official validation from the Roman Catholic Church. 

The story begins in 1859, when Belgian immigrant Adele Brise claimed to have been visited three times by Mary, who appeared to her in a bright light, dressed in white with a yellow sash and a crown of stars, hovering between two trees. According to Brise's account, Mary instructed her to devote her life to teaching Catholic beliefs to children, which Brise did faithfully until her death. 

A two-year investigation by theologians, conducted after a request from Bishop Ricken, concluded that Brise's experiences were "of a supernatural character" and "worthy of belief." The investigation found no evidence of fraud or heresy and documented numerous instances of shrine-related conversions, cures, and other signs of divine intervention. 

Bishop Ricken, who took office in 2008, stated that he was drawn to the shrine due to the numerous accounts of answered prayers and miraculous healings he encountered during his tenure. He initiated the formal investigation, which ultimately led to the declaration of the apparitions' validity. 

The Vatican typically defers to local bishops in evaluating apparitions, and the Church is cautious in its approach, wary of potential fraud. Historically, only about 12 out of 386 reported apparitions have been recognized as valid over the past century. To assess credibility, investigators often employ psychiatric evaluations and brain scans to rule out mental health issues or hallucinations. However, these methods were not available in Brise's case, and the investigation relied on indirect indicators, such as her humility, honesty, and lifelong dedication to serving the Church.

The shrine's grounds and the surrounding area were miraculously spared from the devastating Great Peshtigo Fire of 1871, which destroyed nearby lands and claimed over 1,200 lives. This event is seen as a striking sign of a divine presence. 

Since the announcement, a growing number of visitors have flocked to the shrine, some traveling from as far away as New York. Local church officials are preparing for increased attendance, despite initially building a relatively small parking lot with 75 spaces. For many, including Debbie Banda and her mother, Mary Young, the experience is deeply moving, with Banda describing feeling an intense emotional connection to Mary upon seeing a statue of the Virgin in white, matching Brise's description.

Catholic leaders view the validation as a welcome respite for the Church, which has faced recent scandals related to clergy abuse. The Rev. Johann Roten, director of the International Marian Research Institute, sees the declaration as a "gift to the believers," emphasizing its potential to inspire faith and renewal.

reference: – About a dozen sites worldwide can boast that they have been validated by the Catholic Church as being sites where the Virgin Mary has appeared—and for the first time, that list includes a US location. A tiny chapel 17 miles northeast of Green Bay, Wisc., called Our Lady of Good Hope joins the likes of Mexico's Our Lady of Guadalupe and France's shrine at Lourdes, both of which draw millions each year. In 1859, a Belgian immigrant named Adele Brise said Mary—a dazzling white figure with cascading blond hair and a crown of stars, hovering between a pair of trees—appeared to her three times and instructed her to spend her life teaching Catholic beliefs to children. A two-year investigation that concluded on Dec. 8 found no evidence of fraud or heresy, but did find a long history of shrine-related conversions, cures, and other indications of divine intervention, leading Bishop David L. Ricken of Green Bay to declare “with moral certainty” that Brise had encounters “of a supernatural character” that are “worthy of belief.” The Vatican, wary of fraud, gives local bishops the primary responsibility of evaluating apparitions, in part by using guidelines set by the Vatican in 1978. The New York Times notes one "striking sign of a divine presence:" During 1871's Great Peshtigo fire, which killed 1,200 and destroyed the surrounding lands, the shrine's grounds and the people gathered there were spared.

final_summary:
In a unique initiative aimed at promoting a circular economy and reducing waste, the city of Paris has begun selling thousands of tons of cobblestones from its historic streets and alleys. The project, which kicked off in early 2016, involves extracting the cobblestones from beneath the asphalt during civil works projects such as the construction of a new tramway. The stones, made of granite and dating back to the 11th century, are then sorted, cleaned, and sold to various buyers.

Entrepreneur Margaux Sainte-Lagüe was among the first to capitalize on this opportunity, purchasing five tons of the cobblestones and launching her online store, Mon Pavé Parisien. The store offers a range of hand-painted cobblestones, including a classic version featuring the colors of the French flag, a gilded option, and the "May 68" series, which commemorates the 1968 revolution with the inscription "sous les pavés." Customers can also commission customized designs, with Sainte-Lagüe having created personalized stones for notable figures such as Paris's mayor, Anne Hidalgo, and a Chinese couple celebrating their wedding.

The cobblestones, priced between $63 and $157, are marketed as a unique souvenir or memento for those looking to own a piece of Paris's history. According to Patrick Marchetti, who oversees the sorting and cleaning process at the Paris city maintenance center, the stones have attracted significant interest from tourists and collectors alike, with some buyers coming from as far as China. Despite relatively low sales thus far – with Sainte-Lagüe reporting just 50 sales – the demand remains steady, with the entrepreneur planning to expand her business by selling her wares in luxury boutiques and souvenir shops near the city's landmarks.

On a larger scale, other companies are also embracing the idea of reusing and recycling materials from public works projects. French builder Eiffage SA aims to incorporate recycled materials into cement aggregate, while Cie de Saint-Gobain, Europe's largest supplier of construction materials, is collaborating with Paprec Group to collect discarded residential windows for use in new products. The city of Paris estimates that it may sell as many as 3 million tons of the cobblestones, which will be sourced from beneath the asphalt of streets such as the famous Champs-Élysées, although some areas, like the Arc de Triomphe and Place de la Concorde, will retain their original cobblestone paving.

reference: – Parisian streets have been paved with stones since the 12th century, and while asphalt now covers large swaths of the city, the cobblestones still adorn squares, boulevards, and alleyways. For years as the city dug them up (about 10,000 tons a year) to continue building or repaving, it paid to haul them to the dump, reports the New York Times. No longer. It finally occurred to officials last March to salvage these rare gems of Parisian history and sell them to contractors so that they'd have "a second life," say the head of the city maintenance yard that stores them. "They are cheap and have some charm," he tells Bloomberg. In September, one enterprising woman bought five tons for $215 and set up a souvenir shop of sorts, where her team polishes, weighs, and hand-paints the stones. She sells them online for between $60 and $160, plus $15 to $40 in shipping fees. Tourists from as far afield as China and Oklahoma have paid for what entrepreneur Margaux Sainte-Lagüe calls enduring little pieces of history. (Not bad, considering the stones are worth maybe 8 cents as raw material.) For her, they bring a bit of nostalgia, reminding her of when youth threw them at police during national strikes in 1968. (This happened in 1848 as well.) Even the mayor of Paris, Anne Hidalgo, has one of her decorated stones, notes Architectural Digest. (Paris is designating a wooded area for nudists.)

final_summary:
A devastating mudslide occurred in Oso, Washington, on Saturday, leaving behind a trail of destruction and loss of life. The disaster struck around 11 am, sweeping through the small community of Steelhead Haven, burying dozens of homes and vehicles under a thick layer of mud and debris. The initial reports indicated that at least 14 people had been confirmed dead, but as the search and rescue operations continued, the death toll rose to 16, with two more bodies recovered on Tuesday.

Rescue teams, aided by the Washington Army National Guard and Federal Emergency Management Agency, combed through the debris-filled terrain, facing challenging conditions due to the depth of the mud, which in some areas reached up to 20 feet. Despite the difficulties, they managed to locate eight more bodies, bringing the total number of confirmed fatalities to 16. However, officials stated that they believed there were eight more bodies under the mud, which they were unable to retrieve.

As the search efforts continued, the number of people reported missing rose dramatically, from 18 on Sunday to 176 on Monday. While officials expressed hope that the actual number of fatalities would decrease as they verified the names, they acknowledged that the chances of finding survivors were slim. The last reported survivor, a 4-year-old boy named Jacob Spillers, was rescued on Saturday, but his father, Billy Spillers, and three siblings remained missing.

The cause of the mudslide is still unclear, but experts speculate that heavy rainfall may have played a role in saturating the hillside above the village. A 2010 report commissioned by Snohomish County highlighted the risks of deadly landslides in the area due to the steep gradient of the hills and the soil type. The disaster has sparked concerns about the safety of building in slide-prone areas, particularly in Steelhead Haven, where homes continued to be constructed despite warnings.

President Barack Obama declared a state of emergency, authorizing federal assistance to support local relief efforts. The rescue operation has been ongoing, with over 200 responders working tirelessly to locate victims and recover bodies. However, the harsh terrain and the sheer volume of debris have hindered the search efforts, leading authorities to acknowledge that some victims may be left entombed in the treacherous terrain.

reference: – Positive news is hard to come by when it comes to the mudslide that wiped out a Washington state community, but footage is making the rounds today of a helicopter crew rescuing a 4-year-old boy from the muddy wreckage. Jacob Spillers was home with his two sisters, age 5 and 2, his stepfather, and his 13-year-old half-brother when the slide hit. The home was destroyed, and all were trapped inside, according to the Seattle Times. Everyone except Jacob is still missing; the boy escaped because he was on the second floor. "Jacob told me he got out when nobody else was able to get out," his half-brother's father says. Jacob's mother, however, is alive and well, because she wasn't home at the time. The footage was shot Saturday, the day the slide hit; no survivors have been found in the days since. Other heartbreaking tales from survivors: Gary "Mac" McPherson, 81, survived the landslide—even as it claimed his wife of 46 years, Linda, who was sitting right next to him, the LA Times reports. McPherson says he awoke sitting in the same chair, now crushed, with a ceiling beam across his lap, his house having been pushed 150 yards away. "He was able to dig a hole and see the sky," his daughter says. "The whole time, he was calling for my mom ... but she never responded." US Navy Cmdr. L. John Regelbrugge III's body was found by his two brothers yesterday morning, his sister-in-law tells the Seattle Times. The 32-year veteran's remains were alongside his dog's. "His brother Greg put a work shirt over him. They said he had massive injuries, so at least he didn't suffer." His wife was found a short time later.

final_summary:
In the weeks leading up to her untimely death, Bobbi Kristina Brown's life was marked by constant struggles with substance abuse, intense relationships, and violent altercations. According to her friend Max Lomas, who lived with Brown and her boyfriend Nick Gordon in a townhouse in Roswell, Georgia, their household was plagued by a culture of partying, fighting, and immense pain. Lomas, who was romantically involved with Brown before his incarceration in 2011, described their lives as "pretty bad" due to their heavy use of substances.

After Lomas' release from prison, he claimed that Brown and Gordon began dating, but their relationship was tumultuous, characterized by extreme highs and lows, and frequent fights over jealousy. Despite attempts to quit using drugs and adopt healthier lifestyles, they relapsed within two weeks. Lomas alleged that Gordon became physically abusive towards Brown, a claim that Gordon's lawyers have vehemently denied, labeling Lomas a "drug addict."

Brown's family, including her father Bobby Brown, filed a $40 million civil lawsuit against Gordon, who maintained that he was never abusive and only used substances. In an interview with Dr. Phil, Gordon stated that he was present for Brown while she dealt with her father's absence. However, Lomas recalled that Brown's family planned an intervention, which ultimately did not occur.

On January 31, 2015, Lomas found Brown unresponsive in her bathtub and administered CPR alongside Gordon until medical help arrived. Although no charges were filed against Lomas, Brown spent six months in a coma before passing away on July 26, 2015, at the age of 22. Brown's family later secured a $36 million wrongful death settlement against Gordon.

Tragically, Lomas himself met a similar fate, dying of an apparent drug overdose in a friend's bathroom in Mississippi. His attorney confirmed the news, but further details surrounding the circumstances of his death remain unclear.

reference: – The friend who found Bobbi Kristina Brown face-down in a bathtub after an overdose has suffered a similar death himself, reports TMZ. Max Lomas was found unresponsive on the floor of a friend's bathroom in Mississippi with a syringe nearby and died at the hospital, according to the website. Lomas played no small role in the case of Brown, the daughter of Whitney Houston and Bobby Brown. He was the one who actually pulled the 22-year-old from the tub and helped administer CPR until help arrived, notes Page Six. "We were all bad into drugs," Lomas told People in 2016, referring to himself, Brown, and her boyfriend, Nick Gordon. No charges were ever filed after Brown's death in 2015.

final_summary:
In August 2018, the Catholic Diocese of Dallas faced intense scrutiny over its handling of allegations against Rev. Edmundo Paredes, a priest accused of molesting teenagers and embezzling funds from St. Cecilia Catholic Church, where he served for 27 years. According to Bishop Edward Burns, the diocese first investigated Paredes in May 2017 over allegations of financial misconduct, which included stealing $60,000 to $80,000 in cash from the parish. Paredes admitted to the financial impropriety, was suspended from ministry, and removed from the church in June 2017.

However, it wasn't until February 2018 that the diocese received allegations of Paredes' sexual abuse of three teenage boys over a decade ago. Despite this new information, the diocese chose not to disclose the allegations to the public, citing concerns that it might hinder the ongoing police investigation and compromise the victims' anonymity. 

The decision not to disclose the allegations drew criticism from the Survivors Network of those Abused by Priests (SNAP), a national organization advocating for victims of clergy abuse. Local chapter leaders Lisa Kendzior and Paul Petersen questioned Bishop Burns' motives, asking whether he suppressed the information to protect the diocese from scandal and expressing disappointment that the bishop did not prioritize the safety of children and the well-being of potential survivors.

Bishop Burns eventually disclosed the allegations to parishioners at St. Cecilia's on August 19, 2018, after a meeting with the Diocesan Review Board, a group of non-clergy Catholics advising the diocese on matters related to minor abuse. This decision was reportedly influenced by the recent Pennsylvania grand jury report, which exposed widespread clergy abuse and led to increased pressure on the Catholic Church to address similar issues within its ranks.

Paredes is currently missing, and the diocese has hired two private investigators to locate him. His whereabouts are believed to be in the Philippines, where he had taken annual vacations. As the investigation unfolds, the Diocese of Dallas continues to face questions about its handling of the situation and its commitment to transparency in addressing clergy abuse allegations.

This decision was reportedly influenced by the recent Pennsylvania grand jury report, which exposed widespread clergy abuse and led to increased pressure on the Catholic Church to address similar issues within its ranks.

Paredes is currently missing, and the diocese has hired two private investigators to locate him. His whereabouts are believed to be in the Philippines, where he had taken annual vacations. As the investigation unfolds, the Diocese of Dallas continues to face questions about its handling of the situation and its commitment to transparency in addressing clergy abuse allegations.

reference: – The Catholic Church has hired two private investigators to track down a former Dallas priest who disappeared months before sexual abuse allegations against him surfaced. Pastor at St. Cecilia Catholic Church in Oak Cliff for 27 years, Rev. Edmundo Paredes stands accused of molesting three boys in their midteens between 10 and 20 years ago, though the exact period is being withheld to protect the victims’ identities, reports the Dallas Morning News. Paredes, 69, was removed from St. Cecilia in June 2017 after allegedly stealing $60,000 to $80,000 in cash. This February, church officials also learned of sexual abuse allegations, which were reported to police and found to be credible, Bishop Edward Burns told parishioners on Sunday. "I recognize this diocese cannot cover its ears, its eyes, its mouth. We need to look at this head on," Burns said. Still, the Survivors Network of Those Abused by Priests questions the delay in releasing the information. Officials initially said the allegations weren’t disclosed earlier because of a police investigation. Late Monday, however, the Diocese of Dallas said a Pennsylvania grand jury report describing more than 1,000 victims of abuse by clergy since the 1940s had motivated the disclosure after victims objected to an announcement in March, per Fox 4. Officials suspect Paredes is in his native Philippines, which he visited annually, per WFAA. Per the Morning News, authorities there say they are unaware of his location.

final_summary:
A recent controversy surrounding a Roseville, Michigan resident, Nick Taylor, has sparked debate over the practice of warming up vehicles in driveways. Taylor received a ticket from Officer K. Keary for leaving his 1997 Chrysler Concorde running in his driveway with the keys in the ignition, citing a local ordinance. However, upon further investigation, it appears that there may be confusion regarding the specific ordinance cited on the ticket, with some suggesting it does not exist.

According to the Roseville police chief, James Berlin, the primary concern is public safety, as leaving a vehicle running with the keys in the ignition poses a risk of theft. Berlin stated that using a remote starter is acceptable, as the key is not physically present in the vehicle. However, Taylor claims he was unaware of this distinction and believes the ticket was unwarranted.

The incident has garnered significant attention on social media, with many expressing outrage and support for Taylor. Some argue that warming up a vehicle in a driveway is a common practice, especially in colder climates like Michigan. Others point out that the ordinance cited on the ticket appears to be non-existent.

Chris Lague, a service writer at Partner Tire & Service in Colchester, Vermont, weighed in on the topic, stating that modern engines do not require warming up and that manufacturers recommend starting the engine and driving away within 30 seconds to a minute. Lague attributed this to advancements in fuel injection and onboard computers, which allow engines to warm up faster when driven rather than idled.

In contrast, some individuals, such as Bill Brisson, continue to advocate for warming up their vehicles for longer periods, citing concerns about engine performance in extremely cold temperatures. However, Lague countered that this approach is outdated, as most modern cars are equipped with computer systems that prioritize efficient operation once the engine reaches normal temperature.

Vermont has implemented a statute prohibiting idling for more than five minutes out of any sixty-minute period, with certain exceptions. While the Roseville ordinance in question remains unclear, the incident highlights the complexities surrounding vehicle idling laws and the importance of understanding the specific regulations in each jurisdiction.

reference: – A Michigan man is fuming after he left his car running in a driveway—and got a ticket. "Every person warms up their car," Nick Taylor tells WDIV. "We live in Michigan!" Taylor, 24, left the car idling unlocked at his girlfriend's house on a frigid morning last week. When he returned a few minutes later, he spied the $128 ticket for leaving a vehicle unattended. Taylor posted a photo of the ticket on his Facebook page and railed against the "dip----" Roseville police officer who left it there for "wasting the taxpayer's money." (He later "retracted" the insult.) The post has been shared nearly 14,000 times, but Police Chief James Berlin wasn't backing down, saying you can't leave vehicles running. "This is purely a public safety issue," he tells Fox2. "You see it all the time, people hop in a running car and steal them. Something bad happens when that occurs." And because of the original insult, Berlin says he's not about to tear up the ticket. One footnote for those who practice Taylor's habit of warming up the car: Mechanics say the old-school ritual is no longer necessary in the era of fuel-injected engines, notes USA Today. In fact, today's cars tend to warm up more quickly being driven rather than idling. (This driver faced DUI charges, though he had only caffeine in his system.)

final_summary:
President Donald Trump spent the weekend reviewing the backgrounds of the top contenders to fill the vacant seat on the US Supreme Court, narrowing his selection down to around five finalists, including two women. According to an administration official, Trump conducted extensive phone calls with White House Counsel Don McGahn to gather further insight into the potential nominees. Despite initially suggesting that he might conduct interviews with one or two candidates at his Bedminster, New Jersey, golf club over the weekend, no such meetings took place.

Trump's shortlist consists of five federal appeals court judges: Brett Kavanaugh, Amy Coney Barrett, Thomas Hardiman, Raymond Kethledge, and Amul Thapar. Trump described the group as "a great group of intellectual talent" and stated that they are generally conservative. However, he declined to comment on whether he would ask the candidates about their stance on the landmark 1973 Roe v. Wade abortion-rights ruling, despite having previously pledged to nominate only judges who oppose abortion rights.

The confirmation process is expected to be contentious, with Republicans holding a slim 51-49 majority in the Senate. Senators Susan Collins of Maine and Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, who have expressed support for Roe v. Wade, hold significant sway over the outcome. Trump met with these senators, along with several others, at the White House on Thursday to discuss the nomination.

Among the contenders, Kavanaugh, a former law clerk to Justice Kennedy, has faced controversy due to his involvement in the investigation that led to President Bill Clinton's impeachment. Kethledge, another former Kennedy clerk, has gained praise from conservatives for his opinion in a case involving the IRS and a Tea Party group. Barrett, a Trump appointee to the 7th Circuit Court of Appeals, faced scrutiny over her Catholic faith during her Senate hearing, while Thapar, also a Trump appointee, would be the first Asian-American justice if selected.

Trump plans to announce his pick on July 9, aiming to have the nominee confirmed in time to join the court for its new term in October. The selection process is being expedited, with the FBI conducting background checks and White House officials speaking with over a dozen senators.

final_summary:
According to sources close to Sandra Bullock, she had prior knowledge of Jesse James' impending engagement to Kat Von D, which was announced earlier this week. Despite the sudden news, Bullock is reportedly taking it in stride, prioritizing her role as a mother to her adopted son, Louis, over any potential emotional reaction to James' new relationship. 

Bullock and James had previously split, with their divorce being finalized in June 2010. Although the two are not close friends, they maintain some level of communication, allowing James to inform Bullock about his plans to marry Kat Von D. Sources indicate that Bullock was prepared for this development and has accepted James' decision to move forward with his life.

In contrast, James and Von D's relationship appears to be progressing rapidly, with the couple announcing their engagement after just six months of dating. James credits 2010 as the best year of his life due to his romance with Von D, describing her as an "amazing woman" who is "kind, loving, and committed to making the world a better place." Von D reciprocates these sentiments, stating that James is "the one" and expressing her commitment to their relationship.

The couple has been open about their affection for each other on social media, with James tweeting about his love for Von D and the two sharing public displays of affection. When asked about potentially relocating to Austin, Texas, to be with James, Von D expressed a desire to maintain a presence in both Los Angeles and Texas, citing the idea that "home is where the heart is."

Yes, those crazy kids Jesse James and Kat Von D are betrothed. Despite the aforementioned hardships, “2010 was actually the best year of my life because I fell in love with my best friend,” James tells People. “An amazing woman who stood behind me when the world turned their backs.” The lovebirds go on fawning with such touching sentiments as, from James, “Growing old with her is going to be a f***in' blast!" Click for Sandra Bullock's reaction.

final_summary:
On April 30, US Army Captain Bruce Clark, 43, died while deployed to Tarin Kowt, Afghanistan, approximately 85 miles north of Kandahar. During a scheduled Skype call with his wife, Susan, Clark pitched forward unresponsive around 9 pm local time. Despite Susan's frantic attempts to alert authorities, it took over two hours for military personnel to arrive at her home in El Paso, Texas, where the call was being made from. 

According to Susan's account, which was later corroborated by others present, Clark was suddenly knocked forward, and a bullet hole was visible in the closet behind him. A military member who responded to the scene confirmed the presence of the bullet hole, agreeing it was indeed a gunshot wound. However, the official cause of death initially reported by the Army was listed as natural causes.

Susan has since come forward to correct the record, releasing a statement detailing what she witnessed during the Skype call. She stated that despite her repeated calls to emergency services, it took two hours for assistance to arrive, during which time the call remained active, allowing others to observe Clark's condition. When military personnel finally arrived, they checked his pulse but failed to provide any information to Susan regarding his status.

The delay in response time was attributed to the calls being routed through multiple state-side commands before reaching Afghanistan. The military has maintained that Clark's death is under investigation, with officials stating that the cause of death would not be determined until toxicology and autopsy results were available. 

Captain Clark, a seasoned Army medic, had served since 2006, following his graduation from SUNY Brockport with a nursing degree. He leaves behind his wife, Susan, and their two young daughters, ages 3 and 9. Clark's body was repatriated to the United States on May 2, arriving at Dover Air Force Base.

reference: – The sad and strange tale of the US Army captain who died while Skyping with his wife gets even more mysterious: Army investigators say they found no bullet wound or any evidence of foul play in Bruce Kevin Clark’s death, but wife Susan Orellana-Clark has suggested her husband may have been shot. "Clark was suddenly knocked forward," reads a family statement released yesterday. "The closet behind him had a bullet hole in it.” Others, “including a member of the military, who rushed to the home of Clark's wife, also saw the hole and agreed it was a bullet hole." Orellana-Clark says her husband showed no signs of alarm or discomfort before falling forward onto his desk during their Skype chat, which she kept open for two hours as she tried to get assistance for Clark, 43, who was at his base in Afghanistan. "We can positively say that Captain Clark was not shot," says an Army spokesperson, adding that there was no trauma on the body other than a possibly broken nose from when Clark hit the desk, the AP reports. The Army is waiting on complete autopsy results, but has ruled the death from natural causes, Business Insider adds—but speculation is rampant on Twitter, with some wondering if Clark was killed by a sniper.

final_summary:
On May 29, 2016, a tragic incident occurred on Maui's rocky shore where a Ford Explorer driven by 38-year-old Alexandria Duval, also known as Alison Dadow, plummeted approximately 200 feet off a cliff, resulting in the fatal injury of her identical twin sister, Anastasia, who was seated in the passenger position. According to authorities, the sisters were engaged in a physical altercation over the steering wheel at the time of the crash.

Following the incident, Alexandria Duval was hospitalized and subsequently released, after which she was taken into custody by Maui police on suspicion of murder. However, a judge later ruled that there was insufficient evidence to hold her, and she was released. 

In late October 2016, a grand jury in Hawaii indicted Alexandria Duval on a second-degree murder charge, prompting the Maui Police Department to issue a warrant for her arrest. After being tracked down by a New York State Police investigator at an Albany residence on November 11, 2016, Duval was apprehended and transported to the Latham barracks.

Duval's defense attorney, Terry Kindlon, maintained that the crash was an accident rather than a deliberate act, stating that his client had been "profoundly distraught" since the incident and had attempted to return to upstate New York to attend her sister's funeral. Kindlon further argued that the prosecution's evidence did not meet the necessary standards for extradition, leading him to request an extradition hearing.

The extradition hearing, set for December 16, 2016, aims to determine whether Alexandria Duval is indeed the individual sought by Hawaiian authorities and whether she was present at the scene of the crime. If extradited, Duval would face trial in Hawaii for the alleged murder of her twin sister.

A lawyer for 38-year-old Alexandria Duval said Friday at her court appearance in Albany that his client is "profoundly distraught" and described the siblings as "soul mates," reports the Albany Times Union. He claimed the paperwork as presented by New York and Hawaii prosecutors "is not sufficient to force extradition so far." Duval was tracked down in Albany and arrested a week ago, after a grand jury in Hawaii indicted her on a second-degree murder charge last month. Authorities say Alexandria was driving an SUV in May with her sister, Anastasia Duval, in the passenger seat when the vehicle crashed into a rock wall and plunged about 200 feet. Authorities say the sisters were fighting over the steering wheel, reports AP. A previous murder case against Duval was dismissed because of a lack of evidence. The extradition hearing is scheduled for Dec. 16.

final_summary:
In 1996, Alexa Internet began donating its daily crawl data to the Internet Archive, which were then added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. Meanwhile, two years prior to the airing of the interview, a high-profile murder trial took place involving Casey Anthony, whose parents, George and Cindy Anthony, were central figures. In their first public interview since Casey's acquittal, the couple sat down with Dr. Phil McGraw to address various aspects of the case.

During the interview, Cindy Anthony explained her MySpace post from July 3, stating that while Caylee was not physically missing, she felt her granddaughter was gone in her heart due to being kept away from her. This post occurred 12 days before Caylee's actual disappearance was reported. When questioned about the strong odor found in Casey's car, Cindy expressed uncertainty, saying she still didn't know what caused it, while George stated he preferred not to speculate but trusted the investigators' findings.

Cindy defended her husband, George, against allegations that he might have known about Caylee's disappearance, claiming he wouldn't have endured the six-month ordeal without knowing his granddaughter's whereabouts. She described witnessing George's emotional distress throughout the investigation, emphasizing that he was just as frantic as she was. The full interview, which was arranged through a payment to Caylee's Fund rather than directly to the Anthonys, will be broadcast on Dr. Phil's television show over two consecutive days.

reference: – Casey Anthony’s parents are finally speaking out about their daughter’s case in an interview with Dr. Phil McGraw set to air tomorrow and Wednesday, and tidbits are trickling out, People reports. In a clip aired this morning on Today, McGraw asks Cindy Anthony about a post she wrote on MySpace 12 days before Caylee was reported missing that read, prophetically-seeming, "Caylee is missing." But Cindy had no idea Caylee was actually gone that day. She was "missing in my heart," she explains. "She wasn't missing physically. I wanted Casey to know how much she was hurting me. And I thought she was purposely keeping Caylee away from me." Cindy says she "truthfully" doesn’t know why the trunk of Casey’s car smelled suspicious. "Do I want to believe Caylee was back there?" asks George Anthony. "I don't want to believe it. I'm going by what the investigators have told me." As for questions over whether her husband was aware Caylee was missing: "George would have never have put us through those six months of not knowing where Caylee was if he knew where Caylee was," Cindy says. "I watched his heart break every single day."

final_summary:
Mitt Romney, a native of Detroit and son of George Romney, a former CEO of American Motors, reflects on the auto industry's struggles and the government's response during the 2008 economic crisis. Born in Harper Hospital, Romney grew up surrounded by the industry and developed a passion for American-made cars. His father's leadership at American Motors instilled in him a love for innovation and entrepreneurship. However, Romney believes that the government's bailout of Chrysler and General Motors under President Barack Obama's administration was misguided and exemplified crony capitalism.

Romney argues that instead of a bailout, the companies should have undergone a managed bankruptcy, which would have allowed them to restructure quickly and emerge as competitive entities. He claims that the bailout led to the UAW receiving a 55% stake in Chrysler, while secured creditors were shortchanged, and non-union employees suffered losses. Romney asserts that this outcome was a result of Obama's favoritism towards union bosses and campaign donors.

Romney contrasts his views with those of former President George W. Bush, who supported the bailout, citing the potential for widespread job losses and economic devastation if the companies failed. Romney disputes this assessment, arguing that a managed bankruptcy would have achieved similar results without the negative consequences. He suggests that the bailout created a culture of dependency on government support and hindered the industry's ability to innovate and compete globally.

To revitalize the industry, Romney proposes several reforms, including replacing current management with fresh talent from outside the sector, promoting collaboration between labor and management, and investing in research and development for sustainable technologies. He advocates for increased funding for basic research in areas such as energy sources, fuel efficiency, and materials science. Additionally, Romney recommends rectifying tax penalties that favor foreign carmakers and providing guarantees for post-bankruptcy financing to ensure consumer confidence.

Romney emphasizes that a managed bankruptcy would allow the industry to shed unnecessary costs, including labor, pension, and real estate expenses, and emerge as a more competitive and viable entity. He believes that the federal government should not provide a free pass to shareholders and bondholders, as they took risks by investing in the companies. Romney concludes that a managed bankruptcy would propel the industry towards a brighter future, rather than sealing its fate with a bailout check.

reference: – Three years ago, Mitt Romney wrote that President Obama should "Let Detroit Go Bankrupt," instead of bailing out the struggling auto industry, in a famous New York Times column. And today? Even with the American auto industry on the rebound, Michigan's GOP primary looming on Feb. 28, and Rick Santorum breathing down his neck in the polls, Romney still thinks bankruptcy would have been better than the president's "crony capitalism," he writes in the Detroit News. "I believe that without his intervention things there would be better," he writes. Romney's big complaint with the bailout? That "secured creditors" got screwed and non-union employees had their benefits slashed while a union-controlled trust got 55% of Chrysler and the unions had their pensions saved. "It is not the American way of making cars," writes Romney. None other than former President George W. Bush is taking the opposite side in the post-mortem, saying that he was responsible for the first $25 billion bailout, and it was definitely a good thing. “I didn’t want there to be 21% unemployment,” Bush told the National Automobile Dealers Association last week, reports the Washington Post. "Sometimes circumstances get in the way of philosophy.”

final_summary:
Susan Rice, the former national security adviser under President Barack Obama, requested the unmasking of the names of Trump transition officials caught up in surveillance on numerous occasions, according to US officials familiar with the matter. The unmasking occurred despite the fact that the names of Americans are typically redacted from intelligence reports to protect their privacy. Rice's requests were made to identify individuals mentioned in intelligence reports related to the Trump transition and campaign, which were collected through incidental electronic surveillance of foreign officials. 

These reports included summaries of monitored conversations between foreign officials discussing the Trump transition, as well as direct contact between Trump team members and foreign officials. The information contained in these reports included details about the Trump team's meetings, policy intentions, and views on foreign policy matters. 

Rice's requests for unmasking were reportedly made on dozens of occasions, and the information was shared with various officials, including those at the National Security Council, the Defense Department, and the CIA. Former Director of National Intelligence James Clapper and former CIA Director John Brennan were also informed of the unmasking. 

The unmasking of Trump transition officials was part of a larger pattern of surveillance that began before Trump's inauguration and continued for up to a year. The surveillance was conducted under the Obama administration, and it is unclear how Rice became aware of the existence of the unmasking requests. 

House Intelligence Committee Chairman Devin Nunes has been investigating the matter, and his staff discovered Rice's requests during a review of the unmasking process. Nunes has faced criticism for his handling of the situation, but he maintains that he acted appropriately in bringing the issue to light. 

The revelation has sparked concerns among civil libertarians and lawmakers about the potential abuse of power and the erosion of individual privacy rights. Some have questioned why Rice felt the need to unmask the names of Trump transition officials, particularly given that the information was not related to any national security or law enforcement investigation. 

Rice has denied any wrongdoing, claiming that she was unaware of the unmasking requests and was surprised by Nunes' allegations. However, her previous statements on the matter have been contradicted by evidence, and she has yet to provide a clear explanation for her actions. 

The Senate and House intelligence committees are expected to investigate the matter further, with lawmakers seeking answers about the scope and purpose of the surveillance, as well as the motivations behind Rice's requests. Rice's involvement in the unmasking has raised questions about the Obama administration's use of surveillance powers and the potential for abuse of authority.

reference: – Former Obama national security adviser Susan Rice is back in the news again, this time regarding President Trump's wiretapping allegations. On the right, she is being accused of abusing her power while in office to "unmask," for political advantage, the names of Trump associates caught up in intelligence surveillance. Trump critics, meanwhile, say the story doesn't amount to much and accuse the White House of trying to divert attention from the president's wiretapping allegations. A look at coverage: A key story comes from Eli Lake at Bloomberg News. He reported Monday that Rice requested the identities of Trump transition officials who were mentioned, but not named, in US intelligence reports. Those reports were mostly summaries of monitored conversations between foreign officials talking about the Trump transition, but sometimes between Trump associates and foreign officials. (The National Security Council logs of Rice's requests are apparently what Rep. Devin Nunes went to the White House to view.) Lake's piece raises questions about why Rice wanted those names revealed—she didn't respond to the story—but he also notes that her requests "were likely within the law." He adds that the story doesn't "vindicate" Trump's unsupported accusation that Obama wiretapped Trump Tower. Adam Housley of Fox News also reported on Rice's requests, and his appearance on the network prompted Trump himself to weigh in via tweet: "Such amazing reporting on unmasking and the crooked scheme against us by @foxandfriends. 'Spied on before nomination.' The real story." A post at Slate notes that Trump associates are under FBI investigation over allegedly improper communications with Russians, "so there may well have been urgent, legal reasons for Rice to have wanted information about the relationships between specific Trump-related figures and foreign individuals." But the editorial page at the Wall Street Journal accuses the media and Democrats of hypocrisy. It wants any Trump-Russia ties investigated, but complains that the "media have been running like wildebeest after that story while ignoring how the Obama Administration might have abused domestic surveillance for its political purposes. Americans deserve to know the truth about both." On CNN, Don Lemon accused the "right-wing media" of helping the White House try to divert attention from the controversy over Trump's wiretapping claims, adding that his show will not "aid and abet people who are trying to misinform you, the American people, by creating a diversion," he said, per Mediaite. The conservative NewsBusters blog accused ABC, NBC, and CBS of covering up the story by failing to report on the "massive revelation" Monday in their evening broadcasts. On MSNBC Tuesday morning, frequent Trump critic Joe Scarborough blasted the New York Times for not having the story on its front page. "What is Susan Rice unmasking names for and spreading them across the government," he asked on Morning Joe, per Mediaite. But the Times story on the subject (inside the paper) generally downplayed the significance of Rice's requests: "Former national security officials, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, described the requests as normal and said they were justified by the need for the president’s top security adviser to understand the context of reports sent to her by the nation’s intelligence agencies."

final_summary:
In September 2013, US President Barack Obama ordered an increase in US Special Operations forces deployed to Uganda to hunt down warlord Joseph Kony, the leader of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA). Kony's forces have been responsible for years of brutal attacks on central African villages, mutilations of civilians, and the abduction of children, who are then forced to fight or serve as sex slaves. The LRA has been indicted by the International Criminal Court, and while its numbers have dwindled significantly, with three of its top commanders removed from the battlefield since May 2012, Kony remains at large.

The US has been working with the African Union (A.U.) to track down Kony, with over 100 Special Operations troops initially deployed in 2011. However, the A.U. had identified a need for improved transportation capabilities to quickly move troops to areas where Kony's whereabouts had been reported. To address this issue, the US is deploying at least four CV-22 Osprey aircraft, along with refueling planes and around 150 Air Force Special Operations forces and other airmen, to Uganda. These aircraft will allow for the rapid transport of troops and provide enhanced mobility in the pursuit of Kony.

The deployment is part of a broader effort to support the A.U.'s military task force tasked with tracking Kony and his organization across Uganda, the Central African Republic, South Sudan, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. US personnel are authorized to provide information, advice, and assistance to the A.U. task force, but are prohibited from engaging LRA forces unless in self-defense. The total number of US forces in Uganda is estimated to be around 300.

The deployment comes amidst controversy surrounding Uganda's passage of a law imposing harsh penalties for "homosexual offenses," which the Obama administration has criticized as a step backward for human rights. Despite this, the administration has maintained that ensuring justice and accountability for human rights violators like the LRA and protecting the rights of LGBTQ individuals are not mutually exclusive goals. As part of this effort, the US has shifted funding away from partners that do not align with US values, including $6.4 million previously allocated to the Interreligious Council of Uganda, which supported the anti-LGBTQ legislation.

The LRA's activities have garnered international attention, particularly after the release of a video documentary highlighting the group's atrocities. This has led to widespread public pressure on the US government to take action, with Congress passing legislation in 2009 supporting increased efforts to counter the LRA's threat. The US has also offered a reward of up to $5 million for information leading to Kony's capture.

reference: – A viral video campaign may not have turned up a warlord accused of murder, rape, and kidnapping in central Africa, but the White House is hoping a ramped up effort by the United States will. The Washington Post reports that President Obama has ordered a strengthened US presence in Uganda—including at least four CV-22 Osprey aircraft, 150 Air Force Special Ops forces and other airmen—in an attempt to find Joseph Kony and deliver him to the International Criminal Court. That brings the total of US troops in the country to 300, outnumbering the estimated 250 fighters of Kony's Lord's Resistance Army, which has taken some major blows recently. The parameters for the new troops—which also include refueling planes, the BBC notes—will be much the same as when Obama sent 100 troops to hunt for Kony in 2011: Personnel may "provide information, advice, and assistance" to the African Union task force hunting Kony and the LRA across Uganda, the Central African Republic, South Sudan, and the Congo, but they may not engage in combat unless in self-defense. The Ospreys in particular "will make a significant difference in the ability to respond to leads" about Kony's whereabouts, and can carry 24 troops each, a Defense Department official said.

final_summary:
On Thursday, former Massachusetts Governor Mitt Romney engaged in a heated exchange with hecklers during a Q&A session at the Iowa State Fair, which was organized by The Des Moines Register and broadcast on C-SPAN. The confrontation began when Romney faced aggressive questioning from attendees with a liberal bent, including one individual who asked why he would not raise the cap on Social Security taxes or promise not to cut Medicare benefits. Romney responded by telling the questioner, "If you want to speak, you can speak. But right now, it's my turn."

The tension escalated when a second heckler asked Romney if he supported scrapping the Social Security payroll cap so that wealthy individuals could contribute more to the trust fund. Romney retorted, "There was a time in this country where we didn't celebrate attacking people based on their success. We didn't go after people because they were successful." However, he then raised his voice, saying, "If you want to speak, you can speak. But right now, it's my turn, so let me continue."

Romney went on to defend his stance on corporate taxation, stating, "Corporations are people, my friend... of course they are. Everything corporations earn ultimately goes to the people. Where do you think it goes? Whose pockets? Whose pockets? People's pockets. Human beings my friend." His comments sparked immediate criticism from Democrats, who saw them as evidence of Romney's discomfort on the campaign trail.

Romney's spokesperson, Eric Fehrnstrom, later defended the candidate's statement, arguing that corporations are composed of human beings who create jobs and hire employees. However, the remark has been seized upon by Democrats as a potential liability for Romney, who is perceived as being out of touch with ordinary Americans. The incident occurred just two days before the Ames Straw Poll, a key test of conservative strength in Iowa, and ahead of Thursday night's Republican presidential primary debate in the state.

final_summary:
On a sunny Sunday morning, a star-studded funeral procession took place at New York's Temple Emanu-El to bid farewell to legendary comedian Joan Rivers, who passed away at the age of 81. The ceremony, which was attended by numerous celebrities from the worlds of entertainment, fashion, media, and business, was a testament to Rivers' enduring legacy and her wish for a "huge showbiz affair with lights, cameras, action" and "Hollywood all the way." 

Among those paying tribute to Rivers were fellow comedians Kathy Griffin, Kelly Osbourne, and Howard Stern, who delivered a heartfelt eulogy, recalling Rivers' feisty spirit and her impact on the industry. Stern humorously referenced Rivers' long-standing feud with Johnny Carson, saying, "I hope Joan is somewhere right now chasing Johnny Carson with a baseball bat." He also praised Rivers as his "hero" and "the crazy aunt at a bar mitzvah."

Rivers' daughter, Melissa, accompanied by her 13-year-old son Cooper, read a lighthearted letter she had written to her mother, expressing gratitude for the love and support they received from friends and family. Other notable attendees included film and Broadway star Hugh Jackman, who performed a song from "The Boy from Oz," and television personalities such as David Letterman, Sarah Jessica Parker, Diane Sawyer, and Rosie O'Donnell.

Throughout the ceremony, there were moments of levity and humor, as well as poignant tributes to Rivers' remarkable life and career. The service featured jokes about her plastic surgery, sex life, and jokes themselves, reflecting the irreverent spirit that defined Rivers' comedic style. Fans and well-wishers lined the streets outside the temple, many of whom had dressed up in designer attire to pay homage to the late "Fashion Police" host.

In addition to the celebrity turnout, the funeral program included humorous anecdotes and quotes from Rivers herself, including her desire for a dramatic entrance, complete with a wind machine to blow her hair like Beyoncé's. The booklet also featured three of Rivers' signature catchphrases: "Can we talk?", "Who are you wearing?", and "Because I'm a funny person."

As the ceremony concluded, Melissa Rivers emerged from her mother's home to thank the fans and supporters who had gathered outside, distributing cupcakes and expressing her gratitude for their presence. The outpouring of affection and admiration for Rivers was a testament to her enduring influence and the impact she had on countless lives throughout her career.

The investigation into the cause of Rivers' death, which occurred after she underwent a routine procedure at an Upper East Side clinic, continues, with the New York state health department and the city medical examiner conducting tests to determine the circumstances surrounding her passing. Despite the uncertainty surrounding her final days, Rivers' funeral served as a celebration of her life, wit, and legacy, leaving behind a lasting impression on all who attended.

Luminaries including Howard Stern, Sarah Jessica Parker, Kathy Griffin, Whoopi Goldberg, Barbara Walters, Kelly Osbourne, Diane Sawyer began lining up this morning, reports the AP. Stern paid tribute to Rivers, notes People, calling her "the best friend in the world … a big sister ... a crazy aunt at Bar Mitzvah." The back of the program read thusly, as per People: "Can we talk?" "Who are you wearing?" " ... Because I'm a funny person ... " – JOAN RIVERS

final_summary:
Chelsea Manning, a 29-year-old US Army veteran, reflected on her decision to leak a trove of classified documents to WikiLeaks in her first interview since being released from a military prison on May 18, 2017. Manning stated that she felt a sense of responsibility to the public to disclose the information, citing the devastating impact of war on civilians in Iraq and Afghanistan. As an Army intelligence analyst, Manning witnessed firsthand the "death, destruction, and mayhem" caused by the conflicts, which led her to share the 700,000 military and State Department documents.

Manning emphasized that she acted alone and without direction from anyone, stating, "No one told me to do this. No one directed me to do this. This is me. It's on me." She acknowledged accepting full responsibility for her actions, saying, "Anything I've done, it's me. There's no one else." Manning's motivations for leaking the documents were twofold: to spark public debate and to expose the human cost of war, which she felt was being obscured by statistics and reports.

During her imprisonment, Manning struggled with her gender identity and attempted to take her own life twice due to the lack of access to hormone treatments. After coming out as transgender in 2013, Manning's ACLU lawyer, Chase Strangio, successfully advocated for her to receive medical care, making her the first military prisoner to receive such treatment. Manning credited the hormone therapy with keeping her alive and preventing feelings of intense distress.

Upon her release, Manning expressed gratitude towards former President Barack Obama for commuting her 35-year sentence to time served, allowing her to start anew. Although she respects the military and its purpose, Manning criticized the system for failing to address her concerns through official channels, stating, "the channels are there, but they don't work." Despite facing criticism as a whistleblower, Manning views herself simply as "me," highlighting her desire to be seen as an individual rather than a label.

As she navigates life outside of prison, Manning has documented her experiences on social media, sharing moments of everyday life, including gaming and spending time with friends. While adjusting to her new surroundings can be challenging, Manning remains optimistic, acknowledging that she has been given a second chance and is determined to make the most of it.

reference: – Chelsea Manning believed she had a "responsibility to the public" and didn't think she was risking national security when she leaked a trove of classified documents, the soldier said in her first interview since being released from a military prison last month, per the AP. The 29-year-old formerly known as Bradley Manning said in a pre-taped interview broadcast Friday on ABC's Good Morning America that she was prompted to give the 700,000 military and State Department documents to WikiLeaks because of the human toll of the "death, destruction, and mayhem" she saw as an Army intelligence analyst in Iraq. She told ABC that she has "accepted responsibility" for her actions. "No one told me to do this. No one directed me to do this. This is me. It's on me," she said. Manning was released from a military prison in Fort Leavenworth, Kansas, on May 18 after serving seven years of a 35-year sentence, which was commuted by former President Barack Obama in his final days in office. Obama said in January he felt justice had been served. She hasn't spoken to Obama since her release, but emotionally thanked him in the interview for giving her "another chance." Manning also touched on her struggles dealing with her gender transition while in prison. She says she tried to kill herself twice behind bars and fought for the hormone treatments she says keep her alive. Manning remains in the Army, but is off duty while she appeals her court-martial conviction. "I have nothing but utmost respect for the military," she said in the interview, per ABC.

final_summary:
A US court has ordered Facebook and its subsidiary Oculus to pay $500 million in damages to Zenimax, a video game publisher, after a three-week trial. The verdict stems from allegations that Oculus breached a contract and infringed on Zenimax's copyrights and trademarks when developing its virtual reality headset, the Rift. The jury found that Oculus co-founder Palmer Luckey broke a non-disclosure agreement, resulting in the significant payout. 

Zenimax had initially sought $2 billion in damages, but the jury's decision still represents a substantial blow to Facebook, particularly considering the company's recent acquisition of Oculus in 2014 for $2 billion. The ruling may also raise concerns about the ownership and development of intellectual property within the tech industry.

Despite the setback, Facebook's overall financial performance remains robust. The company's fourth-quarter profits more than doubled to $3.6 billion, driven largely by a 53% increase in advertising revenue. This growth contributed to a stellar quarter for Facebook, with the company reporting record revenue and user growth. Facebook's net profit for the full year increased by 177% to $10.2 billion.

The company's success is attributed to its mobile advertising business, which now accounts for 84% of ad revenue, up from 80% in the previous year. Facebook's focus on video content is also expected to drive future growth, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg announcing plans to invest in short-form, original videos, including episodic content produced weekly. This move could potentially threaten established players such as YouTube and Netflix.

In addition to its financial achievements, Facebook announced that it is inching closer to reaching 2 billion users, with approximately 1.86 billion people accessing the service monthly as of December 31. However, the company's expansion into China appears unlikely in the near term, with Zuckerberg stating that any entry into the market would need to be done in a way that aligns with Facebook's long-term goals.

The verdict against Facebook comes at a time when the company is facing other challenges, including criticism over the spread of fake news on its platform and the need to address issues related to user data protection. Despite these concerns, Facebook's strong financial performance suggests that the company remains a dominant force in the tech industry.

reference: – Facebook is going to have to cough up $500 million in real money for unlawfully using another company's virtual reality technology. A jury decided Wednesday that VR pioneer Oculus, which Facebook bought for $2 billion in 2014, infringed copyright and trademarks when it used code from games maker Zenimax to launch its own VR headset, the BBC reports. Zenimax had accused the company of stealing trade secrets, saying Oculus headsets were "primitive" before a former Zenimax employee used his insider knowledge to improve them. Facebook COO Sheryl Sandberg tells CNBC that the company is "disappointed in certain elements of" the ruling, but Facebook notes that while the jury found the company guilty of copyright infringement, failure to comply with a non-disclosure agreement, and misuse of Oculus trademarks, it did not find it guilty of stealing secrets. She says the $500 million verdict, which Facebook is considering appealing, is "not material to our financials." Reuters reports that Facebook released its latest financial results Wednesday, showing a $3.57 billion profit in the last quarter, up $2 billion year-over-year.

final_summary: Twitter users have the ability to share their geographical location alongside their tweets, allowing others to see where they are posting from. This feature can be accessed through various methods, including adding location information directly from the Twitter website or using third-party applications that integrate with the platform. Additionally, users retain control over their location data, as they can choose to delete their tweet location history at any time.

reference: – Katy Perry is dismissing rumors that she is getting sued for using part of the Beach Boys' "California Girls" in her song "California Gurls". "No one is suing anyone," Perry tweeted, calling the reports "once again" an example of the media's tendency to "fabricate & exaggerate stories to get hits or sell papers." Although she isn't getting sued, Beach Boys published Rondor Music isn't too happy about "California Gurls," Billboard reports. "Using the words or melody in a new song taken from an original work is not appropriate under any circumstances, particularly from one as well known and iconic as 'California Girls,'" a Rondor rep says. The company is not planning to sue Perry, but it has has filed a warning in the form of a diminutive claim. "It is up to the six writers and various publishers of 'California Gurls' to decide whether they honor the claim or not," the rep continues. Check out the video—which features Snoop Dogg, who's the one actually paraphrasing the Beach Boys—on YouTube.

final_summary:
Bob Dylan's Nobel Prize in Literature lecture, delivered in June, drew attention for its unique structure and content. The lecture discussed three influential literary works from Dylan's childhood: Herman Melville's Moby-Dick, Erich Maria Remarque's All Quiet on the Western Front, and Homer's Odyssey. However, a closer examination of the Moby-Dick section revealed that several passages bore striking similarities to SparkNotes, an online study guide. Writer Andrea Pitzer discovered that over a dozen sentences in the lecture matched SparkNotes descriptions, often verbatim.

This is not the first time Dylan has been accused of borrowing from other sources. Throughout his career, he has been accused of plagiarism, with instances dating back to his debut album, The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan, in 1963. Critics have pointed out similarities between his songs and the works of other artists, including Lead Belly and Henry Thomas. In 2006, researchers found that snippets of lyrics from his album Modern Times were inspired by the works of Ovid and Henry Timrod.

Dylan has consistently maintained that he operates within the rules of his art form, citing the rich tradition of quotation in folk and jazz music. In a 2012 Rolling Stone interview, he stated, "In folk and jazz, quotation is a rich and enriching tradition... It's an old thing—it's part of the tradition. It goes way back."

However, some experts disagree. University of Minnesota music professor Alex Lubet suggested that the use of SparkNotes in the Nobel lecture was not typical of Dylan's usual creative process. Cultural critic David Yaffe, a Syracuse University professor, acknowledged the alleged plagiarism but argued that it did not diminish the value of the lecture. He noted that Dylan's speech was a postmodern work of art, and the use of collage techniques was acceptable in this context.

The controversy surrounding Dylan's Nobel lecture raises questions about the nature of artistic expression and the boundaries of intellectual property. While some see Dylan's actions as a legitimate exercise in creative freedom, others view it as a form of plagiarism. The Swedish Academy has yet to respond to the allegations, and it remains unclear whether Dylan's Nobel Prize will be rescinded.

In a separate interview, Dylan discussed his views on creativity, inspiration, and the role of the artist. He emphasized the importance of drawing from various sources, stating, "You make everything yours. We all do it." He also touched on the topic of transfiguration, a concept he explored in his 2004 autobiography, Chronicles: Volume One. According to Dylan, transfiguration refers to a mysterious process of transformation that allows individuals to transcend their circumstances and tap into a higher level of consciousness.

Dylan's conversation also delved into his views on history, politics, and social issues. He expressed his concerns about the persistence of racism in America, stating that the country's founding on the backs of slaves has created a legacy of inequality. He also spoke about the challenges faced by President Barack Obama, suggesting that some of the opposition to the president was rooted in racial resentment.

Throughout the conversation, Dylan demonstrated a willingness to engage with complex ideas and themes, often using his music as a vehicle for exploring these topics. Despite the controversy surrounding his Nobel lecture, Dylan remains a prolific and innovative artist, continuing to push the boundaries of creative expression.
