reference: – He has been convicted of killing two and attempting to murder two more—and he could actually be "Germany's worst post-war killer," reports the BBC. Niels Hoegel, 40, was sentenced to life in prison in 2015 for those crimes, but prosecutors voiced their belief that the nurse had killed many more while working at clinics in Oldenburg (from 1999 to 2002) and Delmenhorst (from 2003 to 2005), reports the AP. Now police are giving a sense of just how many more: at least 84. Hoegel said at trial that he wanted to play the hero and drugged his patients so that he could then resuscitate them and win praise. Except a commission established three years ago to determine the breadth of his crimes has found that many patients ended up receiving lethal doses of drugs that brought on heart failure or destroyed the circulatory system. The BBC reports that 134 bodies have been exhumed and tested for traces of the drug, but "it is simply not possible to say how many people were killed," per Oldenburg police chief Johann Kuhme. That's in part because many of his patients were cremated. The AFP quotes chief police investigator Arne Schmidt as describing the death toll as "unique in the history of the German republic." There is "evidence for at least 90 murders," he said at a press conference, "and at least as many [suspected] cases again that can no longer be proven." Kuhme faulted the clinics for having "hesitated to alert authorities," saying the death toll could have been much lower if they had. New charges against Hoegel will likely be filed next year. (This nurse says she would get a "red surge" before killing one of her patients.)

final_summary:
In a surprise turn of events during Mexico's first televised presidential debate, a model named Julia Orayen, known for her work as a former Playboy playmate, made headlines not for her role as an edecan, a traditional hostess position, but for her revealing outfit. Orayen, described as "well-endowed" and wearing a tight white dress with a plunging neckline, was tasked with drawing lots to determine the speaking order among the four presidential candidates. Her appearance sparked a mixture of reactions from journalists and viewers alike, with some expressing shock and amusement while others criticized the perceived distraction from the debate's purpose.

As the debate unfolded, Orayen's brief appearance quickly became a trending topic on social media, with many users jokingly declaring her the real winner of the evening. Presidential candidate Josefina Vazquez Mota expressed her discomfort with Orayen's attire, stating that it was "not in keeping with the seriousness of the debate." Meanwhile, TV producer Jesus Tapia defended his decision to hire Orayen, citing her short stature and pleasant demeanor as reasons for choosing her for the role.

However, the Electoral Commission, which organized the debate, issued an apology for the "production error" and stated that the focus should have remained on the candidates and their policies rather than Orayen's outfit. Electoral Commission member Lorenzo Cordova condemned the situation as an "outrageous stereotype," highlighting the need for greater respect and professionalism in high-profile events.

Orayen herself expressed surprise at the intense reaction to her appearance, stating that she had simply followed instructions to wear a white dress and was unaware of the controversy it would spark. Despite the brief duration of her appearance – lasting only 24 seconds – Orayen's impact on the debate was undeniable, sparking a national conversation about the intersection of politics, fashion, and societal expectations in Mexico.

reference: – A televised Mexican presidential debate looked more like an episode of Wheel of Fortune thanks to a babealicious production assistant's cleavage-baring gown. She was so eye-popping that Mexico's election commission apologized. "We are sorry about the production error associated with the clothing of one of the assistants, and want to apologize to the citizens and candidates," said a commission statement. An independent production company hired the former Playboy playmate—who held a box with paper slips for the candidates to pick from that determined who would go first—and she was allowed to choose her own dress, reports the BBC. In fact, such women are fixtures in many political, entertainment, and business events in Mexico, and are considered a nod to the machismo culture, notes the Los Angeles Times. Known as edecans, they're frequently long-haired, beautiful women in sexy dresses and heels who serve as hostesses. An election commission member blasted the "outrageous stereotype" that overshadowed the presidential debate. The tight-fitting gown was "not in keeping with the seriousness of the debate," added presidential candidate Josefina Vazquez Mota.

final_summary:
In Larimer County, Colorado, a disturbing trend of youth suicides has emerged, with two middle school students taking their own lives within a span of a week. According to the Larimer County coroner's office, the total number of suicides in the area has reached 77 this year, including three cases involving children under the age of 18. This marks a concerning escalation, particularly given that last year's total of 83 suicides already represented a 25% increase over 2013's count.

The latest incidents involve an 11-year-old girl from Lincoln Middle School, who passed away on November 16, and an 11-year-old boy from Blevins Middle School, who died on Saturday. While authorities have not officially confirmed the causes of the deaths, the family of the girl, identified as Ariana Cordova, has come forward stating that bullying played a significant role in her tragic decision. Ariana's sister, Isabella, revealed that her sibling began experiencing bullying in second grade, which continued throughout her academic career, despite efforts to address the issue through counseling and programs.

Isabella expressed concerns that while schools may claim to prioritize anti-bullying initiatives, the reality falls short, with many instances of harassment going unaddressed. She emphasized that punishment alone would not solve the problem, instead advocating for genuine change and increased support for students struggling with emotional distress. The family has requested donations to organizations focused on preventing bullying in schools, echoing the sentiments expressed in Ariana's obituary, which urged readers to "validate each other's feelings" and recognize that "we're not alone in this world."

In response to the tragedies, the Poudre School District has offered additional counseling services at both schools, building upon existing efforts to promote student mental health. However, the community remains divided on how to effectively tackle the complex issue of bullying, with some calling for stricter consequences for perpetrators and others advocating for a more holistic approach that prioritizes empathy and understanding.

A candlelight vigil has been scheduled for both students, set to take place outside Colorado State University's B.W. Pickett Equine Center in Fort Collins, with the families seeking to raise funds for unexpected medical and funeral expenses. As the community grapples with the aftermath of these devastating losses, the Cordova family's story serves as a poignant reminder of the urgent need for meaningful action to prevent future tragedies and create a safer, more supportive environment for vulnerable students.

reference: – These are troubling times for parents in Fort Collins, Colo., where two 11-year-old children took their own lives within days of each other. The suicide of a girl in sixth grade at Lincoln Middle School last Monday was followed by the Saturday night suicide of a boy who attended Blevins Middle School. Both schools are in the Poudre School District, and authorities are investigating whether the two deaths are linked, reports the Coloradoan. The girl's family says she suffered from bullying and depression, but one of the boy's friends tells the Coloradoan that he was a well-liked prankster; the classmate doesn't believe the boy was bullied. A police spokeswoman tells the Coloradoan that they have "not received any information that bullying was a contributing factor in either incident." But the girl's older sister tells KDVR that the 11-year-old had been bullied since the second grade and the family didn't discover it until she came home with a broken arm. She says the family wants the death to bring about changes in how schools deal with bullying—and to send the message to bullied children that they should always ask for help. "If they're not going to listen to me, then imagine my little sister dancing in the sky wherever she is looking down on you saying that it's going to be OK. You can make it through this," she says. The Denver Post reports that Larimer County has logged 77 suicides year-to-date, including these two and one more of a child under age 18. The year prior, there were two under-18 suicides in the county. (Read about how a mother survived her son's high-profile suicide.)

final_summary:
A heated debate surrounding the National Football League (NFL) and the national anthem has escalated following President Donald Trump's comments urging NFL owners to fire players who kneel during the anthem to protest police brutality and racial inequality. The controversy began when Colin Kaepernick, a former San Francisco 49ers quarterback, started kneeling during the anthem last season to draw attention to systemic issues affecting African Americans. While some conservatives viewed his actions as disrespectful to the troops and the nation, liberals saw it as a courageous act of defiance against racism.

Since then, more players have joined the protest, including those from the Baltimore Ravens and Jacksonville Jaguars, who took a knee during their game in London. Despite Trump's criticism, the NFL players, owners, and coaches have largely condemned his comments, with many choosing to stay seated, lock arms, or take a knee during the anthem. Even some of Trump's allies, such as Patriots owner Robert Kraft and Jaguars owner Shahid Khan, have expressed disappointment with the president's stance.

Trump's involvement in the debate has sparked a broader discussion about the role of the president in cultural wars and the limits of free speech. Critics argue that Trump's comments are an attempt to distract from his legislative agenda, which has stalled, and to whip up his core supporters. Others see it as a continuation of his pattern of using divisive rhetoric to pit middle Americans against various elites.

The NFL has seen a decline in ratings due to the national anthem protests, with an overwhelming majority of Americans believing that politics should be kept off the field. However, the league's response to Trump's comments has been mixed, with some owners and players choosing to defy the president and others remaining silent. The controversy has also highlighted the tension between the NFL and its fans, with some hardcore fans booing players who participate in the protest.

As the debate continues, it remains unclear whether Trump's intervention will ultimately harm or help the NFL. While some argue that his comments will alienate fans and lead to further decline in ratings, others believe that the controversy will galvanize his base and boost the league's popularity. Ultimately, the incident serves as a reminder of the complex and often contentious relationship between sports, politics, and patriotism in modern America.

reference: – NFL players' reaction to President Trump in regard to the national anthem was, not surprisingly, a big topic on Monday's op-ed pages. Here's a sampling: Wall Street Journal: The newspaper's editorial sounds fed up with everybody involved, blaming the left for initially exploiting the Colin Kaepernick situation, then Trump for exploiting the blowback. It adds that players who kneel shouldn't be surprised to hear boos, because "disrespecting the national anthem puts partisanship above a symbol of nationhood that thousands have died for." But ultimately, the real losers in all this "are the millions of Americans who would rather cheer for their teams on Sunday as a respite from work and the other divisions of American life." Daily Caller: In a column, Scott Greer writes that Trump seems to be relishing his role as an "Oval Office culture warrior," perhaps because his legislative agenda keeps hitting roadblocks. This fight may pay off: "In the fight over kneeling, Trump is facing off against out-of-touch athletes who appear to be opposing the flag and the troops. ESPN and most political pundits may think he is a dummy for engaging in this fight, but if [the] NFL continues to lose fans over it, Trump will come out as the victor." New York Times: Its editorial pits Trump as the villain here and Kaepernick as the hero of sorts. The QB still can't find a team, but the weekend protests "might well have been a huge victory for free speech and the cause of racial justice he has so bravely espoused." The editorial gives the last word to LeBron James, who tweeted of Trump that "going to White House was a great honor until you showed up!” Washington Post: The editors don't care what Trump thinks of Kaepernick. But "when his response to protest is to question patriotism rather than engage on the issue of unequal policing—then it is Mr. Trump who 'disrespects our Flag & our Country.'" USA Today: Its editorial faults Trump as "divider in chief" and says his use of the presidential bully pulpit to fault those exercising their right to dissent is "simply indecent." But an op-ed at the newspaper by former Nebraska Congressman Jon Christensen praises Trump's move. "When millionaire athletes refuse to honor America and pay homage to the men and women who died for our freedom, and instead, choose to use their field of occupation to make a statement, then I can choose to turn the channel." They do indeed deserve to be fired, he adds.

final_summary:
In the third quarter of 2015, Democratic front-runner Hillary Clinton's campaign narrowly outpaced Bernie Sanders', raising approximately $28 million compared to around $26 million for the Vermont senator. This development highlights Sanders' growing momentum, particularly in early-voting states New Hampshire and Iowa, where he surpassed Clinton in polling. Despite the close margin, Clinton's overall fundraising machine remains robust, having collected nearly $75 million in six months, including $47.5 million in the second quarter. In contrast, Sanders raised $15 million in his initial few months.

Clinton's campaign had anticipated a decrease in fundraising during the summer due to controversy surrounding her email practices at the State Department and declining poll numbers in key states. However, they expressed gratitude for the support of hundreds of thousands of donors, which helped them achieve a record-breaking $75 million in the first two quarters. A joint fundraising committee with the Democratic National Committee and state parties contributed over $3 million to the effort.

Sanders' impressive online fundraising capabilities were demonstrated when he raised $2.07 million on September 30 alone, sparking concern among party establishment members regarding his ability to compete financially against a well-funded Republican opponent in the general election. Nevertheless, his fundraising prowess challenges assumptions about his viability. Clinton's allies find solace in her affiliation with super PACs, such as Priorities USA Action, which had raised $40 million by mid-August, whereas Sanders refused to associate with similar groups.

The campaigns' financial disclosures for the period ending September 30 revealed additional insights. Clinton's operation reported that 93% of her third-quarter donations came in increments of less than $100, while Sanders' campaign claimed to have surpassed the one-million-contributor mark in the third quarter. As the full disclosure reports become available, scrutiny will focus not only on the origin of the funds but also how each campaign allocated its resources.

reference: – Bernie Sanders is raising a lot more money than many people expected, and he's doing it a lot faster. His campaign announced just before the Wednesday filing deadline that it has received more than a million online donations, making him the first 2016 candidate to pass that milestone and the first ever to pass it so early, reports the Wall Street Journal, which notes that the Obama campaigns got there in February 2008 and October 2011. According to the filing, Sanders' fundraising is rapidly catching up with Hillary Clinton's, with the Vermont senator raising $25 million in the third quarter of this year, not far off the $28 million his rival raised during the same period, although her second-quarter numbers were much higher, CNN reports. Most of Clinton's cash in the third quarter came from the 58 fundraising events she headlined that asked donors to pay $2,700, CNN reports, while almost all of Sanders' take came from the online operation, which had an average donation of $24.86. But he has had to spend money to make money: The New York Times estimates that the Sanders campaign has already spent $15 million, mostly on hiring staff and on online fundraising, without spending much on commercials or polling. Politico calls his haul "eye-popping" and notes that it undercuts arguments that Sanders couldn't compete with a well-funded Republican. Clinton, however, has affiliated super PACs on her side and Sanders shuns such groups. (Polls show that Clinton is falling behind in New Hampshire.)

final_summary:
On a sunny day in Dallas, former US President George W. Bush officially broke ground on his presidential library at Southern Methodist University, marking a significant milestone in his post-presidency. The event drew a star-studded crowd of over 2,500 guests, including hundreds of veterans from his administration, such as former Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice and First Lady Laura Bush. In attendance was also former Vice President Dick Cheney, who made a rare public appearance since undergoing heart surgery earlier in the year. 

Cheney, appearing gaunt and frail, used the opportunity to defend Bush's presidency and express his admiration for his former boss. He stated that two years after leaving office, judgments about Bush's tenure were becoming more measured, and history was starting to vindicate his decisions. Bush reciprocated the sentiment, praising Cheney as the right choice for his running mate in 2000 and commending him as a great vice president.

The groundbreaking ceremony served as a platform for Bush to unveil his vision for the George W. Bush Presidential Center, which will house a public policy institute focused on advancing four key causes: human freedom, global health, economic growth, and education reform. Bush emphasized that the center would serve as a hub for his continued public service and a means to shape his presidential legacy.

In a nod to his recent memoir, "Decision Points," Bush addressed the controversy surrounding his consideration of replacing Cheney as his running mate in 2004. He unequivocally expressed his support for Cheney, stating that he was the right choice then and remains a close friend. The event marked the first time Bush and Cheney had appeared together in public since leaving office in January 2009.

Throughout the ceremony, Bush maintained a relaxed demeanor, winking at the crowd and shaking hands with his friends and advisors. His speech highlighted his commitment to standing up for the principles of his presidency, even if it meant avoiding criticism of his successor. Bush's wife, Laura, concluded the event by emphasizing the importance of promoting women's rights worldwide through her newly launched initiative.

The $300 million Bush Presidential Library is expected to become a prominent institution for public discourse and policy development, solidifying Bush's presence in the national conversation. With the groundbreaking complete, the stage is set for the grand opening of the library, where Cheney hinted he would share further insights into the Bush presidency.

reference: – George W. Bush and Dick Cheney were best buds again today, giving each other high praise as they shared a stage for the first time since their final tense days in office, reports ABC News. The groundbreaking at Bush's presidential library at SMU also marked a rare appearance by Cheney since his heart trouble earlier this year. He appeared "much thinner" and used a cane, and didn't stick around long after the ceremony, reports the New York Times. Cheney on Bush: “Two years after you left office, judgments are a little more measured than they were. When times have been tough or the critics have been loud, you’ve always said you had faith in history’s judgment, and history is beginning to come around.” Bush on Cheney: "As I stand here, there is no doubt in my mind he was the right pick then, he was a great vice president of the United States, and I’m proud to call him friend."

final_summary:
On Mother's Day, May 8, 22-year-old Ashley R. Hautzenrader, from Davenport, gave birth to a baby girl in a bathroom at the University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics (UIHC) in Iowa City. According to a UI Department of Public Safety criminal complaint, Hautzenrader had not known she was pregnant prior to delivering the baby. After giving birth, Hautzenrader believed the newborn was stillborn due to its lack of crying, and attempted to dispose of the infant by flushing it down the toilet. However, the baby was found alive in a trash can shortly after delivery by UIHC employees, who discovered it wrapped in a pillowcase.

Hautzenrader subsequently confessed to placing the baby in the trash can after initially trying to flush it down the toilet. Following the discovery of the baby, police officers confronted Hautzenrader, who admitted to her actions. As a result, she faces a single count of child endangerment, an aggravated misdemeanor carrying a maximum sentence of two years in prison.

In a bizarre twist, Hautzenrader had previously been involved in a paternity and child support recovery case in Scott County in May 2015, which raises questions about her knowledge of pregnancy and parenting responsibilities. Notably, Iowa has a Safe Haven Law allowing parents to leave newborns up to 14 days old at hospitals without prosecution, but this option appears to have been overlooked in Hautzenrader's situation.

Following her arrest, a judge granted Hautzenrader supervised release during her initial appearance in Johnson County court, and a public defender was assigned to represent her. While the current condition of the baby remains unknown, UIHC officials cited federal health privacy laws in declining to provide further details regarding the infant's well-being or Hautzenrader's status as a patient.

reference: – A 22-year-old woman allegedly tried to flush her newborn baby down a toilet before abandoning it in a trash can at an Iowa hospital on Mother's Day, the Iowa City Press-Citizen reports. According to the Gazette, Ashley Hautzenrader delivered the baby into a toilet in a hospital bathroom Sunday night. Police say Hautzenrader later told them she wasn't aware she was pregnant before entering the bathroom, KCRG reports. Hautzenrader allegedly tried to flush the baby down the toilet because it wasn't crying and she thought it was dead. Police say she then placed it in a pillowcase and put it in the garbage. Hautzenrader cleaned the bathroom and left. A hospital spokesperson says the newborn was found "shortly after delivery." Its current condition is unclear. The hospital won't say if Hautzenrader was a patient or why she was there. She was arrested Monday and charged with child endangerment, a misdemeanor punishable by up to two years in prison. She was released from jail and placed on supervised release. Under an Iowa law, Hautzenrader could have legally turned her newborn over to the hospital.

final_summary:
The 89th Academy Awards nominations were announced on Tuesday, revealing a mix of expected and unexpected snubs. Despite its muted box office performance, Martin Scorsese's passion project "Silence" surprisingly secured only one nomination for best cinematography, leaving many to wonder if the director's reputation has taken a hit. Amy Adams, a five-time Oscar nominee, was overlooked for her understated performance in "Arrival", which garnered eight nominations, including best picture. 

In a shocking omission, "Finding Dory" failed to secure a nomination for best animated feature, despite its stellar reviews and over $1 billion at the global box office. Denzel Washington, a seven-time Oscar nominee, was edged out of the best director category by Mel Gibson and Denis Villeneuve, despite his critically acclaimed performance in "Fences". Annette Bening, a four-time Oscar nominee and member of the Academy's Board of Governors, was also snubbed for her role in "20th Century Women".

Other notable snubs include Taraji P. Henson for her powerful performance as Katherine Johnson in "Hidden Figures", which secured a nomination for best picture but not for Henson herself; Hugh Grant for his role in "Florence Foster Jenkins"; and Aaron Taylor-Johnson for his performance in "Nocturnal Animals". The film itself was also overlooked, despite its critical acclaim and recent wins at the BAFTAs and Writers Guild of America.

Ryan Reynolds' "Deadpool" was another surprise snub, despite its unconventional awards campaign and Golden Globes inclusion. The film's absence from the best picture category was seen as a blow to the idea that blockbusters deserve recognition. Other notable snubs include "Sully", which was left out of major categories despite its box office success and Clint Eastwood's involvement; "Trolls", which was overlooked for best animated feature; and "Eye in the Sky", which failed to gain traction with voters despite its timely subject matter and Helen Mirren's performance.

On the other hand, Viggo Mortensen secured a nomination for his role in "Captain Fantastic", marking a rare moment of joy for the often-overlooked actor. The nominations also highlighted a more diverse range of actors, with seven actors of color securing spots, including Denzel Washington, Viola Davis, and Octavia Spencer.

reference: – From #OscarsSoWhite to #OscarsLessWhite: The 2017 Academy Awards nominations included seven actors of color plus diverse nominees in other categories, CNN reports. That's not to say there weren't any snubs, as rounded up by People, Deadline, and USA Today. Some of the big ones: Taraji P. Henson: Hidden Figures was nominated for Best Picture, but lead actress Henson failed to snag a nomination. Janelle Monáe wasn't nominated either; Octavia Spencer was. Pharrell Williams: His Hidden Figures score was nominated for a Golden Globe, but not an Oscar. The Birth of a Nation: Nate Parker's movie about the 1831 slave rebellion led by Nat Turner debuted with Oscar dreams, and Academy Award nominations looked likely at first. But, likely thanks in part to sexual assault allegations against Parker that resurfaced, it was shut out by the Oscars. Denzel Washington. He got an acting nod for Fences, but not a directing one. Amy Adams: The Arrival actress didn't make it into the Best Actress race. Hugh Grant: He received SAG, BAFTA, and Golden Globe nominations for his role in Florence Foster Jenkins, but wasn't able to score his first Oscar nomination. Finding Dory: Despite a massive box office haul and positive reviews, the Pixar flick didn't make it into the Best Animated Feature Film category. Sully: Another movie with a huge box office take and great reviews, the Clint Eastwood flick failed to snag a Best Picture nod. One surprise nominee? Mel Gibson.

final_summary:
A 14-year-old oil leak from a destroyed offshore platform in southeast Louisiana has prompted the US Coast Guard to issue a stern warning to the platform's owner, Taylor Energy, ordering them to develop a new containment plan and face fines of up to $40,000 per day if they fail to comply. The leak, originating from the Taylor Energy Co.'s MC-20 Saratoga platform, has been estimated to release between 10,500 and 29,000 gallons of oil per day, making it one of the largest and longest-running oil spills in North America.

According to the Coast Guard, the new containment system "must eliminate the surface sheen and avoid the deficiencies associated with prior containment systems." However, Taylor Energy disputes the new leak estimates, claiming they are "ridiculous" and "based on erroneous science," and argue that the sheen on the water's surface is caused by oil and gas bubbling up from the oil-saturated seafloor rather than leaky wells.

Environmental groups, including the Gulf Restoration Network and Skytruth, have long disputed Taylor Energy's claims that their containment efforts have limited pollution to a mere trickle. In 2013, Skytruth measured the sheen at over 20 miles long, while the Gulf Monitoring Consortium estimated a daily release rate of between 100 and 400 gallons. Federal regulators did not begin investigating the leak until 2016, when Taylor Energy sued the government over the remaining $432 million from a $666 million trust fund set aside to pay for the leak response.

In a 2017 legal filing, the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement estimated the leak's range at 42 to 2,300 gallons per day, but the government has since cited a vastly larger daily estimate of up to 29,000 gallons. The Coast Guard's order is seen as long overdue by environmental advocates, who point out that the time to clean up the leak was 14 years ago.

Christopher Reddy, a marine scientist hired by Taylor Energy to assess the site, suggests that the sheen is more likely caused by contaminated sediment rather than leaky wells. Sediment sampling has revealed traces of drilling fluid, which is consistent with the presence of drilling fluid in the sheen on the water's surface. Reddy warns that any restoration or cleanup work at the site could release a large quantity of oil, potentially releasing up to 150 million gallons of oil over the past 14 years – comparable to the estimated 168 million gallons spilled during the 2010 BP oil disaster.

While the BP spill's impact was extensively studied, the environmental damage from the Taylor Energy leak has largely gone unexamined. Dustin Renaud, communications director for the Gulf Restoration Network, notes that even smaller leaks can have significant environmental impacts, and calls for further investigation into the effects of the Taylor Energy leak.

reference: – A massive cleanup effort has been ordered on a quietly devastating oil spill that's been contaminating the Gulf of Mexico for 14 years. The Washington Post reports the US Coast Guard has mandated Taylor Energy "institute a … system to capture, contain, or remove oil" from the affected site off the Louisiana coast, infused with up to 30,000 gallons (about 700 barrels) of oil a day since Hurricane Ivan destroyed a company oil platform in 2004, per DOJ estimates. Contractors commissioned by Taylor Energy had previously said the oil spill was minimal, at the most around 2,000 gallons a day. If the company doesn't comply with the Coast Guard's new decree, it could be hit with a fine of up to $40,000 per day. To put it in context, the BP Deepwater Horizon spill dumped 4 million barrels of oil into the Gulf before it was capped after 87 days in July 2010; the Taylor spill could be at 3.5 million barrels. The Taylor spill remained hidden from the public by both the company and the Coast Guard for six years after the hurricane. It was discovered only when nonprofits and environmental groups investigating the BP spill came upon oil slicks that didn't appear to be related to that particular incident. Taylor Energy—which contends the oil "sheens" on the surface of the water aren't from leaking wells, but from oil and gas floating up from the oil-heavy seafloor—says full containment of the leak could cost more than $1 billion, the Times-Picayune reports. Still, "the time to clean this up was 14 years ago," a rep for the Gulf Restoration Network says. "Taylor Energy has shown nothing but negligence all this time." (The largest tanker oil spill in three decades happened earlier this year.)

final_summary:
A long-awaited decision has been made regarding the contentious proposal to construct a four-lane highway beneath the iconic Stonehenge monument in England. After nearly three decades of planning and repeated setbacks, the UK government has announced plans to build a 1.8-mile tunnel along the A303 trunk road, which currently bisects the ancient site. The tunnel aims to alleviate traffic congestion and mitigate the visual and auditory impact of the existing road on the World Heritage Site, attracting over 1.3 million visitors annually.

According to the Department for Transport, the tunnel will significantly reduce traffic noise pollution, allowing visitors to focus on the mystique of the prehistoric monument without being distracted by the constant flow of vehicles. The project is part of a broader £2.45 billion investment in regional infrastructure, aimed at cutting congestion and improving travel times.

However, the decision has sparked controversy among archaeologists and conservationists, who fear that the tunnel will irreparably harm the surrounding landscape and compromise the integrity of the site. The Stonehenge Alliance, a campaign group, argues that a longer tunnel, approximately 2.7 miles in length, is necessary to prevent damage to the site and its setting. They point to a 2015 petition garnering 17,500 signatures in support of their stance.

English Heritage and the National Trust have expressed their backing for the longest possible tunnel, citing the need to preserve the tranquility and appearance of the site. On the other hand, supporters of the current plan, including the National Trust's Director General, Helen Ghosh, believe that the tunnel will ultimately enhance the visitor experience by reducing the visual impact of the road and restoring the natural ambiance of the area.

The debate centers on the delicate balance between preserving the historical significance of the site and addressing the practical issues associated with the existing road layout. Archaeologists argue that recent discoveries have expanded our understanding of Stonehenge as part of a larger, multilayered ceremonial space, encompassing various monuments and earthworks. While acknowledging the importance of this new perspective, critics contend that it should not overshadow the need to create a more accessible and enjoyable experience for visitors.

The history of proposals for a tunnel beneath Stonehenge dates back to 1995, with previous iterations facing criticism and eventual abandonment due to concerns over environmental impact and rising construction costs. The current plan, initially supported by the coalition government in 2014, has undergone revisions and consultations, with a public exhibition scheduled to run until March 5 to gather feedback from stakeholders. Despite ongoing opposition, the UK government remains committed to pushing forward with the project, aiming to transform the A303 and improve the overall visitor experience at one of Europe's most recognizable prehistoric sites.

The current plan, initially supported by the coalition government in 2014, has undergone revisions and consultations, with a public exhibition scheduled to run until March 5 to gather feedback from stakeholders. Despite ongoing opposition, the UK government remains committed to pushing forward with the project, aiming to transform the A303 and improve the overall visitor experience at one of Europe's most recognizable prehistoric sites.

reference: – It could finally be tunnel time at Stonehenge. NBC News reports that the 1.3 million annual visitors to the famous landmark have been dealing with increasing traffic on a nearby highway for years. Officials first proposed a tunnel to cut down on noisy and unsightly traffic yards away on the A303 nearly 30 years ago, but it never materialized. Now the British government has finalized plans to build that tunnel, according to the BBC. The 1.8-mile tunnel would include four lanes and cost an estimated $1.7 billion. The secretary of England's Department of Transport says the tunnel will reduce traffic and travel times near Stonehenge. But the tunnel remains controversial, with Stonehenge fans weighing in on both sides. One supporter says tourists will "once again be able to hear the sounds of skylarks" instead of traffic. And UNESCO, which lists Stonehenge as a World Heritage Site, is in favor of the tunnel. But the Council for British Archaeology is against it, the Guardian reports. The council's president says the area around Stonehenge is the "world's most significant and best-preserved stone-age landscape." That's why the Stonehenge Alliance also opposes the current tunnel plan. The group wants the tunnel length increased to at least 2.7 miles to reduce disruption to the immediate area. Officials are accepting public comment on the tunnel through the beginning of March.

final_summary:
On February 1, 2017, US National Security Adviser Michael Flynn announced that the Trump administration was putting Iran "on notice" following a recent ballistic missile test by the country. The test, which took place on Sunday, was deemed a "provocative move" by the US, particularly given Iran's history of backing Houthi rebels in Yemen who have attacked US allies. Flynn stated that the missile launch defied a UN Security Council resolution that prohibits Iran from testing ballistic missiles designed to deliver nuclear weapons.

The UN Security Council held an emergency meeting on Tuesday to discuss the matter, with US Ambassador Nikki Haley condemning the test as "unacceptable." Iranian Foreign Minister Mohammad Javad Zarif responded by stating that Tehran would never use its ballistic missiles to attack another country, while also emphasizing that the missiles were not part of the nuclear accord reached with world powers in 2015.

In a statement, Flynn criticized the Obama administration's agreements with Iran, labeling them "weak and ineffective," and suggesting that the new administration would take a harder line. He pointed to reported attacks on US allies by Iran-backed Houthi rebels in the Red Sea as evidence of Iran's "destabilizing behavior across the Middle East." Flynn's comments were seen as a warning to Iran, although the exact nature of the consequences remained unclear.

White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer declined to elaborate on the implications of Flynn's statement, while national security officials indicated that the administration was considering a range of options, including economic sanctions and supporting forces that counter Iranian action in the region. They emphasized that the missile test and other destabilizing behavior by Iran were separate from the nuclear deal, and that the administration would not say whether Trump wanted to pull out of the agreement.

Iranian officials responded to Flynn's warning by asserting that the country's missile program was solely for defensive purposes and that it would not seek permission from any country or international organization for its development. The Iranian parliament also reaffirmed its support for the country's missile program, calling international condemnation of the tests "illogical."

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu echoed Flynn's sentiments, calling the missile test a "flagrant violation" of the UN resolution and urging Trump to renew sanctions against Iran. The National Iranian American Council criticized Flynn's statement as "reckless," warning that it risked retaliatory behavior and potentially escalating tensions into war.

final_summary:
Miley Cyrus revealed in an April interview with Billboard magazine that she has been sober from marijuana for three weeks, stating that she wants to surround herself with people who inspire her to grow and evolve. This decision came after realizing that people who use marijuana are not typically the ones pushing her to become a better version of herself. Cyrus expressed her desire to be "super clear and sharp" and have a strong sense of direction, which led her to abstain from marijuana for the first time in her life.

Cyrus is currently working on a new album, which she describes as her most DIY project yet. She wrote the lyrics and melodies herself, and produced the music with Oren Yoel. The album is expected to be released later this year and features a mix of genres, including country and pop. Cyrus stated that she is leaning into her roots and creating music that is honest and authentic to her.

The album's lead single, "Malibu," is a love song about her fiancé, Liam Hemsworth, and is a departure from her previous work. Cyrus explained that she wanted to share her feelings openly and honestly, rather than hiding behind provocative performances or costumes. She believes that this approach will help her connect with a wider audience and promote unity.

Cyrus has been open about her experiences and opinions, particularly regarding social justice and equality. She has been involved in various initiatives, including her Happy Hippie foundation, which aims to support marginalized communities. After the 2016 US presidential election, Cyrus took a break from social media and focused on her music and activism. However, she returned to the public eye with a message of love and acceptance, posting a tearful Instagram video and encouraging her followers to engage in constructive dialogue.

Cyrus has faced criticism and backlash for her outspoken views and unconventional style, but she remains committed to using her platform to promote positivity and inclusivity. She has spoken out against sexism, racism, and homophobia, and has used her music and art to raise awareness about important social issues. Despite facing challenges and controversies, Cyrus continues to evolve and grow as an artist and activist, inspiring her fans and challenging societal norms along the way.

In addition to her music and activism, Cyrus has been open about her personal life, including her relationships and struggles with identity. She has spoken about her experiences as a woman, a non-binary individual, and a member of the LGBTQ+ community. Cyrus has also talked about her relationships with her family members, including her parents and siblings, and has credited her upbringing with shaping her values and worldview.

Throughout her career, Cyrus has navigated the complexities of fame, identity, and social responsibility. She has made mistakes and faced criticism, but has consistently demonstrated a commitment to authenticity, empathy, and compassion. As she continues to create music and advocate for social justice, Cyrus remains a powerful force for positive change and a beacon of hope for those seeking inspiration and guidance.

reference: – Miley Cyrus says she's "completely clean" after giving up marijuana, the AP reports. Cyrus told Billboard magazine in an April interview published online Wednesday that she hadn't smoked marijuana in three weeks. She says that's the longest she's ever gone without it. Cyrus also opened up about her relationship with fiance Liam Hemsworth. She says they had to "refall for each other" after their 2013 breakup. The 24-year-old former Disney star also discussed her performance at the MTV Music Video Awards in 2013. A scantily clad Cyrus shocked audiences with a rump-shaking performance alongside Robin Thicke. But she says she wasn't looking for attention by twerking on stage and was surprised by the reaction. Cyrus is promoting an upcoming album. Its lead single, "Malibu," is set to be released on May 11.

final_summary:
Clemson University food scientist Paul Dawson claims that the widely-held notion of the five-second rule - where food dropped on the floor can be safely consumed if picked up within five seconds - is nothing more than a myth. According to Dawson, he and his students conducted research on the matter, publishing their findings in the Journal of Applied Microbiology, which revealed that bacteria can transfer from surfaces to food within mere seconds. This contradicts earlier studies suggesting that individuals have up to a minute to salvage certain types of dropped food without contamination.

However, other researchers have presented conflicting evidence. A study by Connecticut College students Molly Goettsche and Nicole Moin found that moist foods such as apple slices can remain safe for consumption for up to 1 minute on the floor, while dry foods like Skittles candies can stay uncontaminated for longer periods, including 30 seconds and potentially more. Their experiment involved simulating real-life scenarios by placing food items in the college dining hall and snack bar for varying intervals, from 5 to 60 seconds, and testing for bacterial presence.

In contrast, a separate study by Jillian Clarke, a high school student, discovered that food dropped onto E. coli-contaminated tiles became contaminated almost instantly. Another group of researchers from the University of Maine suggested that adhering to the five-second rule might help minimize food waste and boost children's immunity.

Experts weigh in on the debate, emphasizing that the key factor is not time, but rather the surface type. Dr. Harley Rotbart, a professor of microbiology and pediatric infectious diseases at the University of Colorado School of Medicine, notes that certain areas, such as the kitchen floor, should be considered "zero-second zones" due to the presence of hazardous bacteria from raw meat and poultry. On the other hand, outdoor surfaces like sidewalks may pose less of a health risk compared to indoor environments.

Ultimately, individual perceptions of risk play a significant role in determining whether people choose to consume food that has been dropped on the floor. Research indicates that women are more likely to eat dropped food than men, and sweet treats like cookies and candy are more frequently salvaged than vegetables like cauliflower or broccoli. Despite the controversy surrounding the five-second rule, some medical professionals continue to disregard proper hygiene protocols, as evidenced by a report detailing Illinois clinics' lax attitude towards infection control.

reference: – The 5-second rule may or may not protect your stomach from nasty germs (here's a no, here's a yes), but it probably depends more on location than duration, writes Julie Deardorff, who rounds up recent studies and talks to scientists for her Health Club column in the Chicago Tribune. Kitchen and bathroom floors, for instance, are nasty places where a 0-second rule is wise. The sidewalk, on the other hand, is safer because it's less likely to harbor the germs that cause illness. A kitchen floor "is probably a zero-second zone because the bacteria from uncooked meat and chicken juices are more hazardous than the ‘soil’ bacteria outside,” says one medical expert on the subject. Ditto for a bathroom floor because "it’s a great potential source of bacteria and shorter-lived viruses that can cause gastrointestinal illness if ingested.”

final_summary:
Donald Trump's presidential campaign has raised questions about his motivations and intentions, with some speculating that he may not be seeking the presidency as a means to an end, but rather as a stepping stone for personal gain. Despite being unpopular and facing significant opposition, Trump continues to maintain a dedicated base of support, which he believes can be leveraged into a lucrative media empire. Sources close to Trump suggest that he is exploring the possibility of launching a television or media venture catering to his loyalists, potentially rivaling established networks like Fox News. This idea aligns with Trump's frustration at not receiving a share of the profits generated by media outlets covering his campaign, despite commanding high ratings and attention.

Trump's advisors, including his daughter Ivanka and son-in-law Jared Kushner, believe that he has tapped into a previously untapped market – the conservative base – and that his unique connection with this demographic could be monetized. Kushner reportedly sees Trump's appeal as a game-changer, stating that "even old Fox News didn't have the right read on what the base is. And we do." Trump's desire to capitalize on this opportunity is further fueled by his perception that he has been exploited by the media without reciprocation, leading him to consider bypassing traditional media outlets altogether.

A recent example of Trump's willingness to disregard conventional wisdom and challenge the status quo came to light when he withdrew US troops from Syria following a phone call with Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan. According to officials, Trump abandoned the planned script and agreed to the withdrawal, despite warnings from his advisors and international partners. This decision appears to have been driven by Erdogan's skillful manipulation, with the Turkish leader using the conversation to extract concessions from Trump.

The parallels between Trump's campaign and the 1992 mockumentary film "Bob Roberts" are striking. The film's protagonist, a charismatic and wealthy politician played by Tim Robbins, uses similar tactics to manipulate the media and exploit divisions within the electorate. Like Trump, Roberts is a skilled populist who taps into the fears and anxieties of his supporters, using his charm and charisma to build a devoted following. While "Bob Roberts" is a satirical portrayal of far-right politics, it remains eerily relevant in the context of Trump's campaign, highlighting the dangers of exploiting divisions and promoting hate speech.

reference: – Is Donald Trump running for president simply as a means of launching his own cable news network? That's the theory put forth by Vanity Fair, quoting anonymous sources close to the situation. Trump is reportedly "irked" that he's bringing in big ratings for other networks without getting any of the profits himself. “He wants to figure out if he can monetize it,” a source says. He's allegedly consulting with daughter Ivanka Trump and son-in-law Jared Kushner on a "mini-media conglomerate." His reasoning, according to a source: “We’ve triggered a base of the population that hasn’t had a voice in a long time.” A Trump spokesperson denies Trump is even thinking about such a thing. But immediately after, Trump tweeted: "The press is so totally biased that we have no choice but to take our tough but fair and smart message directly to the people!” New York Magazine says the theory makes sense. This is the first time it's unclear whether a presidential candidate actually wants to be president. Trump is historically unpopular and shows little interest in changing that, and he's half-assing basic tasks of running for president, such as calling donors. Trump TV would be the "logical end" of his campaign, according to New Republic. “If Trump’s TV station gets 30% ratings, it’ll be doing fantastically well," the website states. "But if that’s his share of the vote, then his candidacy will end in failure.” Transitioning from presidential candidate to media baron wouldn't be easy. Trump would still need a partner for funding and would likely need to buy an existing cable channel. “It’s a fool’s errand,” an entertainment executive tells Vanity Fair. “But then again, we are talking about Donald Trump.” (Did this 1992 movie predict Trump's campaign?)

final_summary:
Sir Elton John has reignited his long-standing feud with Madonna, labeling her a "fucking fairground stripper" in an interview on Australian television program Sunday Night. The 65-year-old singer expressed his disdain towards Madonna, stating that her career is over due to the poor reception of her MDNA world tour. Elton also criticized Madonna for being horrible to Lady Gaga, who is the godmother to his and husband David Furnish's 19-month-old son Zachary.

Elton's outburst was triggered by Madonna's criticism of Gaga for her song "Born This Way," which Madonna believed bore similarities to her 1989 hit "Express Yourself." In response, Elton stated that if Madonna had any common sense, she would have focused on creating great pop records rather than venturing into dance music, which he believes she is not suited for.

This is not the first time Elton has publicly clashed with Madonna; earlier this year, he implied that she would lip-sync during her Super Bowl halftime show performance. Their feud escalated when Madonna won the Best Song award at the Golden Globes, beating Elton's nominated song from the animated film Gnomeo and Juliet. Elton's husband, David Furnish, also weighed in on the matter, calling Madonna's win "embarrassing" and "desperate."

In the same interview, Elton spoke fondly of his relationship with his husband, David, whom he credits with helping him turn his life around after a period of destructive behavior. Elton also shared his hopes of making amends with his estranged father through his son Zachary, who is named after Elton's father. To celebrate the 40th anniversary of his hit single "Rocket Man," Elton is embarking on a world tour, including several shows in Australia, where he will be joined by special guests 2CELLOS and Schmidt.

final_summary:
In late August 2018, former Cosby Show actor Geoffrey Owens faced public humiliation after a customer took a photo of him working as a cashier at a Trader Joe's grocery store in New Jersey. The image was subsequently published by several outlets, sparking widespread criticism and shame towards Owens for taking on a "traditional" job. However, the negative reaction quickly turned into an outpouring of support for the 57-year-old actor, with numerous celebrities, including Keshia Knight Pulliam, Malcolm-Jamal Warner, and Sabrina Le Beauf, reaching out to express their solidarity.

Tyler Perry, a renowned film director and producer, took to social media to offer Owens a role on one of his OWN dramas, stating, "I'm about to start shooting OWN's number one drama next week! Come join us!!!" Owens was touched by the offer and expressed his gratitude, acknowledging it as a "very generous" gesture. He eventually accepted the offer and will appear in a recurring role on Perry's hit series, The Haves and the Have Nots, for 10 episodes.

The sudden shift in public opinion towards Owens was largely due to the realization that everyone, regardless of profession or status, needs to work to make ends meet. Many celebrities shared their own experiences of taking on part-time jobs between acting gigs, highlighting the importance of perseverance and resilience in the face of adversity. Owens himself credited his ability to "hang in there" for allowing him to capitalize on the unexpected opportunity.

As a result of the controversy, Owens has received numerous job offers, both on-screen and off, and has decided to take a break from his job at Trader Joe's. Despite initial feelings of humiliation, Owens has chosen to view the experience as a blessing in disguise, using it as a chance to spread awareness about the importance of embracing one's true self and finding strength in vulnerability.

Nicki Minaj, a prominent rapper, also showed her support for Owens by announcing on her Beats 1 Queen Radio show that she would be donating $25,000 to the actor. Minaj stated that she wanted to help Owens, citing her empathy for anyone who may be judged or shamed for their occupation, and emphasizing the value of kindness and compassion towards others.

reference: – The swelling tide of well wishes for the Cosby Show actor who was job-shamed by multiple media outlets may have just turned into something more lucrative. Sources tell TMZ that Geoffrey Owens has accepted a job offer by filmmaker and producer Tyler Perry, who tweeted earlier this week that the 57-year-old actor should "join us" on OWN's The Haves and the Have Nots, which Perry writes for and directs. TMZ reports that Owens is said to be slotted into a recurring role and will appear on 10 episodes, and that he's set to fly to Atlanta next week to begin filming. Owens had previously spoken on Perry's offer, though he simply called it a "generous" one and didn't indicate whether he'd take it, per People. In fact, Owens tells the Hollywood Reporter he's considering "a whole handful" of TV offers. "It's been a real generosity of spirit and a generosity of action," he says. Owens, who has since spoken on the "honor of the working person and the dignity of work," says his first concern when the photo of him broke was his 19-year-old son, People notes. He says he texted his son, who's away at school, to warn him and to apologize for the embarrassment—but his son texted right back to say how proud he was of Owens. "I cried, I just broke down," Owens says. "It was beautiful." One other person is stepping up to do right by Owens: Nicki Minaj, who told Entertainment Tonight she wants to donate $25,000 to the actor. "Some people are on the internet and seeking attention, and he's just the antithesis of that," she says. She notes she's having her rep reach out to Owens' people so he "doesn't take it the wrong way." (The woman who took the Owens photo is now speaking.)

final_summary:
In 2017, Judy Kveton received an anonymous letter expressing concerns about the heart transplant program at Baylor St. Luke's Medical Center in Houston, where her husband, David, had undergone a transplant that ultimately proved fatal. The letter claimed that the program had some of the worst heart transplant outcomes in the country, and that other physicians had voiced concerns about the program's lead surgeon, Dr. Jeffrey Morgan. 

David Kveton's transplant was one of several that ended in tragedy at St. Luke's in recent years. According to data from the Scientific Registry of Transplant Recipients, the program's one-year survival rate for heart transplant patients was lower than the national average, with twice as many patients dying within a year as would have been expected. 

Despite these poor outcomes, the hospital continued to market itself as a leader in heart transplants, boasting above-average survival rates and high transplant volumes. However, the program's troubles ran deeper. Dr. Morgan, who took over as surgical director in 2016, had a history of errors, including sewing shut a major vein during a transplant surgery, which led to the patient's death. 

Another patient, Lazerick Eskridge, suffered severe complications after his transplant, including kidney failure, multiple infections, and an injured diaphragm. An investigation by ProPublica and the Houston Chronicle found that the hospital had withheld key details about his care, including what went wrong during the transplant. 

A third patient, James "Lee" Lewis, received a transplant in 2018 but suffered a series of complications, including a stroke, infections, and organ failure. His wife, Jennifer, was repeatedly misled by hospital staff about the severity of his condition and the risks associated with his treatment. 

An investigation by ProPublica and the Houston Chronicle found that the hospital had a pattern of withholding information from patients and their families about the risks and complications associated with heart transplants. The investigation also found that the hospital had a culture of secrecy and a lack of transparency, which contributed to the poor outcomes and tragic consequences for patients. 

The hospital's troubles were compounded by a series of changes in leadership and a shift towards a more conservative approach to transplant surgery. The program's former director, Dr. Hari Mallidi, left the hospital in 2015, citing the impact of the hospital's acquisition by Catholic Health Initiatives on the program's stability. 

Dr. Morgan, who was hired to replace Mallidi, had a limited track record as a surgeon and a researcher, but was chosen for his academic credentials and his promise to bring new ideas to the program. However, his tenure was marked by controversy, including a series of malpractice lawsuits and allegations of errors in the operating room. 

Despite these challenges, the hospital continued to attract patients from across the country, including the Kvetons and the Eskridges, who were drawn by the program's reputation and the promise of a new lease on life. However, for many patients, the reality fell far short of expectations, and the program's poor outcomes and lack of transparency left families feeling betrayed and seeking justice.

reference: – Baylor St. Luke’s Medical Center is one of America's most storied hospitals when it comes to heart transplants. Or was. Though some of the first heart transplants took place at the Houston hospital, things have disintegrated to the point that it now "has had some of the worst heart transplant outcomes in the country." So write Mike Hixenbaugh and Charles Ornstein in a story co-published by the Houston Chronicle and ProPublica. Their joint investigation found an elevated death and complication count for heart transplant patients there, whose one-year survival rate "now ranks near the bottom nationally" at 85% for those who got a new heart between mid-2014 and 2016; the national rate was 91.4%. Hixenbaugh and Ornstein add a face to those stats via the story of James "Lee" Lewis. Word came on Jan. 1, 2018, that St. Luke's had a heart for Lewis—news that brought hope the 52-year-old would be able to walk his daughter down the aisle in June. But by March 23, he was dead, having never left the hospital. What happened in between is a series of missteps, starting with the Jan. 2 transplant. Two months after the transplant his family learned what went wrong that day: The defibrillator Dr. Masahiro Ono tried to use to "jolt the new heart into rhythm" wasn't working; he said he had to pump it by hand for 10 minutes until a working one was found. The donor heart was damaged and weakened as a result and wouldn't strengthen; it was eventually swapped for an artificial heart. Then came strokes, infection in Lewis' bloodstream, and what the family says was another equipment mishap that left him brain-dead. Read the full story here.

final_summary:
On July 23rd, a controversy erupted in Pembroke Pines, Florida, after a Pembroke Pines Police Sergeant, Jennifer Martin, claimed she was denied service at an Arby's restaurant because she was a police officer. According to the police report filed by Martin, a shift manager, Angel Mirabal, allegedly told her that the employee handling her order, Kenneth Davenport, did not want to serve her because she was a police officer. However, Davenport's grandfather, Thomas McCutcheon, disputed this account, stating that his grandson was simply attempting to make a joke and was misunderstood.

The incident sparked outrage among the local law enforcement community, with the Pembroke Pines Police Department's Chief, Dan Giustino, expressing his disappointment and offense over the alleged treatment of his officer. The police union, the Broward County Police Benevolent Association, condemned the incident and called for a national boycott of Arby's, demanding that the employees involved be fired. The union's president, Jeff Marano, stated that the behavior was unacceptable and reflected a broader issue of disrespect towards law enforcement.

In response to the backlash, Arby's CEO, Paul Brown, apologized to the police department and offered a free combo meal to local police officers in uniform at any of their restaurants in Miami-Dade and Broward counties. However, the police union maintained that the apology was insufficient and demanded a public apology to every law enforcement officer, not just the police chief.

An investigation by Local 10 News revealed that the employee who allegedly refused to serve the officer, Kenneth Davenport, was 19 years old and had been working at Arby's for several months. His grandfather, Thomas McCutcheon, defended his grandson, stating that he was raised to be honest and respectful of everyone, and that the incident was a result of a miscommunication.

As the controversy continued to unfold, the Pembroke Pines Police Department received an outpouring of support from the community, with local businesses and residents donating food and supplies to the department. The incident highlighted the growing tensions between law enforcement and the public, with many viewing it as an example of a broader issue of disrespect towards police officers.

Arby's ultimately fired the shift manager, Angel Mirabal, and suspended Davenport pending further investigation. The company released a statement acknowledging the incident and assuring that the employee's behavior was unacceptable and not representative of the company's values. However, the controversy surrounding the incident continued, with many calling for a national boycott of the fast-food chain until the employees involved were terminated.

reference: – The incident that caused a Florida police union to call for a boycott of Arby's was just a manager's bad joke, says the now-suspended employee involved—but he doesn't expect anybody to believe him because he's a black teenager and it's his word against that of a police officer. Kenny Davenport tells CBS Miami that he told Pembroke Pines Police Sgt. Jennifer Martin that he couldn't serve her not because she was a cop, but because the drive-thru was so busy he had to ask his manager for help. The 19-year-old says the manager then made the bad joke about Davenport not wanting to serve Martin because she was a police officer. When the manager eventually brought the officer some food, she decided she didn't want to eat it. Davenport says he didn't disrespect Martin and he would never refuse to serve an officer. "She hungry, I'm gonna give her some food," he says. The manager involved has been fired and the police department says it has received an apology from the CEO of Arby's and now considers the matter closed, reports Local 10 News. Arby's says all police officers in uniform in Miami-Dade and Broward counties can have a free meal today, reports WSVN, but Pembroke Pines officers may still be dealing with yesterday's leftovers: After the Arby's incident made the news, officers were provided with free meals from McDonald's and Whole Foods, as well as boxes of doughnuts donated by the public.

final_summary:
Tom Hanks, a renowned actor known for his philanthropic efforts, has been providing the White House press corps with high-quality espresso machines since the Bush administration. During a 2001 film shoot in Washington, Hanks noticed the press corps relying on vending machine coffee and promptly purchased a new espresso machine for their break room. The machine was accompanied by a note from Hanks, which read "I hope this machine will make the 24 hour cycle of news a bit more pleasant. Add water, insert pod, press button and report. All good things. Tom Hanks." 

In 2010, while visiting the White House for a screening of HBO's "The Pacific," Hanks observed that the original machine had fallen into disrepair. He subsequently donated a "super deluxe" version of the espresso machine, complete with a new cup set, and included another note advising the press to clean the machine after each use. This gesture further solidified Hanks' reputation as a supporter of the press.

Fast forward to 2017, during President Trump's first 40 days in office, Hanks once again showed his appreciation for the press corps by gifting them a new espresso machine. Alongside the machine, he included a note expressing his support for truth and justice, referencing the iconic phrase from Superman comics and TV shows. The note read, "To the White House Press Corps, Keep up the good fight for Truth, Justice, and the American Way. Especially for the Truth Part." This act of kindness has become a recurring tradition under multiple presidential administrations, demonstrating Hanks' commitment to supporting the press and promoting a free exchange of information.

Members of the White House press corps arrived in the press area break room Thursday morning to find a brand new espresso machine courtesy of Tom Hanks, the Hill reports. The actor left behind a note reading, “Keep up the good fight for Truth, Justice, and the American Way. Especially for the Truth Part.” According to Variety, this has actually become a bit of a tradition for Hanks, starting when he was at the White House for a film shoot during the Bush administration. final_summary:
At the third night of the Democratic National Convention, a surprise appearance by former US President Barack Obama sparked a stir when he planted a kiss on Jill Biden, the wife of his running mate Joe Biden. The nature of the kiss remains unclear, whether intentional or accidental, leaving room for speculation. However, the incident brought attention to a recurring theme in Obama's interactions with women, particularly those in positions of power.

Mediaite reported that Obama had previously smooched Diane Sawyer during an ABC News interview, sparking a humorous reaction from the news anchor. This incident followed a pattern of Obama sharing intimate moments with influential women, including Hillary Clinton, whom he kissed during a healthcare address to Congress. Clinton reportedly preferred Obama's kiss over her husband Bill Clinton's attempt to reciprocate.

Obama also found himself on the receiving end of a rebuff from French First Lady Carla Bruni during a meeting at the NATO summit in Strasbourg, France. While Michelle Obama and Bruni exchanged a traditional Continental-style peck on the cheeks, Bruni hesitated when attempting to kiss Obama, resulting in a handshake instead. The incident sparked a frenzy among the media, with some outlets analyzing the moment frame by frame.

However, the tension was eventually alleviated when Bruni and Obama shared a polite double kiss at a dinner later that evening. According to reports, the delay may have been due to a prior discussion between Michelle Obama and Bruni during a lunch, where they discussed working together to combat AIDS, a cause close to Bruni's heart following the loss of her brother to the disease.

In addition to these high-profile incidents, Obama's interactions with women have been characterized by a lighthearted and playful tone. His ability to connect with female leaders and celebrities has often been noted, with some speculating that he uses his charm to build relationships and diffuse tension. Despite the occasional awkward moment, Obama's affability has contributed to his enduring popularity and charisma.

reference: – When you're the president of the United States, a kiss, apparently, is never just a kiss. Yesterday, the president pecked Diane Sawyer on the cheek before an interview. "It was like a current events-themed Harlequin novel," writes Gawker's Adrian Chen. And it wasn't the first time! A brief history of the president's "public smooching rampage:" The "Accidental" Kiss: During the presidential campaign an enthusiastic Obama "accidentally" kissed Jill Biden on the lips. "This can make for a very awkward situation," the Frisky notes. The "Diss" Kiss: When Barack and Michelle met Carla and Sarko, the president skimped on smooching France's first lady. It caused a major diplomatic kiss-ident, reports the Daily News. The Reward Kiss: The president puckered up to Hillary Clinton at his health care address this fall. It showed, as Gawker put it, that he can "deploy his lips" to "reward friends." Find a complete kiss and tell here.

final_summary:
Universal Pictures' "The Mummy" marks the beginning of the studio's ambitious "Dark Universe" franchise, a reboot of the classic monster movies featuring iconic creatures such as the Wolf Man, the Invisible Man, and the Bride of Frankenstein. Directed by Alex Kurtzman, the film follows the story of Nick Morton, a treasure hunter played by Tom Cruise, who uncovers the tomb of Ahmanet, an ancient Egyptian princess played by Sofia Boutella. Ahmanet, who was mummified and buried alive after attempting to murder her family to claim the throne, is awakened by Morton's actions and seeks to use his body to house the god of death, Set.

The film's narrative is marred by thematic confusion, with elements of action, horror, fantasy, and black comedy awkwardly combined. Critics argue that the story is overly reliant on special effects, with a convoluted plot that fails to engage the audience. Despite having a talented cast, including Russell Crowe as Dr. Jekyll/Mr. Hyde, the chemistry between Cruise and co-star Annabelle Wallis is nonexistent, and the film's pacing is slow and plodding.

The movie's attempts to establish a shared universe, with nods to other classic monster movies, come across as forced and desperate. The film's climax features an underwater chase scene that is described as "painfully lugubrious" and "comical." Critics also note that the film's reliance on CGI-generated effects creates a "murky" visual landscape that fails to engage the viewer.

Some critics praise the film's few genuinely funny moments, including a scene in which Morton is tickled by the mummy's long nails. However, these moments are overshadowed by the film's numerous missteps, including a poorly executed zombie comedy subplot and a wasted performance from Crowe as Dr. Jekyll/Mr. Hyde. Overall, "The Mummy" is seen as a disappointing start to the "Dark Universe" franchise, with many questioning whether the series will take off at all.

The film's marketing strategy, which includes a planned series of reboots and sequels, is seen as overly ambitious and potentially damaging to the franchise. With a reported budget of $125 million, the film's failure to deliver a compelling narrative and engaging characters raises concerns about the viability of the "Dark Universe" concept.

reference: – The Mummy marks a return to the past and a nod to the future for Universal: Taking inspiration from its 1932 and 1999 films of the same name, it's the first installment of a new "Dark Universe" franchise that is set to include flicks like Jekyll & Hyde and Bride of Frankenstein. Tom Cruise stars as an antiquities hunter who disturbs an Egyptian sarcophagus and awakes an evil spirit. Here's what critics are saying: Peter Travers calls The Mummy "a monster fail" that kills anticipation for future franchise flicks. It has too much CGI, and director Alex Kurtzman "can't seem to make sense of a script by numerous writers clearly unashamed of hack work," he writes at Rolling Stone. He adds Russell Crowe appears as Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde for no good reason. Crowe is "surprisingly fun" in this role, but the film's attempts to make him into "head honcho, superhero, and mad scientist all at once" may be its "worst conceptual misfire," Rafer Guzman writes at Newsday. But then, "virtually everything in this movie seems like the wrong decision." It's a "massive mess of a movie" that "seems doomed to repulse anyone who comes across it," he writes. Glenn Kenny was also thrown off by the Jekyll character, but that's just one reason he's warning that the "Dark Universe" franchise might be a "grievously ill-advised" one. Its first installment has "plenty to get irritated about," including some "very old-school sexism," he writes at RogerEbert.com. It's "amazingly relentless in its naked borrowing from other, better horror and sci-fi movies" to boot. It's not all bad, though, writes Stephanie Merry at the Washington Post. "Some of the action is thrilling, especially a brilliantly choreographed plane crash," and there are a "few genuinely funny moments." But those moments "are no match for the cumbersome, convoluted story, not to mention the nonexistent chemistry between Cruise and [Annabelle] Wallis," she notes. Not a good start, Universal.

final_summary:
A massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack on Dyn, a New Hampshire-based internet service provider, brought down a significant portion of the internet on October 21, leaving major websites such as Twitter and Spotify experiencing outages and extreme slowness. The attack, which occurred in three waves, was described as "sophisticated" and "highly distributed" by Kyle York, Dyn's chief strategy officer, with tens of millions of IP addresses involved.

According to Flashpoint, a cybersecurity firm that analyzed the attack, the hackers likely belonged to the English-language hacking forum community, specifically users and readers of the forum hackforums.net. The researchers pointed out that the attack was not financially motivated, as it did not target Bitcoin exchanges or gambling sites, but rather aimed to demonstrate the hackers' power and disrupt the internet for sport.

The investigation revealed that the attackers used a type of malware called Mirai, which hacks poorly protected devices and uses them to overwhelm a server with junk data. The malware was released earlier this month on hackforums.net, making it easier for anyone to access and use it. The attack was similar to previous DDoS attacks against the security blog Krebs on Security and OVH, a French internet service hosting provider.

Dyn's investigation found that the attackers hijacked millions of "Internet of Things" (IoT) devices, including digital video recorders and cameras, to flood their servers with data. However, the company noted that it does not control these devices, and many of them cannot be patched, making them vulnerable to repeated attacks.

Experts warn that the increasing number of hackable devices poses a significant challenge to preventing future DDoS attacks. By 2020, there will be 200 billion objects connected to the internet, up from 2 billion in 2006, giving attackers a substantial advantage. To mitigate the damage, companies can take steps such as resetting IoT devices to their factory settings and changing their passwords, but ultimately, the problem lies with manufacturers designing secure products and providing ongoing support.

Senator Mark Warner, co-chair of the Senate Cybersecurity Caucus, has expressed concern about the influx of insecure IoT devices and has written to the FCC, FTC, and DHS to ask whether ISPs can ban insecure devices from their networks. Warner believes that manufacturers are insulated from accountability and that the security of the internet relies on no one, leading to a "tragedy of the commons" threat.

In a subsequent update, Dyn revised its estimate of the number of Mirai-controlled IoT devices used in the DDoS attacks, stating that it was not tens of millions, but rather a smaller number. Despite the uncertainty surrounding the exact number, the incident highlights the vulnerability of the internet to large-scale DDoS attacks and the need for improved security measures to protect against such threats.
