In a subsequent update, Dyn revised its estimate of the number of Mirai-controlled IoT devices used in the DDoS attacks, stating that it was not tens of millions, but rather a smaller number. Despite the uncertainty surrounding the exact number, the incident highlights the vulnerability of the internet to large-scale DDoS attacks and the need for improved security measures to protect against such threats.

reference: – The distributed denial of service attack that sent Internet users into a tailspin Friday was the most powerful of its kind by far, say cybersecurity researchers, and they're especially alarmed because it looks to be the work of amateur hackers. Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and other sites went down as the Mirai botnet took over poorly protected "Internet of things" devices like DVRs and webcams—whose owners hadn't changed the devices' default passwords—and bombarded domain host company Dyn with traffic so regular users couldn't get through. Apparently there were a lot of insecure devices out there to use. Dyn says there were "100,000 malicious endpoints" leading an attack twice as powerful as any other DDoS attack reported, per the Guardian. Researchers from cybersecurity firm Flashpoint suspect the attack was tied to the community at Hackforums.net—where the Mirai source code was published earlier this month, per Mashable—and was meant to target a video game company, perhaps the PlayStation Network, reports Computer World. If amateur hackers did so much damage, "imagine what a well-resourced state actor could do with insecure IoT devices," says a cybersecurity rep at the Council on Foreign Relations. "We have a serious problem with the cyber insecurity of IoT devices and no real strategy to combat it." Experts recommend users reset an IoT device to its factory settings to erase any existing malware, then create a new password immediately.

final_summary:
Canada's Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) has made a significant move towards promoting diversity within its ranks by granting Muslim women permission to wear a hijab while on duty. This change, announced earlier this year, marks a milestone in the force's efforts to attract more Muslim women to join the RCMP. The decision follows a precedent set in 1990 when the RCMP allowed Sikh officers to wear turbans instead of the traditional Stetson hats, a move aimed at boosting recruitment among the Sikh community.

According to officials, the RCMP tested various designs for the hijab before selecting one that would not compromise the officers' ability to perform their duties comfortably. The decision was approved by the RCMP commissioner and confirmed by Public Safety Minister Ralph Goodale's spokesperson, Scott Bardsley. Bardsley stated that the change aims to better reflect the diversity of Canada's communities and encourage more Muslim women to consider a career with the RCMP.

This development comes amidst a backdrop of controversy surrounding the treatment of Muslim women in other countries. In France, a court recently upheld a ban on "burkinis," a full-body swimsuit worn by some Muslim women, citing concerns that it offends non-Muslims and exacerbates tensions between faith communities. In stark contrast, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau dismissed the idea of implementing a similar ban, emphasizing the importance of acceptance, openness, and understanding in Canadian society.

The RCMP's decision to permit the hijab is not an isolated incident; other law enforcement agencies in Canada and abroad have also relaxed their uniform policies to accommodate Muslim women. For instance, police forces in Toronto, Edmonton, and several US states have already allowed the hijab, while Police Scotland has incorporated it into their official uniform. These moves aim to promote diversity and inclusivity within the police forces, reflecting the multicultural makeup of the communities they serve.

While no Muslim women have yet opted to wear the hijab on duty with the RCMP, the organization remains committed to providing a welcoming environment for female Muslim officers. As Julie Gagnon, a spokesperson for the RCMP, noted, the decision to offer the hijab reflects the diversity of the force's workforce and acknowledges the need to cater to the needs of its employees.

reference: – While cops in France crack down on burkinis, police forces in two other countries have decided to embrace the hijab. The Royal Canadian Mounted Police have decided to allow female officers to wear a hijab while on duty in a bid to encourage diversity among the Mounties, the Independent reports. Police officers in Scotland were already allowed to wear the hijab, but only with approval from senior officers, the Telegraph reports. The headscarf has now been made an official part of the uniform. "Like many other employers, especially in the public sector, we are working towards ensuring our service is representative of the communities we serve," Scottish Chief Constable Phil Gormley said in a statement. In Canada, officials say they hope the shift in uniform policy will encourage more Muslim women to join the Mounties, the AP reports. They note that hijabs were already allowed by police in Toronto, as well as by forces in Sweden, Norway, and London, which added the hijab to the uniform in 2001.

final_summary:
In 1934, Hessy Levinson, a six-month-old Jewish girl from a family of Latvian immigrants living in Berlin, became unwittingly embroiled in Nazi ideology when her photograph was featured on the cover of the Nazi family magazine, Sonne ins Haus. Unbeknownst to the Nazis, the photograph was submitted by her family's acquaintance, renowned photographer Hans Ballin, who deliberately participated in the contest to mock Aryan philosophy. Ballin had known the Levinsons were Jewish and intentionally entered the photo to ridicule Nazi racial theories.

The photograph, believed to have been personally chosen by Nazi Propaganda Minister Joseph Goebbels, was meant to represent the epitome of Aryan perfection. However, the Levinsons' fear of being exposed as Jews due to their foreign birth led them to keep Hessy indoors, away from public view, to avoid drawing attention to their true identity. Despite this precaution, an aunt in Memel, now part of Lithuania, recognized the photograph on a postcard and sent it to the family, further heightening their anxiety.

As the Nazi regime tightened its grip on Germany, the Levinsons faced increasing persecution. In 1936, Hessy's father, Jacob, was arrested by the Gestapo on a trumped-up tax charge, but was released after his Nazi Party-member accountant intervened on his behalf. This incident prompted the family to leave Germany for Latvia, where they stayed for a year before moving to Paris in 1938. However, when the Nazis invaded France, the family found themselves in danger once more.

With the help of the French Resistance, the Levinsons escaped to Nice, and Jacob managed to obtain Cuban visas for the family. They boarded the Serpa Pinto, a Portuguese ship, in Lisbon in 1942, bound for Havana. The family spent the next seven years in Cuba before reapplying for US immigration in 1948 and finally arriving in New York in 1949.

Hessy Taft, now a professor of chemistry, donated her family's yellowing copy of Sonne ins Haus to Yad Vashem, the Israeli Holocaust memorial, sparking renewed interest in her remarkable story. Reflecting on her past, Taft expressed a sense of satisfaction and revenge, acknowledging that her photograph had inadvertently contributed to ridiculing Nazi ideology.

reference: – It turns out the Nazis' vision of the ideal Aryan baby—one that was slapped on the cover of a magazine, on postcards, and disseminated in party propaganda—was a Jewish baby. The Telegraph resurfaces the remarkable story of Hessy Taft (born Hessy Levinsons), a now 80-year-old who in 1935, at just six months, was taken by her mother to a Berlin photographer to have her baby photo taken. Months later, Pauline Levinsons spied her daughter's photo on the cover of the Nazi magazine Sonne ins Hause. Worried their Jewish identity would surface as a result, she hurried back to Hans Ballin, who explained to her that he "wanted to make the Nazis ridiculous"—and so the photographer intentionally entered the young Hessy into a contest looking for the most beautiful Aryan baby, one thought to be judged by Joseph Goebbels himself. The truth of the baby's identity never came to light, though Hessy was kept out of public sight by her parents, who feared she'd be recognized. A short time later the family fled Germany for Latvia, then Paris, Cuba, and finally the United States. Haaretz points out that Taft's story isn't an untold one: In fact, it's published on the US Holocaust Memorial Museum website. But it's getting a fresh look after Taft recently gave Israel's Yad Vashem Holocaust Memorial a copy of the magazine cover. "I feel a little revenge," the Telegraph quotes her as saying. "Something like satisfaction." (In other Nazi news: Was Hitler a billionaire tax dodger?)

final_summary:
Ridley Scott's "Prometheus" is a science fiction film that explores the origin of human life, raising questions about creation and the purpose of humanity. The movie begins with a prologue showing a humanoid creature eating a substance that causes him to disintegrate, leaving behind DNA-like structures. This scene suggests that the origin of human life may have involved an alien self-sacrifice. The story then shifts to Earth in 2089, where two archeologists, Elizabeth Shaw and Charlie Holloway, discover a prehistoric cave painting depicting contact between early humans and an alien visitor. Their theory is backed by similar patterns found in sites worldwide, prompting the Weyland Corporation to fund an expedition to find the origin of human life.

The crew of the spaceship Prometheus, led by Captain Janek, includes Elizabeth, Charlie, and an android named David, created by the corporation's founder, Peter Weyland. David is designed to learn from humans and observe their behavior, and he spends his time on the ship watching Lawrence of Arabia for tips on human emotions. Upon arrival on the distant planet, the crew discovers an ancient structure and soon realizes that they are not alone. They encounter the alien creatures, which have a unique body characteristic – elements can detach from them and enter other bodies as parasites.

As the crew explores the structure, they uncover evidence that the aliens may have created human life, sparking a theological debate between Elizabeth, who believes in a divine origin, and David, who rejects the idea of a creator. However, their investigation goes awry, and the crew faces numerous challenges, including gruesome deaths, strange occurrences, and a catastrophic event that puts the entire crew in danger. The movie descends into implausibility, with a gynecological horror scene and a chaotic finale featuring chases, explosions, and battles.

Throughout the film, Michael Fassbender shines as David, delivering a precise and witty performance that brings depth to the character. Despite its flaws, "Prometheus" boasts impressive visuals, expert horror sequences, and thought-provoking ideas, making it a captivating experience despite its shortcomings. The movie raises fundamental questions about the origin of human life, our purpose, and what happens when we die, leaving audiences with more questions than answers.

reference: – Critics agree: The visuals in Ridley Scott's Prometheus are stunning. As for the story—about a space crew tracking the aliens who may have created us—it's a little too heavy for its own good. "Prometheus could have been an elegant, moody sci-fi actioner if only it didn't strain so hard for weighty existential meaning," writes Dana Stevens at Slate. The film "is more interested in piling on big questions than in answering them. It’s deep without being particularly smart," though its design and effects are so "dazzling" you won't realize "that basic flaw until at least an hour in." Joe Morgenstern echoes Stevens in the Wall Street Journal. "This tale of an interstellar search asks cosmic questions about the meaning of life, but comes up with lame answers in a script that screams attention-deficit disorder," he notes. Still, it's "worth seeing for the quality of its sci-fi sights." In the Los Angeles Times, Kenneth Turan notes that the movie "pushes too hard for significance," which "contrasts badly with the standard nature of some of the story's plotting." For "its wizardly director, it's something of a disappointment." But at the Chicago Sun-Times, Roger Ebert raves that Prometheus is "a seamless blend of story, special effects, and pitch-perfect casting, filmed in sane, effective 3-D that doesn't distract." And Ebert finds it "all the more intriguing because it raises questions about the origin of human life and doesn't have the answers."

final_summary:
On Friday, triple suicide bombers attacked two mosques in the Yemeni capital, Sanaa, killing at least 46 people and injuring 100 others. The mosques, Badr and al-Hashoosh, were crowded with worshippers during midday prayers, a traditionally busy time of the week. According to eyewitness accounts, at least two suicide bombers detonated their devices inside the Badr mosque, causing chaos as people rushed towards the exits, and a second bomber then exploded amidst the fleeing crowd.

Mohammed al-Ansi, a witness at the al-Hashoosh mosque, described the scene as "apocalyptic," with bodies scattered across the floor and blood flowing freely. Many of the injured suffered severe injuries from shattered glass falling from the mosque's windows. The attack was widely condemned by the international community, with the US strongly denouncing the "unconscionable attack on Muslim worshipers."

The bombings occurred just a day after intense clashes in the southern city of Aden between rival troops loyal to Yemen's former and current presidents left 13 dead and forced the closure of the city's international airport. The conflict has escalated significantly since the Houthi rebels, a Shia group, seized control of the capital in September, ousting the internationally recognized government. The Houthis, allied with ousted President Ali Abdullah Saleh, now control at least nine of Yemen's 21 provinces.

In the aftermath of the bombings, the Islamic State (ISIS) claimed responsibility for the attack, stating that it was a "tip of an iceberg." However, the authenticity of the claim could not be verified. If true, it would mark ISIS's first major attack in Yemen, challenging the dominance of Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP), the Sunni extremist group more commonly associated with the country.

The mosque attacks began with blasts inside the buildings, followed by explosions outside, possibly targeting those fleeing the initial blasts. At least 40 people are in critical condition and will be sent to Jordan for treatment. AQAP denied involvement in the bombings, while the US, which had previously supported the Yemeni government in its fight against AQAP, condemned the attacks and called on the Houthis to stop their violent actions.

The conflict in Yemen has drawn international attention, with the US, Europe, and Saudi Arabia suspending embassy operations and providing humanitarian aid to the affected areas. The situation remains volatile, with ongoing clashes between Houthi-controlled forces and military units loyal to President Hadi, who has declared himself the legitimate president from his base in the southern city of Aden.

reference: – Triple suicide bombers hit a pair of mosques crowded with worshippers in the Yemeni capital, Sanaa, today, causing heavy casualties, according to witnesses. The attackers targeted mosques frequented by Shiite rebels, who've controlled the capital since September. A report on the rebel-owned al-Masirah TV channel said the bombers attacked the Badr and al-Hashoosh mosques during midday prayers, traditionally the most crowded time of the week. Casualty numbers differ, with Reuters reporting at least 16 have been killed; CNN says 25, AP says 46, and AFP claims at least 55 have died, according to medics. The attacks come a day after intense gun battles in the southern city of Aden, between rival troops loyal to Yemen's former and current president, left 13 dead and forced closure of the city's international airport. Witnesses said that at least two suicide bombers attacked inside the Badr mosque. One walked inside the mosque and detonated his device, causing panic as dozens of worshippers rushed toward the outside gates. A second suicide bomber then attacked amid panicked crowds trying to escape. One witness at the al-Hashoosh mosque, located in Sanaa's northern district, said that he was thrown 6 feet by the blast. "The heads, legs, and arms of the dead people were scattered on the floor of the mosque," Mohammed al-Ansi told the AP, adding, "Blood is running like a river." Al-Ansi added that many of those who didn't die in the explosion were seriously injured by shattered glass falling from the mosque's windows. The Shiite TV network aired footage from inside al-Hashoosh mosque, where screaming volunteers used bloodied blankets to carry victims. Among the dead: a small child.

final_summary:
A Jacksonville, Florida pediatric dentist, Dr. Howard Schneider, is currently under investigation by the state of Florida and facing multiple lawsuits from former patients accusing him of abusing and torturing them. The lawsuits allege that Schneider's practice is a "front for a sadistic and systematic scheme of physical and psychological torture and abuse" targeting "utterly defenseless" children, particularly those from low-income backgrounds who rely on Medicaid. 

According to the lawsuits, Schneider subjected his young patients to extreme physical and emotional trauma, including choking them to unconsciousness, performing unnecessary procedures without anesthesia, and threatening them to keep quiet about the abuse. The plaintiffs claim that Schneider derived "sexual excitement" from inflicting pain or humiliation on his patients, describing him as a "pathological sadist" suffering from a "psychosexual disorder."

The allegations against Schneider date back decades, with some parents reporting incidents as far back as the 1990s. In recent years, numerous parents have come forward with similar stories of their children experiencing severe physical harm and emotional distress during visits to Schneider's office. Many of these parents claim that their children were left with visible injuries, such as bruises and cuts, and that Schneider's staff often lied to them about the nature of the procedures.

One mother, Brandi Motley, reported that her six-year-old daughter, Bri-el, was left with eight missing front teeth after a routine procedure that was supposed to involve only one extraction. Another mother, Sherraine Christopher, claimed that her three-year-old son, Zion, was fitted with 16 metal crowns without her knowledge or consent.

In response to the mounting allegations, Schneider's clinic was shut down, and he voluntarily surrendered his medical license. However, he still faces 11 counts of Medicaid provider fraud, which carry a maximum sentence of 45 years in prison. Schneider has maintained his innocence, claiming that he used anesthesia and was a skilled dentist with over 50 years of experience.

As part of the ongoing investigation, the state has gathered nearly a dozen accounts from parents detailing their experiences with Schneider's office, including reports of children being thrown, choked, and left with severe injuries. Experts have also raised concerns about Schneider's consent forms, charting practices, and the quality of care provided to his patients.

Schneider's competency to stand trial has become a contentious issue, with both the prosecution and defense hiring separate psychologists to evaluate his mental fitness. The trial has been delayed pending the outcome of this evaluation, which is expected to determine whether Schneider will be deemed competent to stand trial.

reference: – A Florida mother says she brought her then-6-year-old daughter in for a dental appointment to get a tooth extracted with Dr. Howard Schneider in 2014—but, instead, she came in to the procedure room to find her daughter "face-first" on the bloody floor with scratches, bruises, and all eight of her front teeth extracted. When Brandi Motley couldn't get an attorney to take her case, she "turned her minivan into a rolling billboard attack on Schneider," ABC News reports. Then, in April 2015, she posted about her experience on Facebook, and the post went viral. She found other parents who said they'd had similar experiences—they allege Schneider, who catered to children in low-income families, performed unnecessary procedures, harming their kids in the process, in order to collect millions in Medicaid funds—and they started protesting outside Schneider's clinic. Ultimately, the attorney who'd at first refused the case took on 131 individual lawsuits against Schneider, settling 104 of them thus far, but Schneider continues to insist he's done nothing wrong. After the protests had been going on for weeks, Schneider shut down his clinic after more than 50 years of practicing dentistry, and voluntarily gave up his dental license—which meant he avoided a full investigation by the dental board. In November 2015, he was arrested and charged with 11 counts of Medicaid provider fraud; he pleaded not guilty and may go to trial, although his defense team is seeking to have the 78-year-old declared mentally incompetent in order to avoid a trial, News 4 Jax reports. In May, USA Today reported that some parents accuse Schneider of going so far as to choke his patients into unconsciousness rather than using anesthetic.

final_summary:
This year's round of Nobel Prizes began on October 1, 2018, with the announcement of the award for medicine or physiology, but the focus shifted to physics on October 2, 2018, when the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences revealed the winners of the prestigious prize. The trio of scientists, consisting of Arthur Ashkin from the United States, Gerard Mourou from France, and Donna Strickland from Canada, were recognized for their groundbreaking contributions to the field of laser physics.

Donna Strickland, a researcher at the University of Waterloo in Canada, made history by becoming the third woman to receive the Nobel Prize in Physics, following in the footsteps of Marie Curie, who won in 1903, and Maria Goeppert-Mayer, who received the award in 1963. Strickland shared the prize with Ashkin and Mourou, marking a significant milestone in the recognition of women in physics.

Strickland's work focused on the development of a technique known as Chirped Pulse Amplification (CPA), which enabled the creation of the shortest and most intense laser pulses ever produced. This innovation has led to various applications, including laser therapy for cancer treatment and corrective laser eye surgeries, with millions of procedures performed annually worldwide. Her collaboration with Mourou, her former supervisor and mentor, played a crucial role in advancing the technology.

Arthur Ashkin, an American scientist, was honored for his invention of "optical tweezers," a device that utilizes the radiation pressure of light to manipulate and trap tiny particles, such as viruses and living cells, without causing damage. His pioneering work laid the foundation for the use of laser-based tools in studying the intricacies of life.

Gerard Mourou, a French scientist, contributed significantly to the development of CPA, paving the way for the creation of ultra-short and high-intensity laser pulses. His work has far-reaching implications for various fields, including medicine and industry.

The Nobel Prize in Physics carries a monetary value of 9 million Swedish kronor, equivalent to approximately $1.01 million. The award recognizes the trio's outstanding achievements in advancing our understanding of the fundamental principles governing the behavior of light and matter.

In response to her win, Strickland expressed surprise at the 55-year gap since a woman last received the prize, but emphasized that she had always been treated equally alongside her male colleagues. She acknowledged the significance of sharing the award with Ashkin and Mourou, highlighting their respective contributions to the field.

The American Institute of Physics congratulated the winners, noting the numerous practical applications resulting from their work, including laser eye surgery, high-powered petawatt lasers, and the ability to trap and study individual viruses and bacteria. The institute hailed Strickland's achievement as a historic moment, breaking the 55-year hiatus since a woman last received the Nobel Prize in Physics.

This year's round of Nobel Prizes began on October 1, 2018, with the announcement of the award for medicine or physiology, but the focus shifted to physics on October 2, 2018, when the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences revealed the winners of the prestigious prize. The trio of scientists, consisting of Arthur Ashkin from the United States, Gerard Mourou from France, and Donna Strickland from Canada, were recognized for their groundbreaking contributions to the field of laser physics.

Donna Strickland, a researcher at the University of Waterloo in Canada, made history by becoming the third woman to receive the Nobel Prize in Physics, following in the footsteps of Marie Curie, who won in 1903, and Maria Goeppert-Mayer, who received the award in 1963. Strickland shared the prize with Ashkin and Mourou, marking a significant milestone in the recognition of women in physics.

Strickland's work focused on the development of a technique known as Chirped Pulse Amplification (CPA), which enabled the creation of the shortest and most intense laser pulses ever produced. This innovation has led to various applications, including laser therapy for cancer treatment and corrective laser eye surgeries, with millions of procedures performed annually worldwide. Her collaboration with Mourou, her former supervisor and mentor, played a crucial role in advancing the technology.

Arthur Ashkin, an American scientist, was honored for his invention of "optical tweezers," a device that utilizes the radiation pressure of light to manipulate and trap tiny particles, such as viruses and living cells, without causing damage. His pioneering work laid the foundation for the use of laser-based tools in studying the intricacies of life.

Gerard Mourou, a French scientist, contributed significantly to the development of CPA, paving the way for the creation of ultra-short and high-intensity laser pulses. His work has far-reaching implications for various fields, including medicine and industry.

The Nobel Prize in Physics carries a monetary value of 9 million Swedish kronor, equivalent to approximately $1.01 million. The award recognizes the trio's outstanding achievements in advancing our understanding of the fundamental principles governing the behavior of light and matter.

In response to her win, Strickland expressed surprise at the 55-year gap since a woman last received the prize, but emphasized that she had always been treated equally alongside her male colleagues. She acknowledged the significance of sharing the award with Ashkin and Mourou, highlighting their respective contributions to the field.

The American Institute of Physics congratulated the winners, noting the numerous practical applications resulting from their work, including laser eye surgery, high-powered petawatt lasers, and the ability to trap and study individual viruses and bacteria. The institute hailed Strickland's achievement as a historic moment, breaking the 55-year hiatus since a woman last received the Nobel Prize in Physics.

reference: – Three scientists from the United States, France, and Canada have been awarded the Nobel Prize in physics for advances in laser physics, including the first woman to take home the prize in 55 years. The Swedish Royal Academy of Sciences on Tuesday awarded half the $1.01 million prize to Arthur Ashkin of the United States, and the other half will be shared by Gerard Mourou of France and Donna Strickland of Canada, per the AP. Strickland is the third female laureate in physics, but the first in more than half a century, reports the BBC. The academy says Ashkin developed "optical tweezers" that can grab tiny particles such as viruses without damaging them. As the Guardian notes, "this means scientists can hold even living cells in place, allowing them to probe their inner workings." Strickland and Mourou helped develop short and intense laser pulses that have broad industrial and medical applications. The Nobel panel's explanation: "Ultra-sharp laser beams make it possible to cut or drill holes in various materials extremely precisely—even in living matter. Millions of eye operations are performed every year with the sharpest of laser beams." (A CERN scientist just got suspended for saying physics was built by men.)

final_summary:
Doku Umarov, the self-proclaimed leader of the "Caucasus Emirate" and a key figure in the North Caucasus Islamist militant insurgency, has publicly claimed responsibility for the January 24th suicide bombing at Moscow's Domodedovo International Airport, which resulted in 36 deaths and 180 injuries. In a video posted online, Umarov stated that the attack was a response to what he described as "Russian crimes in the Caucasus" and vowed that similar operations would continue, citing the presence of hundreds of additional suicide bombers ready to strike at any moment. This assertion was not unexpected, given Umarov's history of involvement in previous terrorist attacks, including the 2010 Moscow Metro bombings that killed 39 people and the 2009 bombing of a train from Moscow to St. Petersburg that left 26 dead.

The Domodedovo bombing, which occurred in the arrivals area of the airport, targeted a crowd of people who had just disembarked from international flights, resulting in the deaths of at least seven foreigners, including citizens of Britain, Germany, Austria, Ukraine, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan, and Uzbekistan, as well as 16 Russians. Russian President Dmitry Medvedev responded to the attack by sacking several high-ranking officials, including a regional transport chief and a Moscow police deputy head, and ordering enhanced security measures at major transportation hubs throughout the country.

Umarov's video message, which was dated January 24th, the same day as the bombing, also contained a broader threat, warning of a year of "blood and tears" unless Russia withdraws from the Caucasus region. He framed the conflict as a struggle between Islam and the "Zionist and Christian regimes" led by Israel and the United States, and emphasized his group's commitment to establishing a Caucasus Emirate governed by Sharia law. The video featured Umarov flanked by two men, one of whom he identified as Seifullah, who he claimed was preparing a "special operation."

In the aftermath of the bombing, Russian authorities implemented new security protocols at airports, including tightened controls outside terminal buildings and increased document checks. However, these measures have been met with skepticism by many Russians, who question their effectiveness in preventing future attacks. The incident has raised concerns about Russia's counter-terrorism strategy and its ability to protect against such threats, particularly in light of the fact that the bomber was able to enter the airport's arrivals area without undergoing security screening.

reference: – A Chechen militant leader has claimed responsibility for the bombing that killed 36 people at Russia's biggest airport last month. Warlord Doku Umarov appears in a 16-minute video posted online in which he says the suicide bombing at Moscow's Domodedovo airport was carried out on his orders and warns of more attacks to come, AP reports. "You see this special operation carried out by my order. More special operations will be carried out in the future," Umarov says in the video. "Among us there are hundreds of brothers who are prepared to sacrifice themselves" in further attacks. "We can at any time carry out operations where we want," he adds. Chechen rebels, who seek to establish an independent Islamic state in the Caucasus, also claimed responsibility for the Moscow subway bombing last year.

final_summary:
A new study released by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) reveals stark disparities in suicide rates across various occupations in the US. According to the research, which analyzed data from 17 states covering over 12,300 suicide deaths in 2012, certain professions are disproportionately affected by suicidal tendencies.

Farmers, lumberjacks, and fishermen top the list with the highest suicide rate, standing at 85 per 100,000 individuals. This is closely followed by carpenters, miners, electricians, and construction workers, who experience a significantly higher rate of 53 per 100,000. Mechanics and those involved in installation, maintenance, and repair come in third with a rate of 48 per 100,000.

On the opposite end of the spectrum, dentists, doctors, and other healthcare professionals exhibit an 80% lower suicide rate compared to their counterparts in the agricultural sector. Librarians and educators also show a notably low rate of 8 per 100,000.

The study highlights the correlation between manual labor jobs that involve isolation and unstable employment conditions with higher rates of suicidal behavior. While the exact reasons behind these discrepancies are unclear, experts point to the potential impact of economic pressures, pesticide exposure, and lack of social support networks.

Interestingly, when examining female-specific data, the highest suicide rate was observed among police officers, firefighters, and corrections personnel, with a rate of 14.1 per 100,000. The second-highest rate among women was recorded in the legal profession, with a rate of 13.9 per 100,000.

This study marks one of the largest investigations into occupational-based suicide rates in the US, shedding light on the need for targeted prevention strategies tailored to specific industries. By understanding the unique challenges faced by individuals within these sectors, policymakers can develop more effective interventions to mitigate the rising tide of suicidal behavior among vulnerable populations.

According to the CDC's National Violent Death Reporting System (NVDRS), which collected data from 17 states, the majority of suicides occur among middle-aged adults, with the highest numbers and rates observed in men. The study emphasizes the importance of addressing the root causes of suicidal behavior, including enhancing social support systems, reducing stigma around mental health issues, and increasing access to preventative services.

While the findings underscore the complexity of the issue, they also highlight opportunities for intervention and prevention. By acknowledging the distinct needs of various occupations and demographics, the US can take a crucial step towards reducing the alarming rate of suicides nationwide.

reference: – Teachers and librarians have the lowest suicide rate while farmers and fishermen have the highest, according to a CDC study of 12,300 suicides in 17 states in 2012. Why the discrepancy? Researchers say "job-related isolation and demands, stressful work environments, and work-home imbalance, as well as socioeconomic inequities" likely play a role, per the AP. Here are the 10 fields as grouped by the CDC with the highest rates, based on the number of suicides per 100,000 workers: Farming, fishing, and forestry: 84.5 Carpenters, miners, electricians, construction trades: 53.3 Mechanics and those who do installation, maintenance, repair: 47.9 Factory and production workers: 34.5 Architecture and engineering: 32.2 Police, firefighters, corrections workers, others in protective services: 30.5 Arts, design, entertainment, sports, and media: 24.3 Computer and mathematical: 23.3 Transportation workers: 22.3 Corporate executives and managers, advertising, and PR: 20.3 Click for the full list.

final_summary:
In 2015, Mexican actress Kate del Castillo tweeted that she would prefer to trust El Chapo, the leader of the Sinaloa cartel, over the Mexican government. This tweet caught the attention of El Chapo's lawyer, who contacted her and arranged for her to receive flowers from El Chapo. Del Castillo had previously expressed her distrust of the Mexican government and her desire to see El Chapo's story told on film. She partnered with her colleagues, including Sean Penn, to make a film about El Chapo, but due to restrictions on accessing him, they decided to pursue a magazine story instead.

Sean Penn, accompanied by his colleagues Espinoza and El Alto, traveled to Mexico to meet with El Chapo, facilitated by del Castillo. They boarded a charter flight from Los Angeles to a mid-Mexico city, where they were greeted by Alonzo, an associate of El Chapo. They were then taken to a dirt airfield, where they boarded a prop plane piloted by Alfredo Guzmán, El Chapo's son. The plane flew over the jungle, and the group eventually arrived at a clearing where El Chapo was waiting.

During their meeting, El Chapo was charismatic and welcoming, discussing topics such as his business ventures and his family. He revealed that he had a fleet of submarines, airplanes, trucks, and boats, and that he supplied more heroin, methamphetamine, cocaine, and marijuana than anyone else in the world. He also mentioned that he had a bounty on Donald Trump's life, which he found amusing.

After the meeting, Penn and his colleagues returned to Los Angeles, but they soon realized that a military siege on Sinaloa was underway. El Chapo and his men had been tracked by the military and the DEA, and a shootout ensued. El Chapo was injured in the process, but managed to escape.

Penn attempted to contact El Chapo again, but the cartel intermediaries had gone off the grid. He eventually received a video from El Chapo, recorded on a BBM device, in which El Chapo answered Penn's questions about his childhood, his involvement in the drug trade, and his views on the impact of his business on society.

The video showed El Chapo to be a complex and nuanced individual, who acknowledged the negative consequences of his actions but also defended his role in the economy of the regions where he operated. He stated that he did not use violence unless necessary and that he was not responsible for the high level of drug addiction in the world.

Meanwhile, a documentary series titled "The Day I Met El Chapo" was released on Netflix, which explored del Castillo's role in facilitating the meeting between Penn and El Chapo. The series sparked controversy, with Penn's representatives accusing del Castillo and her team of creating a "profoundly false, foolish, and reckless narrative" about Penn's involvement with the meeting.

Penn had initially been approached by the producers of the documentary, but he declined to participate. However, after watching the series, he requested to be interviewed and demanded changes be made. The producers refused, citing that Penn had been given the opportunity to participate but chose not to. Netflix maintained that the series was not intended to sensationalize the meeting, but rather to provide a platform for del Castillo to tell her side of the story.

reference: – Sean Penn's 2015 Mexican sitdown with drug lord Joaquin Guzman (aka "El Chapo"), which he documented in an early 2016 Rolling Stone article, caused its fair share of controversy. And the ruckus isn't over yet, as the actor is now going after Netflix for a documentary on Guzman, set to start airing Friday, that Penn's spokesman says in an email to the New York Times is a "profoundly false, foolish, and reckless narrative" that could put Penn's life in danger. The actor's main bone of contention with The Day I Met El Chapo: The Kate del Castillo Story: that he thinks the three-episode film hints he assisted the DOJ in helping to capture El Chapo, who was busted right around the time Penn's Rolling Stone article went public. Del Castillo, a Mexican actress, was with Penn when he met with Guzman, and the film is said to take place from her POV. Producer David Broome denies there's anything in the film that says Penn "is in cahoots" with the Justice Department and that both he and Netflix tried to see if Penn wanted to take part (Penn reportedly never answered); Netflix backs that up, saying in a statement "Penn was given the opportunity on multiple occasions to participate." Broome says when Penn saw an advance copy of the film, he demanded changes. Deadline notes that Penn's lawyers contacted Netflix shortly after and said Penn was afraid Guzman's associates might go after him if the film wasn't altered. A letter from Penn's lawyer to Netflix notes that "blood will be on [Netflix's] hands if this film causes bodily harm" to Penn. (In the end, Penn thought his Rolling Stone article on El Chapo was a failure.)

final_summary:
On January 28, 2018, Senator Susan Collins advised President Donald Trump to maintain public silence regarding an ongoing investigation into his 2016 campaign's interactions with Russia, following reports that he attempted to fire Special Counsel Robert Mueller. Collins, alongside fellow Republican Senator Lindsey Graham, expressed concern that such actions could compromise Mueller's position. 

Meanwhile, the House Intelligence Committee voted along party lines to release a classified memo alleging FBI and Justice Department misconduct in the Russia probe. The memo, written by Republicans, claims that senior FBI officials abused a surveillance program to monitor a Trump campaign aide. Democrats on the committee opposed the release, arguing it misrepresents intelligence and aims to divert attention away from Mueller's investigation.

House Intelligence Committee Chairman Devin Nunes shared the memo with FBI Director Christopher Wray and Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein after pressure from Representative Trey Gowdy, who advocated for the document's release. However, Wray expressed concerns about the memo, which were not alleviated even after reviewing it. Nunes agreed to allow Wray to flag any factual inaccuracies or potential security risks within the document.

The memo reportedly accuses senior FBI officials of using a dossier compiled by former British spy Christopher Steele to obtain surveillance warrants. Steele created the dossier in 2016 on behalf of research firm Fusion GPS, which received funding from Hillary Clinton's presidential campaign and the Democratic National Committee. The document also allegedly reveals that Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein authorized an extension of surveillance on former Trump campaign aide Carter Page in early 2017.

White House Press Secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders stated that President Trump had not yet decided whether to approve the memo's release, citing a need for full transparency. However, the White House indicated that Trump favored releasing the document. If Trump does not object within five days, the memo will be released to the public.

Representative Trey Gowdy emphasized that the memo should not be used to interfere with Mueller's investigation, stating, "Leave him the hell alone." Representative Will Hurd, a member of the House Intelligence Committee and a former CIA officer, downplayed concerns about releasing the memo without Justice Department review, saying he would continue to protect sources and methods.

reference: – It's all up to President Trump now. The House Intelligence Committee voted along party lines Monday to release a controversial memo that alleges wrongdoing on the part of the Justice Department and the FBI in regard to the Russia investigation, reports Politico. Democrats are fuming over the decision. Trump now has five days to decide whether to allow the memo to be released; his approval is necessary because it's a classified document. The memo alleges that the FBI and the Justice Department improperly used government surveillance during the investigation into Russian election interference and contacts with Trump's campaign, per the AP. The vote to release the memo comes after committee Republicans, led by chairman Rep. Devin Nunes, pushed for its disclosure. The memo addresses a dossier of allegations against Trump compiled by a former British spy, and questions over whether it was improperly used to obtain surveillance warrants. The panel's ranking Democrat, Rep. Adam Schiff, announced the vote results Monday while criticizing the Republicans' decision. Democrats generally say the memo cherry-picks facts and presents a distorted view of things.

final_summary:
On December 17, 2016, a devastating incident occurred at Penn Park in Whittier, California, where a 100-foot-tall eucalyptus tree suddenly toppled over, crushing a wedding party that was taking pictures beneath it. The tragic event resulted in the death of one person, later confirmed to be the mother of the bride, and left five others injured, including a four-year-old girl who was hospitalized in critical condition.

Eyewitnesses described the scene as chaotic, with multiple people trapped under the tree's branches. Firefighters from the Los Angeles County Fire Department responded quickly, using chainsaws to free those trapped. The injured individuals were taken to a nearby hospital for treatment, with all but one suffering minor to moderate injuries.

According to eyewitness accounts, the bride was among those affected by the disaster, reportedly limping and screaming about her mother, who was unable to be revived. A search dog was deployed to ensure no further victims remained trapped.

Police guarded the scene the next day, keeping trespassers away while crews retrieved personal belongings from the debris. The park, known for hosting weddings and other celebrations, was closed indefinitely pending an investigation into the cause of the tree's collapse.

Arborists were scheduled to inspect the area to determine whether the tree's fall was related to the recent rainfall or the prolonged drought that had affected the region. However, officials emphasized that speculation would only begin once the arborist's findings were available.

In a similar incident in 2015, eight children were injured, two critically, when an Italian stone pine tree toppled over at the Kidspace Museum in Pasadena. An investigation revealed that the tree's roots were weakened due to drought conditions and excessive water intake.

As the Whittier community mourned the loss of life and injury, officials expressed their condolences to the affected families and pledged to support them during this difficult time. The incident served as a poignant reminder of the risks associated with sudden tree falls, particularly in regions prone to drought and extreme weather conditions.

reference: – "It’s just again showing that the vegetation in southern California is still very stressed from the drought we’ve had for five years." So said LA County Fire Department Deputy Chief John Tripp in the wake of a Saturday tragedy in Whittier, reports USA Today: A family was taking wedding photos in Penn Park near a 100-foot-tall eucalyptus tree when it uprooted and fell, trapping some of the party underneath. A woman was killed and five others, including 4-year-old girl, injured in the incident, which happened at 4:30pm local time, reports the San Gabriel Valley Tribune. The relationship of the woman and girl to the bride and groom has not been released. NBC Los Angeles reports as many as 20 people were trapped under the tree, and chainsaws were used to extricate them. A witness shared this wrenching observation about the bride: "She was limping a little bit, but she was screaming about her mom—they couldn’t wake her up." An arborist will investigate Monday or Tuesday. NBC notes it rained heavily the previous night.

final_summary:
Nora Ephron, a renowned American journalist, essayist, novelist, and filmmaker, passed away on June 26, 2023, at the age of 71 due to complications from myelodysplasia, a blood disorder she had been battling for six years. Born on May 19, 1941, in Manhattan, Ephron was the eldest of four sisters, all of whom became writers. Her parents, Henry and Phoebe Wolkind Ephron, were successful Broadway playwrights and Hollywood screenwriters who instilled in their daughter a passion for storytelling and writing.

Ephron's early life was marked by a strong desire to pursue a career in journalism. She attended Wellesley College, where she began writing for the school newspaper, and later landed an internship in the Kennedy White House in the summer of 1961. During her time at the White House, Ephron claimed to have rescued Speaker of the House Sam Rayburn from a men's restroom and was also the only intern who avoided President John F. Kennedy's advances.

After graduating from Wellesley in 1962, Ephron moved to New York City, determined to become a journalist. She started as a mail girl at Newsweek and eventually landed a job at The New York Post, where she covered various stories, including the Beatles' visit to the United States and a pair of hooded seals at the Coney Island aquarium that refused to mate. Ephron's experience at The Post taught her to write concisely and effectively, skills that served her well throughout her career.

In the late 1960s, Ephron transitioned to magazine journalism, writing for Esquire and New York magazines. Her essays and profiles were characterized by their humor, honesty, and sharp observations, earning her a reputation as a talented and incisive writer. Ephron's work often tackled topics related to feminism, culture, and society, and she was unafraid to tackle sensitive subjects, including her own experiences with breast size and her complicated relationships.

Ephron's entry into the film industry was accidental, resulting from her marriage to Carl Bernstein, a Washington Post journalist, in 1976. Together, they attempted to rewrite the screenplay for "All the President's Men," although their version was ultimately rejected. However, this experience brought Ephron to the attention of Hollywood professionals, leading to her first screenplay, "Silkwood," a biographical drama based on the life of Karen Silkwood, which earned her an Academy Award nomination.

Throughout her career, Ephron collaborated with notable actors and directors, including Meryl Streep, Tom Hanks, and Rob Reiner. Her romantic comedies, such as "When Harry Met Sally..." and "You've Got Mail," have become cultural touchstones, and her ability to craft relatable, flawed characters has been praised by critics and audiences alike. Ephron's work often explored themes of love, relationships, and identity, and she was unafraid to tackle difficult subjects, including her own struggles with aging and mortality.

In her later years, Ephron continued to write and publish essays, including her bestselling collection "I Feel Bad About My Neck: And Other Thoughts on Being a Woman." Her final book, "I Remember Nothing: And Other Reflections," was published posthumously in 2010. Ephron's legacy extends beyond her impressive body of work, as she paved the way for future generations of female writers and filmmakers. Her wit, intelligence, and generosity of spirit have left a lasting impact on the literary and cinematic worlds.

history.draft:
Nora Ephron, a renowned American journalist, essayist, novelist, and filmmaker, passed away on June 26, 2023, at the age of 71 due to complications from myelodysplasia, a blood disorder she had been battling for six years. Born on May 19, 1941, in Manhattan, Ephron was the eldest of four sisters, all of whom became writers. Her parents, Henry and Phoebe Wolkind Ephron, were successful Broadway playwrights and Hollywood screenwriters who instilled in their daughter a passion for storytelling and writing.

Ephron's early life was marked by a strong desire to pursue a career in journalism. She attended Wellesley College, where she began writing for the school newspaper, and later landed an internship in the Kennedy White House in the summer of 1961. During her time at the White House, Ephron claimed to have rescued Speaker of the House Sam Rayburn from a men's restroom and was also the only intern who avoided President John F. Kennedy's advances.

After graduating from Wellesley in 1962, Ephron moved to New York City, determined to become a journalist. She started as a mail girl at Newsweek and eventually landed a job at The New York Post, where she covered various stories, including the Beatles' visit to the United States and a pair of hooded seals at the Coney Island aquarium that refused to mate. Ephron's experience at The Post taught her to write concisely and effectively, skills that served her well throughout her career.

In the late 1960s, Ephron transitioned to magazine journalism, writing for Esquire and New York magazines. Her essays and profiles were characterized by their humor, honesty, and sharp observations, earning her a reputation as a talented and incisive writer. Ephron's work often tackled topics related to feminism, culture, and society, and she was unafraid to tackle sensitive subjects, including her own experiences with breast size and her complicated relationships.

Ephron's entry into the film industry was accidental, resulting from her marriage to Carl Bernstein, a Washington Post journalist, in 1976. Together, they attempted to rewrite the screenplay for "All the President's Men," although their version was ultimately rejected. However, this experience brought Ephron to the attention of Hollywood professionals, leading to her first screenplay, "Silkwood," a biographical drama based on the life of Karen Silkwood, which earned her an Academy Award nomination.

Throughout her career, Ephron collaborated with notable actors and directors, including Meryl Streep, Tom Hanks, and Rob Reiner. Her romantic comedies, such as "When Harry Met Sally..." and "You've Got Mail," have become cultural touchstones, and her ability to craft relatable, flawed characters has been praised by critics and audiences alike. Ephron's work often explored themes of love, relationships, and identity, and she was unafraid to tackle difficult subjects, including her own struggles with aging and mortality.

In her later years, Ephron continued to write and publish essays, including her bestselling collection "I Feel Bad About My Neck: And Other Thoughts on Being a Woman." Her final book, "I Remember Nothing: And Other Reflections," was published posthumously in 2010. Ephron's legacy extends beyond her impressive body of work, as she paved the way for future generations of female writers and filmmakers. Her wit, intelligence, and generosity of spirit have left a lasting impact on the literary and cinematic worlds.

reference: – Filmmaker and writer Nora Ephron has died at age 71, reports TMZ. Ephron, who wrote the screenplays for Sleepless in Seattle and When Harry Met Sally, had "emerged over the past four decades as one of America's warmest and most acute chroniclers of contemporary culture," says the Washington Post in a photo tribute. But she was also, as People notes, "one of the most powerful figures in Hollywood." Other big titles include Silkwood, You've Got Mail, and Julie and Julia, not to mention her novel Heartburn and her best-selling 2006 collection of essays I Feel Bad About My Neck. Ephron died of complications from a blood disorder called myelodysplasia, which was diagnosed six years ago, reports the Post. (Its full obituary is here.) Salon: "Her cultural influence is so elemental ... she's like hydrogen." The site rounds up its interviews with her here. New York Times: "She was a journalist, a blogger, an essayist, a novelist, a playwright, an Oscar-nominated screenwriter and a movie director—a rarity in a film industry whose directorial ranks were and continue to be dominated by men." What's more, she was successful in all those things. Huffington Post: She "belonged to America's top tier of filmmakers, but her talents extended far beyond Hollywood."

final_summary:
In a move that has sparked widespread controversy, Poland's Senate has approved a bill making it illegal to accuse the Polish nation or state of complicity in the Nazi Holocaust, punishable by fines or up to three years in prison. The bill, which still requires the signature of President Andrzej Duda to become law, aims to counter what the Polish government perceives as inaccurate and hurtful language, particularly the use of the term "Polish death camps" to describe the concentration camps established by Nazi Germany in occupied Poland during World War II. 

However, the bill has been met with fierce opposition from Israel, with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu describing it as an attempt to rewrite history and deny the Holocaust. Israeli lawmakers have responded by proposing a bill that would expand the country's existing Holocaust denial laws to include a five-year jail sentence for anyone denying or minimizing the role of Nazi collaborators, including Poles, in the Holocaust. The new bill would also provide legal aid to Holocaust survivors who are prosecuted in foreign countries for sharing their stories.

Historians argue that while the Polish state was not directly involved in the Holocaust, many Poles collaborated with the Nazis, either by informing on Jews in hiding or participating in massacres. According to Efraim Zuroff, a historian and "Nazi-hunter" at the Simon Wiesenthal Center, the number of Polish collaborators runs into "many thousands."

The Polish government maintains that the bill is necessary to protect the country's reputation and defend historical truth, with President Duda stating that Poland has the right to do so. However, critics argue that the bill could stifle free speech and academic discourse, and undermine efforts to uncover the complexities of the Holocaust. The US State Department has also expressed concerns, urging Poland to reconsider the bill due to its potential impact on free speech and strategic relationships with the US and Israel.

The controversy has led to a deterioration in relations between Poland and Israel, with the Israeli ambassador to Poland, Anna Azari, speaking out against the bill. In response, Polish politicians have expressed surprise and sadness at the Israeli reaction, with Senator Stanislaw Karczewski stating that the bill is intended to protect the truth and dignity of Poles.

The Polish government's stance on the matter is closely tied to its nationalist agenda, which emphasizes the country's status as a victim of both Nazi and Soviet aggression during World War II. However, the use of the term "Polish death camps" is seen as a misrepresentation of history, implying that the Polish state was complicit in the atrocities committed by the Nazis.

The controversy has also highlighted the complexities of Polish-Jewish relations during the war, with some Poles having saved Jewish lives while others participated in their persecution. The Polish government has acknowledged that some Poles profited from blackmailing Jews in hiding, but maintains that the majority of the population suffered under Nazi occupation.

As the dispute continues, the Polish parliament has approved the bill, and it remains to be seen whether President Duda will sign it into law. The move has sparked an intense debate about the limits of free speech, historical accuracy, and national identity, with far-reaching implications for Poland's relationship with Israel and the international community.

reference: – Despite US pleas to rethink proposed legislation regulating Holocaust speech, Poland's Senate went ahead and passed the bill, reports the BBC. The legislation has sparked a diplomatic dispute with Israel, and the US had argued that it could hurt freedom of speech as well as strategic relationships. US State Department spokeswoman Heather Nauert voiced her government's concerns as the Polish Senate was preparing to approve the bill Wednesday; the bill later passed by 57 votes to 23. The measure next needs to be signed into law by the president, who supports it. Poland's conservative ruling Law and Justice party authored the bill, which calls for up to three years in prison for any intentional attempt to falsely attribute the crimes of Nazi Germany to the Polish state or people, the AP reports. Law and Justice says it is fighting against the use of phrases like "Polish death camps" to refer to death camps operated by Nazi Germany in occupied Poland during World War II. Israel, however, sees the move as an attempt to whitewash the role some Poles played in the killing of Jews during World War II. Nauert said the US understands that phrases like "Polish death camps" are "inaccurate, misleading, and hurtful," but voiced concern the legislation could "undermine free speech and academic discourse." Members of Poland's Jewish community also weighed in Wednesday, saying in an open letter that they strongly oppose usage of the phrase "Polish death camps," but also oppose the law.

final_summary:
In Washington D.C., Senator Rand Paul staged an almost 11-hour filibuster to express his opposition to the renewal of the Patriot Act, labeling it an infringement on civil liberties. Meanwhile, former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton emphasized the importance of making childcare a national priority, urging Congress to allocate more funds to support working families. 

On the presidential campaign trail, Jeb Bush criticized President Barack Obama's decision to withdraw troops from Iraq, but stopped short of committing to send additional ground forces to combat ISIS militants. 

Internationally, protests erupted in Burundi over President Pierre Nkurunziza's bid for a third term, resulting in one death and the postponement of parliamentary elections. In Ireland, citizens prepared to vote on same-sex marriage, marking a significant shift from the country's previous stance on LGBTQ+ rights.

In the United States, the Miami-Dade County elections office struggled to finalize results due to a large number of absentee ballots, with officials expecting to complete the count by Thursday. The delay led to concerns about the accuracy of the outcome, particularly in the presidential election, where President Obama held a narrow lead over Mitt Romney. 

The presidential election saw several minor candidates receive notable votes, including Jill Stein, Virgil Goode, Roseanne Barr, Rocky Anderson, and Thomas Hoefling, although none posed a significant challenge to the major candidates. According to preliminary estimates, approximately 8% of votes may still be uncounted, potentially affecting the final tally.

reference: – Not a bad result for Roseanne Barr: Only five people finished ahead of her in this year's presidential election, notes the Independent Political Report. The comedian ran on the pro-pot Peace and Freedom ticket and drew more than 49,500 votes, according to PBS election results. That puts her in sixth, behind Obama (61 million), Romney (58 million), Gary Johnson (1.2 million), Jill Stein (410,000), and Virgil Goode (115,000). Note: an earlier version of this file based on Google's election results said she placed fifth, but those results left out Goode.) MyFox Tampa Bay notes that Barr drew about 8,000 votes in Florida, which, by the way, remains too close to call for either Obama or Romney. (Don't hold your breath for a result tonight—the Miami Herald says votes in Miami-Dade won't be fully counted until tomorrow.) No hard feelings from Roseanne, though. She tweeted congratulations to President Obama, notes the Huffington Post.

final_summary:
On October 24, 2014, a devastating school shooting occurred at Marysville-Pilchuck High School in Washington state, resulting in the deaths of two students and injuring four others. The shooting took place during an early lunch break at approximately 10:39 a.m., with the first 911 call received shortly thereafter. 

According to eyewitness accounts, the shooter, identified as freshman Jaylen Fryberg, targeted a table of students, specifically his friends, and fired multiple rounds from a Beretta.40-caliber handgun, which was later found to have been owned by his father. Witnesses described the scene as chaotic, with students screaming and running for cover.

Jaylen Fryberg, who was recently named the school's freshman homecoming prince, appeared to have acted deliberately, targeting specific individuals. His motives remain unclear, although some students suggested he may have been upset about a romantic relationship. Fryberg's social media accounts depicted a complex individual, showcasing both joyous moments and expressions of angst.

The victims included two girls, who were both critically injured and underwent surgery, and two boys, ages 14 and 15, who were also seriously hurt. The 14-year-old boy suffered a jaw injury, while the 15-year-old was critically injured in the head. 

As authorities worked to piece together the events surrounding the shooting, they discovered that Fryberg had been involved in a physical altercation two weeks prior, reportedly sparked by a racial slur directed at him. However, it remains uncertain whether this incident contributed to his actions.

Fryberg's family is prominent within the Tulalip Tribes, with his grandfather serving as the director of fish and wildlife. Community members described Jaylen as a well-liked individual, and his death sent shockwaves throughout the community.

In response to the tragedy, authorities launched an investigation, with police officers conducting door-to-door searches to ensure the campus was secure. The school was subsequently closed through the following week, and classes were canceled. The incident highlighted concerns about mental health services, particularly in light of Marysville's recent receipt of a $10 million federal grant aimed at improving student mental health support.

As the community grappled with the aftermath, parents and students alike struggled to comprehend the senseless violence that had unfolded. The incident served as a stark reminder of the fragility of life and the importance of addressing underlying issues that may contribute to such tragedies.

reference: – A shooter who opened fire in a high school near Seattle is dead, police say. He reportedly shot other students at Marysville-Pilchuck High School before taking his own life, but the details are still firming up. The Seattle Times reports that one of his victims is dead and that four other students are injured. A student at the school says the gunman, reportedly a freshman, walked up to a table in the cafeteria with four to six people and opened fire. Police say they are confident the gunman acted alone, reports CNN. A lockdown at the school, located in Washington state's Marysville, was being lifted. “Everyone just started running," recalls 14-year-old Adam Holston. "I could hear the gunshots, and my heart was racing and we didn’t know what was going on.”

final_summary:
On a Halloween evening, a group of four friends visited Nashville Nightmare, a popular haunted house attraction in Tennessee, expecting a thrilling experience. However, their night took a dark turn when a man, later identified as a possible employee, approached them while dressed in a costume and handed one of the women, Tawnya Greenfield, a knife. Believing it to be a prop, Greenfield played along, responding affirmatively to the man's question about whether one of her friends was "messing around" with her. The man then instructed her to "stab" another member of the group, James Yochim, who was subsequently stabbed in the left arm.

Greenfield, thinking the knife was fake, complied with the instruction, plunging the blade into Yochim's arm. It wasn't until she pulled back that she realized the horrifying truth – the knife was real, and Yochim was bleeding profusely. A hole was visible in his shirt, and blood was squirting from his wound. Medics were called, and Yochim was rushed to Skyline Hospital for treatment.

According to eyewitness accounts, two other members of the group corroborated Greenfield's story, stating that they too believed the knife was a prop. The perpetrator, described as a thin man wearing a straw hat, fled the scene after the incident. An investigation by the Metro Police is ongoing, with Nashville Nightmare confirming that the individual involved is believed to be an employee who has been placed on administrative leave pending further inquiry.

Nashville Nightmare has maintained that the safety and security of its patrons is its top priority, citing an eight-year history of hosting the event without major incidents. The company has robust safety protocols in place, including metal detectors and on-site medical and security personnel. While the exact circumstances surrounding the incident are still being reviewed, the company has pledged to cooperate fully with the police investigation and is re-examining its safety procedures to prevent similar incidents in the future.

reference: – Halloween is James Yochim's favorite time of year: "I get really excited about going to haunted houses." His Friday night at Nashville Nightmare in Madison—a sprawling attraction with four haunted houses, an escape room, and scary cast members running around with fake weapons—might dull the thrill, however. Yochim says a thin man with skull makeup, assumed to be an actor, approached his friend, Tawnya Greenfield, asking if Yochim was "f—ing around with her," per BuzzFeed. When she jokingly said yes, the man handed her a knife and said "stab him," Yochim tells the Nashville Tennessean. "Keep in mind, we'd been chased by chainsaws, holding other weapons, all kinds of stuff all night, and it was all fake," he adds. "So she stabs at me with it, and everything got really black." The blade—very much real—went through the 29-year-old's forearm. Though it didn't hit any major arteries, bones, or tendons, "his arm was gushing blood like something out of a horror scene," Greenfield tells Fox 17. Nine stitches later, Yochim recalls the unknown man apologizing as he lay bleeding, saying, "I didn't know my knife was that sharp." But according to Yochim, there were no weapons allowed inside the attraction and visitors were required to go through "very thorough" metal detectors. In a Tuesday statement, Nashville Nightmare's organizers said an employee believed to be "involved in some way" had been placed on leave "until we can determine his involvement." An initial police report said the suspect had not been identified. No charges have been laid.

final_summary:
Actress Ariel Winter, known for her role in the popular television show "Modern Family", has been unapologetic about expressing herself through her fashion choices on social media. In an interview with Elite Daily, Winter stated that she no longer cares about what others think of her posts on Instagram, saying "If I can't win, I'm going to work on a relationship with myself...And for me, now I post a photo and I don't care." This sentiment reflects her confidence and self-assurance, which she encourages others to adopt, stating that individuals should focus on feeling good about themselves rather than conforming to societal expectations.

However, Winter faced criticism for her choice of attire at a recent event for the ABC series "Modern Family". She wore a glamorous, gold minidress with mesh panels that exposed parts of her body, which led to negative comments from some viewers. One commenter called her "insecure" and suggested she was trying to look "slutty", while another compared her to actress Lindsay Lohan. Winter responded to these criticisms on Instagram, asking "Why TF does anyone care that I didn’t dress casual like everyone else for the panel?" and asserting that people should "wear whatever you want people!" and "let other people feel good about themselves and do what they want."

Winter's defense of her fashion choice highlights her commitment to individuality and self-expression. She emphasized that as long as someone feels confident and comfortable in their own skin, that's all that truly matters. This message resonates with her fans, who appreciate her unwavering confidence and willingness to speak out against negativity.

final_summary:
On Wednesday, conservative radio talk show host Rush Limbaugh expressed astonishment at the Republican establishment's shock over Rick Santorum's three-state win, describing them as "insulated" and unaware of the growing support for the former Pennsylvania senator. Limbaugh attributed the media's portrayal of conservative voters as "mind-numbed robots" following his lead as an insult and demeaning, emphasizing that voters are making informed decisions based on their genuine dissatisfaction with Mitt Romney rather than solely due to his influence. 

Limbaugh, who has avoided endorsing a candidate to maintain credibility, stated that he was not surprised by Santorum's success, citing his understanding of the Republican base's sentiments. He argued that Santorum's conservative credentials and lack of corruption set him apart from other candidates, resonating with voters who seek a true conservative leader.

Meanwhile, Santorum addressed a gathering of approximately 100 pastors in McKinney, Texas, where he clarified that his presidential bid is not driven by a desire to become the "pastor-in-chief" but rather to uphold the nation's founding principles rooted in morality. He acknowledged his victories in Missouri, Minnesota, and Colorado, attributing his success to being underestimated by opponents and the media.

Santorum emphasized the importance of freedom of religion, criticizing the Obama administration's stance on requiring institutions to cover contraception and emergency contraception, which he believes infringes upon individuals' right to practice their faith freely. He shared his personal testimony, discussing his conversion experience through Bible study groups and his experiences with loss, including the death of his newborn son Gabriel in 1996 and his daughter Bella's struggles with Trisomy 18.

During the event, Santorum also discussed his involvement in restricting abortion rights, highlighting the risks associated with speaking out on the issue, and addressed the California Proposition 8 ruling, labeling it an example of the "tolerance of the left" and accusing those who oppose same-sex marriage of being labeled as "bigots." The gathering concluded with the attendees surrounding Santorum and praying for his well-being, safety, and candidacy.

Meanwhile, Santorum addressed a gathering of approximately 100 pastors in McKinney, Texas, where he clarified that his presidential bid is not driven by a desire to become the "pastor-in-chief" but rather to uphold the nation's founding principles rooted in morality. He acknowledged his victories in Missouri, Minnesota, and Colorado, attributing his success to being underestimated by opponents and the media.

Santorum emphasized the importance of freedom of religion, criticizing the Obama administration's stance on requiring institutions to cover contraception and emergency contraception, which he believes infringes upon individuals' right to practice their faith freely. He shared his personal testimony, discussing his conversion experience through Bible study groups and his experiences with loss, including the death of his newborn son Gabriel in 1996 and his daughter Bella's struggles with Trisomy 18.

During the event, Santorum also discussed his involvement in restricting abortion rights, highlighting the risks associated with speaking out on the issue, and addressed the California Proposition 8 ruling, labeling it an example of the "tolerance of the left" and accusing those who oppose same-sex marriage of being labeled as "bigots." The gathering concluded with the attendees surrounding Santorum and praying for his well-being, safety, and candidacy.

reference: – That triple bogey must have stung: Mitt Romney made a point to call out Rick Santorum by name today. Speaking to reporters in Atlanta, Romney said Santorum and Newt Gingrich were part of the Republican party that "lost its way" by spending too much federal money while in office, reports CNN. "In this race, I'm the only guy that hasn't spent time in Washington," he said. "And Senator Santorum and Speaker Gingrich, they are the very Republicans who acted like Democrats." Santorum, for his part, spoke to a group of pastors in Texas and said he has "no desire to be pastor of this country," notes ABC News. "But that doesn’t mean I’m not going to stand and fight for the things that are consistent with what this country was founded upon, which was a moral foundation." He also alluded to his big night: "The gift of being underestimated is a wonderful gift." Rush Limbaugh, meanwhile, took delight in pointing out how "shocked" the GOP establishment was after Santorum's wins, which he says only proves "how insulated they are," reports Politico.

final_summary:
In the final months of his presidency, the Obama White House underwent significant changes, including modifications to its online presence. A series of crawls of the Obama White House website and subdomains between November 2016 and January 2017 revealed updates to various sections, although specific details about these changes were not specified.

However, one notable development occurred earlier in President Obama's term when he launched the official Twitter account @POTUS from the Oval Office on May 18, 2015. In his inaugural tweet, President Obama introduced himself to the platform, stating "Hello, Twitter! It's Barack. Really! Six years in, they're finally giving me my own account." This marked a new channel for direct engagement with the American public, aligning with the administration's goal of being the most open and participatory in history.

The @POTUS account was intended to provide a unique opportunity for President Obama to share his thoughts on pressing issues, fostering dialogue with citizens. To facilitate this interaction, the White House maintained multiple official Twitter handles, each associated with different officials, including the First Lady Michelle Obama (@FLOTUS), Vice President Joe Biden (@VP), and Press Secretary Josh Earnest (@PressSec).

Additionally, users could access live coverage from the White House through @WHLive, while those interested in Spanish-language updates could follow @LaCasaBlanca. Other notable accounts included those belonging to senior advisors Valerie Jarrett (@VJ44) and communications director Jen Psaki (@Psaki44), as well as the chief official White House photographer Pete Souza (@PeteSouza).

Furthermore, the White House emphasized the importance of user control over their online presence, particularly regarding location data. Users could opt to include location information in their tweets, either from the web or through third-party apps, but also had the ability to delete their location history if desired. This feature was highlighted in two separate tweets, which provided additional context on how to manage location settings.

Meanwhile, @WhiteHouse features a picture of Obama with his phone, captioned: "President Obama. In the Oval Office. Tweeting." He's also been welcomed to Twitter by his wife and his VP, among others.
