final_summary:
At a Senate Intelligence Committee hearing focused on foreign interference on social media, Twitter's Jack Dorsey and Facebook's Sheryl Sandberg testified alongside other industry leaders. Notably, Infowars host Alex Jones, who has been banned from several major platforms, including Twitter, YouTube, and Facebook, made a surprise appearance at the hearing. Despite being barred from these platforms for violating their terms of service, Jones claimed his online presence remained strong, although data suggests otherwise.

Jones's attendance at the hearing seemed motivated by a desire to regain attention and provoke a reaction. During a recess, he approached Republican Senator Marco Rubio of Florida in the hallway, interrupting Rubio's interview with a reporter. Jones accused the US government of practicing censorship similar to China's, prompting Rubio to attempt to respond before eventually ignoring him. However, Jones continued to heckle Rubio, calling him a "snake" and a "frat boy" while laughing.

As the situation escalated, Rubio told Jones not to touch him again, which Jones interpreted as an attempt to get him arrested. Rubio denied this claim, stating he would "take care of" Jones himself. The exchange became increasingly heated, with Jones suggesting Rubio had threatened physical violence. Meanwhile, Jones livestreamed his arrival at the hearing and sat in the front row, mere feet away from Dorsey and Sandberg, sparking controversy given his recent bans from major social media platforms.

reference: – The biggest stars of Wednesday's Senate Intelligence Committee hearing seem to be an empty chair and Alex Jones of InfoWars. The former was reserved for Google's leadership, which unlike Twitter and Facebook was a no-show. The latter got into it with Sen. Marco Rubio outside the hearing, which Fast Company notes is open to the public; Politico reports Jones sat in the first row just behind Sheryl Sandberg and Jack Dorsey. During a hearing recess, Daily Beast reporter Will Sommer tweeted that Jones went into the hallway outside the room and got into a "heated exchange" with Rubio. Video Sommer tweeted of the exchange shows Rubio speaking with reporters as Jones keeps interrupting with questions and statements before moving on to calling Rubio a "snake" and "frat boy." Rubio replies, "I swear to God, I don't know who you are, man." As Jones tries to clarify, Rubio tells him "not to touch me again." Replies Jones, "I'm just patting you nicely," before suggesting Rubio wants to get him arrested. Rubio says, "You're not going to get arrested man. I'll take care of you myself." Jones tells him, "You are literally like a little gangster thug." As Rubio excuses himself to go back to the committee, he refers to Jones as "this clown." Responds Jones, "Go back to your bathhouse."

final_summary:
In a recent interview with "Face to Face With Jon Ralston," Republican Senate candidate Sharron Angle attempted to soften her stance on several key issues, including her previous calls to "phase out" Social Security and her suggestion that citizens might resort to "Second Amendment remedies" if conservatives failed to win the election. However, despite some moderation, Angle remained resolute in her opposition to extending unemployment benefits, claiming that they discourage people from seeking employment.

Angle's comments on Social Security were significantly toned down, shifting from advocating for the program's elimination to proposing that existing benefits be protected, with additional funding and offering younger workers the option of personal accounts alongside traditional Social Security benefits. Her stance on gun rights, however, remained unchanged, with Angle stating that she had only slightly overstated her initial assertion regarding the use of Second Amendment remedies.

On the topic of abortion, Angle reiterated her unwavering commitment to anti-abortion policies, asserting that government should not intervene in reproductive decisions, citing the 1973 Roe v. Wade ruling as the catalyst for government involvement. This stance aligns with her past legislative actions, including sponsoring a bill requiring the dissemination of information linking abortion to breast cancer risks.

Regarding Yucca Mountain, Angle suggested abandoning Nevada's long-standing efforts to prevent the storage of nuclear waste near Las Vegas, instead advocating for creating jobs through the acceptance of the waste. Angle consistently shifted focus towards criticizing her opponent, Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid, attributing Nevada's economic woes to his policies and calling for a debate on high unemployment, foreclosures, and bankruptcies.

A separate radio interview with Bill Manders revealed Angle's unyielding stance on abortion, suggesting that even in cases of rape or incest, women should not have control over their reproductive choices due to her Christian faith. This position has garnered support from the far-right, who see it as a defining aspect of her candidacy. However, some Republicans, including former Congresswoman Barbara Vucanovich, have expressed reservations about Angle's rigidity and difficulty in articulating her positions, potentially impacting her chances in the general election.

reference: – A little thing like rape isn't a good enough excuse for an abortion, as far as Sharron Angle's concerned. Harry Reid's conservative, Tea Party-backed opponent gave an interview in January, in which she told conservative host Bill Manders that rape victims need to trust in God's plan. “Is there any reason at all for an abortion?” Manders asks in the clip, which was dug up by Think Progress. “Not in my book,” Angle replies. Not even, Manders presses, in the case of rape or incest? “Well, you know, I'm a Christian,” Angle says, “and I believe that God has a plan and a purpose for each one of our lives and that he can intercede in all kinds of situations and we need to have a little faith.” Angle got pressed on the issue yesterday in her first interview with the mainstream press since winning the GOP nomination. (More details from it at the Las Vegas Sun.) “You want government to go and tell a 13-year-old child who’s been raped by her father she has to have that baby?” asked her interviewer. “I didn’t say that," she responded. "I always say that I value life.”

final_summary:
At the box office, Sony Pictures scored a much-needed victory with the debut of "Sausage Party", an animated comedy that raked in $33.6 million, marking the largest August opening ever for an animated film. The film, backed by Annapurna Pictures, features a voice cast including Seth Rogen, Kristen Wiig, Edward Norton, Jonah Hill, and Bill Hader, and boasts an R-rating due to its liberal use of sex jokes and profanity. According to analysts, the film's success can be attributed to its unique and original concept, which resonated with adult audiences seeking a break from the usual summer blockbuster fare.

"Sausage Party" held the number two spot at the box office, while Warner Bros.' "Suicide Squad" took the top spot for a second consecutive weekend, albeit with a significant 67% decline in ticket sales from its opening weekend. Despite receiving scathing reviews, the film has still managed to rake in $465.4 million globally, with comScore senior media analyst Paul Dergarabedian considering it a commercial success.

Disney's "Pete's Dragon" opened in third place with a modest $21.5 million, despite receiving positive reviews from both audiences and critics. However, the film's underwhelming performance raises questions about the viability of traditional family-friendly movies in today's market. Meanwhile, the Matt Damon-starrer "Jason Bourne" continued to perform steadily, adding $13.6 million to its domestic total, while the comedy "Bad Moms" rounded out the top five with $11.5 million.

Paramount's "Florence Foster Jenkins", a biographical drama starring Meryl Streep, opened in sixth place with $6.6 million, appealing primarily to older audiences. The film received positive reviews and may benefit from word-of-mouth and potential award season buzz. In limited release, the Western heist film "Hell or High Water" impressed with a strong $592,000 from 32 theaters, boasting a 99% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes.

Overall, the weekend's box office results indicate a shift towards more original and edgy content, with August emerging as a prime platform for unconventional releases like "Sausage Party". As the industry continues to navigate changing audience preferences, studios are taking note of the success of films that push boundaries and defy expectations.

reference: – In case anyone wondered whether Seth Rogen could make what amounts to a movie-length penis joke and get people to flock to it, the answer is yes. Sausage Party scored this weekend with a solid $33.6 million debut, reports Variety. That wasn't enough to unseat Suicide Squad, which USA Today notes chalked up a $43.8 million win that was nevertheless "a brutal 67% drop from its August-record $133.7 million opening." Coming in third was Pete's Dragon, with a lackluster $21.6 million debut. And rounding out the top five were Jason Bourne with $13.6 million and Bad Moms with $11.5 million.

final_summary:
President-elect Donald Trump has continued to cast doubt on the intelligence community's assessment of Russian interference in the 2016 US presidential election, despite overwhelming evidence suggesting otherwise. On Tuesday, Trump took to Twitter to mock the intelligence agencies, claiming they had delayed a scheduled briefing on alleged Russian hacking because they "needed more time to build a case." This assertion was contradicted by US intelligence officials, who stated that the briefing was never scheduled for Tuesday and that the meeting with top officials, including Director of National Intelligence James Clapper, National Security Agency Director Adm. Mike Rogers, FBI Director James Comey, and CIA Director John Brennan, was actually set for Friday.

Trump's comments follow a pattern of skepticism towards the intelligence community, which has concluded that Russia directed hacks against the Democratic Party and the campaign of Hillary Clinton. Trump has repeatedly questioned the assessment, stating that hacking is a "very hard thing to prove" and that he knows "things that other people don't know, so they can't be sure of the situation." His views were echoed by WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange, whom Trump supported in a tweet, saying that Assange claimed Russia was not the source of the leaked emails and that a 14-year-old could have carried out the hack on John Podesta's email account.

The intelligence community has maintained that Russia intervened in the election to aid Trump's candidacy, and multiple US agencies, including the FBI and CIA, have concluded that the information released through WikiLeaks and other outlets was intended to help Trump win. Trump's refusal to accept the intelligence community's assessment has raised concerns among lawmakers, with Senator Chuck Schumer describing his actions as "really dumb" and warning that taking on the intelligence community can lead to repercussions.

Meanwhile, Trump's transition team has faced criticism over the lack of transparency regarding the president-elect's meetings with intelligence officials. Trump has received sporadic classified briefings, but has yet to meet with the heads of US intelligence agencies, unlike President Barack Obama, who met with them in his first weeks as president-elect. The delay in receiving a full briefing on Russian hacking has led to speculation about Trump's intentions and whether he will ultimately accept the intelligence community's assessment.

In related news, Trump's nominee for Secretary of State, Rex Tillerson, has agreed to cut ties with ExxonMobil and comply with conflict-of-interest requirements, departing with a $180 million retirement package. Additionally, a plan to gut the independent Office of Congressional Ethics was scrapped after a backlash, with lawmakers reversing the decision in an emergency meeting.

reference: – Donald Trump launched what the BBC describes as a "fresh assault on America's intelligence community" with a tweet Tuesday night accusing them of delaying a briefing—and putting "intelligence" in quotes. "The 'Intelligence' briefing on so-called 'Russian hacking' was delayed until Friday, perhaps more time needed to build a case," Trump tweeted. "Very strange!" Officials, however, tell CNN that the briefing had never been scheduled for Tuesday, and even President Obama has yet to receive the final report from top intelligence officials on the alleged Russian hacking of the presidential election. Officials say Trump or his team may have mistakenly thought his regular intelligence briefing Tuesday would be the Russia-related one. A senior intelligence official tells NBC News that the director of national intelligence and heads of the NSA, CIA, and FBI are among those scheduled to meet with Trump on Friday. The Washington Post reports that Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called Trump's attitude toward intelligence services "really dumb" Tuesday night. "Let me tell you, you take on the intelligence community, they have six ways from Sunday at getting back at you," he told Rachel Maddow. "From what I am told, they are very upset with how he has treated them and talked about them." A source tells the Post that the report on Russian hacking could be on Obama's desk as soon as Thursday. A declassified version might be released to the public early next week, the source says. (At least one Trump adviser disagrees with the president-elect's skepticism.)

final_summary:
In the highly publicized court-martial of US Army Major Nidal Hasan, the prosecution concluded its case against the former army psychiatrist, who is accused of perpetrating the deadliest mass shooting on a US military base in history. The incident occurred on November 5, 2009, at Fort Hood in central Texas, resulting in the deaths of 13 people and injuring over 30 others. 

Prosecutor Colonel Steve Henricks delivered a detailed closing argument, emphasizing the evidence that demonstrated Hasan's premeditation and intent to kill. He highlighted Hasan's visit to a gun store just months prior to the attack, where he requested the highest-tech weapon available, and his subsequent practice sessions at a gun range using laser sights. The prosecutor also pointed out that Hasan specifically targeted soldiers, sparing most civilians, and later claimed the attack was a "training exercise" when confronted by bystanders.

Hasan, who acted as his own attorney, surprisingly chose not to deliver a closing statement, stating simply, "The defense chooses not to make a closing statement." This marked a stark contrast to his earlier assertion that the evidence would "clearly show" he was the shooter. Throughout the 13-day trial, Hasan raised few objections and questioned only three of the nearly 90 witnesses called by the prosecution. He presented a single piece of evidence – an evaluation from his boss praising him as a good soldier.

The prosecution's case relied heavily on the testimony of survivors and witnesses, painting a harrowing picture of the events unfolding inside the deployment processing center. The jury, comprising 13 high-ranking military officers, was tasked with determining Hasan's level of guilt, as he had previously acknowledged being the shooter. The prosecution sought a unanimous conviction on premeditated murder charges, which would enable them to pursue the death penalty.

Colonel Henricks emphasized that Hasan's actions were motivated by a perceived jihad duty to kill as many soldiers as possible, citing his desire to prevent the deployment of troops to Afghanistan and Iraq. The prosecution also highlighted Hasan's communication with Anwar al-Awlaki, a US-born cleric linked to Al-Qaeda, although the judge excluded this evidence due to concerns about its relevance.

Hasan's defense strategy centered around his assertion that he was motivated by a sense of justice and a desire to protect his fellow Muslims. In a mental health evaluation, he stated that he believed his actions were justified because they served a higher purpose. He also expressed a willingness to become a martyr, suggesting that he saw his potential execution as a means to achieve that goal.

As the trial drew to a close, the jury deliberated for several hours before adjourning for the night, with plans to resume deliberations the following morning. The outcome of the trial remains uncertain, with the possibility of a death sentence hanging in the balance.

reference: – It seems clear now that Nidal Hasan's decision to act as his own attorney in the Fort Hood shooting trial was really a decision to not act as an attorney of any kind. A day after he rested his case without calling a single witness, Hasan skipped his closing argument, reports the AP. After prosecutors laid out in detail Hasan's role in the 2009 rampage that killed 13 people, the judge told Hasan he could go. "The defense chooses not to make a closing statement," he said. Hasan's decisions over the last two days seem to support the claim made by his defense team earlier in the military trial that he hopes to get the death penalty, notes CNN. He admitted in his opening statement that he was, in fact, the shooter. The jury of 13 officers will now begin deliberations. If they reach a unanimous guilty verdict, the death penalty is possible.

final_summary:
World of Beer, a Tampa, Florida-based chain of taverns with 77 locations nationwide, is offering a unique internship opportunity for three individuals to travel the world and taste various beers while documenting their experiences. The internship, dubbed "Drink It Intern," aims to provide a global experience for the selected candidates, aligning with the company's motto "drink it in." 

The internship will last for four months during the summer, allowing the chosen applicants to visit breweries across the United States and abroad, attend beer festivals, and write about their adventures. In addition to the $12,000 stipend, the company will also cover travel expenses, including food and lodging. 

To qualify for the position, applicants must be at least 21 years old and possess strong writing skills, particularly in social media content creation. The ideal candidate should also have a significant social media presence. 

Applications were initially accepted through two methods: submitting a video application online or participating in one of eight in-person interviews held at select World of Beer locations. Although some of the in-person interviews have already taken place in cities such as Washington D.C., Orlando, Columbus, Ohio, and Chicago, additional opportunities remain available in Orlando, Tempe, Arizona, College Station, Texas, and Tampa, Florida. 

Alyssa Ahern, a World of Beer representative, explained that the company developed the internship to offer a unique experience that reflects its brand values. She emphasized that the selected interns will have their work published in Draft Magazine. 

After reviewing the initial pool of applicants, the top ten candidates will undergo Skype interviews with World of Beer's corporate team, followed by the selection of the final three interns. Once chosen, they will embark on a four-month journey of beer exploration and documentation, culminating in a summer of beer-related activities.

history.draft:
World of Beer, a Tampa, Florida-based chain of taverns with 77 locations nationwide, is offering a unique internship opportunity for three individuals to travel the world and taste various beers while documenting their experiences. The internship, dubbed "Drink It Intern," aims to provide a global experience for the selected candidates, aligning with the company's motto "drink it in." 

The internship will last for four months during the summer, allowing the chosen applicants to visit breweries across the United States and abroad, attend beer festivals, and write about their adventures. In addition to the $12,000 stipend, the company will also cover travel expenses, including food and lodging. 

To qualify for the position, applicants must be at least 21 years old and possess strong writing skills, particularly in social media content creation. The ideal candidate should also have a significant social media presence. 

Applications were initially accepted through two methods: submitting a video application online or participating in one of eight in-person interviews held at select World of Beer locations. Although some of the in-person interviews have already taken place in cities such as Washington D.C., Orlando, Columbus, Ohio, and Chicago, additional opportunities remain available in Orlando, Tempe, Arizona, College Station, Texas, and Tampa, Florida. 

Alyssa Ahern, a World of Beer representative, explained that the company developed the internship to offer a unique experience that reflects its brand values. She emphasized that the selected interns will have their work published in Draft Magazine. 

After reviewing the initial pool of applicants, the top ten candidates will undergo Skype interviews with World of Beer's corporate team, followed by the selection of the final three interns. Once chosen, they will embark on a four-month journey of beer exploration and documentation, culminating in a summer of beer-related activities.

reference: – Want to get paid to travel and drink beer all summer? Of course you do—and World of Beer could make it happen. The tavern chain based in Tampa, Fla., is looking for three interns to send around the world for four months "to explore beer for themselves and share their stories with WOB drinkers across the country." The gig even comes with an impressive salary: $12,000 plus travel, food, and lodging expenses, reports Fox6. In other words, this might be "the best internship ever offered in the godforsaken history of internships," per Vice. World of Beer is particularly looking for strong writers—the interns' work will be published in Draft Magazine—and people with large followings on social media, a rep says. Even beer novices will be considered, per the company website. To apply, drop by one of three locations—in Tempe, Ariz.; College Station, Texas; and Tampa, Fla.—for a live interview or submit a one-minute video online. But make it snappy: Applications will only be accepted until Saturday, and 10 finalists will be chosen the first week of April. The three interns will be selected after Skype interviews.

final_summary:
Aaron Schock, a 27-year-old Republican congressman from Illinois, is taking an unconventional approach to governance by prioritizing health and fitness as a personal responsibility. Born in the 1980s, Schock is significantly younger than his colleagues, with the average age of a member of Congress being 57. Despite his youth, Schock has already been in politics for over a decade, winning his first election at 19 as a write-in candidate for a local school board position.

Schock's commitment to health and wellness is evident in his daily routine, which includes a 60- to 75-minute P90X training session, a 60- to 75-minute weight routine, and regular cardio exercises such as running or swimming. He also emphasizes the importance of nutrition, stating that maintaining a healthy physique requires discipline and planning. Schock's lead-by-example approach aims to inspire others to prioritize their health, citing Michelle Obama's "Let's Move" campaign as a positive step towards addressing childhood obesity.

As a member of Congress, Schock faces unique challenges, including navigating the pressures of the "hors d'oeuvres horror show," where lobbyists often use food to curry favor and influence policy. To combat this, Schock relies heavily on exercise to relieve stress and maintain his physical shape. He believes that exercising regularly is essential for managing the demands of public service and staying focused on long-term goals.

Schock's focus extends beyond individual health to broader issues affecting his generation, including energy independence, clean water, and education. He advocates for investing in renewable energy, developing a long-term strategy for managing water resources, and improving education by promoting innovative teaching methods and increasing funding for math and science programs.

Despite his youth, Schock has already gained recognition within the Republican Party, earning the title of "hottest freshman" in Congress and being named a deputy whip by House Republican leader Eric Cantor. Schock's ability to balance his media presence with a serious approach to policy has earned him respect from his peers, and he continues to work towards establishing himself as a leader in his party.

reference: – Republican Rep. Aaron Schock has already been named the "hottest freshman" in Congress—and now Men's Health declares that he's also the fittest. The Congressman from Illinois flaunts his abs on the magazine's June cover, and can brag that he's the first politician to appear shirtless in the mag—though President Obama has appeared in it, fully clothed, twice, the Chicago Tribune reports. Schock, who at nearly 30 is also the first US Congressman born in the 1980s, has already found himself featured on TMZ wearing only a bathing suit. A desire to use that chiseled-chest notoriety for a positive purpose led him to Men's Health, which dubbed him "pretty fly for a Republican from Peoria" and noted that "he looks more like a hit man from a European spy thriller than a boring politician." His appearance is meant to promote Fit for Life Summer Challenge, a healthy lifestyle campaign that urges Americans to lose 30 pounds by Labor Day.
